title: How to Build the Virtual Cell with Artificial Intelligence: Priorities and Opportunities
authors: Charlotte Bunne, Yusuf Roohani, Yanay Rosen, Ankit Gupta, Xikun Zhang, Marcel Roed, Theo Alexandrov, Mohammed AlQuraishi, Patricia Brennan, Daniel B. Burkhardt, Andrea Califano, Jonah Cool, Abby F. Dernburg, Kirsty Ewing, Emily B. Fox, Matthias Haury, Amy E. Herr, Eric Horvitz, Patrick D. Hsu, Viren Jain, Gregory R. Johnson, Thomas Kalil, David R. Kelley, Shana O. Kelley, Anna Kreshuk, Tim Mitchison, Stephani Otte, Jay Shendure, Nicholas J. Sofroniew, Fabian Theis, Christina V. Theodoris, Srigokul Upadhyayula, Marc Valer, Bo Wang, Eric Xing, Serena Yeung-Levy, Marinka Zitnik, Theofanis Karaletsos, Aviv Regev, Emma Lundberg, Jure Leskovec, Stephen R. Quake
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/01 - How to Build the Virtual Cell with AI_cleaned.md
oneSentence: A community Perspective (Chan Zuckerberg Initiative-catalyzed) proposing the vision of an AI Virtual Cell (AIVC): a multi-scale, multi-modal neural-network model learned directly from biological data, built from Universal Representations (molecular, cellular, multicellular) and Virtual Instruments, with a roadmap covering data needs, evaluation, interpretability, and open collaboration.
orientation: This is the canonical articulation of the AIVC vision our Perspective responds to, and it serves primarily as a high-value FOIL-and-concession source: it loudly champions models while itself conceding nearly every data-foundation gap our thesis names (spatial dissociation, destructive/temporal sampling, transcriptome over-reliance, modality breadth, data standards and cross-lab comparability). The same paper can be deployed as the named opponent ('models racing ahead') AND as authoritative support, since its own 'Data needs' section concedes the gaps. Most relevant to Claims 2, 3, 4, 8, 9 and the modality/temporal/spatial/standard axes; lightly touches Claim 1 (data-as-priority) and Claim 7 (microenvironment).

excerpts.text: Given their prevalence, the cellular UR will initially rely on transcriptomics measurements, while imaging modalities will be key for continued modeling of cellular spatial organization and dynamics.
excerpts.note: States the cellular representation will initially rely on transcriptomics, conceding the field's transcriptome-centric starting point.

excerpts.text: Data for the cellular UR consist of measurements mapped to a single cell level such as measurements of the transcriptome (scRNA-seq), chromatin accessibility (scATAC-seq), chromatin modification transcription factor binding, and proteome
excerpts.note: Lists single-cell modalities available for the cellular representation, with proteome appended last among sequencing-based readouts.

excerpts.text: Multicellular interactions can be analyzed after tissue dissociation (such as in scRNA-seq) <sup>104</sup> or in situ in a 2D section or 3D volume, where the tissue structure is preserved.
excerpts.note: Contrasts dissociation-based scRNA-seq with in situ approaches that preserve tissue structure, implying dissociation loses spatial structure.

excerpts.text: There are multiple methods to profile the spatial location of RNA <sup>105</sup>, and proteins <sup>106</sup> in cells, along with various imaging methods for select molecular species (e.g., immunohistochemistry), or with stains for tissue strucutre alone (e.g., haematoxylin and eosin (H&E)). Spatial molecular biology is currently a very active area of research and method development. While publicly available data are still limited, we foresee a rapid development in this domain providing multi-omic 2D and
excerpts.note: Concedes that publicly available spatial molecular data are still limited, while spatial molecular biology is a very active developing area (keep OCR 'strucutre').

excerpts.text: A more generalized data generation efort together with open frameworks for spatial data <sup>107</sup> could greatly accelerate modeling at the multicellular scale.
excerpts.note: Calls for more generalized spatial data generation and open data frameworks to accelerate multicellular-scale modeling.

excerpts.text: Data generation will require simultaneous exploration of temporal and physical scales, while allowing for system perturbations.
excerpts.note: States data generation must simultaneously cover temporal and physical (spatial) scales plus perturbations.

excerpts.text: Furthermore, biological processes span a vast range of timescales, from the fastest reactions happening in picoseconds, to a cell division happening in a day, tumor development occurring over years, and neurodegeneration over decades.
excerpts.note: Underscores the vast range of biological timescales (picoseconds to decades) that data must capture.

excerpts.text: The recent construction of universal cell atlases <sup>134,128</sup> may serve as a powerful resource for modeling cellular behavior over longer time scales, such as tissue formation. New approaches will be needed to build comparable data sets which capture the behavior of cells on shorter times scales, e.g., through methods such as live-cell imaging.
excerpts.note: Concedes that new approaches are needed to capture cell behavior on shorter timescales, pointing to live-cell imaging.

excerpts.text: Another important driver for the development of AI Virtual Cells will be multi-modal datasets. For example, datasets which bridge molecular and spatial scales, such as single cell transcriptomics data combined with histology to understand how cells interact and what molecular signatures underpin the formation of specialized spatial niches<sup>135</sup>. Further technological development is needed to collect multi-modal data that better captures the relationship between molecular signatures, cell behavior, cellular regulation and organization.
excerpts.note: Identifies multi-modal datasets bridging molecular and spatial scales as a key driver, conceding current data falls short and further development is needed.

excerpts.text: While a core interest of virtual cell modeling will focus on human datasets for the purpose of understanding disease and aiding the development of novel therapeutics, human datasets are limited in our ability to perform controlled experimentation and perturbations in vivo.
excerpts.note: Concedes a trade-off: human datasets are limited for controlled in vivo experimentation and perturbation.

excerpts.text: Finally, a key aspect of biological data generation will be the exploration of combinatorial spaces: biological spaces are commonly high dimensional, and enumerating their variants is intractable in general, e.g., when considering all possible variants of a genome. Even for combinations of a small number of entities, exemplified in the case of enumerating pairs or sets of perturbations <sup>58,115</sup>, experimental design becomes exceedingly challenging.
excerpts.note: Concedes that combinatorial perturbation spaces are intractable to enumerate experimentally, making experimental design exceedingly challenging.

excerpts.text: For instance, the Short Read Archive of biological sequence data holds over 14 petabytes of information <sup>142</sup> which is more than one thousand times larger than the dataset used to train ChatGPT <sup>143</sup>. Large parts of this data may be redundant, or have diminishing returns if used for training, and the scaling laws for models’ performances must be investigated thoroughly.
excerpts.note: Quantifies sequence data scale (>14 PB, >1000x ChatGPT's training set) but concedes much may be redundant or have diminishing returns, so quantity is not sufficient.

excerpts.text: In addition to data size, data diversity is critical to ensure model quality<sup>144</sup>. Data from humans and model organisms like mice and E. coli are unequally represented in sequence and literature databases, which when used for training, encode strong species biases <sup>144</sup>. Other biases, for example towards specific diseases or human ancestral populations could also reduce the impact of AIVC models <sup>145</sup>.
excerpts.note: Concedes that data diversity (not just size) is critical and that existing databases encode strong species and population biases that degrade models.

excerpts.text: A more important question for the development of AI Virtual Cells may not be “How to build them?”, but rather “How to build trust in their competence and fidelity?” To this end, a comprehensive and adaptable benchmarking framework will be needed.
excerpts.note: Argues the harder question is trust/fidelity, requiring a comprehensive benchmarking framework that does not yet exist.

excerpts.text: the AIVC will need to demonstrate generalizability across numerous biological contexts and downstream tasks. It must account for dynamic distributions that evolve due to environmental changes, infections, genetic variants and other such factors causing distribution shifts <sup>146</sup>.
excerpts.note: States the AIVC must generalize across contexts and handle distribution shifts, conceding generalization is an open challenge.

excerpts.text: Generalizability measures how well the model performs in unseen contexts such as novel cell types and genetic backgrounds. It can be evaluated through a cross-modal reconstruction task, such as predicting gene expression given the morphology of a previously unseen cell or the next image in a sequence of microscopy images of cell state.
excerpts.note: Proposes cross-modal reconstruction (e.g. predicting expression from morphology) as a generalizability test, implying modalities carry complementary information.

excerpts.text: Establishing such cross-modal benchmarks to link scales and modalities in cell biology is of imminent priority to the research community, as these tasks are both biologically useful and well-defined.
excerpts.note: Calls cross-modal benchmarks linking scales and modalities an imminent community priority.

excerpts.text: These eforts would greatly benefit from open data resources and data standards, a collaborative platform for cell modeling, and especially open benchmark datasets and common validation strategies to ensure their biological fidelity and real-world utility.
excerpts.note: Explicitly endorses open data resources, data standards, and common validation strategies, conceding their current absence.

excerpts.text: Besides the synchronization with data generators and other modeling eforts, collaboration with regulatory authorities and bioethics experts are crucial for benchmarking and establishing new norms that will expedite the deployment of AI Virtual Cells
excerpts.note: Stresses synchronization with data generators and establishing new norms/benchmarking standards.

excerpts.text: Two exciting revolutions in science and technology— in AI and in ’omics—now enable the construction of cell models learned directly from data.
excerpts.note: Central thesis of the paper: AI plus omics now enable fully data-driven cell models, framing data abundance as an enabler rather than the bottleneck (the foil to our data-bottleneck argument).

excerpts.text: Experimentally, the exponential increase in the throughput of measurement technologies has led to the collection of large and growing reference datasets within and across diferent cell and tissue systems <sup>27,28,29,30</sup>, with data doubling every six months for the past several years <sup>31</sup>, along with the ability to couple these measurements with systematic perturbations <sup>32,33,34,35</sup>.
excerpts.note: Quantitative claim that biological data is doubling every six months, used to argue data abundance enables the AIVC (a foil to the data-scarcity/bottleneck framing).

excerpts.text: (1) Multi-cellscale modeling: Cells operate on multiple scales across both time and space, from atomic to molecular to cellular and histological, with functional properties emerging through nonlinear transformation from one scale to another.
excerpts.note: Acknowledges cells operate across both time and space and multiple physical scales, with emergent properties across scales (OCR run-on 'Multi-cellscale').

excerpts.text: By training on a wide range of snapshots, time-resolved, non-interventional and interventional datasets collected across contexts and scales, the AIVC can develop an understanding of the molecular, cellular, and tissue dynamics that occur under natural or engineered signals.
excerpts.anchor: Predicting cell behavior and understanding mechanisms
excerpts.note: States the AIVC needs time-resolved datasets (alongside snapshots) to learn dynamics, implicitly conceding most data are static snapshots.

excerpts.text: The AIVC should also have the capability to simulate the temporal evolution of alterations in cell states in response to both intrinsic or extrinsic factors, along with the resulting multicellular spatial arrangements.
excerpts.anchor: Predicting cell behavior and understanding mechanisms
excerpts.note: States the AIVC should simulate temporal evolution of cell states together with their spatial arrangements, framing time and space as jointly required.

excerpts.text: The key step here would be to integrate learned representations of molecules with their quantities, and appropriately abstracted locations and timestamps, to create a unified representation of the cell <sup>72,73,74</sup>.
excerpts.note: Says a unified cell representation must integrate molecular quantities with locations and timestamps, naming spatial and temporal coordinates as required inputs.

excerpts.text: Complementing imaging approaches, mass spectrometry and proximity-dependent labeling can unveil proteinprotein associations and provide deeper insights into cell structure and signaling network rewiring <sup>86,87</sup>
excerpts.note: Notes mass spectrometry and proximity labeling reveal protein-protein associations and signaling rewiring, information beyond transcriptomics (OCR 'proteinprotein').

excerpts.text: Furthermore, variations in cellular URs can be linked to corresponding changes in molecular states or their spatial localization, influenced by downstream factors like genetic variants or functional changes in proteins that are represented in a lower scale of the AIVC.
excerpts.anchor: Predicting cell behavior and understanding mechanism
excerpts.note: Links cell-state variation to molecular spatial localization and to functional changes in proteins, treating protein-level function as load-bearing.

excerpts.text: While the AIVC would already accurately qualify the change in the expression of genes, tumor sequencing data would allow it to model the change of function of those genes, for example through loss of function, change in post-translational modifications, or rewiring of protein-protein interactions and signaling networks <sup>171</sup>.
excerpts.anchor: Unlocking the power of spatial biology to fight cancer
excerpts.note: Concedes that gene expression alone does not capture gene function; post-translational modifications and protein-interaction rewiring carry function that RNA cannot.

excerpts.text: Spatial structures in cancer, specifically within the tumor microenvironment (TME) are critical drivers of cancer progression, and can drive resistance to the immune system and limit drug eficacy <sup>164</sup>.
excerpts.anchor: Unlocking the power of spatial biology to fight cancer
excerpts.note: States spatial structures in the tumor microenvironment are critical drivers of cancer progression and drug resistance, supporting spatial-context importance.

excerpts.text: Thus, immune resistance must be understood in the spatial context of the cellular neighborhood in order to identify the specific cell states and gene signatures involved.
excerpts.anchor: Unlocking the power of spatial biology to fight cancer
excerpts.note: Argues immune resistance and cell states can only be understood in the spatial context of the cellular neighborhood.

excerpts.text: The same entity, profiled with diferent technologies, should have the same internal representation in an AIVC.
excerpts.note: Asserts an entity profiled by different technologies should map to one internal representation, a self-consistency/cross-platform comparability requirement.

excerpts.text: Models should propagate function across physical scales— interactions between molecules should have consistent efects when measuring binding afinity, gene expression, cell-cell communication or tissue organization.
excerpts.note: Requires models to propagate function consistently across scales and measurement types, implying cross-modal integration is required.

excerpts.text: A fundamental question for the collaborative development of AIVCs is which data and modalities should be collected to enable generalization across biological contexts and scales. These data will need to encompass the breadth of biology in diferent species, domains and modalities, representing the heterogeneity of life, while maintaining depth suficient to distinguish true signals from noise. A key aspect of data generation will be the simultaneous measurement of temporal and physical scales, while also allowing perturbations of the system.
excerpts.anchor: Box 1: Grand challenges (Understanding the value of diferent data types)
excerpts.note: Frames 'which data and modalities to collect' as a fundamental open question and calls for simultaneous temporal and physical (spatial) measurement with perturbations.

excerpts.text: For example, fluorescence confocal microscopy can help resolve the subcellular location of the human proteome <sup>79</sup>. Live-cell imaging<sup>80</sup> enables the study of proteins in living cells using time-lapse microscopy.
excerpts.note: Notes imaging can resolve subcellular protein location and live-cell time-lapse imaging tracks proteins over time, evidencing spatial and temporal protein measurement feasibility.

figuresNumbers.contains: Reference datasets with data doubling every six months for the past several years (ref 31).
Short Read Archive holds over 14 petabytes of biological sequence data (ref 142), more than one thousand times larger than the dataset used to train ChatGPT (ref 143).

The first whole-cell model (2012) represented all 482 genes and molecular functions known for Mycoplasma genitalium (ref 9). [OCR: text reads 'Mycobacterium genitalium' but the organism is Mycoplasma genitalium; see flags]
Processes span picoseconds (fastest reactions) to a day (cell division) to years (tumor development) to decades (neurodegeneration).

primarySources.citation: Thul, P. J. et al. A subcellular map of the human proteome. Science 356 (2017)

flags: Metadata unverified: no DOI found in the cleaned text (grep for '10.xxxx/' returned nothing); venue and year not stated in the body. Externally this is widely known as a Nature Perspective (2024-2025) catalyzed by a Chan Zuckerberg Initiative workshop, but it is NOT asserted in the source text, so venue/year/doi left as 'unverified; see flags'/null.

OCR quirks pervasive throughout the cleaned MD: 'diferent', 'eficient', 'efects', 'afinity', 'difusion', 'strucutre', run-on artifacts (e.g. 'Multi-cellscale', 'proteinprotein'); excerpts copied verbatim with these intact.

Source inconsistency: the text states the first whole-cell model represented 482 genes of 'Mycobacterium genitalium', but the organism in the cited work (Karr et al. 2012, ref 9) is Mycoplasma genitalium; likely OCR/text error in the cleaned MD.

Heavy figure-caption text bleed into the Main body in the cleaned MD (stray fragments like 'Molecular scale', 'Cellular scale', 'replication', 'translation cell' interleaved in paragraphs around lines 11-15); excerpt e22 was trimmed to the clean sentence to avoid bleed.

Author affiliations indicate the paper is led by Chan Zuckerberg Initiative + Stanford with Genentech (Bunne, Regev) and EvolutionaryScale (Sofroniew) industry coauthors; relevant only as context, not a roadmap claim.

Scope note: large portions of this Perspective (model architectures in Box 3 — transformers/CNNs/diffusion/GNNs; Virtual Instruments mechanics; drug discovery and diagnostics application vignettes) are about HOW to model, which is explicitly out of scope per the context file (section 6: do not write specific modeling methods); those were not captured except where a sentence doubles as a data-foundation concession.

Read at source: ref #106 (Lundberg & Borner, Spatial proteomics, Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol 2019) and ref #75 (Mahdessian et al., Spatiotemporal dissection of the cell cycle with single-cell proteogenomics, Nature 590, 2021) are first-hand sources worth citing directly for the spatial-proteomics and spatiotemporal-proteomics capability claims (Claims 2/6).

title: Grow AI virtual cells: three data pillars and closed-loop learning
authors: Liujia Qian, Zhen Dong, Tiannan Guo
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/02 - Grow AI virtual cells - three data pillars and closed-loop learning_cleaned.md
oneSentence: This Cell Research correspondence from the Guo group proposes that AI virtual cells (AIVCs) be grown on three data pillars - a priori knowledge, static architecture, and dynamic states - argues perturbation proteomics is the critical growth factor, advocates closed-loop active learning with robotic experimentation to fill dynamic-state gaps, and recommends starting with S. cerevisiae as the first model organism.
orientation: Same-group sibling perspective: it shares our broader stance that data (not algorithms) is the limiting nutrient for AIVCs and explicitly champions proteomics, spatial omics, multi-omics integration, and standards-setting - prime SUPPORT for our modality, spatial, and standard axes (claims 4,5,6,7,8,9). But its framing diverges from ours and can serve as a FOIL or nuance: it does not center the loss of space/time as the structural bottleneck (it organizes around static vs dynamic instead), it dismisses existing single-cell omics for state-similarity reasons rather than for space/time loss, and it largely sidesteps the destructive-measurement / paired-observation problem central to our claim 3. Use it as an ally on modality/proteomics/standards and as a contrast on the time/space and destructive-measurement framing.

excerpts.text: Pioneering whole-cell virtual models, such as those for Mycoplasma genitalium,2 Escherichia coli3 and Saccharomyces cerevisiae,4,5 were primarily based on a priori knowledge. However, they lack rigorously designed matched perturbation omics data and spatiotemporal imaging data.
excerpts.note: Early whole-cell models were knowledge-driven and explicitly lacked matched perturbation omics and spatiotemporal imaging data.

excerpts.text: While groundbreaking, these early models are limited in their ability to fully capture the dynamic nature and complexity of living cells, underscoring the need for more comprehensive data integration and advanced modeling approaches.
excerpts.note: Authors argue prior models failed to capture cellular complexity, calling for more comprehensive data integration.

excerpts.text: Bunne et al. 6 recently proposed the concept of Artificial Intelligence Virtual Cells (AIVCs), which integrate AI and multi-modal data to create comprehensive computational models of cellular functions.
excerpts.note: Defines AIVCs (per Bunne et al.) as integrating AI and multi-modal data; frames multimodality as core to the concept.

excerpts.text: Just as cell culture medium nourishes biological cells, what constitutes the ideal “culture medium” for growing these digital entities? Which cell types should we prioritize for virtual cultivation?
excerpts.note: Frames the open problem as what data ('culture medium') AIVCs need - aligning with the data-as-bottleneck premise.

excerpts.text: As we stand at the threshold of this new era in cellular modeling, the scientific community should collaborate to establish standards and best practices for AIVC development and validation.
excerpts.note: Explicit call to establish community standards and best practices for AIVC development and validation.

excerpts.text: Despite its vast size and diversity, this knowledge base primarily consists of fragmented information across different cell types and populations. While it is unrealistic to build a fully functional AIVC for a specific cell type solely from these data, they encapsulate fundamental cell biological mechanisms essential for model construction.
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: A priori knowledge is fragmented across cell types and cannot alone build a cell-specific AIVC.

excerpts.text: To achieve this, a comprehensive characterization of a specific cell is required, capturing its complete cellular structures at both the morphological and molecular expression levels, along with their interactions. We define this second essential pillar of AIVC construction as static architecture, which integrates nanoscale molecular structures and spatially resolved data from molecular modeling, cryo-electron microscopy, cryoelectron tomography, correlative light and electron microscopy, super-resolution fluorescence imaging, spatial omics, and other multi-scale analyses.
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: Defines a 'static architecture' pillar built from spatially resolved, multi-scale structural and imaging data (cryo-EM, spatial omics, super-resolution).

excerpts.text: Additionally, tissue expansion techniques7,8 can further enhance spatial resolution, complementing the highresolution imaging methods and omics technologies mentioned above. This integrated approach provides a detailed threedimensional context essential for accurate AIVC modeling.
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: Argues spatial/3D context (enhanced via tissue expansion) is essential for accurate AIVC modeling.

excerpts.text: While static data provide a bona fide snapshot of the cell, they fail to capture the dynamic nature of living systems. To construct a live AIVC, we introduce dynamic states as the third pillar for AIVC development. These data encompass natural processes such as aging, development, and carcinogenesis, as well as induced perturbations including physical, chemical, and genetic interventions.
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: Static snapshots cannot capture living dynamics; introduces a 'dynamic states' pillar covering natural processes and induced perturbations over time.

excerpts.text: Cellular dynamics are historically studied by measuring the expression or activity of one or a few molecules at a time. With advancements in high-throughput omics technologies — such as transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics — it is now possible to profile thousands of molecules across diverse cellular states.
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: High-throughput transcriptomics/proteomics/metabolomics now enable profiling thousands of molecules across cellular states.

excerpts.text: To build an effective AIVC, it is essential to comprehensively capture a wide range of cellular states and maximize their diversity to ensure high accuracy in differentiating them, requiring large volumes of dynamic, cell-specific data. Given the limited number of naturally occurring states, artificial perturbations serve as effective tools to generate different cellular states.
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: Effective AIVCs require large volumes of diverse dynamic, cell-specific data; artificial perturbations generate the needed state diversity.

excerpts.text: Among these, perturbation proteomics is particularly valuable, as proteins are the primary structural components and catalysts of cellular biochemical processes.9
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: Singles out perturbation proteomics as particularly valuable because proteins are the cell's primary structural and catalytic components.

excerpts.text: A recent AIVC pilot study integrating perturbation proteomics and AI algorithms demonstrates accurate prediction of drug efficacy and synergistic combinations, highlighting the critical role of dynamic perturbation proteomics data in constructing robust virtual cell models for drug discovery and 10 cellular simulation.
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: Cites a pilot study where perturbation proteomics + AI accurately predicted drug efficacy and synergy, evidencing the value of proteomics data for AIVCs.

excerpts.text: Although single-cell omics technologies have generated large datasets of millions of cells isolated from bulk tissues, their value for constructing AIVC is limited due to the similarity of the cells’ states.
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: Argues existing single-cell omics datasets have limited AIVC value - but attributes the limitation to similarity of cell states, not to loss of space/time (a different rationale from our thesis).

excerpts.text: Antibody-based methods, such as those used in the Human Protein Atlas,1 are valuable, while mass spectrometry-based proteomics offers distinct advantages in analyzing thousands of proteins, protein post-translational modifications, and complex dynamics without the need for affinity-based reagents.12
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: Mass-spectrometry proteomics measures thousands of proteins, PTMs, and complex dynamics without affinity reagents - distinct modality information beyond antibody/RNA methods.

excerpts.text: To better understand the impact of perturbations on cellular behavior, emerging spatial omics technologies enable large-scale mapping of molecular distribution, providing insights into how perturbations alter cellular processes in their native spatial context. In particular, spatial proteomics represents the forefront of this advancement.8,13
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: Spatial omics maps molecular distribution in native spatial context to reveal perturbation effects; positions spatial proteomics at the forefront.

excerpts.text: In addition, innovative sample preparation methods now allow simultaneous multi-omics analysis of the same sample.14 We argue that the AI-driven integration of static and dynamic data is essential for constructing a functionally robust and predictive AIVC.
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: Simultaneous multi-omics of the same sample now feasible; argues AI integration of static + dynamic data is essential for robust AIVCs.

excerpts.text: The integration of multimodal data from a priori knowledge, static architecture, and dynamic states demands sophisticated AI frameworks capable of hierarchical reasoning, cross-modal alignment, and predictive simulation.
excerpts.anchor: Three data pillars for growing AIVCs
excerpts.note: Multimodal data integration requires AI capable of cross-modal alignment - emphasizing the modality-integration challenge.

excerpts.text: To grow AIVCs, we foresee a transition from static, data-driven models to adaptive systems capable of evolving intelligence. While traditional approaches relied on passive data assimilation, modern closed-loop architectures will enable AIVCs to actively interrogate biological reality through AI model, autonomous robots, and dynamic data.
excerpts.anchor: Evolution of AIVC: closed-loop active learning systems
excerpts.note: Proposes moving from static data-driven models to closed-loop adaptive systems that actively generate dynamic data - reframing the data bottleneck as solvable via active experimentation rather than via a fixed data standard.

excerpts.text: Central to this vision is the establishment of closed-loop frameworks that integrate computational prediction with robotic experimentation, specifically targeting gaps in dynamic state data (Fig. 1).
excerpts.anchor: Evolution of AIVC: closed-loop active learning systems
excerpts.note: Closed-loop AI + robotics aimed specifically at filling gaps in dynamic-state (temporal) data.

excerpts.text: A critical challenge in modeling dynamic states is the combinatorial complexity of cellular responses to perturbations. While existing datasets capture snapshots of induced or natural states, they often lack systematic coverage of the parameter space.
excerpts.anchor: Evolution of AIVC: closed-loop active learning systems
excerpts.note: Existing perturbation datasets are sparse snapshots lacking systematic parameter-space coverage - a combinatorial coverage gap.

excerpts.text: For example, an AIVC trained on baseline proteomic profiles might identify understudied phosphorylation events in stress response pathways, prompting robotic platforms to perform time-resolved phosphoproteomics under targeted metabolic perturbations.
excerpts.anchor: Evolution of AIVC: closed-loop active learning systems
excerpts.note: Illustrative scenario invoking time-resolved phosphoproteomics (PTM/temporal modality) under metabolic perturbation to fill model gaps.

excerpts.text: While extensive experimental data exist for these cell types, significant gaps remain across the three pillars, particularly in their dynamic states. Notably, perturbation proteomics data, which are essential for building a comprehensive and dynamic AIVC, are scarce across all the candidate cell types.
excerpts.note: States that perturbation proteomics data are scarce across all candidate cell types, a key gap for dynamic AIVCs.

excerpts.text: As a eukaryote, yeast presents a multi-compartmented cellular structure, providing an excellent platform to model complex intracellular organization and dynamics.
excerpts.note: Highlights yeast's multi-compartmented (subcellular spatial) structure as valuable for modeling intracellular organization.

excerpts.text: Developing an AIVC for these simpler organisms can serve as a proof of concept, allowing us to address fundamental questions posed by Bunne et al.6: What are the specific data needs and requirements for building an AIVC? How much data is necessary to construct a robust and predictive AIVC? How can we develop a comprehensive and adaptable benchmarking framework to evaluate AIVC performance?
excerpts.note: Frames the core open questions as data needs, data quantity, and benchmarking frameworks for AIVCs - directly the data-standard agenda.

excerpts.text: Among these, perturbation-based omics data — transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics — emerge as the critical growth factors.
excerpts.note: Concludes that perturbation-based multi-omics (transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics) are the critical growth factors for AIVCs.

figuresNumbers.contains: Schematic of three data pillars for AIVC growth - a priori knowledge, static architecture, dynamic states - integrated via AI, with model organisms (E. coli, yeast, cell lines), and evolution via closed-loop active learning where computational predictions guide automated perturbation-omics experimentation.

flags: Same-group source: authored by the Guo group (Westlake), the same group writing our perspective; treat as an internal sibling statement, not independent external corroboration.

Framing divergence from our thesis: this paper organizes data needs as a priori knowledge / static architecture / dynamic states, and does NOT foreground the loss of space and time as THE structural bottleneck the way our thesis does; useful as nuance/foil on claims 1-3.

Counter-rationale on single-cell limits (e1/e14): authors attribute limited AIVC value of single-cell omics to 'similarity of the cells' states', NOT to dissociation-driven loss of space/time - a different argument from our claim 2/3 rationale.

Destructive-measurement / paired-observation constraint (our claim 3) is essentially not addressed; the paper instead proposes closed-loop robotic experimentation to generate dynamic data, which sidesteps rather than confronts that hard constraint.

OCR quirks preserved verbatim in excerpts: superscript reference markers fused into text (e.g. 'discovery and 10 cellular simulation' in e13; 'Human Protein Atlas,1' uses ref number 11 in the reference list - apparent OCR mismatch of superscript), and 'fi'/'fl' ligatures dropped throughout the source (e.g. 'rst', 'speci c', 'pro le'); quotes were normalized only where the ligature was reconstructable in surrounding prose but in-quote markers kept as-is.

Read at source: ref #10 (Sun et al. bioRxiv 2025) is the perturbation-proteomics AIVC pilot - worth first-hand for the drug-efficacy prediction claim; ref #6 (Bunne et al. Cell 2024) for the AIVC definition.
Metadata verified from text: Cell Research (2025) 35:319-321; doi:10.1038/s41422-025-01101-y (line 105). No separate received/accepted dates printed in the cleaned source.

title: From modality-specific to compositional foundation models for cell biology
authors: M.B., T.R., N.A.S., F.J.T. (initials from Acknowledgments; Theis group). Full author list not present in cleaned source
venue: Cell Systems, Perspective (year unverified; see flags)
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/03 - From modality-specific to compositional foundation models for cell biology_cleaned.md
oneSentence: A perspective arguing that multimodal single-cell foundation models should be built through compositional AI—modular assembly of independently pretrained, modality-specific expert modules aligned via transformer attention—rather than monolithic unified models, reviewing attention building blocks, architectures (bi-encoder/cross-encoder/language encoding), existing methods, prior-knowledge integration, multiscale modeling, data management, and evaluation toward agentic virtual cell models.
orientation: A Theis-group modeling perspective that sits opposite our data-first thesis on emphasis (it advocates an architectural fix—compositional AI—for fragmented, scarce, weakly-paired data) yet repeatedly concedes the very data-foundation facts we build on: biological modalities are fragmented and complementary (not redundant), no single technology captures all modalities, paired multimodal measurements are rare, samples are scarce relative to biological complexity, and current single-cell studies capture only a tiny fraction of cellular states. Strong supplier for claims on modality narrowness, modality orthogonality/non-substitutability, the scarcity/imbalance of paired data, integration needs, and data-as-bottleneck; can also be deployed as a named foil since it treats the problem as solvable by modeling composition and largely underweights the temporal axis and cross-experiment standardization. Routing hint only.

excerpts.text: However, in cell biology, training a unified non-compositional model is significantly more difficult because paired multimodal measurements are rare relative to the vast amount of available unimodal data. Unlike vision and language, biological modalities are fragmented and may encode useful complementary information, and no single technology currently captures all these distinct aspects simultaneously.
excerpts.note: States biological modalities are fragmented and encode complementary information, and no single technology captures all modalities at once.

excerpts.text: Furthermore, the scarcity of biological samples relative to the immense complexity and dimensionality of cellular systems makes it challenging for monolithic architectures to learn robust, generalized representations without the modular flexibility offered by a compositional approach.
excerpts.note: Concedes that the scarcity of biological samples relative to system complexity hampers robust generalized representations.

excerpts.text: By contrast, recent large-scale single-cell studies only capture a tiny fraction of possible cellular states and conditions given the complexity of biological systems. This scarcity of samples, combined with the high dimensionality and heterogeneity of biological data, makes it challenging to learn robust representations that generalize across different biological contexts and experimental conditions while enabling the inference of biological mechanisms.
excerpts.note: States current large-scale single-cell studies sample only a tiny fraction of cellular states, limiting generalization.

excerpts.text: A fundamental distinction between biological multimodal learning and web-scale multimodal representation learning lies in both data availability and assumptions about cross-modal information content. In vision-language models, modalities are typically designed to be redundant, and large, high-quality paired datasets enable joint optimization of modality-specific encoders using alignment-based objectives. By contrast, biological modalities often encode complementary information, while paired multimodal measurements are rare compared with unimodal data.
excerpts.note: Contrasts redundant vision-language modalities with biological modalities that encode complementary (non-redundant) information and are rarely paired.

excerpts.text: While effective for retrieval or matching tasks, such objectives are poorly suited for settings in which modalities must be integrated to recover information absent from any single view. From a methodological perspective, this suggests a need to move beyond alignment-centric interfaces toward integration mechanisms that explicitly model how complementary biological signals interact under limited and noisy pairing.
excerpts.note: Argues some modalities carry information absent from any single view, so integration must recover complementary signals rather than assume semantic overlap.

excerpts.text: Functionally, interpretable integration seeks to uncover causal and mechanistic relationships across modalities, enabling the identification of regulatory programs, signaling pathways, and cell-cell communication processes that are not observable from any single modality alone.
excerpts.note: States regulatory programs, pathways, and cell-cell communication are not observable from any single modality alone.

excerpts.text: Cross-modality integration aims to reconstruct coherent cellular states by jointly modeling complementary molecular layers—such as transcription, chromatin accessibility, protein abundance, and spatial context—that together govern gene regulation, cell identity, and cellular responses to perturbations.
excerpts.note: Enumerates complementary molecular layers (transcription, chromatin, protein abundance, spatial context) that together govern cell state.

excerpts.text: While single-cell transcriptomics has been a primary driver of this endeavor thanks to substantial efforts in collecting, organizing, and maintaining a wealth of publicly available datasets.
excerpts.note: Notes single-cell transcriptomics has been the primary driver of foundation-model efforts due to abundant public datasets.

excerpts.text: Going beyond transcriptomic profiles alone, recent studies have begun to take initial steps toward constructing large pretrained single-cell models that integrate multiple modalities.
excerpts.note: Frames most prior foundation-model work as transcriptome-only, with multimodal integration only at an initial stage.

excerpts.text: Single-cell proteomics quantifies protein levels and modifications,<sup>67</sup> offering a functional perspective on cellular states.
excerpts.note: States single-cell proteomics quantifies protein levels and modifications, giving a functional view of cellular states.

excerpts.text: Such cross-modality supervision could be acquired through technologies that allow simultaneous profiling of multiple molecular modalities within a single cell,<sup>84</sup> providing deeper insights into gene regulatory mechanisms.
excerpts.note: Lists co-assay technologies (SNARE-seq, SHARE-seq, sci-CAR, CITE-seq, TEA-seq, etc.) that jointly measure modalities within one cell.

excerpts.text: In addition to spatial omics,<sup>73,98–100</sup> spatial multiomics technologies, including DBiT-seq,<sup>101</sup> spatial CITE-seq,<sup>102</sup> SPOTS,<sup>103</sup> SM-OMICS,<sup>104</sup> Stereo-seq-based multiomics,<sup>105,106</sup> SlideseqV2 extensions,<sup>74</sup> and spatial ATAC-RNA co-profiling methods (e.g., spatial-ATAC-seq and spatial CUT&Tag RNAseq<sup>75</sup>) enable joint modality measurements, providing spatial organization of cells and subcellular structure.
excerpts.note: Lists spatial multiomics technologies that provide spatial organization of cells and subcellular structure.

excerpts.text: However, since no single technology currently captures all modalities simultaneously, an anchoring strategy is required to align and integrate different modalities into a shared space as illustrated in Figure 2. For instance, transcriptomics can serve as an anchor modality (a modality shared between different multimodal assays) to integrate Multiome and CITE-seq datasets, connecting chromatin accessibility and protein abundance.
excerpts.note: Reiterates no technology captures all modalities at once, requiring transcriptomics as an anchor modality to integrate assays into a shared space.

excerpts.text: By contrast, unpaired multimodal assays measure different modalities from different biological samples. For instance, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics performed on similar or replicate samples still measure different cells.
excerpts.note: Notes unpaired assays measure different cells, so even similar/replicate samples do not give matched cell-to-cell observations across modalities.

excerpts.text: However, the restricted amount of paired multimodal measurements limits their capabilities for learning from independent modalities. Composing a foundation model from a smaller model approach is a promising way to take advantage of the large amount of existing unpaired measurements of distinct modalities by learning the connections between different modalities with minimum direct supervision.
excerpts.note: States paired multimodal measurements are restricted in amount, limiting models that need to learn across independent modalities.

excerpts.text: The relative abundance of unpaired measurements motivates the use of distribution alignment strategies, such as optimal transport-based methods,<sup>113–116</sup> which align feature distributions across modalities without requiring direct cell-tocell correspondence.
excerpts.note: Notes alignment uses distribution-level methods (optimal transport) because direct cell-to-cell correspondence across modalities is unavailable.

excerpts.text: In addition, cross-modal pairing is often imperfect due to technical effects or biological heterogeneity. These constraints motivate compositional multimodal architectures that reuse pretrained unimodal models and restrict cross-modal learning to lightweight interfaces.
excerpts.note: States cross-modal pairing is often imperfect due to technical effects or biological heterogeneity.

excerpts.text: However, although high performance on classification tasks demonstrates a model's ability to learn meaningful cellular representations, it may not directly translate to improvements in downstream applications such as drug discovery or disease modeling.
excerpts.note: Concedes high classification accuracy may not translate to downstream utility, questioning standard benchmarks.

excerpts.text: A key limitation is that most metrics used to evaluate multimodal models are inherited from unimodal settings and primarily quantify downstream performance, without indicating whether improvements arise from true cross-modal integration or from aggregating redundant information already present in a dominant modality. This obscures whether a model actually exploits complementary signals across modalities.
excerpts.note: States current multimodal metrics are inherited from unimodal settings and cannot tell true cross-modal integration from redundancy in a dominant modality.

excerpts.text: Addressing this gap will require evaluation criteria and benchmark tasks that are explicitly sensitive to cross-modal complementarity, rather than accuracy alone, particularly in settings where relevant information is distributed across modalities.
excerpts.note: Calls for benchmarks sensitive to cross-modal complementarity because relevant information is distributed across modalities.

excerpts.text: An example is cross-modality prediction, which evaluates a model's ability to predict one modality from another, for example predicting protein abundance from transcriptomics data.<sup>23,191</sup> This task can provide a metric for mutual information across different modalities while also evaluating a multi-modal foundation model's capability to recover the underlying mutual dependency.
excerpts.note: Frames protein abundance prediction from transcriptomics as an evaluation of cross-modal mutual information (implying partial, non-trivial RNA-protein dependency).

excerpts.text: High-throughput technologies have enabled unprecedented data generation, but a significant imbalance persists between unimodal and paired multimodal measurements. While multimodal measurements naturally include diverse information, unimodal readouts are often cheaper, and one modality may be more economic than another.
excerpts.note: States a significant imbalance persists between abundant unimodal and scarce paired multimodal measurements, partly driven by cost.

excerpts.text: However, data from separate modalities are often available in different amounts, and adding more modalities is usually associated with different costs. Images, for example, are often cheap to acquire and comparatively simple to add as an additional modality, as demonstrated in spatial transcriptomics and -proteomics techniques.<sup>77,190</sup> However, imaging data, due to the absence of rich spatial labels by default, are usually harder to interpret than, for example, transcriptomics data. In contrast to imaging, profiling the transcriptome jointly with molecular modalities such as chromatin accessibility<sup>62</sup> or protein abundance<sup>93</sup> is more limited in depth and throughput.
excerpts.note: States joint profiling of transcriptome with chromatin accessibility or protein abundance is more limited in depth and throughput than imaging, naming a depth/throughput trade-off across modalities.

excerpts.text: In addition to molecular-level priors, models can also benefit from spatial and temporal information. Spatial transcriptomics and time-series single-cell data provide insights into the organization of cells within tissues and their progression over time during development, differentiation, or disease.
excerpts.note: States spatial and time-series single-cell data give insight into tissue organization and temporal progression.

excerpts.text: Likewise, longstanding insights from developmental biology and time-resolved lineage tracing studies have immense potential to provide critical context for modeling dynamic cellular processes.
excerpts.note: Points to time-resolved lineage tracing and developmental biology as prior knowledge for modeling dynamic (temporal) cellular processes.

excerpts.text: Gene regulatory networks (GRNs) offer another critical form of prior knowledge, as they encode directed relationships between transcription factors and their target genes. ... In contrast to co-expression patterns learned from data, GRNs introduce causality and control into the modeling process, enabling biologically grounded inference of transcriptional regulation over time.
excerpts.note: States GRNs encode directed TF-target relationships and add causality/control beyond data-driven co-expression.

excerpts.text: Likewise, protein-protein interaction (PPI) networks capture functional or physical associations between proteins, reflecting post-transcriptional regulation and co-regulated modules that extend beyond transcriptional readouts.
excerpts.note: States PPI networks capture post-transcriptional regulation and co-regulated modules that extend beyond transcriptional readouts.

excerpts.text: Incorporating biological prior knowledge into multimodal singlecell foundation models can improve learning efficiency, model robustness, and interpretability—particularly in settings where purely data-driven approaches fall short due to data sparsity, noise, or limited coverage across biological contexts.
excerpts.note: Concedes purely data-driven approaches fall short under data sparsity, noise, and limited coverage, motivating prior knowledge.

excerpts.text: Extending uni-scale foundation models to integrate both across modalities and across biological scales will be crucial for the vision of a virtual cell.<sup>49</sup>
excerpts.note: States virtual-cell models must integrate across modalities and across biological scales (subcellular to tissue).

excerpts.text: Multimodal single-cell foundation models have the potential to advance our understanding of cellular states by integrating diverse molecular and spatial data.
excerpts.note: Frames the goal as integrating diverse molecular and spatial data into multimodal foundation models.

excerpts.text: Without diverse observations, even the most effective models and largest datasets will fall short of capturing the true complexity of the underlying biology.
excerpts.note: States that without modality diversity, even the best models and largest datasets cannot capture true biological complexity.

excerpts.text: Cells contain diverse molecular entities such as genetic material, transcripts, proteins, and metabolites that interact to carry out biological functions. ... Each of these measurement modalities provides a unique perspective on cellular function and regulation, and together they offer a more comprehensive view of cellular states and processes than individual measurements.
excerpts.note: States each modality (genetic material, transcripts, proteins, metabolites) gives a unique perspective and together they are more comprehensive than any single one.

excerpts.text: Imaging techniques offer spatial and temporal context, capturing subcellular structures and live dynamics.
excerpts.note: States imaging provides spatial and temporal context, capturing subcellular structure and live dynamics.

excerpts.text: These networks, often constructed from ChIP-seq, CUT&RUN, and CUT&- Tag experiments,<sup>63–65</sup> perturbation assays, or literature mining, represent mechanistic underpinnings of transcriptional regulation.
excerpts.note: Notes epigenetic/chromatin-state assays (ChIP-seq, CUT&RUN, CUT&Tag) underpin transcriptional regulation networks.

figuresNumbers.contains: Comparison of bi-encoder, cross-encoder, and language encoding approaches across architecture, processing, interaction, output, accuracy, efficiency, scalability, and typical use cases. Bi-encoder: twin-tower independent encoding, interaction after encoding via similarity metric, high efficiency/scalability with precomputable embeddings, lower fine-grained accuracy, used for large-scale retrieval and unpaired data. Cross-encoder: single encoder joint encoding, interaction during encoding via attention, higher fine-grained accuracy, low efficiency/scalability (must process each pair). Language encoding: encodes biological data as text, leverages LLM pretraining, accuracy/efficiency/scalability depend on the LLM.

Overview of existing multimodal omics models by architecture and modality. Bi-encoders: scCLIP (scRNA-seq, scATAC), SCARF (scRNA-seq, scATAC), CellWhisperer (bulk RNA, scRNA-seq + text), LangCell (scRNA-seq, text), spEMO (spatial transcriptomics, spatial multi-omics, text, histopathology), OKR-Cell (scRNA-seq, text), CAPTAIN (scRNA-seq, proteomics). Cross-encoders: Nicheformer, CellPLM, scGPT-spatial (each scRNA-seq + spatial transcriptomics), Precious3GPT (transcriptomics, epigenetics, proteomics, knowledge graphs). Language encoding: GenePT, Cell2Sentence, scChat, CELLama, scELMo, CellHermes (scRNA-seq +/- spatial/text/PPI). STPath (image + spatial transcriptomics).
Four attention building blocks for multimodal transformers: fused self-attention, all-to-all self-attention, within-modality self-attention, cross-modality attention.

Evaluation tasks split into expert-annotation-based (subcellular/function prediction, cell-type/state annotation, tissue/niche/spatial-domain annotation, disease-state classification, trajectory/lineage-stage labeling) and intrinsic expert-agnostic (masked feature reconstruction/denoising, cross-modality prediction/modality matching, perturbation response prediction, joint embedding of modalities).

title: Zero-shot evaluation reveals limitations of single-cell foundation models
authors: Kasia Z. Kedzierska, Lorin Crawford, Ava P. Amini, Alex X. Lu
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/04 - Zero-shot evaluation reveals limitations of single-cell foundation models_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors perform zero-shot evaluations of two popular single-cell foundation models (Geneformer and scGPT) and find that, even under the most favorable conditions, both underperform simpler baselines (HVG, scVI, Harmony) at cell-type clustering and batch integration, and reconstruct gene expression poorly.
orientation: Strong support for our Claim 1 (data/evaluation is the bottleneck; pure data-driven foundation models generalize poorly): a direct empirical demonstration that scaled single-cell foundation models fail to beat simple methods zero-shot. Also feeds Claim 9 (cross-paper non-comparability, need for standardized held-out benchmarks) via its call for reserved benchmark datasets, and modality axis A (RNA-only narrowness) via its self-conceded transcriptomics-only scope. Can serve as both an ally (foundation-model skepticism) and a foil (it still frames the fix as better models/benchmarks, not richer multimodal spatiotemporal data).

excerpts.text: Our evaluation of the zero-shot performance of Geneformer and scGPT suggests that, in some cases, these models may face reliability challenges and could be outperformed by simpler methods.
excerpts.note: States the paper's headline finding that the two foundation models can be beaten by simpler methods in zero-shot settings.

excerpts.text: In our work, we test this claim and show that even with a set of benchmarks representing the most favorable setting where datasets consist of tissues and are generated using technologies similar to those used to pretrain these models (Additional fle 2: Figs. S1 and S2), both Geneformer and scGPT underperform simpler methods.
excerpts.note: Emphasizes that the underperformance holds even under conditions deliberately favorable to the foundation models.

excerpts.text: Both models perform worse than selecting highly variable genes (HVG) and using more established methods such as Harmony and scVI in cell type clustering, as measured by average BIO (AvgBio) score (Fig. 1B, Additional fle 1: Table S1).
excerpts.note: Quantifies the cell-type clustering result: both foundation models trail HVG, Harmony, and scVI on AvgBIO.

excerpts.text: Notably, HVG outperforms Geneformer and scGPT across all metrics (Additional fle 2: Fig. S4, Additional fle 1: Tables S1 and S2).
excerpts.note: States that simple highly-variable-gene selection beats both foundation models on every metric.

excerpts.text: However, scGPT and Geneformer do not consistently outperform baselines on datasets already seen during pretraining, and the only dataset not seen during pretraining where scGPT outperforms both baselines is the the PBMC 12k study.
excerpts.note: Notes the models fail to beat baselines even on data they were pretrained on, weakening the generalization claim.

excerpts.text: Evaluating variants of the scGPT model highlights that while pretraining confers some beneft, beyond a certain limit, larger and more diverse datasets may no longer confer additional benefts.
excerpts.note: Reports diminishing returns: scaling pretraining data past a point yields no further benefit.

excerpts.text: Surprisingly, scGPT human slightly underperforms scGPT blood, even for datasets involving tissue types beyond blood and bone marrow cells (Additional fle  2: Fig. S5, Additional fle 1: Table S4).
excerpts.note: Observes that the larger, more diverse 33M-cell model is beaten by a smaller blood-only model, complicating the scaling narrative.

excerpts.text: While Geneformer and scGPT-human can integrate diferent experiments conducted with the same experimental technique, they generally fail to correct for batch efects between techniques.
excerpts.note: Finds the foundation models fail at cross-technique batch integration, i.e. cannot reconcile data produced by different platforms.

excerpts.text: Qualitatively, Geneformer’s cell embedding space fails to retain information about cell type, and any clustering is primarily driven by batch efects.
excerpts.note: States Geneformer's embeddings are dominated by batch (technical) signal rather than biological cell-type signal.

excerpts.text: Surprisingly, the best batch integration scores for all datasets were achieved by selecting HVG (Additional fle 1: Table S2).
excerpts.note: Reports the simplest baseline (HVG) achieves the best batch-integration scores across all datasets.

excerpts.text: Notably, scGPT without embeddings underperforms against a naive baseline of predicting the mean, and only marginally improves with the cell embeddings (Fig. 2D).
excerpts.note: Shows scGPT does worse than a trivial mean-prediction baseline on its own gene-expression reconstruction task.

excerpts.text: Geneformer’s gene ranking exhibits modest correlations with the actual true rankings, with median and best correlations of 0.56 and 0.95, respectively, across datasets (Fig. 2E, Additional fle 2: Fig. S7).
excerpts.note: Quantifies Geneformer's reconstruction fidelity as only modest (median Pearson 0.56) on its own pretraining task.

excerpts.text: Our fndings indicate that both models, in their current form, do not consistently outperform simpler baselines and face challenges in dealing with batch efects. Tis is in spite of these models reporting strong performance when fne-tuned, as exhibited in their original papers, demonstrating that zero-shot evaluation can reveal vulnerabilities that are not evident if models are exclusively evaluated with fne-tuning.
excerpts.note: Concludes that fine-tuned performance overstates capability and zero-shot evaluation exposes hidden weaknesses.

excerpts.text: Our fndings raise questions about the general suitability of MLM for learning single-cell embeddings.
excerpts.note: Questions whether the masked-language-model pretraining objective is even appropriate for single-cell data.

excerpts.text: Additionally, we demonstrate that larger pretraining datasets do not always increase the performance of scGPT, and that datasets seen in pretraining still have poor cell type clustering performance.
excerpts.note: Restates that more pretraining data does not guarantee better performance and that even seen data clusters poorly.

excerpts.text: We concentrated on specifc models and selected a limited array of datasets that do not exhaustively represent all applications of single-cell analysis (for example, our datasets only include transcriptomic data).
excerpts.note: Author-conceded scope limitation: the evaluation datasets are transcriptomic-only, reflecting the RNA-centric state of the field.

excerpts.text: In fact, some more recent advances occurring at time of our work have already sought to address limitations exposed by our analysis, for example, in improving the representation of genes by transferring knowledge from proteins [11]. However, in many cases methods do not make their pretraining code publicly available [11, 12].
excerpts.note: Notes that addressing the exposed limitations is being pursued by transferring knowledge from proteins, pointing beyond RNA-only inputs.

excerpts.text: One limitation of our analyses was that some of our evaluation datasets were used in pretraining, confounding our ability to say if performance trends are general or due to prior exposure to specifc datasets.
excerpts.note: Concedes that train/test data overlap confounds the evaluation, an instance of the comparability/standardization problem.

excerpts.text: To tackle this, we propose the creation of benchmark tasks and datasets reserved exclusively for model evaluation that should never be used to pretrain any future model, as has been done for large language models (LLMs) [26–29].
excerpts.note: Calls for standardized held-out benchmark datasets to enable unbiased, comparable model evaluation.

excerpts.text: Te curation of biologically grounded, standalone benchmark datasets would provide a substantial advancement towards more reliable and robust evaluation methods.
excerpts.note: Argues curated standalone benchmark datasets would substantially improve evaluation reliability and comparability.

excerpts.text: We believe that more focused evaluations, particularly in zero-shot contexts, will be instrumental to the methodological development and deployment of foundation models in single-cell research.
excerpts.note: Frames the remedy as better evaluation/methods for foundation models, locating the problem in models/benchmarks rather than in the underlying data being spatiotemporally and modally impoverished.

excerpts.text: We chose these models because they ofer pretrained weights and have been trained using unsupervised objectives on extensive datasets (ca. 30M single-cell transcriptomes).
excerpts.note: States the evaluated foundation models were pretrained on roughly 30M single-cell transcriptomes, i.e. RNA-only data at scale.

figuresNumbers.contains: Cell-type clustering benchmark: HVG outperforms Geneformer and scGPT across all metrics; both foundation models score worse than HVG, Harmony, and scVI on average AvgBIO across five datasets.

Batch integration: Geneformer consistently ranks last across all metrics; scVI and Harmony outperform scGPT on Pancreas and PBMC (technical batch only) but scGPT beats them on the more complex Tabula Sapiens and Immune datasets (which were in scGPT pretraining).
scGPT without cell embeddings underperforms a naive mean-prediction baseline on gene-expression reconstruction; conditioning on cell embeddings yields only marginal improvement.
Geneformer gene-ranking reconstruction has median correlation 0.56 and best 0.95 with true rankings across datasets.
Tested four scGPT variants: randomly initialized; scGPT kidney (814,000 kidney cells); scGPT blood (10.3 million blood and bone marrow cells); scGPT human (33 million non-cancerous human cells).

Geneformer pretrained on 27.4M human single-cell transcriptomes (excluding malignant and immortalized cells); both models ca. 30M single-cell transcriptomes; scGPT has 3x Geneformer's parameters (12 Transformer layers, 8 attention heads vs 6 layers, 4 heads).
