Scope note: this paper is a benchmark/evaluation of RNA-only single-cell foundation models; it bears most directly on Claim 1 (model generalization / data-as-bottleneck) and Claim 9 (held-out benchmarks, comparability). It does NOT directly treat spatial (axis C) or temporal (axis B) data gaps; the modality (axis A) touch is limited to its self-conceded transcriptomic-only scope (e10/e16).
Read at source: refs #6 (Geneformer, Nature 2023) and #7 (scGPT, Nat Methods 2024) are the primary papers being critiqued and are worth citing first-hand for the foundation-model claims they make.

title: Benchmarking algorithms for generalizable single-cell perturbation response prediction
authors: Zhiting Wei, Yiheng Wang, Yicheng Gao, Shuguang Wang, Ping Li, Duanmiao Si, Yuli Gao, Siqi Wu, Danlu Li, Kejing Dong, Xingbo Yang, Chen Tang, Shaliu Fu, Xiaohan Chen, Wannian Li, Yuzhou You, Chen Zhang, Aibin Liang, Guohui Chuai & Qi Liu
venue: Nature Methods, 2025; unverified vol(issue):pages, see flags
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/05 - Benchmarking algorithms for generalizable single-cell perturbation response prediction_cleaned.md
oneSentence: A comprehensive benchmark of 27 single-cell perturbation response prediction methods (including foundation models) across 29 datasets and 6 metrics, evaluating generalizability under cellular-context and perturbation generalization scenarios, finding no one-size-fits-all method, that all methods generalize poorly to dissimilar new contexts (often worse than baselines), and that foundation models only help when fine-tuning data are large.
orientation: Strong ammunition for our claim that data, not models, is the bottleneck (Claim 1): it shows current single-cell perturbation models—including foundation models—fail to generalize to unseen, dissimilar cellular contexts and often lose to trivial baselines, and that generalization is governed by training-test data similarity more than dataset size. It also feeds Claim 9 (incomparability/standards) by documenting how preprocessing/control/split choices flip conclusions across papers, and Claim 10 (measurement trade-offs) via the infeasibility of large-scale/combinatorial screens. It operates entirely on dissociated scRNA-seq (RNA-only, no space/time/protein), so it can be cited both as support for our thesis AND, by what it omits, as evidence of the field's modality/spatial/temporal blind spots. Non-binding routing hint.

excerpts.text: However, recent studies have continued to raise concerns regarding the true effectiveness of these models<sup>10–12</sup>. Specifically, many of these methods do not outperform baseline models or linear models on the basis of straightforward assumptions when they are evaluated across multiple datasets.
excerpts.note: Reports that many perturbation-prediction methods, including foundation models, fail to beat simple baselines/linear models across multiple datasets.

excerpts.text: Our study indicated that there is no ‘one-size-fits-all’ method that works well across all datasets.
excerpts.note: No single method generalizes across all datasets; performance is dataset-dependent.

excerpts.text: In the cellular context generalization scenario, owing to limitations in algorithm design, that is, neglecting the specificity of perturbation responses across different cellular contexts (intercellular heterogeneity, or for simplicity inter-heterogeneity), all methods likely exhibit poor generalizability. Specifically, when predicting the effects of perturbations in a new cellular context that is quite different from the training datasets, all methods performed poorly, even worse than the baseline models;
excerpts.note: All methods, including foundation models, generalize poorly to a new cellular context dissimilar from training, performing even worse than baselines.

excerpts.text: By analyzing datasets of varying sizes, it is found that baseline models tend to perform better on smaller training datasets. In contrast, foundation models tend to perform better in terms of both prediction accuracy and generalizability, when the fine-tuning datasets are sufficiently large.
excerpts.note: Foundation models only outperform baselines when fine-tuning data are sufficiently large; baselines win on small datasets.

excerpts.text: Conducting large-scale perturbation screens, particularly for combined perturbations, is infeasible because of the exponential growth in complexity.
excerpts.note: Large-scale and combinatorial perturbation screens are infeasible due to exponentially growing experimental complexity, motivating computational prediction.

excerpts.text: Accuracy evaluation presents a particular challenge because of the lack of consensus on the most suitable evaluation metrics in the feld. A variety of metrics have been proposed for evaluating single-cell perturbation efect prediction performance, but their reliability and informativeness are still debated.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Evaluation metrics and assessment of methods
excerpts.note: No consensus exists on suitable evaluation metrics in the field; metric reliability is still debated, complicating comparison.

excerpts.text: This result indicated that it is still challenging for the current methods to be generalized to a new cellular context that is dissimilar to the training cellular context (Extended Data Fig. 4).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Limitations of current methods in the cellular context generalization scenario
excerpts.note: Current methods cannot generalize to a new cellular context dissimilar from the training context.

excerpts.text: In summary, the analysis of the benchmark results in the cellular context generalization scenarios shows that the performance of the evaluated methods is highly dependent on the inter-heterogeneity. When predicting perturbation effects in a new cellular context that differs substantially from the training datasets, all methods likely performed unsatisfactorily, highlighting their limited generalizability.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Limitations of current methods in the cellular context generalization scenario
excerpts.note: Method performance depends on similarity between train and test contexts; all methods fail when the new context differs substantially.

excerpts.text: Further analysis revealed that model performance declined on perturbations with high inter-heterogeneity, suggesting that generalization is more strongly influenced by data quality—particularly the similarity between training and testing perturbations—than by dataset size (Supplementary Fig. 29).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Limitations of current methods in the perturbation generalization scenario
excerpts.note: Generalization is governed more by data quality and train-test similarity than by dataset size.

excerpts.text: As shown in our results, increasing the number of cells or perturbations in the training data did not lead to performance gains across all evaluation metrics (Supplementary Note 11 and Supplementary Figs. 13 and 14).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Limitations of current methods in the perturbation generalization scenario
excerpts.note: Simply adding more cells or perturbations to training data did not improve generalization across metrics.

excerpts.text: In our study, the four baseline models and linearModel performed relatively poorly, which differs from the findings of previous studies—potentially due to differences in data preprocessing and the size of the training set (Supplementary Note 18).
excerpts.note: Their baseline/linear-model results contradict previous studies, attributed to differences in data preprocessing and training-set size — i.e., conclusions are not comparable across papers.

excerpts.text: In addition, recent studies suggest that for predicting gene expression profiles under combined perturbations, the simplest additive model—which predicts the effects of combined perturbations as the sum of the effects of two single perturbations—often outperforms complex deep learning models or foundation models such as scGPT<sup>12</sup>. However, these conclusions were derived from a single dataset.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Benchmarking analysis of the perturbation generalization scenario
excerpts.note: Cites prior finding that a simple additive model often beats deep/foundation models for combined perturbations, while noting that conclusion came from only a single dataset.

excerpts.text: It is surprising that models relying on unaligned natural language embeddings outperform those leveraging transcriptome-informed embeddings.
excerpts.note: LLM/natural-language-derived gene embeddings outperform transcriptome-informed (single-cell foundation model) embeddings for genetic perturbation prediction.

excerpts.text: These results indicate that LLM-derived gene embeddings may capture high-level functional and contextual knowledge that is otherwise hard to extract from gene expression data alone.
excerpts.note: Suggests functional/contextual knowledge useful for prediction is hard to extract from gene expression (RNA) data alone.

excerpts.text: In the combined-perturbation dataset, baseReg outperformed CPA on all six metrics, highlighting the current gap in methods for chemical perturbation prediction, particularly in combinatorial contexts (Fig. 5b).
excerpts.note: A simple regression baseline beat the deep model CPA on all six metrics for combined chemical perturbations.

excerpts.text: We observed that all methods likely performed poorly on population-distribution metrics especially on the Common-DEGs metric in genetic perturbation scenarios. For instance, in genetic single-perturbation datasets, even the best-performing model, baseMLP, identified fewer than 10 of 100 true most differentially expressed genes (Fig. 5f).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Limitations of current methods in the perturbation generalization scenario
excerpts.note: Even the best model recovers fewer than 10 of 100 true most differentially expressed genes, showing poor distribution-level prediction.

excerpts.text: However, biolord and scDisInFact did not outperform the tools that did not incorporate covariates on these datasets (Fig. 2c).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Benchmarking analysis for the cellular context generalization scenario
excerpts.note: Methods that incorporate time-point/dosage covariate metadata did not outperform methods that ignore covariates.

excerpts.text: Models that consider covariates affecting cellular responses provide more accurate predictions of single-cell perturbation effects. However, most existing tools do not incorporate covariates, and those that do are still far from being sufficiently accurate. Given the increasing availability of multi-condition data in the single-cell perturbation field, there is a pressing need to develop high-performing models tailored for multi-condition data, which can fully exploit covariate information to achieve improved prediction accuracy.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Benchmarking analysis for the cellular context generalization scenario (summary)
excerpts.note: Most tools ignore covariates (e.g., time-point/dosage); incorporating them helps but current models remain insufficiently accurate, calling for models tailored to multi-condition data.

excerpts.text: However, understanding the heterogeneity within cellular subpopulations in response to perturbations is vital for revealing gene functions and regulatory networks.

touchpoints.otherLabel: Single-cell heterogeneity / distribution-level information matters beyond population averages

excerpts.note: Argues capturing subpopulation-level heterogeneity in perturbation response is essential, not just average effects.

excerpts.text: All current methods likely exhibited poor generalizability, especially when predicting perturbation effects in novel cellular contexts. To alleviate this, we propose incorporating prior knowledge through cell-line embeddings, which can improve model generalizability (Supplementary Notes 12 and 13 and Supplementary Figs. 27, 28 and 38).
excerpts.note: All methods generalize poorly to novel cellular contexts; authors propose cell-line embeddings (prior knowledge) as a mitigation.

excerpts.text: While the proposed cellular context embedding strategy represents a conceptually promising direction, its current performance demonstrates only limited improvements—mainly in the PCC-delta and Common-DEGs metrics—and does not consistently surpass baseline methods across evaluation metrics on all the evaluated datasets.
excerpts.note: Their own proposed cell-context embedding fix gives only limited, inconsistent improvement and does not reliably beat baselines.

excerpts.text: Our proposed cellular context embedding strategy is currently applicable only to cell-line-derived contexts and cannot be directly applied to patient-derived contexts (Supplementary Note 12);
excerpts.note: Authors concede their embedding strategy works only for cell-line contexts and does not transfer to patient-derived contexts.

excerpts.text: Our current work does not address the challenge of poor performance on population-distribution metrics, which reflect a model’s ability to capture intracellular heterogeneity. Addressing this limitation remains an important direction for future research;
excerpts.note: Authors concede the field has not solved poor distribution-level prediction, which reflects failure to capture single-cell heterogeneity.

excerpts.text: There is an urgent need to establish more diverse and comprehensive single-cell perturbation atlases. Similarly to how large-scale single-cell atlases have accelerated algorithmic development in the broader single-cell field, systematically building perturbation-focused cell atlases could catalyze progress in perturbation effect prediction.
excerpts.note: Calls for larger, more diverse, systematically built perturbation data atlases as the prerequisite to advance prediction methods — data-first framing.

excerpts.text: Notably, Rood et al. have proposed the concept of a ‘perturbation cell atlas’, highlighting the value of such systematic efforts for the community<sup>55</sup>. Large-scale resources such as Tahoe-100M and X-Atlas have begun to fill this gap by providing comprehensive perturbation datasets, which lay a strong foundation for future benchmarking and systematic evaluation of foundation model performance<sup>56,57</sup>;
excerpts.note: Points to a 'perturbation cell atlas' concept and large new datasets (Tahoe-100M, X-Atlas) as the foundation needed for benchmarking and foundation-model evaluation.

excerpts.text: Future work may further explore the integration of prior biological knowledge—such as gene regulatory networks or pathway information—into model design to enhance generalization across a wide range of cellular contexts. Incorporating biological priors could substantially improve the robustness and interpretability of perturbation prediction models;
excerpts.note: Suggests integrating prior biological knowledge (gene regulatory networks, pathways) to improve generalization — information beyond raw expression.

excerpts.text: One limitation of our current robustness evaluation framework is that it primarily focuses on the effects of technical artifacts, such as noise and sparsity. While this allows us to systematically assess the sensitivity of predictive models to common experimental distortions, it does not capture biological variability in perturbation responses across different cellular contexts or genetic backgrounds.
excerpts.note: Concedes robustness evaluation covers only technical noise/sparsity, not biological variability across contexts or genetic backgrounds.

excerpts.text: As the noise and sparsity in the data increased, the performance of all methods decreased markedly (Fig. 2g). We also noted that the performance of the algorithms deteriorated more severely for high-sparsity data, indicating that their performance is more sensitive to data sparsity (Fig. 2b).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Benchmarking analysis for the cellular context generalization scenario
excerpts.note: All methods degrade markedly with increasing data noise and sparsity, and are especially sensitive to sparsity (a data-quality factor).

figuresNumbers.contains: 27 methods (23 published + 4 baseline) benchmarked across 29 datasets using 6 metrics (MSE, PCC-delta, E-distance, Wasserstein distance, KL-divergence, Common-DEGs).
14 methods compared across 12 datasets (8 cross-cell-line, 3 cross-patient, 1 cross-species; 7 single-condition, 5 multi-condition); 477,677 cells and 73 perturbations.
18 methods across 17 datasets (13 genetic: 9 single + 4 combined; 4 chemical: 3 single + 1 combined); perturbation counts range 25 to 1,618; 1,588,215 cells and 4,874 perturbations.

Top method reached average PCC-delta 0.86, but UMAP showed substantial discrepancy between predicted and ground-truth distributions; on average only ~10% of true most differentially expressed genes recovered.
Afriat: trainMean baseline reached median PCC-delta 0.92, average 0.85. KaggleCrossCell: all ML models poor, median PCC-delta 0.54, average 0.52.
Best model baseMLP identified fewer than 10 of 100 true most differentially expressed genes.

figuresNumbers.item: ANOVA of inter- vs intra-heterogeneity (text tables, p.391/393)
figuresNumbers.contains: MSE: inter-heterogeneity F=45.481, P=9.16e-09; intra-heterogeneity F=1.628, P=0.207. PCC-delta: inter-heterogeneity F=8.463, P=0.005; intra-heterogeneity F=0.304, P=0.584. Inter-heterogeneity (between-context) dominates, intra not significant.

Literature survey over 28 tools/benchmarks identified 19 evaluation metrics; 6 retained (3 population-average: MSE, E-distance, PCC-delta; 3 population-distribution: Wasserstein, KL-divergence, Common-DEGs); metrics computed for all genes and for top 100 most differentially expressed genes.

flags: Metadata partially unverified: venue confirmed as Nature Methods (DOI prefix s41592; peer-review note 'Nature Methods thanks Yi Zhao'; corresponding author Qi Liu), year 2025 (copyright 2025; 'Last updated by author(s): Oct. 31, 2025'), but exact volume/issue/page numbers are not present in the cleaned text — left unasserted in venue.

Its relevance to those axes is by omission, not by explicit statement — touchpoints conservatively confined to Claims 1, 4, 9, 10 plus one 'Other'.

title: Insights, opportunities, and challenges provided by large cell atlases
authors: Martin Hemberg, Federico Marini, Shila Ghazanfar, Ahmad Al Ajami, Najla Abassi, Benedict Anchang, Bérénice A. Benayoun, Yue Cao, Ken Chen, Yesid Cuesta-Astroz, Zachary DeBruine, Calliope A. Dendrou, Iwijn De Vlaminck, Katharina Imkeller, Ilya Korsunsky, Alex R. Lederer, Jessica Jingyi Li, Pieter Meysman, Clint L. Miller, Kerry A. Mullan, Uwe Ohler, Pratibha Panwar, Nikolaos Patikas, Jonas Schuck, Jacqueline H. Y. Siu, Timothy J. Triche Jr., Alex Tsankov, Sander W. van der Laan, Masanao Yajima, Jean Yang, Fabio Zanini and Ivana Jelic
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/06 - Insights, opportunities, and challenges provided by large cell atlases_cleaned.md
oneSentence: A CZI-affiliated multi-author review surveys the building, maintenance, and use of large single-cell cell atlases (e.g., CELLxGENE, HuBMAP, DISCO), cataloging current resources and the computational opportunities and challenges around data ingestion, metadata, integration, benchmarking, multimodal/spatial expansion, and biomedical use.
orientation: A review of the dominant dissociated-scRNA-seq atlas paradigm; it generally champions that paradigm but openly concedes the very gaps our thesis builds on (atlases are almost entirely scRNA-seq, spatial and other modalities are still 'beyond' the norm, batch effects cannot be eliminated, metadata/standards are immature, cross-dataset integration struggles, proteomics/metabolomics single-cell profiling is not yet available at scale). Most useful as conceded-fact support for the modality-breadth, spatial, standards/integration, and benchmarking-comparability claims (Claims 2, 4, 8, 9; axes A/C/standard), and as a named mainstream-perspective foil; some lines on biological replicates and trajectories also touch the temporal axis (Claim 3 / axis B). A routing hint, non-binding.

excerpts.text: By making sure that data is uniformly processed and adheres to standard formats, it also becomes interoperable and reusable. Although straightforward in principle, the scale and complexity of a cell atlas make it difcult to achieve these goals.
excerpts.note: Concedes that achieving interoperability/reusability via uniform processing and standard formats is difficult at atlas scale.

excerpts.text: Although preprocessing can improve the internal consistency, it does not prevent the emergence of discrepancies within and across atlases. A particular concern for cell atlases is batch efects, technical artifacts that emerge due to diferences in how the data was obtained and processed. Although batch efects can be reduced, they cannot be eliminated altogether.
excerpts.note: States that discrepancies arise within and across atlases and that batch effects can be reduced but never fully eliminated.

excerpts.text: As no repository will be able to span all conditions, populations, organisms, cells, and modalities of interest, maintaining this possibility is a key requirement for enabling meta-analyses.
excerpts.note: Concedes no single repository spans all conditions/modalities, so cross-dataset meta-analysis machinery is required.

excerpts.text: However, tools for indexing, metadata standardization, and cross-cohort queries are in their infancy, and this limits the ability of users to identify suitable datasets [2, 17].
excerpts.note: States metadata standardization and cross-cohort query tooling are still in their infancy.

excerpts.text: On the other hand, missing or incomplete metadata could mislead data interpretation (e.g., in a human study, efects that are actually due to donor sex or age would erroneously be attributed to treatment).
excerpts.note: Warns that missing/incomplete metadata (e.g., donor sex/age) can confound and mislead interpretation.

excerpts.text: For example, metadata capturing timepoints post-infection enables reconstruction of disease trajectories.
excerpts.note: Notes that timepoint metadata across samples enables reconstruction of disease trajectories (cross-sample time, not paired single-cell tracking).

excerpts.text: However, when aggregating datasets, it is often the case that they have not been annotated consistently, and assigning a consistent set of cell labels remains a major challenge. No universally accepted defnition of cell types exists; however, as our knowledge of cell biology and identities is ever improving, consequently, ontologies must remain fexible to accommodate the diversity of cell types, states, and conditions.
excerpts.note: Concedes inconsistent annotation across aggregated datasets and the lack of a universally accepted cell-type definition.

excerpts.text: The integration of disease data into atlases also presents its own set of difculties as disease-associated cells may acquire distinct cell states, requiring expanded annotations in the metadata. Moreover, when querying a “normal” atlas with disease-specifc data, researchers must account for the potential lack of representation or mismatch in cell types [26].
excerpts.note: Disease/perturbed cell states acquire distinct identities that may not be represented in a normal reference atlas, complicating mapping.

excerpts.text: Te typical workfow when using a cell atlas requires a user to frst identify and download the relevant datasets. Given their size, already exceeding 1 TB using standard data structures, this can require signifcant bandwidth and be prohibitive to many research labs without high-memory computing resources.

touchpoints.otherLabel: Atlas scale creates compute/storage barriers to reuse

excerpts.note: Reports atlas datasets already exceed 1 TB with standard structures, creating bandwidth/memory barriers for many labs.

excerpts.text: Subsampling can ease computation over diverse datasets and help reduce bias of highly represented signals, but may also compromise the unprecedented modeling power that comes with a dataset of this size. Issues include racial and gender bias in samples [28], over-representation of specifc cell types [29], opportunistic collection of rare samples [30], but the best approach depends on the scientifc goals of the study.
excerpts.note: Notes biases (racial/gender, cell-type over-representation, opportunistic rare sampling) embedded in atlas data and the tradeoffs of subsampling.

excerpts.text: Simple random subsampling does not address signal representation and can miss rare subpopulations [31, 32], and it is desirable to balance the tradeof between representing the true proportions of cell types and full extent of cell identities of rare cell types [33].
excerpts.note: States random subsampling can miss rare subpopulations and distort proportions; a representation tradeoff.

excerpts.text: In modern single-cell analysis, this data integration is often key for harmonizing distinct datasets, and it serves as an initial step for meta-analysis of single-cell datasets. Appropriate meta-analyses will not only have to consider corrected cell type labels, but also other statistical factors that remain challenging to deal with.
excerpts.note: Data integration to harmonize distinct datasets is a prerequisite for meta-analysis, with residual challenging statistical factors.

excerpts.text: Numerous methods for carrying out batch integration have been proposed in recent years, resulting in substantial progress in terms of performance, as demonstrated by independent benchmarks [37, 40, 41]. However, several challenges remain, and existing methods often struggle in more complex scenarios, e.g., involving diferent species [42], imbalanced datasets [43], or very large numbers of cells.
excerpts.note: Concedes integration methods still struggle with cross-species, imbalanced, and very large datasets despite benchmarked progress.

excerpts.text: Importantly, even for single-cell atlases, biological replicates should consist of samples procured from independent biological sources/individuals, and not just independent cells from the same sample [50–52]. Tus, a key feature of context-aware cell atlases is the inclusion of sufcient independent biological samples across conditions to account for inter-individual variability. Tis requires sufcient numbers of true biological replicates, similar to bulk approaches [53].
excerpts.note: Argues single cells are not biological replicates; true independent biological replicates across conditions are required, as in bulk.

excerpts.text: One of the main challenges when comparing methods is that for most problems, we do not have an independent ground truth. Hence, evaluating the performance will involve some degree of subjectivity. One way of circumventing this challenge is to use simulations to create synthetic data. However, most synthetic datasets are unable to capture the full spectrum of complexities found in real datasets, and more work is required to build on recent developments [56].
excerpts.anchor: Benchmarking and development of novel methods
excerpts.note: Concedes the lack of independent ground truth in benchmarking and that synthetic data fail to capture real-data complexity.

excerpts.text: Today, most analysis tools and strategies are designed within the context of a smaller number of datasets or total cells. Many methods that are commonly used may not scale well to tens of millions of cells and thousands of conditions, and consequently, there is a need to increase computational and algorithmic efciency.
excerpts.anchor: Benchmarking and development of novel methods

touchpoints.otherLabel: Existing methods do not scale to atlas-size data

excerpts.note: States most current methods do not scale to tens of millions of cells and thousands of conditions.

excerpts.text: Foundation models learn generalizable representations of cell type and state from gene expression profles, and they can be used to annotate new datasets, project them into shared latent spaces, infer missing modalities, and simulate responses to genetic or pharmacological perturbations. Despite these advances, widespread practical use remains limited. Current challenges include technical barriers to applying these models in user-friendly interfaces, dealing with memory and computational infrastructure requirements, limited interpretability and explainability of model predictions and representations, and the lack of widespread stress-testing of models on noisy, rare and disease-specifc single cell datasets to improve trust.
excerpts.anchor: Benchmarking and development of novel methods
excerpts.note: Concedes single-cell foundation models are learned from gene-expression profiles and have limited practical use, interpretability, and stress-testing.

excerpts.text: Single-cell RNA-seq was the frst high-throughput method that allows for a high-plex characterization of individual cells, and thus it has been the most widely used approach [1, 88]. However, there are numerous other single-cell technologies under active development, and we foresee that over the coming years cell atlases will see an infux of other modalities [89].
excerpts.anchor: Beyond atlases of dissociated single-cell transcriptomes
excerpts.note: Concedes scRNA-seq has been the dominant approach and that other modalities are only anticipated to flow into atlases in coming years.

excerpts.text: This starts with the organizations supporting the cell atlas, which must develop standards and protocols for how to process and curate other modalities [90]. Ensuring that diferent modalities can be combined for joint analyses is key, but it will present some major challenges, e.g., developing pre-processing pipelines, ontologies, and determining what metadata to include.
excerpts.anchor: Beyond atlases of dissociated single-cell transcriptomes
excerpts.note: States that supporting other modalities requires new standards, pipelines, ontologies, and metadata, and that joint multimodal analysis is a major challenge.

excerpts.text: As multi-omics datasets become more complex and heterogeneous, future integration frameworks will need to account for missing modalities, difering noise characteristics, and scale.
excerpts.anchor: Beyond atlases of dissociated single-cell transcriptomes
excerpts.note: States future multi-omics integration must handle missing modalities, differing noise, and scale.

excerpts.text: Perhaps the most important direction of new technologies is toward spatial methods, primarily for transcriptomics and proteomics, but other modalities are likely to follow. Spatial data brings numerous challenges along with great potential for additional insights [94].
excerpts.anchor: Beyond atlases of dissociated single-cell transcriptomes
excerpts.note: Frames spatial transcriptomics and proteomics as the most important emerging direction, signaling spatial is not yet standard in atlases.

excerpts.text: Tis representation is relatively straightforward for individual datasets, and indeed is implemented in cell atlas interfaces such as CxG, but for multiple samples it becomes much more challenging. For multiple samples, it may be more useful to map cells/spots to a common coordinate system, either informed by relative landmarks [95] or by merging across multiple samples [96].
excerpts.anchor: Beyond atlases of dissociated single-cell transcriptomes
excerpts.note: Notes that handling spatial data across multiple samples is challenging and motivates a common coordinate system.

excerpts.text: Mining spatial data typically involves fnding subcellular patterns in cells associated with disease, type, and outcomes [97], as well as spatial patterns from histopathological image data that can then be used across archival histology data without the companion omics layers [98]. As technologies evolve and are able to profle larger tissue sections at subcellular resolution, a particular challenge will be to develop methods that can bridge molecular, cellular, and tissue-level patterns.
excerpts.anchor: Beyond atlases of dissociated single-cell transcriptomes
excerpts.note: Identifies subcellular spatial patterns and bridging molecular-cellular-tissue scales as challenges/value of spatial omics.

excerpts.text: Most likely, within the next few years, advancements in the felds of proteomics and metabolomics will enable the profling of large numbers of single cells via these modalities, unlocking more accurate modeling of metabolism, signaling, and cell–cell communication.
excerpts.note: Concedes single-cell proteomics and metabolomics are not yet available at scale and projects that they will unlock more accurate modeling of metabolism, signaling, and cell-cell communication.

excerpts.text: To realize the potential not just for biomedical research, but for other aspects of biology, we need better coverage of human populations across the age spectra and for multiple disease states.
excerpts.note: Concedes current atlases lack adequate coverage across human age and disease states.

excerpts.text: Single-cell data is information dense, and there is little chance that the lab that generated the data has the capacity to make all the discoveries that are possible, in particular those that are only possible by combining with other datasets.
excerpts.note: Argues that combining datasets across labs is necessary to extract the full value of single-cell data.

Read at source: ref #87 (Hrovatin K, et al. 2025, Nat Methods, 'Considerations for building and using integrated single-cell atlases') and ref #51 (Squair JW, et al. 2021, Nat Commun, false discoveries / replicate argument) are worth first-hand consultation for standards and biological-replicate claims.

Direction note: this paper mostly concedes our gaps as facts (spatial, modality breadth, standards, scale) rather than disputing them; few lines actively challenge the thesis, so most touchpoints are 'supports' (conceded-fact) — it can also be deployed as a mainstream-perspective foil that defends the scRNA-seq atlas paradigm.

title: Nicheformer: a foundation model for single-cell and spatial omics
authors: unverified; see flags
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/07 - Nicheformer - a foundation model for single-cell and spatial omics_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors introduce Nicheformer, a transformer foundation model pretrained on SpatialCorpus-110M (over 110 million dissociated single-cell and image-based spatial transcriptomics cells across 73 tissues, human and mouse), and show it learns spatially aware cell representations that predict spatial niche labels, neighborhood composition and density and transfer spatial context onto dissociated scRNA-seq data, with models trained on dissociated data alone failing to recover spatial microenvironment complexity.
orientation: Strongly on-roadmap for the spatial-loss and multiscale-integration arguments: it explicitly concedes that scRNA-seq dissociation loses microenvironment information and demonstrates empirically that dissociated-only training cannot recover spatial structure (direct support for Claim 2 and the spatial axis). It is also a useful foil/nuance: it is itself an RNA-only (transcriptomics-only) foundation model that does NOT encode physical coordinates, does NOT use protein/metabolite/epigenetic modalities, and concedes paired multimodal data are lacking and benchmarks are non-standardized (feeds Claims 1, 4, 9 and the modality/standard axes, often as the very approach we critique conceding the gap we build on). Routing hint, non-binding.

excerpts.text: However, single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) requires cell dissociation, losing information about the cellular microenvironment and hindering a complete understanding of molecular variation⁴.
excerpts.note: States that scRNA-seq dissociation loses cellular microenvironment information and impedes a complete understanding of molecular variation.

excerpts.text: Critically, we show that models trained only on dissociated data fail to recover the complexity of spatial microenvironments, underscoring the need for multiscale integration.
excerpts.note: Empirically claims dissociated-only models cannot recover spatial microenvironment complexity, motivating multiscale (dissociated + spatial) integration.

excerpts.text: Specifically, training on dissociated data alone (even three times the amount of spatial data) resulted in lower performance across downstream tasks (Extended Data Fig. 2a,b), indicating that dissociated data alone cannot capture spatial variation.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Model design and training / p.2
excerpts.note: Quantitative ablation: more dissociated data (3x the spatial amount) still underperforms, evidence that dissociated data alone cannot capture spatial variation.

excerpts.text: A limiting factor for image-based spatial transcriptomics data is the targeted feature space, measuring only hundreds to a few thousands of genes, depending on technology and panel³¹.
excerpts.anchor: Results — A transformer-based foundation model / p.2
excerpts.note: Concedes a trade-off of image-based spatial transcriptomics: gaining spatial resolution costs feature breadth, measuring only hundreds to a few thousand targeted genes.

excerpts.text: Previous works³¹ have demonstrably shown technology-dependent biases between spatial and dissociated transcriptomics data, with spatial data often yielding higher gene counts due to preprocessing steps³².
excerpts.note: States there are technology-dependent biases (batch effects) between spatial and dissociated data, requiring technology-specific normalization for cross-technology comparability.

excerpts.text: Notably, these models do not account for spatial relationships of cells during training, with the exception of CellPLM²⁹, which, however, is trained on a limited dataset of 9 million dissociated and 2 million spatial transcriptomics cells and not fine-tuned on spatial tasks beyond gene imputation.
excerpts.note: Notes that prior single-cell foundation models do not account for spatial relationships during training (CellPLM the partial exception), i.e. the dominant paradigm ignores space.

excerpts.text: Current results are promising but not entirely replicated in independent benchmarks²⁶⁻²⁸.
excerpts.note: Concedes that single-cell foundation model results are not entirely reproduced in independent benchmarks, signalling generalization and comparability concerns.

excerpts.text: These findings are statistically significant (analysis of variance, adjusted for false discovery rate (FDR); Extended Data Fig. 2a,c) and highlight the importance of data diversity in model training for optimal performance across context³³.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Model design and training / p.2
excerpts.note: Argues that data diversity (not sheer cell count) drives model performance across contexts, attributing the bottleneck to the input data composition.

excerpts.text: Importantly, this result is not influenced by the sheer number of cells since all models are trained with the same number of cells; the only difference is the diversity of the data.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Model design and training / p.2
excerpts.note: Clarifies that performance differences come from data diversity rather than cell count, isolating data composition as the limiting factor.

excerpts.text: Importantly, we did not integrate datasets into a unified latent space. Our goal was to preserve biological and technical variability while offering a large-scale resource for model training. Like CellXGene, SpatialCorpus-110M provides curated raw inputs, allowing researchers to choose their own normalization and integration strategies.
excerpts.note: Describes a curated, harmonized raw corpus with standardized ontologies but explicitly leaves normalization/integration to the user rather than imposing one integrated standard.

excerpts.text: For all datasets in the SpatialCorpus-110M, we curated metadata, such as assay, sex, organism and tissue, based on the original publica tions by using official ontology term identifiers (Fig. 2c and Methods).
excerpts.note: Reports harmonizing heterogeneous datasets via official ontology term identifiers, an example of standardization/metadata curation to make siloed datasets comparable.

excerpts.text: Cell types are defined ignoring the spatial context, which provides additional value for understanding cellular microenvironments⁵³.
excerpts.note: States cell-type definitions ignore spatial context, and that spatial context adds value for understanding cellular microenvironments.

excerpts.text: Differences in neighborhood composition have been shown to have an important effect on gene expression and can be associated with cell–cell communication events⁶.
excerpts.note: Claims neighborhood/microenvironment composition meaningfully affects a cell's gene expression and links to cell-cell communication.

excerpts.text: Furthermore, the cellular composition of neighborhoods in the tumor microenvironment may hold prognostic value, because immune cell infiltration in the spatial context is a predictor for cancer survival⁵⁵.
excerpts.note: States spatial neighborhood composition (immune infiltration) has prognostic value, tying spatial microenvironment to functional/clinical phenotype.

excerpts.text: Consistent with literature observations⁶³,⁶⁴, we observed a higher average cellular density in the cancer sections (colon, 12.3 cells; lung, 12.1 cells) compared to healthy tissue (colon, 10.7 cells; lung, 10.7 cells) when extracting cellular neighborhoods at the same radius (Fig. 6a,f and Methods).
excerpts.note: Quantitative: cancer tissue shows higher local cell density than healthy tissue, an example of disease-state-dependent spatial microenvironment differences.

excerpts.text: More generally, it will be necessary to operate on multimodal data to generate a true representation of the cellular state. While spatial transcriptomics captures the cellular microenvironment in tissues well, integrating additional data modalities, such as protein abundance or epigenetic modifications, will provide a more complete picture of the cellular state.
excerpts.note: Concedes that transcriptomics alone is insufficient and that a true representation of cellular state requires multimodal data including protein abundance and epigenetic modifications.

excerpts.text: The development of multimodal foundation models faces multiple challenges. One key hurdle is the lack of sufficient paired data measured across multiple or even all cellular modalities.
excerpts.note: Identifies the lack of sufficient paired multi-modality data as a key bottleneck for multimodal foundation models.

excerpts.text: Incorporating additional modalities will remain a challenge in the future as, for example, epigenetic modifications, protein abundance and gene expression all have unique characteristics, and effectively combining them in a way that leverages their strengths remains an ongoing research area.
excerpts.note: States that protein abundance, epigenetic modifications and gene expression have distinct characteristics and that combining modalities to leverage their complementary strengths is unsolved.

excerpts.text: Secondly, Nicheformer does not explicitly incorporate the physical location of a cell during pretraining, limiting its capability to fully leverage the available information on spatial context. We deliberately chose not to include spatial coordinates during pretraining because we wanted to learn a general representation of gene expression variation across both modalities, fully supervised by gene expression alone.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: the model does not encode physical coordinates during pretraining and is supervised by gene expression alone, so explicit spatial geometry is not used.

excerpts.text: Firstly, Nicheformer performance depends on the data abundance and transcriptional diversity of the cells under study. Indeed, we showed that Nicheformer's performance for predicting spatial labels and spatial compositions is impacted by cell-type and tissue-type abundance in a spatial transcriptomics dataset.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: performance is bounded by data abundance and transcriptional diversity in the spatial dataset, i.e. the model is input-data-limited.

excerpts.text: However, unlike more established AI domains, there's a crucial gap in the form of standardized benchmarks for evaluating these models. Establishing robust benchmarks is a critical next step to compare and improve performance, rigorously assess methodological progress and guide future model development to unleash the full potential of foundation models for single-cell biology.
excerpts.note: Concedes a crucial gap: the field lacks standardized benchmarks for evaluating single-cell/spatial foundation models, making cross-model comparison and progress assessment difficult.

excerpts.text: Only expression data were used during pretraining to train the model to integrate data from dissociated and targeted spatial technologies, both of which show substantial batch effects (Fig. 1a).
excerpts.anchor: Results — A transformer-based foundation model / p.2
excerpts.note: States that only (RNA) expression data were used in pretraining and that both modalities show substantial batch effects requiring integration.

excerpts.text: Transferring labels between dissociated and spatial data is challenging due to limited gene overlap⁵⁴, and modality-specific methods are not designed to learn from reference atlases at the scale of hundreds of million of cells.
excerpts.note: Notes that limited gene overlap between dissociated and spatial data makes label transfer / integration across modalities challenging.

figuresNumbers.contains: over 110 million cells: 57.06 million dissociated cells and 53.8 (53.83) million image-based spatial cells; human and mouse; 73 different organs/tissues; over 350 datasets; eight sequencing technologies.
20,310 unique gene tokens: 16,981 orthologous, 3,178 human-specific, 151 mouse-specific genes.

Spatial: MERFISH/Xenium/CosMx/ISS; 15 tissues from 158 individuals/animals; over 10,600 tissue sections; brain 60.46% (n=32,146,779), lung 9.95% (n=3,199,548); 55.23% unannotated; 64.07% healthy and 31.98% cancer.
1,500-token context length; 12 transformer encoder layers; 16 attention heads; feed-forward size 1,024; 512-dimensional embedding; 49.3 million parameters.
Higher mean cellular density in cancer (colon 12.3, lung 12.1 cells) vs healthy (colon 10.7, lung 10.7 cells) at the same radius.
Overlap of targeted spatial panels with scGPT gene context: MERFISH mouse brain 471/483, CosMx human liver 997/999, CosMx human lung 958/960 genes.
CellPLM trained on 9 million dissociated and 2 million spatial transcriptomics cells.

title: Visualization and analysis of gene expression in tissue sections by spatial transcriptomics
authors: Patrik L. Ståhl, Fredrik Salmén, Sanja Vickovic, Anna Lundmark, José Fernández Navarro, Jens Magnusson, Stefania Giacomello, Michaela Asp, Jakub O. Westholm, Mikael Huss, Annelie Mollbrink, Sten Linnarsson, Simone Codeluppi, Åke Borg, Fredrik Pontén, Paul Igor Costea, Pelin Sahlén, Jan Mulder, Olaf Bergmann, Joakim Lundeberg, Jonas Frisén
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/08a - Spatial transcriptomics - Stahl et al. Science 2016_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors devised "spatial transcriptomics," a method that places histological tissue sections onto a slide arrayed with positionally barcoded reverse-transcription primers, so that RNA-sequencing of the section yields genome-wide expression while retaining two-dimensional positional information, demonstrated in mouse brain and human breast cancer.
orientation: This is a foundational spatial-omics method paper that most directly feeds our claim that the standard dissociation/homogenization paradigm destroys spatial information, and our claim that high-throughput spatial measurement is feasible. It concedes verbatim that conventional RNA-seq averages the transcriptome and loses spatial context (support for our spatial-gap argument) while presenting a remedy (support for spatial feasibility). It can also serve as a nuance/foil on the modality axis: spatial transcriptomics restores space but is still RNA-only, and its sensitivity is far below FISH, so it illustrates the resolution-throughput trade-off.

excerpts.text: Tissue transcriptomes are typically studied by RNA-sequencing (RNA-seq) ( ) of ho-1mogenized biopsies, which results in an averaged transcriptome and loss of spatial information.
excerpts.note: States that the standard RNA-seq workflow homogenizes biopsies, producing an averaged transcriptome and discarding spatial information.

excerpts.text: The positional context of gene expression is of key importance to understanding tissue functionality and pathological changes.
excerpts.note: Asserts that spatial/positional context of expression is essential to understanding tissue function and disease.

excerpts.text: Several strategies have recently been developed with this aim ( – ), but they have limitations in 2 5the number of transcripts that can be analyzed, rely on rich preexisting data sets, and/or are costly and labor-intensive, and none of them are operational in the standard research and diagnostic setting of regular histological tissue sections.
excerpts.note: Concedes that prior spatial methods are limited in transcript number, dependent on preexisting data, costly/labor-intensive, and not usable in standard histology settings.

excerpts.text: We have devised a strategy, which we call “spatial transcriptomics,” that allows visualization and quantitative analysis of the transcriptome with spatial resolution in individual tissue sections.
excerpts.note: Introduces spatial transcriptomics as a method for quantitative, spatially resolved transcriptome analysis in tissue sections.

excerpts.text: By positioning histological sections on arrayed reverse transcription primers with unique positional barcodes, we demonstrate high-quality RNA-sequencing data with maintained two-dimensional positional information from the mouse brain and human breast cancer.
excerpts.note: Demonstrates that positional barcoding preserves 2D spatial information alongside RNA-seq data in two tissue types.

excerpts.text: We deposited 200 million oligonucleotides in each of 1007 features, with a diameter of 100 m and a center-to-center distance of 200 m, over an area of 6.2 mm by 6.6 mm (fig. S2).
excerpts.note: Specifies the array geometry: 1007 features of 100 um diameter at 200 um center-to-center spacing, defining the spatial resolution of the platform.

excerpts.text: Furthermore, we compared spatial transcriptomics with the near-100% sensitivity of single-molecule fluorescent in situ hybridization in adjacent tissue sections. The sensitivity of spatial transcriptomics was 6.9 ± 1.5% of singlemolecule fluorescent in situ hybridization (fig. S6). By comparison, single-cell RNA sequencing has been reported to have about 5 to 40% sensitivity ( ).
excerpts.note: Quantifies a sensitivity trade-off: spatial transcriptomics detects only 6.9% of transcripts relative to smFISH, comparable to the 5-40% sensitivity reported for single-cell RNA-seq.

excerpts.text: The number of genes and transcripts captured was at least twice as high as when using laser capture microdissection ( ), and spatial transcriptomics detected 11almost twice as many genes as examination by in situ hybridization in the Allen Brain Atlas (fig. S5, C and D).
excerpts.note: Reports that spatial transcriptomics captures at least twice as many genes/transcripts as laser capture microdissection and nearly twice as many genes as in situ hybridization.

excerpts.text: Analysis of the ductal cancer in situ areas revealed a surprisingly high degree of heterogeneity in gene expression between these regions, probably reflecting different subclones, with varying expression of several genes implicated in cancer progression (Fig. 4E and fig. S11C).
excerpts.note: Finds spatially resolved heterogeneity in gene expression across separate ductal-cancer-in-situ regions of one biopsy, attributed to distinct subclones.

excerpts.text: Thus, spatial transcriptomics revealed unexpected heterogeneity within a biopsy, which would not be possible to detect with regular transcriptome analysis and which may give more detailed prognostic information.
excerpts.note: States that intra-biopsy spatial heterogeneity detected here is invisible to conventional (bulk, non-spatial) transcriptome analysis.

excerpts.text: In contrast to standard methods, different domains of the tissue are processed in the same reaction in spatial transcriptomics, which removes technical variation between samples.
excerpts.note: Claims that processing all tissue domains in a single reaction removes between-sample technical variation, a comparability advantage over standard methods.

excerpts.text: Finally, in contrast to when different regions of a tissue are dissected for analysis, the information for the whole section is maintained; hence, the analysis is not limited to the initially selected regions. An individual spatial transcriptomics experiment thus serves as a permanent resource to investigate gene expression patterns for future research questions.
excerpts.note: States that unlike dissection-based analysis, the whole-section information is retained, making each experiment a reusable spatial resource rather than a region-limited measurement.

excerpts.text: By comparing the hematoxylin-and-eosin and fluorescent signals, we could measure the average distance of diffusion outside the border of a cell to 1.7 ± 2 m (mean ± SD) (fig. S1, E to H).
excerpts.note: Quantifies lateral mRNA diffusion as 1.7 um outside a cell border, bounding the spatial fidelity of the capture.

figuresNumbers.contains: 200 million oligonucleotides per feature; 1007 features; 100 um feature diameter; 200 um center-to-center distance; area 6.2 mm x 6.6 mm.
Spatial transcriptomics sensitivity = 6.9 ± 1.5% of single-molecule FISH; single-cell RNA-seq reported at ~5 to 40% sensitivity.
95% of genes found by one method also found by the other (surface vs in-solution); surface vs in-solution correlation r = 0.94; replicate adjacent-section correlation r = 0.97.
At least twice as many genes/transcripts as laser capture microdissection; almost twice as many genes as in situ hybridization in the Allen Brain Atlas.
Average mRNA diffusion distance outside a cell border = 1.7 ± 2 um (mean ± SD).
t-SNE plus hierarchical clustering of 551 features from two replicates yields five distinct clusters mapping onto morphological layers.
170 differentially expressed genes between distinct domains; 196 and 328 differentially expressed genes between interneuron-marker feature profiles.

flags: OCR quirks preserved verbatim: reference markers appear as empty parentheses '( )', interleaved digits (e.g. 'ho-1mogenized', '6.9 ... ( )'), and dropped micron symbols (e.g. '1.7 ± 2 m' for 1.7 ± 2 um, '100 m'/'200 m' for um). Quotes copied as-is per skill rule.

Metadata verified from text: DOI 10.1126/science.aaf2403 (line 64/70); venue Science 353(6294):78-82, published 30 June 2016 (Editor's Summary line 70); received 12 January 2016, accepted 31 May 2016 (line 63).

Scope note: This paper is RNA-only spatial transcriptomics; it restores the spatial axis but does not address the modality-breadth gap (no protein/PTM/metabolite). Useful as a nuance/foil on axis A (modality) even though it strongly supports axis C (spatial).
Scope note: Method is on fixed tissue sections (a static snapshot); it does not provide time-course/trajectory data, so it does not bear on the temporal axis (B).

title: Spatially resolved, highly multiplexed RNA profiling in single cells
authors: Kok Hao Chen, Alistair N. Boettiger, Jeffrey R. Moffitt, Siyuan Wang, Xiaowei Zhuang
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/08b - MERFISH - spatially resolved highly multiplexed RNA profiling in single cells_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors developed MERFISH, a single-molecule imaging method that uses combinatorial labeling and sequential hybridization with error-robust encoding to simultaneously measure copy numbers and spatial localizations of 140 to 1001 RNA species in hundreds of individual human fibroblast cells.
orientation: An in situ, image-based spatial transcriptomics method paper. It most directly feeds Claim 2 (scRNA-seq loses spatial information -> spatial omics methods) and the spatial axis (Claim C), demonstrating that thousands of RNA species can be profiled while preserving subcellular and native spatial context. It also bears on the modality axis as a transcriptome-only (RNA) method, and can serve as a foil for the temporal axis (it is a single fixed snapshot of fixed cells, no time course). Its finding that RNA spatial distribution correlates with the function/localization of the encoded protein can be used both to support spatial information's value and to nuance modality complementarity.

excerpts.text: Knowledge of the expression profile and spatial landscape of the transcriptome in individual cells is essential for understanding the rich repertoire of cellular behaviors. Here, we report multiplexed error-robust fluorescence in situ hybridization (MERFISH), a single-molecule imaging approach that allows the copy numbers and spatial localizations of thousands of RNA species to be determined in single cells.
excerpts.note: States MERFISH measures copy numbers AND spatial localizations of thousands of RNA species in single cells, framing spatial landscape as essential.

excerpts.text: System-wide analyses of the abundance and spatial organization of RNAs in single cells promise to transform our understanding in many areas of cell and developmental biology, such as the mechanisms of gene regulation, the heterogeneous behavior of cells, and the development and maintenance of cell fate (1).
excerpts.note: Asserts the importance of spatial organization of RNAs for understanding cell behavior and fate.

excerpts.text: Single-molecule fluorescence in situ hybridization (smFISH) has emerged as a powerful tool for studying the copy number and spatial organization of RNAs in single cells either in isolation or in their native tissue context (2, 3). Taking advantage of its ability to map the spatial distributions of specific RNAs with high resolution, smFISH has revealed the importance of subcellular RNA localization in diverse processes such as cell migration, development, and polarization (4–8).
excerpts.note: States smFISH preserves native tissue context and that subcellular RNA localization matters for migration, development, and polarization.

excerpts.text: However, application of the smFISH approach to many systemslevel questions remains limited by the number of RNA species that can be simultaneously measured in single cells. State-of-the-art efforts by using combinatorial labeling with either colorbased barcodes or sequential hybridization have enabled simultaneous measurements of 10 to 30 different RNA species in individual cells (16–19), yet many interesting biological questions would benefit from the measurement of hundreds to thousands of RNAs within a single cell.
excerpts.note: Concedes prior spatial/imaging methods were limited to 10-30 RNA species, motivating the throughput increase to hundreds-thousands.

excerpts.text: knowledge of the subcellular organizations of numerous RNAs and their correlations could help elucidate molecular mechanisms underlying the establishment and maintenance of many local cellular structures, and RNA profiling of individual cells in native tissues could allow in situ identification of cell type.
excerpts.note: Argues subcellular spatial organization of RNAs and in-situ native-tissue profiling enable cell-type identification and study of local cellular structures.

excerpts.text: We demonstrated this transcriptome imaging approach by simultaneously measuring 140 RNA species with an encoding scheme that can both detect and correct errors and 1001 RNA species with an encoding scheme that can detect but not correct errors.
excerpts.note: Reports demonstrated throughput of 140 genes (error-correcting) and 1001 genes (error-detecting) per single cell.

excerpts.text: We first imaged 140 RNA species in human fibroblast cells using MERFISH with 16 rounds of hybridization and a modified Hamming code capable of both error detection and correction. We obtained \~80% detection efficiency and observed excellent correlation of RNA copy numbers determined with MERFISH with both bulk RNA sequencing data and conventional smFISH measurements of individual genes.
excerpts.note: Reports ~80% detection efficiency for the 140-gene MHD4 measurement and good correlation with bulk RNA-seq and smFISH.

excerpts.text: In order to validate the copy numbers derived from our MERFISH experiments, we performed conventional smFISH measurements on 15 of the 130 genes, spanning the full measured abundance range of three orders of magnitude. For each of these genes, both the average copy number and the copy number distribution across many cells agreed quantitatively between our MERFISH and conventional smFISH measurements (fig. S8, A and B).
excerpts.anchor: Measuring 140 genes with MERFISH by use of a 16-bit MHD4 code
excerpts.note: Cross-validates MERFISH copy numbers against an orthogonal gold-standard (smFISH) over three orders of magnitude of abundance.

excerpts.text: Given that the agreement between the MERFISH and conventional smFISH results extended to the genes at the lowest measured abundance (<1 copy per cell) (fig. S8B), we estimate that our measurement sensitivity was better than 1 copy per cell.
excerpts.anchor: Measuring 140 genes with MERFISH by use of a 16-bit MHD4 code
excerpts.note: Reports measurement sensitivity better than 1 copy per cell, i.e. without amplification bias.

excerpts.text: As a final validation, we compared the abundance of each RNA species averaged over hundreds of cells to those obtained from a bulk RNA sequencing measurement that we performed on the same cell line. Our imaging results correlated remarkably well with bulk sequencing results, with a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.89 (Fig. 2H).
excerpts.anchor: Measuring 140 genes with MERFISH by use of a 16-bit MHD4 code
excerpts.note: Validates the imaging method against bulk RNA-seq (Pearson r = 0.89) on the same cell line.

excerpts.text: Next, we used an alternative MERFISH encoding scheme, which is capable of detecting but not correcting errors, to image 1001 RNA species in individual cells using only 14 rounds of hybridization. The observed RNA copy numbers again correlate well with bulk sequencing data. However, the detection efficiency is only one-third that of the error-correcting encoding scheme.
excerpts.note: Reports a throughput-vs-accuracy trade-off: scaling to 1001 genes via MHD2 drops detection efficiency to one-third of the error-correcting MHD4 scheme.

excerpts.text: As the degree of multiplexing is increased, it is important to consider the potential increase in the density of RNAs that need to be resolved in each round of imaging. On the basis of our imaging and sequencing results, we estimate that including the whole transcriptome of the IMR90 cells would lead to a total RNA density of \~200 molecules/mm<sup>3</sup>. Using our current imaging and analysis methods, we could resolve 2 to 3 molecules/mm<sup>3</sup> per hybridization round (38), which would reach a total RNA density of \~20 molecules/mm<sup>3</sup> after 32 rounds of hybridization. This density should allow all but the top 10% most expressed genes to be imaged simultaneously or a subset of genes with even higher expression levels to be included.
excerpts.note: Concedes a molecular-crowding/density limit: current methods cannot resolve the whole transcriptome simultaneously, only all but the top ~10% most expressed genes.

excerpts.text: Last, theoretical predictions (17) indicate that the use of superresolution imaging (41, 42) could increase the resolvable density to \~10<sup>5</sup> molecules/mm<sup>3</sup>, which should be ample to address the entire transcriptome, even in cell types with RNA densities substantially higher than that of IMR90. However, RNAs in densely packed structures, such as p-bodies and stress granules, may still elude measurement.
excerpts.note: Concedes that even with super-resolution, RNAs in densely packed structures such as P-bodies and stress granules may still escape measurement.

excerpts.text: As an imaging-based approach, MERFISH also allowed us to investigate the spatial distributions of many RNA species simultaneously. Several patterns emerged from the visual inspection of individual genes, with some RNA transcripts enriched in the perinuclear region, some enriched in the cell periphery, and some scattered throughout the cell (Fig. 4A).
excerpts.note: Demonstrates subcellular spatial distribution patterns (perinuclear, peripheral, scattered) are directly resolvable, which dissociation-based sequencing would erase.

excerpts.text: Group I contained genes encoding extracellular proteins such as FBN1, FBN2, and THSB1; secreted proteins such as PAPPA; and integral membrane proteins such as LRP1 and GPR107. These proteins have no obvious commonalities in function. Rather, a GO analysis showed significant enrichment for location terms, such as extracellular region, basement membrane, or perivitelline space (Fig. 4E). To reach these locations, proteins must pass through the secretion pathway, which often requires translation of mRNA at the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) (34, 35). Thus, we propose that the spatial pattern that we observed for these mRNAs reflects their cotranslational enrichment at the ER.
excerpts.note: Links mRNA subcellular spatial pattern to the localization/destination of the encoded protein (ER cotranslational enrichment), connecting RNA spatial position to protein-layer information.

excerpts.text: We further analyzed correlations in the spatial distributions of different RNA species and identified groups of RNAs with different distribution patterns in the cell.
excerpts.note: Reports that spatial distribution patterns of RNAs can be correlated and grouped across genes, a readout unavailable to dissociated sequencing.

excerpts.text: Such covariation analysis can constrain regulatory networks, suggest new regulatory pathways, and predict function for unannotated genes based on associations with covarying genes (11, 28).
excerpts.anchor: Analysis of expression covariation among different genes
excerpts.note: States that single-cell covariation analysis can constrain gene regulatory networks and predict function for unannotated genes.

excerpts.text: The groups identified here included 46 RNA species lacking any previous GO annotations, for which we can now hypothesize function on the basis of their group association (table S4).
excerpts.anchor: Measuring 1001 genes with MERFISH by use of a 14-bit MHD2 code
excerpts.note: Quantifies functional-hypothesis output: 46 unannotated RNA species assigned candidate functions via covariation grouping in the 1001-gene data.

excerpts.text: Given its ability to quantify RNAs across a wide range of abundances without amplification bias while preserving native context, we envision that MERFISH will enable many applications of in situ transcriptomic analyses of individual cells in culture or complex tissues.
excerpts.note: Frames MERFISH as in-situ transcriptomics that preserves native spatial context without amplification bias, in culture or complex tissues.

excerpts.text: This work focuses on mRNAs, which are enriched in the cytoplasm. To estimate the fraction of mRNAs missed by excluding the nucleus region, we used conventional smFISH to quantify the fraction of molecules found inside the nucleus for six different mRNAs species. We found that only 5 T 2% (mean T SEM across six RNA species) of these RNA molecules are found in the nucleus. Use of super-resolution imaging and/or optical sectioning could potentially allow individual molecules in these dense nucleus regions to be identified, which will be particularly useful for probing those noncoding RNAs that are enriched in the nucleus.
excerpts.note: Concedes a coverage limitation: dense nuclear regions had to be excluded, so nucleus-enriched and noncoding RNAs were largely not measured (modality/coverage caveat).

figuresNumbers.contains: 130 RNA species encoded + 10 misidentification controls; 16 rounds of hybridization; ~80% calling rate after error correction; ~192 encoding probes per RNA; ~400 cells across seven independent experiments.
985 words encode RNAs + 16 misidentification controls; 14 rounds of hybridization; ~94 encoding probes per RNA; ~200 cells in three experiments; calling rate ~1/3 (~30%) of the MHD4 code.
1->0 error rate 10% on average; 0->1 error rate 4% on average.

Two shuffled MHD4 codebooks Pearson r = 0.94 (P = 1e-53); MERFISH vs bulk RNA-seq (140-gene) r = 0.89 (P = 3e-39); 1001-gene high-confidence (73%) vs bulk r = 0.76 (P = 3e-133), remaining 27% r = 0.65 (P = 3e-33); 107 shared genes 1001-gene vs 140-gene r = 0.89 (P = 9e-30).
MERFISH/smFISH copy-number ratio 0.82 +/- 0.06 (mean +/- SEM across 15 genes); sensitivity better than 1 copy per cell.
MHD4: 95% of 130 words counted above control median, 91% above max control confidence ratio; MHD2: 77% above control median, 73% above max control confidence ratio.
140-gene: 7 correlated gene groups; 1001-gene: ~100 groups; 46 unannotated RNAs and 61 transcription factors/partially-annotated proteins given hypothesized functions.

Two spatial groups: group I perinuclear (secreted/membrane proteins, ER cotranslational), group II cell-periphery (actin-binding, cytoskeletal proteins); 15 +/- 1% of RNA molecules outside fixed focal plane; 5 +/- 2% of mRNA molecules inside nucleus.
IMR90 whole-transcriptome RNA density ~200 molecules/mm^3; resolvable 2-3 molecules/mm^3 per round, ~20 molecules/mm^3 after 32 rounds; super-resolution could reach ~10^5 molecules/mm^3.
32-bit MHD4 code with four or six 1 bits would address 1240 or 27,776 RNA species; with just 16 hybridizations a simple binary code addresses >64,000 species but at high misidentification.
