flags: Metadata verified from text: DOI 10.1126/science.aaa6090, Science 348(6233):aaa6090, received 5 January 2015, accepted 19 March 2015, published online 9 April 2015 (lines 414, 420).

OCR quirks preserved verbatim in excerpts and figuresNumbers: '\~' for approximately, 'T' used where source means '+/-' (e.g. '0.82 T 0.06', '15 T 1%', '5 T 2%'), 'THSB1' likely THBS1, superscript tags like <sup>3</sup> in molecules/mm^3.

Scope note: This paper measures only the RNA/transcriptome modality (it is image-based smFISH); it carries no protein, PTM, metabolite, or interactome measurement. Useful as the spatial-transcriptomics exemplar (Claim 2 / axis C) and as a single-modality datapoint, not as multimodal evidence.

Temporal axis note: MERFISH here is performed on chemically fixed cells (4% paraformaldehyde) and is a destructive, single-snapshot measurement with no time course or lineage tracing; relevant as a foil for the temporal axis (Claim B / Claim 3) though the paper does not discuss time explicitly.

Read at source: ref #17 (Lubeck & Cai 2012, Nat. Methods 9:743-748) is the theoretical basis for the resolvable-density / super-resolution scaling claims and is worth first-hand citation if those numbers are used.

title: A practical guide for choosing an optimal spatial transcriptomics technology from seven major commercially available options
authors: Hyun Ju Lim, Ye Wang, Anton Buzdin, Xinmin Li
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/09 - A practical guide for choosing an optimal spatial transcriptomics technology_cleaned.md
oneSentence: A review that compares seven commercially available spatial transcriptomics platforms (10X Visium, GeoMx DSP, Visium HD, Stereoseq, Merscope, Xenium, CosMx) across cellular resolution, sensitivity, gene/protein coverage, throughput, species/tissue compatibility, and cost, and gives a decision guide for choosing a platform per research aim.
orientation: Feeds the spatial axis and the resolution-throughput-destructiveness trade-off claims: it explicitly states that single-cell and bulk sequencing destroy spatial/structural information, motivating spatial methods (supports Claim 2). It is itself RNA-only spatial transcriptomics, so it documents the modality-narrowness gap our thesis builds on: most platforms profile transcriptome only, protein is limited or unavailable, and no platform reaches whole-transcriptome at true single-cell resolution (can serve as foil for Claims 4/6 and support for the trilemma Claim 10). It also surfaces cross-platform incomparability and lateral-diffusion/sensitivity variability (Claim 9). Non-binding routing hint.

excerpts.text: However, this functionally critical spatial information is lost in widely used bulking sequencing and popular single cell sequencing due to the disruption of tissue structural organization during the sample process.
excerpts.note: States that bulk and single-cell sequencing lose spatial information because tissue structural organization is disrupted during sample processing.

excerpts.text: This sophisticated organization of cells in space defines the structure of living organisms and their specific functions.
excerpts.note: Asserts that the spatial organization of cells determines tissue structure and function.

excerpts.text: Therefore, comprehending the precise locations where genes are expressed within highly structured tissues is essential for elucidating various fundamental biological processes, such as gene functions, gene-gene interactions, cell-cell communication, dynamic molecular and cellular processes, and microenvironmental influences [1].
excerpts.note: Argues that knowing spatial gene-expression locations is essential for cell-cell communication and microenvironmental influence.

excerpts.text: Sequencing-based technologies provide whole transcriptome analysis, though most lack single-cell resolution. They typically have lower RNA capture eficiency and detection sensitivity compared to imaging-based technologies, especially for low-abundance transcripts [20].
excerpts.note: States the sequencing-vs-imaging trade-off: whole transcriptome but most lack single-cell resolution and have lower capture efficiency/sensitivity.

excerpts.text: imaging-based technologies ofer singlecell or subcellular resolution, high RNA detection sensitivity, specificity, and reproducibility. However, they are limited to gene panels ranging from several hundred to six thousand genes, require longer imaging times varying from two days to a week or more, and have low throughput.
excerpts.note: States imaging-based platforms give single-cell/subcellular resolution but are limited to gene panels (hundreds to ~6000 genes), long imaging time, and low throughput — a resolution-vs-coverage/throughput trade-off.

excerpts.text: The main limitation is the lack of single-cell resolution, which is due to the ≥ 55 μm spot size in Visium (the average size of a mammalian cell is typically around 10  μm in diameter) and the ≥ 50-cell ROI size (Vendor recommended) in GeoMx. Consequently, each spot or ROI captures transcripts from multiple cell types, resulting in an average or mixed gene expression signal across these cell types.
excerpts.note: Quantifies that Visium 55 um spots and GeoMx >=50-cell ROIs exceed the ~10 um cell size, so each spot/ROI mixes multiple cell types into an averaged signal.

excerpts.text: To address this limitation, deconvolution tools have been developed and commonly used in analyzing G1 data. By integrating spatial transcriptomic data with singlecell RNA sequencing data from adjacent tissue sections, deconvolution can assign specific gene expression profiles to distinct cell types within each spot, allowing for a more accurate understanding of cellular architecture in tissues [21, 22].
excerpts.note: States that deconvolution integrates spatial data with single-cell RNA-seq from adjacent sections to assign cell-type profiles to spots — a multi-data integration workaround for low resolution.

excerpts.text: However, it is important to note that feature size is not the only factor influencing actual resolution; lateral difusion during tissue permeabilization also significantly impacts the spatial accuracy of transcript detection. Stereoseq has shown notable lateral difusion in some tissues [2].
excerpts.note: Notes that nominal feature size overstates true spatial accuracy because lateral diffusion during permeabilization degrades it, with Stereoseq showing notable diffusion in some tissues.

excerpts.text: Additionally, the term ‘single-cell resolution’ can be somewhat misleading. Platforms like Visium HD and Stereo-seq can detect only a few hundred to a thousand genes when data are exported to achieve single-cell scale/resolution. This level of resolution comes at the expense of a reduced number of detectable genes. As of now, sequencing-based spatial technologies are not yet capable of delivering whole-transcriptome data at true single-cell resolution.
excerpts.note: States the resolution-vs-gene-coverage trade-off: at single-cell scale Visium HD/Stereoseq detect only a few hundred to a thousand genes, and no sequencing-based spatial technology yet delivers whole-transcriptome at true single-cell resolution.

excerpts.text: As of November 28, 2024, G2 group is limited to transcriptome analysis only.
excerpts.note: States that Visium HD and Stereoseq are limited to transcriptome (RNA) analysis only, with no protein readout.

excerpts.text: As of November 28, 2024, Visium HD, Stereoseq, and Xenium are limited to transcriptomic (RNA) analysis only. The remaining platforms are capable of both RNA and protein profiling.
excerpts.note: States that three of seven platforms are RNA-only, while the others can profile both RNA and protein — documenting limited protein coverage in spatial platforms.

excerpts.text: Merscope enables simultaneous RNA and protein profiling on the same slide, while CosMx performs this sequentially, and Xenium does not currently support protein panel.
excerpts.note: Notes platform-specific protein capability: Merscope co-profiles RNA and protein on one slide, CosMx does so sequentially, Xenium has no protein panel.

excerpts.text: Both Xenium and Merscope demonstrate superior specificity compared to CosMx [23]. Xenium exhibits exceptional sensitivity with FFPE tissues, while Merscope outperforms Xenium with high-quality tissue [24].
excerpts.note: Reports benchmarking results that platforms differ in sensitivity/specificity by tissue type (Xenium/Merscope > CosMx; Xenium best on FFPE, Merscope best on high-quality tissue).

excerpts.text: Imaging-based platforms generally ofer superior detection sensitivity and specificity compared to sequencing-based platforms. Among sequencing technologies, probe-based methods outperform polyAbased approaches, particularly for detecting lowly expressed genes. Studies indicate that 10X Xenium and Merscope technologies provide better sensitivity and specificity than CosMx [23, 24]. Probe-based Visium technology is considered more efective than Stereo-seq [2].
excerpts.note: States that sensitivity/specificity vary systematically across platforms and capture chemistries (imaging > sequencing; probe-based > polyA; Xenium/Merscope > CosMx; Visium > Stereoseq).

excerpts.text: Spatial transcriptomics technologies are still in the early stages of development and are rapidly evolving. The performance of each technology is likely to improve over time with ongoing advancements.
excerpts.note: Concedes that spatial transcriptomics is early-stage and rapidly changing, so platform performance and any guide will need updating over time.

excerpts.text: Spatial technologies are advancing rapidly, with key areas for further improvement, such as enhancing spatial resolution without significantly reducing the number of detectable genes for sequencing-based technologies and increasing the number of profiled genes without substantially extending scanning time for imaging-based technologies.
excerpts.note: Frames the central unresolved trade-offs: resolution vs gene count (sequencing-based) and gene count vs scanning time (imaging-based).

excerpts.text: One future direction for spatial genomics is the development of 3D spatial multiomics technologies. StellarOmics’ imaging-based in-situ sequencing technology shows great promise in this area [25]. Unlike the standard 5–10 μm thick sections used in current spatial technologies, StellarOmics can image tissue sections up to 200 μm thick, enabling multi-cell layer profiling for deeper insights into tissue architecture and function.
excerpts.note: Identifies 3D spatial multiomics (e.g. StellarOmics imaging thick sections up to 200 um vs standard 5-10 um) as a future direction, implying current spatial methods are largely 2D and single-modality.

excerpts.text: the spatial content in the dataset is crucial for answering your questions that cannot be satisfactorily addressed by other simpler or more cost-efective technologies. Examples of such questions include understanding how tumor cells interact with immune cells, how the tumor microenvironment afects tumor metastasis, and how drug treatment activates immune pathways, facilitates immune cell infiltration, and impacts tumor cell viability.
excerpts.note: Gives examples where spatial context is essential (tumor-immune interaction, microenvironment effects on metastasis, drug-induced immune infiltration) that non-spatial methods cannot address.

figuresNumbers.contains: Comparative summary across seven platforms. Resolution: 10X Visium 55 um; GeoMx >55 um (>=50 cells/ROI); Visium HD single-cell scale (2 um feature); Stereoseq single cell (0.22 um feature); Merscope/Xenium/CosMx single cell/subcellular. Genes: Visium/GeoMx/Visium HD/Stereoseq whole transcriptome; Merscope panels ~500+~500 custom; Xenium up to 5000 genes +~100 custom; CosMx up to 6000+~200 custom. Proteins: Visium 35 sequential, GeoMx 570 sequential, Merscope 6 simultaneous, CosMx 68 sequential; Visium HD/Stereoseq/Xenium not available. Cost: Visium $3293+seq, GeoMx $3837+seq, Visium HD $6645+seq, Stereoseq $3054+seq, Merscope ~$6733, Xenium $3878, CosMx $6325 (estimated at UCLA TCGB).
Visium 55 um; Visium HD 2 um (no gap between spots); Stereoseq DNB ~0.2 um diameter with 0.5 um center-to-center distance (~0.22 um feature). Mammalian cell average ~10 um diameter.
Visium HD 6.5 mm x 6.5 mm; Stereoseq options 10 mm x 10 mm and 20 mm x 30 mm. Visium/Visium HD limited to 6.5 mm x 6.5 mm; other platforms >= 10 mm x 10 mm.
RIN >= 7 recommended for fresh tissue; DV200 >= 30% needed for FFPE tissue.
Visium HD is twice as expensive as Stereoseq for library construction despite analyzing fewer cells; Xenium is the most affordable imaging-based platform.
Standard spatial technologies use 5-10 um thick sections; StellarOmics can image up to 200 um thick.

title: Systematic benchmarking of high-throughput subcellular spatial transcriptomics across human tumors
authors: Pengfei Ren, Rui Zhang, Yunfeng Wang, Peng Zhang, Ce Luo, Suyan Wang, Xiaohong Li, Zongxu Zhang, Yanping Zhao, Yufeng He, Haorui Zhang, Yufeng Li, Zhidong Gao, Xiuping Zhang, Yahui Zhao, Zhihua Liu, Yuanguang Meng, Zhe Zhang, Zexian Zeng
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/10 - Systematic benchmarking of subcellular spatial transcriptomics across human tumors_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors uniformly processed serial sections from three human tumor types and benchmarked four high-throughput subcellular spatial transcriptomics platforms (Stereo-seq v1.3, Visium HD FFPE, CosMx 6K, Xenium 5K) against CODEX protein and matched scRNA-seq references across sensitivity, specificity, diffusion, segmentation, annotation, clustering, and transcript-protein concordance, finding platform-specific strengths and trade-offs.
orientation: This is a methods/resource benchmarking paper that primarily feeds the spatial axis and the standards/comparability claim (Claim 2, Claim 9): it argues spatial transcriptomics fills the spatial gap left by scRNA-seq, and demonstrates first-hand why prior cross-study benchmarks are incomparable for lack of unified conditions and ground truth. It also feeds Claim 10 (resolution/throughput/coverage trade-offs across platforms) and Claim 8/Claim 4 (using orthogonal protein and scRNA-seq references, with imperfect transcript-protein concordance). It can serve as support for 'spatial matters / data is siloed and incomparable' and as nuance for 'modalities are not interchangeable.'

excerpts.text: Spatially resolved transcriptomics integrates high-throughput transcriptomic profiling with spatially contextualized tissue architecture, bridging the gap in single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) by linking molecular profiles to their spatial context. With preserved spatial information, this technology offers unprecedented insights into cellular states, intercellular interactions, and tissue organization
excerpts.note: States that scRNA-seq lacks spatial context and spatial transcriptomics fills that gap by preserving spatial information.

excerpts.text: Furthermore, many benchmarking studies rely on public datasets generated under varying experimental conditions or with varying tissue types, which often lack consistent ground truth data for robust evaluation. As a result, existing efforts offer only a partial understanding of the latest advancements in ST technologies. This underscores the urgent need for a systematic benchmarking study conducted under unified experimental conditions to comprehensively evaluate the performance and comparative strengths of current high-resolution, high-throughput ST platforms.
excerpts.note: Argues prior benchmarks use varying conditions and lack consistent ground truth, motivating unified-condition benchmarking; direct support for cross-study incomparability and lack of standards.

excerpts.text: To establish comprehensive ground truth datasets for robust evaluation, we profiled proteins using CODEX on tissue sections adjacent to those used for each ST platform. In parallel, we performed scRNA-seq on matched tumor samples (Fig. 1a). The uniformly generated reference datasets enabled integrative and cross-modal comparisons across diverse platforms.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Sample preparation and multi-omics profiling
excerpts.note: Describes integrating an orthogonal protein modality (CODEX) and scRNA-seq as references, generated uniformly to enable cross-modal comparison.

excerpts.text: The resulting uniformly generated, processed, and annotated multi-omics dataset, comprising 8.13 million cells, serves as a valuable resource for advancing computational method development and enabling biological discoveries.
excerpts.note: Frames the uniformly processed multi-omics dataset as a resource for method development, an instance of building comparable standardized data.

excerpts.text: These complementary approaches underscore the strengths of ST, with sST providing unbiased transcriptome-wide coverage and iST offering high resolution and sensitivity for the detection of target genes.
excerpts.note: Frames a coverage-versus-resolution/sensitivity trade-off between sequencing-based and imaging-based spatial platforms.

excerpts.text: Compared to the ST platforms, scRNA-seq consistently detected more transcripts and genes per cell (Fig. 4c). Marker gene expression levels also remained higher in scRNA-seq (Supplementary Fig. 10b). However, when restricting the analysis to a shared gene set (2522 genes), the iST platforms demonstrated comparable transcripts and genes per cell to scRNA-seq (Fig. 4c).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Evaluation of the single-cell segmentation
excerpts.note: Quantifies a sensitivity gap: scRNA-seq detects more transcripts/genes per cell than spatial platforms, except within shared gene panels where iST is comparable.

excerpts.text: Transcript diffusion beyond tissue boundaries was observed in both Stereo-seq v1.3 and Visium HD FFPE (Fig. 2d and Supplementary Fig. 5f).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Evaluation of transcript background noise and diffusion control
excerpts.note: Reports a spatial-fidelity limitation (transcript diffusion / spatial leakage) in sequencing-based platforms, a measurement trade-off.

excerpts.text: Although CosMx 6K detected a higher total number of transcripts than Xenium 5K (Supplementary Fig. 3b), its gene-wise transcript counts showed substantial deviation from matched scRNA-seq reference (Fig. 1d).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Evaluation of molecular capture efficiency across entire gene panels
excerpts.note: Higher raw transcript count does not equal better fidelity; one platform deviates substantially from the scRNA-seq reference, illustrating cross-platform discordance.

excerpts.text: Our findings provide practical guidance for selecting ST platforms based on study objectives. For single-cell level analyses, iST platforms are preferable due to their use of multichannel staining and full-cell segmentation, which enable more accurate cell boundary delineation and reduce transcript spillover. Their targeted capture strategies also enhance the sensitivity and specificity of marker gene detection, supporting cell state inference. In contrast, for spatial analyses focused on tissue-region level patterns, sST platforms may be more suitable due to their broader gene coverage, which improves sensitivity for pathway-level enrichment analyses.
excerpts.note: Explicitly frames a no-single-winner trade-off: imaging-based platforms suit single-cell resolution while sequencing-based suit broad-coverage region-level analysis.

excerpts.text: Notably, both Xenium 5K and Visium HD FFPE exhibited higher spatial concordance with CODEX-based protein maps, underscoring their spatial accuracy.
excerpts.note: Uses an orthogonal protein modality (CODEX) as the benchmark for spatial accuracy of transcript localization, an instance of cross-modal validation.

excerpts.text: Looking ahead, each platform class faces distinct technical challenges. For iST technologies, expanding toward whole-transcriptome coverage while maintaining high specificity and detection efficiency is a critical frontier. However, optical crowding remains a limiting factor, as larger gene panels may reduce detection efficiency due to signal overlap.
excerpts.note: Concedes a hard trade-off for imaging-based platforms: optical crowding limits panel size versus detection efficiency, bounding modality breadth.

excerpts.text: The development of three-dimensional spatial transcriptomics for thick tissue sections, relaxation of sample input constraints, progress in nondestructive spatial profiling, and integration with multiomic modalities will collectively expand the scope, accessibility, and impact of ST technologies in both research and clinical settings.
excerpts.note: Names nondestructive spatial profiling and multiomic integration as future directions, implicitly conceding current destructiveness and single-modality limits.

excerpts.text: Second, CODEX was performed on adjacent tissue sections instead of the original ST sections, which provided essential reference but also introduced morphological discrepancies that may impact direct comparisons across platforms.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: cross-modal protein reference is from adjacent (not the same) sections, a structural barrier to truly paired multimodal measurement.

excerpts.text: Compared to previous benchmarking efforts that primarily relied on histologically homogeneous tissues, our study focused on tumor samples characterized by high cellular heterogeneity, complex tissue architecture, and spatially irregular gene expression, thereby offering a more stringent and biologically relevant context.
excerpts.note: Positions the study as a more rigorous benchmark than prior efforts, reinforcing that earlier comparisons were limited and standards immature.

excerpts.text: Among the ST platforms, iST technologies achieved better resolution of transcriptomic differences between cell clusters, highlighting the advantage of their higher spatial resolution (Fig. 5b).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Evaluation of cell clustering and cell type annotation
excerpts.note: Links higher spatial resolution of imaging-based platforms to better cluster resolution, a resolution-driven performance difference.

excerpts.text: Lastly, for applications involving host–microbe interactions, Stereo-seq v1.3 holds unique advantages due to its unbiased poly(A)-based capture, which allows for the detection of both human and nonhuman transcripts.
excerpts.note: Notes a capture-strategy trade-off: unbiased poly(A) capture gives broader (non-targeted) transcript scope unavailable to probe-based platforms.

figuresNumbers.contains: Uniformly generated, processed, annotated multi-omics dataset comprising 8.13 million cells across COAD, HCC, and OV tumor samples.

Four high-throughput subcellular ST platforms: Stereo-seq v1.3 (0.5 um, BGI), Visium HD FFPE (2 um, 10x, 18,085 genes), CosMx 6K (NanoString, 6175 genes), Xenium 5K (10x, 5001 genes). References: 16-plex CODEX protein and matched scRNA-seq.

Stereo-seq v1.3 resolution 0.5 um; Visium HD 2 um targeting 18,085 genes; CosMx 6K profiles 6175 genes; Xenium 5K profiles 5001 genes; analyses binned at 8 um. Earlier iST: ISS 39 genes, MERFISH 1000 genes, STARmap 1020 genes.
72,405 cells manually annotated for nuclear boundaries across five 500 x 500 um regions per dataset.
2522 genes shared between CosMx 6K and Xenium 5K; restricting to this set, iST platforms reach transcripts/genes per cell comparable to scRNA-seq.
Pearson gene-wise correlation between ST and scRNA-seq; Stereo-seq v1.3, Visium HD FFPE, Xenium 5K show high correlation, CosMx 6K deviates substantially.
CosMx 6K shows elevated negative-control signals and stronger spatial aggregation (Moran's I) of background; Xenium 5K has lower negative-control proportion after normalization.
Ratio of mean transcript count in extra-tissue vs intra-tissue bins shows substantially greater diffusion in Stereo-seq v1.3 than Visium HD FFPE (n=3 cancer types).

title: A technical comparison of spatial transcriptomics platforms across six cancer types
authors: Sergi Cervilla, Daniela Grases, Elena Perez, Francisco X. Real, Eva Musulen, Julieta Aprea, Manel Esteller, Eduard Porta-Pardo
venue: Genome Biology, 2026 (DOI prefix s13059; venue inferred, see flags)
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/11 - A technical comparison of spatial transcriptomics platforms across six cancer types_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors systematically benchmark five commercial spatial transcriptomics platforms (Visium v1, Visium v2/CytAssist, VisiumHD, Xenium, CosMx) plus a 35-protein Visium panel on matched FFPE sections from six cancer types, quantifying trade-offs in sensitivity, resolution, panel size, sampling area, and RNA-protein concordance.
orientation: This paper sits squarely on our spatial axis and on Claims 2, 9, and 10: it documents that single-cell methods destroy spatial context (supporting our spatial-loss premise), that platforms are hard to compare without same-sample benchmarking and harmonization (data-island / standards), and it surfaces explicit resolution-vs-coverage-vs-sensitivity trade-offs. Its RNA-protein decoupling finding is strong ammunition for Claim 4 (modalities are non-substitutable). It can serve as support (spatial preserves context, modalities orthogonal) and as a sober foil (even spatial transcriptomics has severe per-platform limitations, sparsity, segmentation bias, sampling bias).

excerpts.text: Yet, these methods require dissociation of the tissue, thereby destroying the spatial relationships between cells and their microenvironment. Tis information is critical the context of most diseases, including solid tumors, where spatial organization infuences cell signaling, immune infltration, and therapeutic response.
excerpts.note: States that single-cell methods require dissociation, destroying spatial relationships, and that spatial context is critical to disease biology.

excerpts.text: Spatial omics technologies bridge this gap by capturing molecular information while preserving spatial architecture [13]. Advances in spatial profling now allow measurement of DNA alterations [14], epigenetic modifcations [15], and, most commonly, RNA [16–23] and protein [24–26] expression, directly within intact tissue sections.
excerpts.note: Positions spatial omics as preserving spatial architecture while measuring RNA and protein within intact tissue.

excerpts.text: However, by averaging signals across heterogeneous cell populations, bulk profling obscures the cellular diversity and spatial context that shape tumor behavior.
excerpts.note: Notes that bulk profiling averages over heterogeneous cells and loses cellular diversity and spatial context.

excerpts.text: However, technical benchmarking across platforms remains limited, particularly in formalin-fxed, parafnembedded (FFPE) clinical samples, which represent the most common tissue format in oncology.
excerpts.note: States that cross-platform technical benchmarking is limited, motivating standardized comparison.

excerpts.text: Collectively, this work provides a harmonized dataset and technical reference for the spatial transcriptomics community, ofering insight into the relative strengths, limitations, and design considerations associated with high-throughput spatial profling of FFPE tumors.
excerpts.note: Frames the study as producing a harmonized dataset and technical reference, i.e. a step toward comparability/standardization.

excerpts.text: Uniquely, our study includes both sequencingbased and imaging-based platforms profled on the same samples, enabling direct technical comparisons across spatial capture modalities.
excerpts.note: Emphasizes that same-sample profiling across platforms is what enables direct, comparable technical comparison.

excerpts.text: Prior studies have compared in situ platforms across various tissues [41–44], but few have included matched sequencing-based platforms, and none have systematically profled Visium v1, Visium v2, and VisiumHD within the same framework.
excerpts.note: Notes a gap: prior benchmarks rarely use matched/same-framework comparison across sequencing-based platforms.

excerpts.text: Te platforms evaluated thus far require a trade-of between spatial resolution and transcriptome breadth: Visium (v1 and v2) provides whole-transcriptome coverage at multicellular resolution, whereas CosMx and Xenium ofer subcellular resolution but are limited to targeted gene panels. VisiumHD represents a new class of technology that may ofer both: single-cell or subcellular resolution with whole-transcriptome coverage.
excerpts.note: States an explicit trade-off between spatial resolution and transcriptome breadth across platform classes.

excerpts.text: However, larger panels may sufer from reduced sensitivity due to optical crowding, an efect in which high probe density compromises accurate signal resolution [35].
excerpts.anchor: Efect of panel size in Xenium results
excerpts.note: Describes optical crowding: a sensitivity-vs-coverage trade-off intrinsic to high-plex imaging panels.

excerpts.text: In summary, our comparison of the Xenium Multi-Tissue and 5 K panels revealed a clear trade-of between gene coverage and per-gene sensitivity. While the larger panel improved cell type annotation and enabled deeper immune subtyping, it exhibited lower transcript detection efciency and higher sparisity.
excerpts.anchor: Efect of panel size in Xenium results
excerpts.note: Quantitative-trade-off summary: broader gene coverage costs per-gene sensitivity and increases sparsity.

excerpts.text: While this brings the resolution to a sub-cellular level, it has extraordinary computational costs. To mitigate this, coarser bin aggregations (e.g., 8 µm or 16 µm per side) are typically used, reducing bin counts to \~ 700  K and \~ 160  K, respectively. However, this binning strategy introduces tradeofs: larger bins may include transcripts from multiple cells, while smaller bins may fragment single-cell transcriptomes.
excerpts.note: Describes VisiumHD binning trade-off: subcellular resolution costs compute and forces bins that either mix or fragment cells.

excerpts.text: Across the fve analyzed tumor samples, we observed weak global correlation between transcript and protein levels. Te average Pearson correlation coefcient across all genes and samples was approximately 0.1 (Fig.  6a), substantially lower than the \~ 0.7 average correlation observed in matched bulk tumor samples from the Clinical Proteomic Tumor Analysis Consortium (CPTAC) across seven cancer types (Fig. 6b).
excerpts.anchor: Correlation of transcriptomics and proteomics measurements across space
excerpts.note: Reports weak spatial RNA-protein correlation (~0.1) versus ~0.7 in bulk, evidence that RNA does not reconstruct protein.

excerpts.text: Finally, we integrate Visium targeted protein data with matched RNA profles, uncovering widespread RNA–protein decoupling and spatial heterogeneity in concordance.
excerpts.note: Headline finding of widespread RNA-protein decoupling and spatially heterogeneous concordance.

excerpts.text: While bulk data suggests consistent RNA–protein coupling, our spatial analysis demonstrates that these relationships are context-dependent and may vary across regions, cell types, and technologies.
excerpts.anchor: Correlation of transcriptomics and proteomics measurements across space
excerpts.note: Concludes RNA-protein relationships are context-dependent in space, contrasting bulk-aggregate coupling.

excerpts.text: Tis suggests that local microenvironmental efects, post-transcriptional regulation, and protein stability contribute to RNA–protein decoupling in situ, fndings that are masked in aggregate measurements but critical for translational insight.
excerpts.note: Attributes RNA-protein decoupling to post-transcriptional regulation and protein stability, hidden in aggregate data.

excerpts.text: In the second pattern, correlations were near zero, typically involving genes with low transcript abundance despite protein presence, such as KRT5 in the same lung sample (Fig. 6c, bottom).
excerpts.anchor: Correlation of transcriptomics and proteomics measurements across space
excerpts.note: Concrete example (KRT5) of protein present while transcript is low, illustrating non-substitutability of RNA for protein.

excerpts.text: We further incorporated targeted spatial protein measurements using the Visium FFPE multi-omic assay, enabling side-by-side RNA and protein mapping.
excerpts.note: Describes spatial multi-omic co-profiling of RNA and protein from the same tissue, an integration example.

excerpts.text: We evaluate platform performance across multiple dimensions, including transcript and UMI detection, gene–histology concordance, cell type recovery, and integration with a targeted protein panel (Visium v2, 30 proteins), enabling spatial multi-omics.
excerpts.note: States the study enables spatial multi-omics by integrating a targeted protein panel with transcriptomics; note panel size given as 30 here but 35 elsewhere (see flags).

excerpts.text: However, sampling only a single FOV yielded highly variable estimates. For example, the inferred proportion of cancer cells ranged from 5 to 55%, whereas the true value for the entire sample was 22% (Fig. 3e, Additional fle 2: Fig. S14).
excerpts.anchor: Biological concordance and interpretability across in situ platforms
excerpts.note: Quantifies sampling bias: a single small region gives cancer-cell fraction 5-55% vs true 22%, showing measured cell composition depends heavily on scanned area.

excerpts.text: Tese results illustrate how spatial constraints, even when total cell numbers are matched, can introduce substantial variability in cell type composition estimates, highlighting the importance of scanned area in the design and interpretation of spatial transcriptomics experiments.
excerpts.anchor: Biological concordance and interpretability across in situ platforms
excerpts.note: Concludes that scanned-area / sampling design materially changes spatial results, a comparability/standards concern.

excerpts.text: When comparing the proportion of negative control signals relative to gene signals, normalized by the panel size, we found that CosMx exhibited a negative probe signal rate of approximately 10.31%, while Xenium demonstrated a markedly lower rate of 0.06% (Additional fle  3: Table  S2).
excerpts.anchor: Technical comparison of imaging-based platforms: CosMx vs Xenium
excerpts.note: Quantifies large cross-platform background-noise difference (CosMx ~10.31% vs Xenium 0.06% negative-probe rate), evidence of platform incomparability without harmonization.

excerpts.text: In matched FFPE samples, Visium v2 consistently detected more unique genes (\~ 2,000 vs. \~ 200; Fig. 1c), captured more UMIs per spot, and exhibited stronger spatial autocorrelation (Moran’s I), particularly for lowly expressed genes (Additional fle 2: Fig. S1).
excerpts.anchor: Generation of matching spatial transcriptomics and proteomics data
excerpts.note: Within-platform upgrade (Visium v1 to v2) changes detected genes ~200 to ~2,000, showing even chemistry versions are not directly comparable.

excerpts.text: Tese observations highlight the potential for sampling bias when spatial studies rely on small tissue regions.
excerpts.anchor: Biological concordance and interpretability across in situ platforms
excerpts.note: Flags sampling bias from small tissue regions as a reproducibility/comparability hazard.

excerpts.text: Importantly, neither currently ofers full transcriptome coverage but instead rely on targeted panels.
excerpts.anchor: Technical comparison of imaging-based platforms: CosMx vs Xenium
excerpts.note: States imaging-based subcellular platforms (CosMx, Xenium) lack whole-transcriptome coverage and use targeted panels.

excerpts.text: First, spatial transcriptomics is a fast-moving feld, and our data refect the capabilities of each platform as of mid-2025. For example, CosMx now ofers a whole-transcriptome panel, which was not available at the time of data generation.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: platform capabilities are a moving target, so benchmarks are time-stamped and not durable references.

excerpts.text: Second, while we included several major platforms, our study does not cover other important technologies such as MERFISH, Slide-seq, or Stereo-seq.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded scope limitation: several major spatial platforms were not benchmarked.

excerpts.text: Tird, although we processed serial sections from the same FFPE blocks when possible, some comparisons (particularly those involving newer chemistries like Xenium Prime) used tissue sections generated at later time points, potentially introducing Z-axis variability.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded confounder: serial sections at different times introduce Z-axis variability undermining strict same-sample comparison.

excerpts.text: Despite its potential, VisiumHD presented challenges, including lower UMI density, incomplete library saturation, and spatial artifacts related to its gridded capture design.
excerpts.note: Concedes that the whole-transcriptome subcellular platform (VisiumHD) trades in sparsity, saturation and grid artifacts.

excerpts.text: A third, more complex pattern involved partial concordance, where RNA and protein signals overlapped in some regions but diverged in others.
excerpts.anchor: Correlation of transcriptomics and proteomics measurements across space
excerpts.note: Notes a partial-concordance pattern where RNA and protein agree in some regions and diverge in others (spatially heterogeneous coupling).

excerpts.text: To explore this discrepancy, we performed cross-platform validation using Xenium data from the same tissue block, registering the Xenium and Visium v2 images and generating pseudo-spots for direct comparison. Te RNA expression of CD68 inferred from Xenium largely matched the protein spatial pattern, including the extended signal in the lower-right region (Fig.  6e), suggesting that the divergence may result from platformspecifc technical variability rather than true biological dissociation.
excerpts.anchor: Correlation of transcriptomics and proteomics measurements across space
excerpts.note: Counter-nuance: some RNA-protein divergence is attributed to platform-specific technical variability rather than true biology, showing cross-platform incomparability can masquerade as decoupling.

figuresNumbers.contains: Visium v1 55 um / 17,943 genes; Visium v2 CytAssist 55 um / 18,085 genes + 35 proteins; VisiumHD 2 um / 18,085 genes; Xenium Multi-Tissue single-cell / 377 genes; Xenium Prime single-cell / 5000 genes; CosMx single-cell / 1000 genes.
18,672 spots (Visium v1); 33,117 spots (Visium v2); 2,551,540 8x8 um bins (VisiumHD); 667,916 cells (CosMx); 5,809,890 cells (Xenium).

figuresNumbers.item: Fig. 1c (Visium v1 vs v2 gene detection)
figuresNumbers.contains: Visium v2 ~2,000 unique genes vs Visium v1 ~200 in matched FFPE samples.

figuresNumbers.contains: Negative probe rate CosMx ~10.31% vs Xenium 0.06%; signal-to-noise ~3x (CosMx) vs ~450x (Xenium).
UMI-density-per-gene correlation Xenium Pearson R=0.93 vs CosMx R=0.86 across all tumor types.
Full panels: CosMx detected 2-3x more genes/transcripts per cell than Xenium (larger panel); for 125 shared genes Xenium gave higher transcript counts per cell.
Xenium detected 5%-30% more cells than CosMx; Xenium-segmented cells larger than CosMx across all tumors except lymphoma; outliers >1,000 um2 from nucleus expansion.

~13-fold panel-size difference; for 225 shared genes XeniumMT detected more; both R=0.92-0.93 vs Visium; Xenium5K ~threefold fewer transcripts per gene; MYC-positive cancer cells 70% (XeniumMT) vs 30% (Xenium5K).
Fibroblasts misclassified as cancer 71.6% (CosMx) vs 10.2% (Xenium) in stroma; endothelial cells 8.51% (CosMx) vs 28.7% (Xenium); ovarian misclassified epithelial 12% (XeniumMT) vs <1% (Xenium5K).
Single-FOV inferred cancer-cell proportion ranged 5-55% vs true 22% for whole sample.
At 2 um most bins one cell; 8 um bins 2-4 cells; 16 um bins 5-10 cells; bins per cell ~10-25 at 2 um vs ~1-5 at 16 um; ~11 million bins at native 2 um, ~700K at 8 um, ~160K at 16 um.
VisiumHD vs Visium v2 R>0.97 and vs Xenium R>0.85; UMI density ~3x lower than Visium v2 and ~15x lower than Xenium.
Average spatial Pearson RNA-protein correlation ~0.1 across all genes/samples vs ~0.7 in bulk CPTAC across seven cancer types (35-protein Visium v2 IO panel).

title: A comprehensive review of spatial transcriptomics data alignment and integration
authors: Muiz Khan, Suzan Arslanturk, Sorin Draghici
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/12 - A comprehensive review of spatial transcriptomics data alignment and integration_cleaned.md
oneSentence: A methods review of 24 computational tools for aligning and integrating multiple spatial transcriptomics (ST) tissue slices, categorizing them into statistical-mapping, image-registration, and graph-based groups, and proposing a generalized pipeline while cataloguing the persistent challenges of heterogeneity, batch effects, 3D reconstruction, and cross-platform comparability.
orientation: Primary feed for our spatial axis and Claim 2 (scRNA-seq loses spatial information): it explicitly states that single-cell omics requires cell isolation that disrupts spatial context, motivating the entire ST field. It also feeds Claim 9 (data islands / cross-paper non-comparability / lack of standards) via its repeated emphasis on batch effects, protocol/technology variability, and the closing call for standardization and benchmark datasets. It touches the temporal axis (Claim 3) where ST slices at different time points are used as a proxy for development, and the multi-modal/integration axis (Claims 6/8) where it names spatial proteomics and metabolomics as a future direction. Largely supportive of our spatial/standards arguments; can also serve as a foil showing the field's heavy RNA-only, single-modality focus.

excerpts.text: In contrast, single-cell “omics” methods provide higher resolution and capture rare cell areas, but they require cell isolation, which also disrupts spatial context information.
excerpts.note: States that single-cell omics requires cell isolation that disrupts spatial context, the motivating loss our thesis names.

excerpts.text: Spatial transcriptomics (ST) technologies have significantly advanced our capacity to quantify gene expression within tissue sections while preserving crucial spatial context information.
excerpts.note: Positions ST as the technology class that preserves spatial context, supporting the spatial-resolution requirement of our standard.

excerpts.text: However, it is important to note that a single 2D coordinate space only represents a single slice of the tissue section, limiting the comprehensive analysis of the entire tissue context.
excerpts.note: Notes that 2D single-slice ST still misses the full tissue context, nuancing how much spatial information current ST recovers.

excerpts.text: On the other hand, the 2D nature of the sliced tissue sections leads to the loss of z-axis information, making it challenging to accurately reconstruct the 3D structure of the tissue.
excerpts.note: Identifies loss of z-axis information from 2D slicing as a residual spatial gap even within ST.

excerpts.text: Although this is a significant improvement over bulk RNA expressions while capturing the spatial information, the gene expression resolution and scale are lower than in scRNA expression.
excerpts.note: States a resolution/throughput trade-off: ST captures spatial info but at lower gene-expression resolution than scRNA, a measurement-side tradeoff.

excerpts.text: This task is nontrivial as it requires the consideration of spatial relationships and entails inherent challenges such as biological and technical variability, spatial warping, and differences in experimental protocols across ST platforms.
excerpts.note: Lists technical variability and protocol differences across platforms as core integration obstacles.

excerpts.text: Tools focusing solely on single-slice ST analysis or multi-omics data integration tools, such as those integrating ST data with scRNAseq data, were excluded from this review.

touchpoints.otherLabel: Review scope is single-modality (RNA-only ST); multi-omics integration explicitly excluded

excerpts.note: Defines the review's scope as ST-only, explicitly excluding multi-omics integration, illustrating the field's RNA-centric framing.

excerpts.text: Furthermore, as ST data can be collected at different time-points or stages, it allows for the observation and analysis of tissue development or growth, providing insights into cell progression, disease dynamics, and the impact of marker genes [10, 13, 16–19].
excerpts.note: Frames time as cross-sectional ST slices collected at different stages, not paired tracking of the same tissue over time.

excerpts.text: This includes aligning nonconsecutive slices from different locations or time-points, where spatial regions may partially or fully overlap. For example, aligning heart tissue slices taken at different stages of development, such as from early embryonic stages to mature adult tissue, requires handling variations in structure, cell composition, and spatial organization as the heart grows and changes over time, but these slices still exhibit overlap or share similar spatial regions [10].
excerpts.note: Describes temporal analysis as alignment of distinct slices sampled at different developmental stages rather than longitudinal observation of one sample.

excerpts.text: ST datasets also exhibit variability, noise, and uncertainty, which can arise from various factors such as experimental conditions, tissue preparation, and sequencing artifacts [16].
excerpts.note: Attributes ST data variability and noise to experimental conditions, tissue prep, and sequencing artifacts.

excerpts.text: Additionally, batch effect removal is needed to ensure the accuracy of the alignment and integration process [10, 11].
excerpts.note: States that batch-effect removal is required for cross-dataset alignment accuracy.

excerpts.text: For instance, adjacent tissue slices often exhibit better overlap than tissue slices that are farther apart [7]. Third, across dataset alignment is likely to involve data collected at different time points, using different protocols and technical configurations. There are essential factors when dealing with batch effects.
excerpts.note: Identifies different protocols, time points and technical configurations across datasets as the source of batch effects to be corrected.

excerpts.text: However, heterogeneity, technical variability, and biological differences arising from developmental stages or disease progression often lead to misalignment in soft tissues. Additionally, scalability poses a major hurdle, as existing methods struggle with large datasets and lack efficient strategies for accurate volumetric reconstruction.
excerpts.note: Concedes that heterogeneity and technical variability still cause misalignment and that scalable, accurate 3D/volumetric reconstruction remains unsolved.

excerpts.text: While progress has been made in aligning and integrating heterogeneous tissue slices across datasets, challenges remain in achieving automatic alignment that is independent of dataset origins, experimental conditions, or technical configurations, relying solely on tissue context.
excerpts.note: Concedes that alignment independent of dataset origin/protocol/configuration is not yet achieved, underscoring the comparability gap.

excerpts.text: Integrating multi-omics spatial data, including spatial proteomics and metabolomics, alongside ST could provide more insightful biological context for understanding complex molecular interactions.
excerpts.note: Names spatial proteomics and metabolomics as needed complements to ST for richer biological context, a future direction beyond RNA.

excerpts.text: Another key direction is improving cross-species ST alignment and integration techniques, allowing the comparison of gene expression patterns across model organisms and human tissues for better translational research.

touchpoints.otherLabel: Cross-species ST alignment as a future direction for comparability

excerpts.note: Proposes cross-species alignment as a future direction, a comparability angle beyond the roadmap's within-modality scope.

excerpts.text: Moreover, ensuring standardization and reproducibility in ST data alignment through benchmark datasets and evaluation frameworks is crucial for advancing the field.
excerpts.note: Calls standardization, reproducibility, benchmark datasets, and evaluation frameworks crucial, directly echoing our no-standards argument.

excerpts.text: Moving forward, an ideal solution should be a versatile tool capable of aligning and integrating tissue slices with shared gene expression patterns, regardless of data collection techniques, datasets, or tissue regions.
excerpts.note: States the goal of technique/dataset-agnostic integration, implying current data are not comparable across collection methods.

excerpts.text: This 3D reconstruction preserves the spatial relationships between tissue structures across slices, offering insights into cellular organization, interactions, and spatial gradients of gene expression that cannot be captured in isolated 2D slices.
excerpts.note: Argues spatial organization, cell-cell interactions, and expression gradients require multi-slice 3D context unavailable in isolated 2D slices.

excerpts.text: For instance, some technologies require 8–10 μm tissue thickness, which may lead to challenges such as cells overlapping within sections. This overlap can obscure spatial relationships, necessitating the use of bioinformatics tools designed to deconvolute mixed signals and infer underlying cellular compositions. To the best of our knowledge, no computational tool currently exists to specifically address this challenge.
excerpts.note: Concedes a resolution limit: tissue-thickness-induced cell overlap obscures spatial relationships, with no tool yet addressing it.

excerpts.text: 10 Visium [30] technology achieves a spatial resolution of 55 μm, with each capture site (spot) containing <sub>∼</sub>1–10 cells. Slide-seq [40] enhances the spatial resolution to nearly cellular level (10 μm), while Stereo-seq [43] achieves subcellular resolution (0.22 μm) [10].
excerpts.note: Gives concrete per-platform spatial resolutions spanning multicellular to subcellular, quantifying the resolution range across ST technologies.

excerpts.text: Advanced ST technologies such as Visium from 10 Genomics allows expression measurement of up to 5000 spots per slice, with each spot in the 2D space capturing between 1 and 30 cells [7].
excerpts.note: Quantifies Visium spot count and per-spot cell mixing, showing the spot-level (non-single-cell) granularity of a widely used ST platform.

excerpts.text: However, the distribution-based approaches require prior assumptions to ensure correctness and incorrect assumptions can lead to inaccurate mappings. This is also related to hyperparameter selection, as the results can be sensitive to changes in these hyperparameters.
excerpts.note: Concedes statistical-mapping tools are sensitive to prior assumptions and hyperparameters, a robustness caveat for these computational methods.

excerpts.text: While this review serves as a detailed guide and highlights the potential for robust ST alignment and integration solutions, it remains theoretical in nature and does not provide a proof of concept.

touchpoints.otherLabel: Self-conceded limitation: review is theoretical, no proof of concept / benchmark

excerpts.note: Authors concede the review is theoretical with no empirical benchmark of the surveyed tools.

figuresNumbers.contains: Catalog of 24 ST alignment/integration tools across three categories: Statistical mapping (10 tools: Splotch, GPSA, Eggplant, PRECAST, PASTE, PASTE2, OTVI, DeST-OT, ST-GEARS, GraphST), Image processing & registration (4 tools: STIM, STaCker, STalign, STUtility), Graph-based (10 tools: SpatiAlign, STAligner, Graspot, ATAT, MaskGraphene, STAIR, SLAT, SPIRAL, BiGATAE, SPACEL), with datasets, downstream analyses, evaluation measures, and homogeneous/heterogeneous scope.

10x Genomics / Visium 55 μm; Slide-seq 10 μm; Slide-seqV2 10 μm; MERFISH 0.1 μm; SeqFISH+ 0.6 μm; Stereo-seq 0.5 μm; GeoMX DSP varies; Xenium 0.2 μm; Visium HD 2 μm; HDST 2 μm; STARmap 2 μm. Input materials range across Fresh Frozen, Fixed Cells, FFPE; species Human/Mouse.
Up to 5000 spots per slice; each spot captures between 1 and 30 cells (1–10 cells per capture site cited elsewhere).
10x Visium ~55 μm (~1–10 cells/spot); Slide-seq ~10 μm (near-cellular); Stereo-seq ~0.22 μm (subcellular).

primarySources.topic: PASTE — original alignment and integration of ST data via fused Gromov-Wasserstein optimal transport (foundational tool the review repeatedly anchors to)
primarySources.citation: Zeira R, Land M, Strzalkowski A et al. Alignment and integration of spatial transcriptomics data. Nat Methods 2022;19:567-75

primarySources.topic: STAligner — integrating ST across conditions, technologies, and developmental stages (autoencoder + triplet loss; the review's top recommendation)
primarySources.citation: Zhou X, Dong K, Zhang S. Integrating spatial transcriptomics data across different conditions, technologies and developmental stages. Nat Comput Sci 2023;3:894-906

primarySources.topic: STalign — diffeomorphic-metric-mapping image registration for ST alignment, used for cross-resolution MERFISH/Visium alignment
primarySources.citation: Clifton K, Anant M, Aihara G et al. STalign: alignment of spatial transcriptomics data using diffeomorphic metric mapping. Nat Commun 2023;14:8123

primarySources.topic: SLAT — graph-based heterogeneous-slice alignment, cross-scale Visium/Xenium and seqFISH/Stereo-seq alignment
primarySources.citation: Xia CR, Cao ZJ, Tu XM et al. Spatial-linked alignment tool (SLAT) for aligning heterogenous slices. Nat Commun 2023;14:7236

primarySources.topic: GPSA — alignment of spatial genomics data using deep Gaussian processes (models spatial warping; used on breast-cancer tumor integration)
primarySources.citation: Jones A, Townes FW, Li D et al. Alignment of spatial genomics data using deep Gaussian processes. Nat Methods 2023;20:1379-87

primarySources.topic: Original spatial transcriptomics method (Ståhl) — the foundational Visium-precursor dataset reused across surveyed tools
primarySources.citation: Stahl PL, Salmén F, Vickovic S et al. Visualization and analysis of gene expression in tissue sections by spatial transcriptomics. Science 2016;353:78-82

primarySources.topic: Prior benchmarking review of ST clustering/alignment/integration the authors position against
primarySources.citation: Hu Y, Xie M, Li Y et al. Benchmarking clustering, alignment, and integration methods for spatial transcriptomics. Genome Biol 2024;25:212

flags: Metadata unverified: the cleaned MD contains no explicit venue, year, or DOI for THIS paper. Journal is likely Briefings in Bioinformatics (inferred from article style and the cluster of 'Brief Bioinform 2024' self/peer citations, e.g. ref 48), but this is NOT stated in the body; venue/year set to 'unverified; see flags' and doi to null.

Author initials: Sorin Draghici is corresponding author (Email: sorin@advaitabio.com); affiliations are Wayne State University (Dept. of Computer Science, Detroit) and Advaita Bioinformatics (Ann Arbor, Michigan).

Scope note: This is a methods review of ST (RNA-only) alignment/integration tools; it explicitly EXCLUDES multi-omics integration (e.g. ST+scRNA) and single-slice tools (Review selection criteria). Its value to our multi-modal claims (6/8) is mostly via the Conclusion's forward-looking call for spatial proteomics/metabolomics, not via reported multi-omics results.
OCR quirks kept verbatim: '10 Genomics'/'10 Visium' (should read '10x Genomics'/'10x Visium'); '<sub>∼</sub>1–10 cells'; 'spatiAlign'/'PRE-CAST'; '0.22 μm' subcellular resolution.

Read at source: ref #7 (PASTE, Nat Methods 2022) and ref #10 (STAligner, Nat Comput Sci 2023) are the most load-bearing primaries and worth citing first-hand; ref #30 (Ståhl 2016, Science) is the canonical original-ST primary.

title: Challenges and Opportunities in the Clinical Translation of High-Resolution Spatial Transcriptomics
authors: Tancredi Massimo Pentimalli, Nikos Karaiskos, Nikolaus Rajewsky
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/13 - Challenges and Opportunities in Clinical Translation of High-Resolution Spatial Transcriptomics_cleaned.md
oneSentence: A review of high-resolution (subcellular) spatial transcriptomics technologies, surveying how they work, their preclinical applications in disease-mechanism discovery and personalized medicine, key clinical study-design considerations, and the remaining challenges (method, data-interpretation, and implementation) for clinical translation.
orientation: Mainly feeds the spatial axis and Claim 2 (dissociation/scRNA-seq loses spatial information) as strong support, and Claim 7 (spatial neighborhood/microenvironment shapes cell state and therapeutic response). It also supplies counter/conceded material for Claim 9 (lack of standards/best practices, cross-study incomparability), Claim 10 (measurement trade-offs across resolution/coverage/preservation), Claim 8 (multimodal integration of orthogonal layers), and Claim 3 (its own admission that ST gives only a single snapshot, conceding the destructive/temporal limitation). The paper advocates ST as the solution, so on the temporal axis it can serve as a named foil since it does not solve time-course observation.

excerpts.text: However, the spatial organization of cells in complex tissues is central to understanding how molecular phenotypes emerge, are regulated, and affect tissue function (13). While computational tools for the spatial reconstruction of single-cell data with little or no prior knowledge exist (14–16), they rely on the assumption that transcriptomic profiles in neighboring cells are more similar than those that are farther apart, which does not always hold true when tissue organization is disrupted by diseases.
excerpts.note: States spatial organization is central to molecular phenotype, and that computational spatial reconstruction of dissociated single-cell data rests on an assumption that breaks down in diseased tissue.

excerpts.text: tissue dissociation inevitably leads to the loss of spatial information.
excerpts.note: Direct statement that tissue dissociation destroys spatial information.

excerpts.text: Finally, local interactions in cellular neighborhoods drive coordinated cellular processes, and the loss of spatial positions following tissue dissociation prevents their mapping, limiting our mechanistic understanding of disease processes.
excerpts.note: Argues that loss of spatial position upon dissociation prevents mapping local neighborhood interactions that drive coordinated cellular processes.

excerpts.text: Despite the power of single-cell technologies, the need for tissue dissociation and the lack of standardized protocols for isolating living cells have so far limited their clinical translation on a broader scale (8).
excerpts.note: Identifies tissue dissociation requirement and absence of standardized cell-isolation protocols as limits on single-cell clinical translation.

excerpts.text: Furthermore, tissue dissociation can alter both cellular (e.g., depletion of specific cell types) and molecular readouts [e.g., activation of stress responses (9)], hindering the development of robust and reproducible protocols needed for clinical applications.
excerpts.note: States dissociation itself perturbs both which cells are recovered and their molecular state (stress responses), a known dissociation artifact.

excerpts.text: However, current applications mostly rely on bulk readouts, which average gene expression across millions of cells from different cell types and are unable to distinguish clinically relevant disease heterogeneity.
excerpts.note: Notes that bulk NGS averages over millions of heterogeneous cells and cannot resolve clinically relevant heterogeneity.

excerpts.text: In particular, high-resolution spatial transcriptomics (ST) technologies, which profile billions of individual transcripts at subcellular resolution (18), enable the systematic mapping of cell–cell interactions in clinical samples and hold great potential to advance biomedical research and patient care (19).
excerpts.note: Defines high-resolution ST as profiling billions of transcripts at subcellular resolution and mapping cell-cell interactions in clinical samples.

excerpts.text: While spatial analyses are possible in individual 2D sections, cells live and communicate in 3D. Therefore, reconstructing tissue three dimensionally can improve our understanding of disease mechanisms.
excerpts.anchor: From Single Cells to Virtual Tissue Blocks
excerpts.note: Points out that single 2D sections under-capture biology because cells communicate in 3D, motivating 3D reconstruction.

excerpts.text: While a discussion of specific computational tools is beyond the scope of this review, we would like to point out how best practices still have to be established at this stage. For example, more than 100 different tools exist to map cell–cell interactions (43), and the field still has to converge on standardized approaches, which are urgently needed for clinical applications of ST methods.
excerpts.anchor: From Single Cells to Virtual Tissue Blocks
excerpts.note: Concedes best practices are not yet established and that over 100 cell-cell interaction tools exist without convergence on a standard.

excerpts.text: Although infrastructure (e.g., scalable file formats and structures) and ST-specific databases exist [e.g., STOmicsDB (42)], a unified ecosystem and a set of best practices are still lacking.
excerpts.note: States that a unified ecosystem and best practices for ST data are still lacking despite existing databases.

excerpts.text: Similarly, spatial molecular atlases will be required to standardize the analysis and guide the interpretation of novel ST data, which are central to advancing our understanding of health and disease.
excerpts.anchor: Building Cellular and Molecular Atlases of Complex Tissues
excerpts.note: Argues spatial molecular atlases are needed as shared references to standardize analysis and interpretation of ST data.

excerpts.text: Cellular networks also shape the tumor microenvironment (TME), determining the balance between tumor growth and regression, immune recognition and escape, and tissue invasion or confinement (70).
excerpts.anchor: Unveiling Coordinated Cellular Responses Driving Disease Processes
excerpts.note: States cellular networks in the tumor microenvironment determine growth/regression, immune escape, and invasion outcomes.

excerpts.text: Recently, Cadinu and colleagues (69) reconstructed the remodeling of cellular neighborhoods during injury, inflammation, and repair in a mouse colitis model and revealed how inflammation-associated fibroblasts orchestrate tissue remodeling through receptor–ligand interactions with neighboring epithelial, stromal, and immune cells.
excerpts.anchor: Unveiling Coordinated Cellular Responses Driving Disease Processes
excerpts.note: Example that spatial neighborhood remodeling and fibroblast receptor-ligand interactions with neighbors orchestrate tissue repair.

excerpts.text: While these methods have proven powerful to identify the spatial architecture and heterogeneity of complex tissues, they were limited in either resolution or gene coverage and often required combination with scRNA-seq data (20, 21).
excerpts.anchor: Unveiling Coordinated Cellular Responses Driving Disease Processes
excerpts.note: Concedes earlier spatial-proteomics/low-resolution methods traded off resolution against gene coverage and often needed scRNA-seq to fill gaps.

excerpts.text: Magen and colleagues (87) compared a subset of HCC patients with high intratumoral immune infiltration enrolled in a neoadjuvant anti-PD-1 trial showing variable responses to immunotherapy. Using a combination of MERFISH with scRNA-seq and T cell receptor (TCR) sequencing, they suggest that the local differentiation of $\mathrm { P D - 1 ^ { + } \ T C F { - } 1 ^ { + } \ C D 8 ^ { + } \ T }$ cells occurred only in responders due to their interaction with $\mathrm { C X C L 1 3 ^ { + } ~ T }$ helper cells and dendritic cells in cellular triads.
excerpts.anchor: Leveraging Multicellular Niches as Personalized Biomarkers
excerpts.note: Example that spatially localized multicellular triads (T cell differentiation via local interactions) distinguish immunotherapy responders from non-responders.

excerpts.text: PD-L1 (programmed cell death ligand 1) positivity is not sufficient to predict patient responses to anti-PD-1 agents (86). Anti-tumoral immunity is a highly coordinated response across different components of the TME, which may be only partially captured by the detection of one immune checkpoint molecule.
excerpts.anchor: Leveraging Multicellular Niches as Personalized Biomarkers
excerpts.note: Argues a single-molecule biomarker (PD-L1) is insufficient because response depends on coordinated multicellular TME context.

excerpts.text: a holistic understanding of disease processes would benefit from the integrative analysis of cellular genomic, epigenomic, transcriptomic, proteomic, and metabolic layers.
excerpts.anchor: Beyond Gene Expression: Multimodal Spatial Omics
excerpts.note: States a holistic understanding requires integrating genomic, epigenomic, transcriptomic, proteomic, and metabolic layers.

excerpts.text: Rather than analyzing adjacent cells in consecutive sections, true multimodal profiling of the same cells would be desirable to directly relate features across modalities.
excerpts.anchor: Beyond Gene Expression: Multimodal Spatial Omics
excerpts.note: Concedes that true same-cell multimodal profiling (not adjacent-section registration) is needed to directly relate features across modalities.

excerpts.text: Similarly, a combination of ST with mass spectrometry–based proteomics and metabolomics (MALDI-MSI) has been applied to correlate neurotransmitter levels with gene expression in Parkinson’s disease (130) and to study tumor metabolic heterogeneity and immunometabolic rewiring at the invasion margin in gastric cancer (131).
excerpts.anchor: Beyond Gene Expression: Multimodal Spatial Omics
excerpts.note: Example of integrating ST with spatial proteomics and metabolomics to relate metabolite/protein layers to gene expression in tissue.

excerpts.text: However, the bulk nature of these gene expression measurements, which average across millions of cells in the TME, limited the prediction accuracy of these HE2RNA models. Furthermore, while advances in explainable artificial intelligence (AI) can point to which image regions are relevant for gene expression predictions (e.g., by generating explanatory heat maps) (142), the spatial agnostic nature of bulk RNA-seq limited both the interpretability and the validation of such tools, both of which are central to their clinical application and the advancement of scientific knowledge.
excerpts.note: Argues bulk, spatially agnostic RNA-seq labels limit accuracy, interpretability, and validation of deep-learning gene-expression-from-histology models.

excerpts.text: First, sample preservation strategy imposes a trade-off between sample availability and depth of the molecular readouts: Formalin fixation is well established in routine workflows but inevitably results in RNA fragmentation, while snap-freezing allows the study of intact RNA molecules but requires the implementation of novel sample procedures and prevents the study of archived samples.
excerpts.anchor: Challenges Related to the Methods Themselves
excerpts.note: States a preservation trade-off: FFPE (available but fragmented RNA) versus fresh-frozen (intact RNA but new procedures, no archives).

excerpts.text: Second, there is currently a trade-off between the number of genes studied and their detection efficiency. On the one hand, imaging-based approaches feature higher sensitivity but are limited to predefined targeted genes, and detection efficiency decreases with increasing panel size. On the other hand, sequencing-based methods can—in principle—capture the whole polyadenylated transcriptome; however, they are biased toward the detection of highly expressed genes.
excerpts.anchor: Challenges Related to the Methods Themselves
excerpts.note: States a coverage-versus-sensitivity trade-off between targeted imaging-based (sensitive, limited genes) and unbiased sequencing-based (broad but biased to high expressers) ST.

excerpts.text: Furthermore, scalability represents another limit to the implementation of current methods in diagnostic routines. On the one hand, imaging-based methods are largely automated, but they are limited to two to four slides per run and feature long acquisition times, especially when large tissue areas or numerous targets are imaged simultaneously. On the other hand, sequencingbased approaches can process several samples in parallel, but automated workflows are not yet commercially available.
excerpts.anchor: Challenges Related to the Methods Themselves
excerpts.note: Describes a throughput/scalability limit: imaging-based ST is slow and capped at a few slides per run; sequencing-based ST lacks commercial automation.

excerpts.text: Finally, best practices for accurate cell segmentation, data normalization, clustering, and downstream computational data analyses are still areas of active research.
excerpts.anchor: Challenges Related to the Methods Themselves
excerpts.note: Concedes that best practices for segmentation, normalization, clustering, and downstream analysis are not yet standardized.

excerpts.text: Apart from the existence of a healthy tissue reference, extracting actionable insights from ST data will require the existence of a large pool of similarly processed datasets for the tissue and disease under study.
excerpts.anchor: Challenges Related to Result Interpretation and Actionability
excerpts.note: States actionable interpretation requires large pools of similarly (i.e., comparably) processed datasets, implying current data are not uniformly processed.

excerpts.text: This will require standardized operating procedures controlling all steps from tissue extraction inside the operating room to the generation of the ST datasets and subsequent evaluation of the spatial biomarkers to ensure robust and reproducible readouts for clinical decision-making.
excerpts.note: Calls for standardized operating procedures across the full pipeline to ensure robust, reproducible ST readouts.

excerpts.text: Even if the current challenges are overcome, ST methods will still be limited to a single snapshot, highlighting the continued need for experimental models to link clinical trials with perturbation studies to move from hypotheses generated by these high-dimensional assays to mechanistic insights.
excerpts.note: Concedes ST is limited to a single (static) snapshot and cannot itself provide temporal/perturbation trajectories; the paper's own method does not solve the time axis.

excerpts.text: At the same time, international efforts should also be directed toward the benchmarking of different sample collection and preservation protocols, the balancing of molecular readouts with costs, and the identification of personnel and infrastructure required for different experimental methods. Furthermore, the standardization of data analysis and reporting pipelines leveraging publicly available tools and large-scale atlases of healthy and disease samples will be central to the generation of robust clinical reports.
excerpts.note: Calls for international benchmarking of protocols and standardization of analysis/reporting pipelines as central to robust ST clinical use.

excerpts.text: Ultimately, the integration of ST and proteomic readouts with emerging spatial genomics, epigenomics, and metabolomics technologies will provide a holistic representation of complex tissues in 2D and 3D (151).
excerpts.note: States integrating ST/proteomic with spatial genomics, epigenomics, and metabolomics is needed for a holistic multimodal tissue representation.

excerpts.text: Recently, the availability of extensive digital imaging and transcriptomic datasets enabled the self-supervised training of large-scale AI models, such as Virchow (152) and RudolfV (153), which were trained on billions of histological images, and scGPT, which was trained on more than 33 million single-cell profiles. Fine-tuning these foundation models for several downstream applications can then improve the robustness and generalizability of deep learning predictions when limited training data are available.
excerpts.note: Notes foundation models (Virchow, RudolfV, scGPT trained on 33M+ single-cell profiles) and that fine-tuning improves robustness/generalizability when training data are limited.

excerpts.text: While single-cell technologies reveal the extent of cell types and states across different brain regions (47, 48), they cannot accurately map the spatial organization of neuronal and nonneuronal cells into layers and circuits (49).
excerpts.anchor: Building Cellular and Molecular Atlases of Complex Tissues
excerpts.note: States that single-cell technologies cannot accurately map spatial organization of cells into tissue layers and circuits.

figuresNumbers.contains: Spatial resolution by platform: CosMx single molecule (FFPE); Curio Seeker 10 µm (FF); MERSCOPE 500 nm (FF and FFPE); Molecular Cartography single molecule (FF and FFPE); STOmics 0.5 µm (FF); Visium HD 2 µm (FFPE, ~18,000 genes probe-based); Xenium In-Situ single molecule (FF and FFPE).
DBiT-seq 10/20/50 µm (FF and FFPE); HDST 2 µm (FF); Open-ST 0.6 µm (FF); SeqFISH single molecule (FF); Seq-scope 0.6 µm (FF); STARmap 1 µm (FF).
Spot size varies from 0.6 µm in Open-ST to 10 µm in Curio Seeker.
Limited to two to four slides per run with long acquisition times.
Roughly 37 trillion cells compose the human body.
Trained on more than 33 million single-cell profiles.
5-µm-thick (and even thinner) sections, mostly spanning part of a single cell along the z-axis.
Coexpressed genes upregulated within 100 µm from amyloid plaques.
