Scope note: This is a review/perspective on high-resolution spatial transcriptomics for clinical pathology; many disease-application examples (atlas-building, individual tumor case studies) are off-roadmap filler and were not captured except where they bear on neighborhood/microenvironment (Claim 7) or measurement trade-offs (Claim 10).
OCR quirk preserved in e15: LaTeX-style math markup ($\mathrm{...}$) appears in the source for cell-marker phenotypes (e.g., PD-1+ TCF-1+ CD8+ T); kept verbatim.

Roadmap routing note: claimId values 'A · modality', 'B · temporal', 'C · spatial', 'Standard' correspond to the context file's four organizing axes (modality / temporal / spatial / cross-cutting standard-and-integration).

On temporal axis (Claim 3 / axis B): the paper SUPPORTS the premise that current methods give only a static snapshot (e27), but it does NOT itself solve pairwise time-course observation — usable as a named foil for the temporal gap.

title: Deep Profiling of Mouse Splenic Architecture with CODEX Multiplexed Imaging
authors: Yury Goltsev, Nikolay Samusik, Julia Kennedy-Darling, Salil Bhate, Matthew Hale, Gustavo Vazquez, Sarah Black, Garry P. Nolan
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/14 - Deep Profiling of Mouse Splenic Architecture with CODEX Multiplexed Imaging_cleaned.md
oneSentence: Introduces CODEX, a DNA-barcode/primer-extension highly multiplexed in situ imaging method that quantifies ~30 protein markers at single-cell resolution in intact tissue sections, and applies it to normal and lupus (MRL/lpr) mouse spleens to map cell types, cellular neighborhoods (i-niches), and how local neighborhood shapes surface-marker expression and disease-associated tissue reorganization.
orientation: Strong support for the spatial axis (Claim 2 / axis C) and for protein/imaging modality (axis A) as a non-disruptive in-situ alternative to dissociated cytometry; it directly demonstrates that dissociation loses resident/stromal cell types and that local neighborhood (i-niche) drives a measurable fraction of surface-protein expression variance (Claim 7). It also bears on multimodal/integration (Claim 8) and on quantitative cross-method comparability/standardization (Claim 9). Can serve as both ally (protein + spatial in situ is feasible and informative) and as a foil illustrating what dissociated single-cell measurement discards. Note: this is a single-timepoint imaging method with no temporal/lineage tracking, so it is mostly silent on the temporal axis.

excerpts.text: Compared to CyTOF data on splenocytes isolated from non-enzymatically homogenized spleen, CODEX in situ analysis produced a similar distribution of cell counts for major cell type. Yet being a non-disruptive technique CODEX identified larger numbers of resident and stromal cell types such as erythroblasts and F4/80 macrophages than CyTOF did (Figure 3E).
excerpts.note: In situ (non-dissociative) measurement recovers resident and stromal cell types that dissociation-based cytometry under-counts.

excerpts.text: We observed an unexpected, profound impact of the cellular neighborhood on the expression of protein receptors on immune cells.
excerpts.note: States the central spatial finding: cellular neighborhood strongly affects protein receptor expression on cells.

excerpts.text: Taken together, we see that surface-marker expression alone is insufficient to associate many cell subsets with a given tissue subcompartment (e.g., CD4<sup>+</sup> T cells can be found both in the PALS and in the red pulp). However, i-niche designation does provide such mapping data
excerpts.note: Marker identity alone cannot place a cell in a tissue subcompartment; spatial neighborhood (i-niche) information is required.

excerpts.text: These observations suggest that the spread of the CD79b-B220 levels as well as of other marker levels on splenic B cells could be, to a large degree, accounted for by the niche composition around those B cells—and that the expression levels on these cells might be influenced by (or influences) the cells in their immediate surrounding.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Cell-Surface-Marker Expression Depends on Local Neighborhood
excerpts.note: Variance in protein marker levels on B cells is largely attributable to spatial niche composition, not just intrinsic noise.

excerpts.text: Notably, adding the i-niche information as a dependent variable significantly improved the fitness of the model for all markers (Table S4) with highest improvement F-values for CD90, B220, CD21/35, and ERTR7 and the lowest prediction rates for Ly6G, CD5, CD11b, CD5, and TCR-b.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Cell-Surface-Marker Expression Depends on Local Neighborhood
excerpts.note: Quantitative regression: spatial neighborhood identity significantly improves prediction of protein marker expression for all markers, strongly for some.

excerpts.text: As a counter point, the data also show that i-niche does not reliably predict expression of other proteins, such as CD5 or TCR-b, the expression levels of these receptors is relatively constant across the i-niches (Figure 4E).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Cell-Surface-Marker Expression Depends on Local Neighborhood
excerpts.note: Conceded nuance: spatial neighborhood does not drive all proteins; some marker levels are neighborhood-independent.

excerpts.text: Such a distribution of expression is sometimes attributed to staining variability, measurement noise, or a simple lack of understanding of the underlying biology. However, as seen on Figure 4H, upper-left panel, there is a non-random pattern of CD79b and B220 expression across the central cell of the corresponding i-niches, and, depending on the B220/CD79b levels, the i-niches (the central cells) map to specific regions in the splenic architecture
excerpts.anchor: Results — Cell-Surface-Marker Expression Depends on Local Neighborhood
excerpts.note: Protein-level spread once dismissed as noise is shown to be spatially structured and to map to anatomical location.

excerpts.text: In other words, what immunologists previously thought of as a single cell type could be subdivided into more subtle cell subsets that are defined by the neighborhood in which they reside.
excerpts.note: Spatial context resolves additional functional cell subsets beyond what marker phenotype alone defines.

excerpts.text: Unexpectedly, a consistently high degree of association was observed between the cells of the same phenotypic class (Figure 3G, red diagonal), suggesting that homotypic adhesion constitutes a major force driving the architecture of immune tissue.
excerpts.note: Spatial organization of tissue is non-random and governed by cell-cell association rules detectable only with spatial data.

excerpts.text: From these observations and their quantitative analysis, we concluded that it is largely the change in cell numbers rather than in cellular interaction strength (estimated from ratio of observed to expected probability of interaction) that is involved in reorganization of spleen during transition from norm to autoimmunity.
excerpts.note: Disease-associated tissue reorganization is driven mainly by shifts in cell-type frequency rather than changes in pairwise interaction affinity.

excerpts.text: We found some examples supporting that, whereby the proximity of CD4 T cells to B220<sup>+</sup> DN T leads to CD4 T cell activation in spleens of MRL/lpr mice: Figure 6C shows increased levels of CD27 expression in CD4 T cells present in i-niches dominated by B220<sup>+</sup> DN T cells
excerpts.anchor: Results — Reorganization of Cells in Disease-Associated Tissue Substructures
excerpts.note: Spatial proximity to a disease-associated cell type changes a protein activation marker (CD27) on neighboring CD4 T cells.

excerpts.text: Antigen co-expression signals from CODEX, obtained from image segmentation (see STAR Methods), were consistently similar to CyTOF (Figure 2B, for direct comparability, both CODEX and CyTOF data are plotted on a linear scale).
excerpts.note: In situ protein quantification benchmarks consistently against an orthogonal single-cell method (CyTOF), supporting cross-platform comparability.

excerpts.text: Accurate highly multiplexed singlecell quantification of membrane protein expression in densely packed lymphoid tissue images (which was once deemed impossible [Gerner et al., 2012]) was achieved using polymerase-driven incorporation of dye-labeled nucleotides into the DNA tag of oligonucleotide-conjugated antibodies, combined with an image-based expression estimation algorithm.
excerpts.note: Demonstrates feasibility of high-plex single-cell protein quantification in situ in intact tissue.

excerpts.text: A consistent performance of CODEX in codetecting up to 66 antigens was demonstrated, and the ‘‘activation primer’’-based extension of the system could enable a potentially vast expansion of CODEX multiplexing capacity.
excerpts.note: Reports multiplexing depth (up to 66 antigens) with a design path to higher multiplexing for spatial protein measurement.

excerpts.text: Thus, panel-activator design extends CODEX to a theoretically unlimited multiplexing capacity, bounded only by the speed and resolution of the imaging process itself and the time required for each imaging cycle.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Single Base Primer Extension Enables Multiplexed Antigen Staining
excerpts.note: Spatial protein multiplexing is bounded by imaging speed/resolution and per-cycle time — a throughput trade-off on the measurement side.

excerpts.text: the data collected in this study lays the foundation for a pan-cellular reference database defining cellular types not only by identities of proteins expressed, but also by definitions for specific cell-to-cell interactions. We performed deep characterization here for normal and diseased tissue from such a perspective of cell-cell arrangements and present here, for the research community, a large ( 700,000 cells) public dataset encompassing segmentation, quantification, and, most uniquely, spatial data from normal and diseaseafflicted spleens
excerpts.note: Proposes that a cell-type reference should be defined by protein identity plus spatial cell-cell interactions, and releases a large spatial single-cell dataset.

excerpts.text: Recent advances in genomics suggest that, despite the vastness of a genetic repertoire, there exist only a limited number of cellular states with a concomitantly limited gene expression pattern. These countable, limited patterns are reflected in the expression of surface-marker phenotypes recognizable as cell types. It is therefore reasonable to suggest that cell-to-cell interactions should be limited as well and falling into repeated patterns.
excerpts.note: Argues spatial cell-cell interaction patterns are finite and recurrent, hence learnable/standardizable like cell-state catalogs.

excerpts.text: Tissue locale (i-niches) is a powerful indicator of potential differential function (to the extent tissue locale drives function), and these deterministic changes in surface marker protein expression are surrogate indicators of this locale or function.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Cell-Surface-Marker Expression Depends on Local Neighborhood
excerpts.note: Links spatial tissue location to differential function, read out via protein marker changes.

figuresNumbers.contains: A total of 734,101 segmented cells across 9 spleens; reduced to 707,466 after removing artifact clusters; grouped into 27 broadly defined phenotypic groups (from 58 X-shift clusters).
3 wild-type/normal BALBc spleens and 6 MRL/lpr spleens (early: MRL-4,-5,-6; intermediate: MRL-7,-8; late: MRL-9); 9-month-old female mice.
Strong variation in cell counts between norm and disease for 19 of 27 cell types identified by X-shift clustering.

No correlation (R2 = 0.058) between interaction count change and odds-ratio change; correlation (R2 = 0.288) between cell-count change and interaction count change. Of 26 top-scoring pairs (FDR <0.05, count change >150), only 2 showed significant odds-ratio change.

Signal-to-noise ~85:1 (avg over 15 cycles); fluorophore removal efficiency ~98%; signal deterioration ~0.79% per cycle; background starts ~1.1% and increases ~0.06% per cycle; spillover compensation gives ~2-fold reduction in spillover signal.

30-antibody panel for spleen; 22-plex detected thrice (66 total detections) via primer-dependent subpanels; main spleen imaging acquired 28 DNA-conjugated antibodies over 14 cycles plus 2 fluorescent antibodies and 1 nuclear stain = 31 channels.
Algorithm correctly identified 87.25% +/- 2.89% of cells (BALBc), of which 89.88% +/- 1.12% were singlets; MRL 88.50% +/- 5.31%, singlets 92.09% +/- 4.41%.

Fully convolutional network (VGG-16 initialized layers 5-16) classified normal vs MRL/lpr image patches at ~90% precision in cross-validation; a spatial-information-free logistic-regression baseline could not distinguish patches.
40x oil objective, 188 nm/pixel lateral, 900 nm axial, 11 z-planes per tile, 7x9 tiled acquisition; final images x=9702 y=9072 z=11; ~3.5 hr for a 30-antibody visualization.

OCR quirks kept verbatim in excerpts (e.g. '<sup>+</sup>', '<sub>-</sub>85:1' style markers in source); section header 'BEFERENCES'/'DECLARATIONOE INTERESTS' are OCR errors in the source.

title: Spatial omics: applications and utility in profiling the tumor microenvironment
authors: Ji-Eun See, Sarah Barlow, Wani Arjumand, Hannah DuBose, Felipe Segato Dezem, Jasmine Plummer
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/15 - Spatial omics - applications and utility in profiling the tumor microenvironment_cleaned.md
oneSentence: A mini review of spatial omics (transcriptomics and proteomics) technologies, computational tools, and their applications in profiling the tumor microenvironment, covering platform trade-offs, cancer-research use cases, and challenges around resolution, multi-omics integration, standardization, and cost.
orientation: This review is a strong reservoir for the spatial axis and the modality axis (protein vs RNA) of our thesis: it repeatedly argues that bulk/single-cell sequencing loses spatial context and that spatial omics preserves native tissue structure, and it documents multimodal spatial proteomics. It also feeds the standard/integration claim (it concedes a lack of standardized protocols, reproducibility, and clinical validation). It can serve as support for our spatial and multimodal arguments AND as a foil/nuance source where it shows imaging-based methods need predefined probe panels and data remain sparse/noisy. Note it is a TME/cancer-focused review and a secondary source.

excerpts.text: Unlike conventional bulk or single-cell sequencing approaches, spatial omics has the advantage of preserving the spatial structure of tissues, allowing researchers to directly map molecular information onto histological structures.
excerpts.note: States that spatial omics preserves tissue spatial structure that bulk and single-cell sequencing do not.

excerpts.text: Traditional genomic approaches, such as bulk RNA sequencing, have been instrumental in analyzing RNA to infer overall tissue characteristics. However, these methods lack spatial resolution, which limits their ability to identify where specifc transcripts originate within the tissue architecture and hinders the understanding of cellular interactions in situ.
excerpts.anchor: 1.1 The evolution of spatial omics technology
excerpts.note: Bulk RNA-seq lacks spatial resolution, limiting localization of transcripts and understanding of in situ cellular interactions.

excerpts.text: The advent of single-cell transcriptomics marked a major advance, enabling the dissection of transcriptomes at the level of individual cells. This allowed for fner classifcation of cell states and types, and provided new insights into intercellular dynamics. Yet, despite this granularity, single-cell approaches could not defnitively resolve spatial context or confrm physical interactions between cells within native tissue environments [1, 2].
excerpts.anchor: 1.1 The evolution of spatial omics technology
excerpts.note: Single-cell transcriptomics cannot resolve spatial context or confirm physical cell-cell interactions in native tissue.

excerpts.text: Techniques such as multiplexed error-robust fluorescence in situ hybridization (MERFISH) allow for direct visualization of RNA transcripts within individual cells, preserving spatial integrity at subcellular resolution [6].
excerpts.anchor: 1.1 The evolution of spatial omics technology
excerpts.note: MERFISH visualizes RNA transcripts within individual cells at subcellular spatial resolution.

excerpts.text: Platforms like GeoMx Digital Spatial Profler—combining sequencing with region-specifc profling—and DBIT-seq—using fuidic delivery of the barcodes, allowing for multimodal spatial profling assays (e.g., RNA, protein and epigenomics)—further expand the utility of sequencing-based approaches [10, 11].
excerpts.note: DBiT-seq enables multimodal spatial assays measuring RNA, protein, and epigenomics together.

excerpts.text: Imaging-based spatial proteomics leverages similar principles to transcriptomic assays but focuses on detecting and spatially mapping protein expression across tissues.
excerpts.note: Imaging-based spatial proteomics spatially maps protein expression across tissues.

excerpts.text: Sequential immunofuorescence (seqIF-COMET) and other cyclic immunofluorescence (CycIF) methods expand on traditional immunohistochemistry by performing repeated rounds of staining, imaging, and signal removal to profle up to 100 protein targets in the same tissue section [16, 17].
excerpts.note: Cyclic immunofluorescence methods profile up to 100 protein targets in one tissue section.

excerpts.text: Proteomic assays are often integrated with transcriptomic spatial data to provide a more comprehensive, multimodal view of tissue organization and cellular phenotypes. While proteomics is inherently limited by the availability and specifcity of antibodies, advances in antibody validation and new multiplexing chemistries continue to expand target panels.
excerpts.note: Spatial proteomics is integrated with transcriptomics for a multimodal view but is limited by antibody availability and specificity.

excerpts.text: Trajectory inference methods adapted for spatial contexts (e.g., Monocle 3 with spatial anchoring or spatial pseudotime models) can reveal developmental or pathological gradients across tissue landscapes [31, 32].
excerpts.anchor: 1.5 Computational methods for spatial data analysis
excerpts.note: Spatial trajectory/pseudotime inference reconstructs developmental or pathological gradients across tissue rather than measuring real time courses.

excerpts.text: Similarly, reference-based mapping frameworks such as Tangram and InsitutypeML depend heavily on the quality and robustness of single-cell reference atlases, potentially including biased cell-type annotation in poorly characterized tissue areas.
excerpts.anchor: 1.5 Computational methods for spatial data analysis
excerpts.note: Reference-based mapping tools depend on single-cell reference atlas quality and can introduce biased annotation in poorly characterized tissue.

excerpts.text: SiGra [33] and xSiGra [34] employ graph neural networks to integrate spatial transcriptomics and proteomics information, revealing latent spatial domains and higher-order cellular interactions.
excerpts.anchor: 1.5 Computational methods for spatial data analysis
excerpts.note: Graph neural network methods integrate spatial transcriptomics and proteomics to reveal spatial domains and cellular interactions.

excerpts.text: As data complexity increases—especially with 3D spatial data or multi-omics integration—scalable, interpretable, and biologically grounded computational frameworks will be vital to fully capitalize on the power of spatial omics.
excerpts.anchor: 1.5 Computational methods for spatial data analysis
excerpts.note: Multi-omics integration of spatial data needs scalable, interpretable computational frameworks.

excerpts.text: Spatial omics uniquely revealed how immune and tumor cells were organized and interacted within the PCNSL TME— specifically, the location-dependent immune suppression and signaling patterns that traditional single-cell or bulk analyses could not detect.
excerpts.anchor: 2 Application of spatial omics technology to cancer research
excerpts.note: Spatial omics revealed location-dependent immune suppression and signaling in the tumor microenvironment that single-cell or bulk analyses could not detect.

excerpts.text: These spatial patterning—distancebased segregation of cell clusters and EcoCellTypes enrichments—correlate with patient outcomes.
excerpts.anchor: 2 Application of spatial omics technology to cancer research
excerpts.note: Spatial distance-based segregation of cell clusters in the TME correlates with patient outcomes.

excerpts.text: These studies demonstrate that spatial omics goes beyond conventional bulk or single-cell analyses to understand cellular identity and interactions within the TME in a spatial context. Spatial information allows researchers to understand not only the role of each cell, but also where and how they communicate with one another, providing crucial insights for a deeper understanding of cancer behavior and treatment response.
excerpts.anchor: 2 Application of spatial omics technology to cancer research
excerpts.note: Spatial information reveals where and how cells communicate, beyond what bulk or single-cell analyses capture.

excerpts.text: Sun et al. mapped spatial gene expression in hypoxic and normoxic tumor regions and found that hypoxia induced signifcant changes in tumor cell subpopulations that increase resistance and promote invasion, particularly in hypoxic areas.
excerpts.anchor: 2 Application of spatial omics technology to cancer research
excerpts.note: Spatially mapped hypoxic microenvironment regions drive tumor subpopulation changes increasing resistance and invasion.

excerpts.text: Spatial profling further revealed that PD-L1 + endothelial cells in acinar regions contribute to immune evasion and tumor progression by inhibiting CD8 + T-cell infltration.
excerpts.anchor: 2 Application of spatial omics technology to cancer research
excerpts.note: Spatial profiling localized PD-L1+ endothelial cells driving immune evasion via inhibition of CD8+ T-cell infiltration.

excerpts.text: These results reveal the limitations of bulk molecular profling in these highly plastic tumors and demonstrate the value of spatial approaches in improving subtype classifcation and establishing treatment strategies.
excerpts.anchor: 2 Application of spatial omics technology to cancer research
excerpts.note: Bulk molecular profiling is limited in highly plastic tumors; spatial approaches improve subtype classification.

excerpts.text: Pseudotime spatial omics analysis showed that the activation of oncogenic pathways and higher copy number variations were observed in IDC, and T cells were converted to exhaustion through co-inhibitory interactions such as NECTIN2–TIGIT.
excerpts.anchor: 2 Application of spatial omics technology to cancer research
excerpts.note: Pseudotime (inferred, not measured) spatial analysis ordered breast cancer progression and T-cell exhaustion via co-inhibitory interactions.

excerpts.text: Vahid et al. performed spatial proteomics profling of tumor and stromal compartments in 102 non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC) patients to identify protein signatures associated with overall survival rates [51]. This study has shown that stromal CD56 expression correlates with better survival, whereas tumoral B-cell lymphoma extra large (BCLXL) and B7-H3 were linked to poorer outcomes.
excerpts.anchor: 2 Application of spatial omics technology to cancer research
excerpts.note: Spatial proteomics of 102 NSCLC patients found protein signatures (stromal CD56, tumoral BCLXL, B7-H3) prognostic for survival.

excerpts.text: As these technologies evolve beyond simple mapping tools into integrated platforms, they are revealing that core functional characteristics of tumor biology such as subclonal evolution, immune evasion, metabolic adaptation, and therapeutic resistance are intimately linked to spatial organization.
excerpts.anchor: 2 Application of spatial omics technology to cancer research
excerpts.note: Functional tumor characteristics including subclonal evolution and metabolic adaptation are intimately linked to spatial organization.

excerpts.text: A major technical challenge with current spatial omics platforms is their limited resolution, especially when it comes to distinguishing individual cells. For these applications, because it is not assigned at an individual cell level, it becomes computationally challenging to separate gene activity accurately and restricts the analysis of cellular heterogeneity, which is important for cancer research.
excerpts.note: Limited single-cell resolution of current spatial platforms restricts accurate separation of gene activity and heterogeneity analysis.

excerpts.text: High-resolution imagingbased transcriptomic approaches ofer greater resolution but are limited in scale and gene coverage and require predefned probe panels, which may limit the discovery of novel transcripts or rare cell populations.
excerpts.note: Imaging-based approaches gain resolution but are limited in scale, gene coverage, and require predefined probe panels (the resolution-throughput trade-off).

excerpts.text: Developing platforms that balance high resolution, comprehensive transcriptome coverage (which was recently launched by the CosMX platform) and scalability is still a signifcant challenge, particularly when studying tumors where the spatial distribution of rare cell populations is important.
excerpts.note: Balancing high resolution, transcriptome coverage, and scalability simultaneously remains a significant unsolved trade-off.

excerpts.text: Tumors are formed for various reasons, including gene expression, mutations, chromatin state, protein expression, and metabolic state, each of which has its own impact on cancer progression and treatment resistance. Integrating spatial omics with multiomics data can provide a more comprehensive understanding of cancer biology.
excerpts.note: Distinct modalities (gene expression, mutations, chromatin, protein, metabolic state) each independently impact cancer; integrating them gives a more comprehensive understanding.

excerpts.text: However, spatial omics data tend to be sparse and noisy, and in certain cases, genes can be under-detected due to molecular capture limitations, which limit biological interpretability and reduce the accuracy of integration with other modalities at single-cell resolution [52].
excerpts.note: Spatial omics data are sparse and noisy with under-detected genes, limiting interpretability and cross-modality integration accuracy.

excerpts.text: Moreover, spatial datasets may contain novel or rare cell types that are not present in scRNA-seq references, and assuming complete overlap in cell populations during data integration can introduce bias or misinterpretation [53].
excerpts.note: Integration assuming complete cell-population overlap between spatial and scRNA-seq references can introduce bias when rare cell types are absent from references.

excerpts.text: Few standardized protocols and analytical frameworks exist for integrating multi-modal datasets, which hinder efective data alignment among data sets.
excerpts.note: Few standardized protocols/frameworks exist for multi-modal integration, hindering data alignment across datasets.

excerpts.text: There is a need for robust and generalizable algorithms and integration tools capable of handling multiomics data at the single-cell level across diferent experimental settings and biological contexts [56].
excerpts.note: Robust, generalizable multi-omics integration tools that work across experimental settings are still needed.

excerpts.text: The absence of these standardized protocols reduces reproducibility across platforms and laboratories, preventing spatial omics from being efectively implemented in the clinical area [57].
excerpts.note: Absence of standardized protocols reduces cross-platform and cross-laboratory reproducibility.

excerpts.text: Moreover, the clinical predictive or prognostic utility of spatial omics has not been sufciently validated through large-scale clinical trials to date. For spatial omics to be a routine part of precision oncology, further studies are essential to link spatial gene expression patterns with clinical outcomes across various cancer types and patient populations.
excerpts.note: Clinical predictive/prognostic utility of spatial omics is not yet validated by large-scale trials; cross-cohort comparability is lacking.

excerpts.text: In addition, the high cost of spatial omics experiments is a hurdle to clinical translation. It requires infrastructure such as specialized equipment, consumables, and data storage solutions, as well as personnel trained in sample preparation, imaging, and data analysis. These economic constraints limit the scale of studies that include heterogeneous patient cohorts and ultimately slow the clinical translation of spatial omics in cancer research.
excerpts.note: High cost and infrastructure demands of spatial omics limit study scale and slow clinical translation.

excerpts.text: To move toward clinical translation, spatial omics assays must meet stringent regulatory and technical standards akin to those that facilitated the clinical adoption of next-generation sequencing (e.g., MACQC guidelines).
excerpts.note: Spatial omics needs stringent regulatory and technical standards (like NGS QC guidelines) for clinical adoption.

excerpts.text: However, spatial proteomics has a clear path to clinical practice because it is uniquely positioned with current diagnostic use antibodybased panels.
excerpts.note: Spatial proteomics has a clearer clinical path due to existing antibody-based diagnostic panels.

excerpts.text: Foundation models like CellLM and scFoundation link cellular states to drug response and metastatic potential, highlighting the increasingly important role AI and ML play as predictive engines for precision oncology [58] (Table 2).
excerpts.note: Foundation models (CellLM, scFoundation) link cellular states to drug response and metastatic potential as predictive engines.

excerpts.text: A major frontier lies in the development of standardized protocols for integrating multi-omics modalities— such as spatial transcriptomics, proteomics, epigenomics, and metabolomics—which would allow for a more holistic understanding of tumor biology and disease progression.
excerpts.note: Standardized protocols for integrating spatial transcriptomics, proteomics, epigenomics, and metabolomics are a major frontier for holistic tumor understanding.

excerpts.text: Ultimately, the convergence of high-resolution spatial measurements, multi-modal integration, and advanced computational tools will redefne how we study human disease—bringing spatial biology from bench to bedside.
excerpts.note: Convergence of high-resolution spatial measurement and multimodal integration is framed as the future of disease study.

figuresNumbers.contains: Sequencing-based, ~0.5-2 μm spot (single-cell, subcellular); whole transcriptome; FFPE, FF; high spatial resolution, large area capture; limitations: limited instrument access, complex data analysis.
Sequencing-based, ~55 μm spot (10-20 cells); whole transcriptome; not single-cell resolution, coarse spatial context.
Sequencing-based, ~2 μm pixel (single-cell, subcellular); whole transcriptome; limitation: large data size.
Sequencing-based, resolution dependent on ROI size (20-300 cells); whole transcriptome + proteins; non-destructive (slides reusable); not single-cell resolution.
Sequencing-based, ~10 μm pixel (single-cell); whole transcriptome + proteins + epigenetic marks; multi-omics (RNA + protein); limitation: complex microfluidic setup.
Imaging-based, ~200 nm pixel (single-cell, subcellular); targeted panels (~5000 genes); limitations: not whole-transcriptome, high cost.
Imaging-based, ~100-200 nm localization precision (subcellular); targeted panels (~10,000 genes); limitations: complex imaging and analysis, high cost.
Imaging-based, ~0.5-1.2 μm pixel (single-cell, subcellular); targeted panels (~1000-whole transcriptome+proteins); limitations: limited gene count, long imaging time.
seqIF-COMET / CycIF profile up to 100 protein targets in the same tissue section.
Spatial proteomics of tumor and stromal compartments in 102 NSCLC patients; stromal CD56 correlates with better survival, tumoral BCLXL and B7-H3 with poorer outcomes.
Spatial omics on 92 triple-negative breast cancer patients identified nine spatial archetypes linked to clinical outcomes.
Spatial omics on 11 triple-negative tumors (3 claudin-low, 4 metaplastic breast carcinoma) identified tumor-specific markers BMPER, POPDC3, SH3RF3 despite stromal contamination.

title: Integration of imaging-based and sequencing-based spatial omics mapping on the same tissue section via DBiTplus
authors: A. Enninful et al. (initials not fully printed in cleaned source; corresponding lab: R. Fan; first/last author full given names not in body)
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/16 - DBiTplus - imaging-based and sequencing-based spatial omics on the same tissue section_cleaned.md
oneSentence: Presents DBiTplus, a method that runs sequencing-based spatial transcriptomics and multiplexed protein imaging (CODEX/CellScape) on the SAME tissue section via RNase H-mediated cDNA retrieval, plus a computational pipeline that uses the imaging data to deconvolve spatial-transcriptomics spots into single-cell-resolved transcriptome atlases, demonstrated on mouse embryos and human lymph node / lymphoma FFPE samples.
orientation: Strong ammunition for the spatial axis and Claim 6 (high-throughput spatial multi-omics is now feasible) and Claim 8 (same-section multimodal integration). Its 'Evaluating RNA-protein concordance' section is a high-value FOIL/support for Claim 4 (modalities are non-substitutable: it measures near-zero mRNA-protein correlation). Its motivation paragraph concedes Claim 9 (computational integration of adjacent/serial sections is suboptimal because perfect concordance between sections is unattainable). Can serve as both an ally (a concrete spatially-resolved multimodal data-production method) and, via its conceded limitations (low capture depth, dropout, panel-bound protein side), as a nuancing voice on the resolution-throughput-destructiveness trade-off (Claim 10).

excerpts.text: The advent of high-throughput single-cell technologies has revolutionized our understanding of biological systems, enabling comprehensive analyses at the molecular level across diverse biological contexts. These approaches lack spatial context, limiting our understanding of intercellular interactions within tissues.
excerpts.note: States that high-throughput single-cell (dissociation-based) technologies lack spatial context, limiting understanding of intercellular interactions within tissues.

excerpts.text: Broadly, in situ sequencing or imaging techniques offer higher spatial resolution and sensitivity but may lack transcriptome-wide coverage, whereas sequencing-based methods provide transcriptome-wide coverage, often at the expense of high spatial resolution.
excerpts.note: Articulates a spatial-resolution vs transcriptome-coverage trade-off across spatial omics platform families (imaging-based high resolution but limited coverage; sequencing-based wide coverage but lower resolution).

excerpts.text: Currently, most spatial multi-omic technologies involve running single spatial omics assays separately on adjacent or serial tissue sections, followed by computational data integration of the multimodal datasets. Due to heterogeneity in cellular composition and tissue architecture, even between adjacent sections from the same block, integrating multimodal data computationally may be suboptimal, as perfect concordance between tissue sections is almost unattainable. Thus, new methods for spatially resolved multimodal and multi-omics measurements on the same tissue sections are necessary.
excerpts.note: Concedes that computational integration of separate assays on adjacent/serial sections is suboptimal because perfect concordance between sections is unattainable, motivating same-section multimodal measurement.

excerpts.text: Here, we developed DBiTplus, which combines spatially resolved transcriptomics sequencing with multiplexed immunofluorescence (mxIF) imaging for the unbiased co-profiling of the wholetranscriptome and protein markers on the same tissue section.
excerpts.note: Defines DBiTplus as same-section co-profiling of whole transcriptome (RNA) plus protein markers, a concrete spatially resolved multimodal measurement method.

excerpts.text: We developed computational pipelines to integrate these modalities, allowing imaging-guided deconvolution to generate single-cell-resolved spatial transcriptome atlases.
excerpts.note: Claims imaging-guided deconvolution turns sequencing-based spatial transcriptomic spots into single-cell-resolved spatial transcriptome atlases.

excerpts.text: This is important because the relationship between protein levels and their coding transcripts can be discordant, influenced by spatial and temporal mRNA variations and the protein biosynthesis machinery.
excerpts.note: States that protein levels and their coding transcripts can be discordant, influenced by spatial and temporal mRNA variation and protein biosynthesis machinery.

excerpts.text: Given the documented discrepancies between transcript and protein expression in immune cells, we evaluated the correlation between average normalized mRNA and protein levels in our dataset. As expected, we observed minimal correlation (R = 0.23, P = 0.18), consistent with prior findings in peripheral blood mononuclear cells, particularly for T cell markers such as CD4.
excerpts.note: Reports minimal measured correlation (R = 0.23, P = 0.18) between average normalized mRNA and protein levels in the same tissue, with the discordance especially pronounced for T cell markers such as CD4 — direct evidence that RNA does not predict protein.

excerpts.text: Of the 35 markers in the human lymph node CODEX panel, CD68 was the only gene transcript missing (Extended Data Fig. 6a).
excerpts.note: Notes a protein (CD68) detected by imaging whose transcript was missing in the matched transcriptomic data, illustrating modality-specific detection gaps (protein detected where RNA is not).

excerpts.text: These findings underscore the strength of DBiTplus in co-mapping RNAs and proteins on the same tissue section, allowing for cross-validation and enhancing confidence in cell-type annotation and spatial localization.
excerpts.note: Argues that same-section co-mapping of RNA and protein enables cross-validation and more confident cell-type annotation and spatial localization.

excerpts.text: Joint embedding of DBiTplus and CODEX (Fig. 3g) and violin plots of the cell-type-specific modality weights from WNN analysis, showed that the CODEX (protein) contributed more for T cell-subtype identification, relative to DBiTplus (transcriptome), and the converse was observed for B cell subtypes such as GCB and plasma B cells (Fig. 3h).
excerpts.anchor: Multimodal mapping of normal human lymph nodes
excerpts.note: Shows that protein and transcriptome modalities carry complementary, non-redundant information — protein weighted more for T cell subtyping, transcriptome more for B cell subtypes — so each modality identifies cell types the other cannot.

excerpts.text: DBiTplus further enables spatial profiling of small noncoding RNAs such as microRNAs (miRNAs), which regulate mRNA synthesis and gene expression.
excerpts.anchor: Investigating the role of miRNAs in Richter’s transformation
excerpts.note: Claims DBiTplus can spatially profile small noncoding RNAs (miRNAs) in addition to mRNA, broadening the measurable molecular layers.

excerpts.text: While Xenium and CosMx achieve single-cell spatial resolution through direct imaging of transcripts, DBiTplus even allows for unbiased profiling of total RNAs including small noncoding RNAs (that is, miRNAs), which is unique for discovery of new RNA biological mechanisms.
excerpts.note: Contrasts panel-bound imaging platforms (Xenium, CosMx; single-cell resolution but targeted transcripts) with DBiTplus's unbiased total-RNA profiling — a resolution-vs-coverage trade-off framing.

excerpts.text: DBiTplus shares common limitations with sequencing-based spatial transcriptomics approaches; low capture depth can lead to dropout of low-abundance transcripts, which may be exacerbated by the lower transcript recovery rate of DBiTplus, compared to the standard DBiT-seq workflow, an important consideration when profiling rare cell populations.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: low capture depth causes dropout of low-abundance transcripts, worsened by DBiTplus's lower transcript recovery than standard DBiT-seq — a sensitivity cost of the same-section multimodal approach.

excerpts.text: Further, the tight clamping of the tissue during the DBiTplus workflow may disrupt tissue architecture outside the clamped region and may lead to reduced imaging signal.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: physical clamping during the workflow can disrupt tissue architecture and reduce downstream imaging signal.

excerpts.text: Nonetheless, these limitations are counterbalanced by several strengths: DBiTplus enables whole-transcriptome spatial profiling without being constrained by predesigned panels, can be integrated with high-plex protein imaging on the same tissue section, and is flexible and cost-effective for broad adoption.
excerpts.note: States strengths: panel-free whole-transcriptome spatial profiling integrated with high-plex protein imaging on the same section, framed as flexible and cost-effective for broad adoption.

excerpts.text: Spatial multi-omics integrates genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics and metabolomics while preserving spatial context, providing a comprehensive view of molecular processes in tissues. Traditional approaches utilize separate assays on adjacent tissue sections limiting alignment and multimodal integration accuracy due to tissue heterogeneity.
excerpts.note: Frames the value of spatial multi-omics (preserving spatial context across molecular layers) and reiterates that adjacent-section approaches limit alignment and integration accuracy due to tissue heterogeneity.

excerpts.text: Thus, leveraging mxIF data to guide the splitting of DBiT spatial transcriptomes can enable the creation of truly single-cell-level spatially resolved transcriptome atlases.
excerpts.note: Claims imaging-guided spot splitting enables truly single-cell-level spatially resolved transcriptome atlases from a spot-based sequencing assay.

excerpts.text: Single-cell spatial multimodal metabolomics approaches such as REDCAT combine protein and metabolite measurements in the same tissue section. IN-DEPTH was also recently developed for same-slide spatial multi-omics integration.
excerpts.note: Cites prior same-section multimodal methods (REDCAT combining protein + metabolite; IN-DEPTH for same-slide multi-omics), situating DBiTplus among emerging same-section multimodal efforts.

figuresNumbers.contains: Average normalized mRNA vs protein correlation R = 0.23, P = 0.18 (minimal correlation) in human lymph node; discordance most pronounced for T cell markers such as CD4.

figuresNumbers.item: CODEX panel transcript coverage (Extended Data Fig. 6a)
figuresNumbers.contains: Of 35 markers in human lymph node CODEX panel, CD68 was the only gene transcript missing in the matched transcriptomic data.
Normalized gene expression vs Bai et al. Patho-DBiT lymph node reference: R = 0.89, P < 2.2 x 10^-16, consistent especially for abundant transcripts (PTPRC, ACTB, HLA.DRA).
DBiTplus correlated R = 0.99 with standard DBiT-seq on adjacent section; 27,884 overlapping genes; ~1,200 genes and ~3,300 UMIs captured per spot.

figuresNumbers.item: E13 embryo gene detection (Extended Data Fig. 2a)
figuresNumbers.contains: Sequencing detected 24,102 genes (proteinase K control) and 20,973 genes (RNase H test); test/control replicate correlations R = 0.87 (within), R = 0.72 (between), R = 0.84 (between test replicates); 1,109 overlapping genes between matched clusters.
Against CODEX-informed ground truth, TACCO performed best with 50% of spots Pearson > 0.6 vs 36% for Cell2location; ASW with vs without CODEX 0.47 vs 0.46 (similar).
Recovered cDNA size ranged from 200 to 800 base pairs in human cerebellum and lymph node FFPE.
DBiTplus CODEX staining quality vs control lymph node sections: Pearson > 0.85 for key markers CD3e, CD20, podoplanin.
MZL tumor microenvironment ~55% malignant B cells (large+small) and ~30% T cells; in CLL/DLBCL case B cells ~60% of all cells; large B cells largest diameters ~20 um.

title: Deep Visual Proteomics defines single-cell identity and heterogeneity
authors: Andreas Mund, Fabian Coscia, András Kriston, Réka Hollandi, Ferenc Kovács, Andreas-David Brunner, Ede Migh, Lisa Schweizer, Alberto Santos, Michael Bzorek, Soraya Naimy, Lise Mette Rahbek-Gjerdrum, Beatrice Dyring-Andersen, Jutta Bulkescher, Claudia Lukas, Mark Adam Eckert, Ernst Lengyel, Christian Gnann, Emma Lundberg, Peter Horvath, Matthias Mann
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/17 - Deep Visual Proteomics defines single-cell identity and heterogeneity_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The paper introduces Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP), a workflow combining AI-driven image analysis, automated single-cell laser microdissection and ultra-high-sensitivity mass spectrometry to measure single-cell proteomes while preserving spatial and subcellular context, and applies it to cultured cells and archived cancer tissues to resolve cell-state and disease-progression proteome changes in situ.
orientation: A spatial single-cell proteomics method paper that most directly feeds the protein-modality axis and the spatial axis: it demonstrates that high-sensitivity proteomics of ~50-100 cells with retained spatial context is now feasible (supports claim 6 capability and claim 2's spatial gap as a remedy), recovers protein-level information not captured by transcriptomics (claim 4 modality orthogonality), and localizes microenvironment-dependent state changes in tumor tissue (claim 7). It can also serve as a foil: it concedes that dissociation/single-cell transcriptomics loses spatial and ECM context (claim 2), and its destructive endpoint MS measurement underscores the no-paired-observation constraint relevant to claim 3 (temporal), though its cell-cycle classes only infer dynamics from a snapshot.

excerpts.text: Despite the availabilty of imaging-based and mass-spectrometry-based methods for spatial proteomics, a key challenge remains connecting images with single-cell-resolution protein abundance measurements.
excerpts.note: States the unsolved problem the method targets: linking imaging to single-cell-resolution protein abundance in spatial proteomics.

excerpts.text: DVP links protein abundance to complex cellular or subcellular phenotypes while preserving spatial context.
excerpts.note: Claims the method retains spatial context while measuring single-cell protein abundance.

excerpts.text: The ability of DVP to retain precise spatial proteomic information in the tissue context has implications for the molecular profiling of clinical samples.
excerpts.note: Frames retaining spatial proteomic information in tissue as the key clinical implication.

excerpts.text: Currently, a predefined subset of proteins is usually targeted, far short of the actual complexity of the proteome.
excerpts.note: Notes that imaging-based proteomics is usually limited to a small predefined antibody panel, far short of full proteome complexity.

excerpts.text: Taking advantage of substantially increased sensitivity in technology based on mass spectrometry (MS), we set out to enable the analysis of proteomes within their native, subcellular context to explore their contribution to health and disease.
excerpts.note: States the goal of measuring unbiased MS proteomes within native subcellular context, enabled by increased MS sensitivity.

excerpts.text: After optimization, the LMD7 can autonomously excise 1,250 high-resolution contours per hour, equivalent to 50 to 100 cells per sample (Methods).
excerpts.note: Quantifies automated laser microdissection throughput (1,250 contours/hour; 50-100 cells per proteomic sample).

excerpts.text: Current LMD methods preserve the spatial context but are mostly limited to human-eye-observable phenotypes and require manual selection of cells, often resulting in admixing of different cell types, which constrains throughput and de novo discovery11.
excerpts.note: Concedes that prior laser-microdissection methods, while spatially preserving, are throughput- and discovery-limited by manual selection and cell-type admixing.

excerpts.text: we obtained normal human fallopian tube tissue and separated ciliated from secretory cells—the two major cell types of the fallopian tube epithelium12—using the cell-lineage-specific transcription factor FOXJ1, a master regulator of cilia function, and measured their proteomes (Fig. 2e–h, Extended Data Fig. 1c–f and Supplementary Table 2). We solely detected FOXJ1 (ciliated cells) in FOXJ1-stained cells (Fig. 2e,g), along with more than 5,000 other quantified proteins with excellent correlations of biological replicates
excerpts.note: Reports separating two epithelial cell types in situ and quantifying >5,000 proteins per cell type with reproducible replicates.

excerpts.text: Such in vivo cell type comparisons will allow the discovery of cell type and cell state markers and provide unbiased information to understand disease states at the global proteome level.
excerpts.note: Argues unbiased global proteome measurement of defined cell types yields cell-state markers and disease-state information.

excerpts.text: Replicates of cell and nucleus proteomes demonstrated high quantitative reproducibility (Pearson r = 0.96), and proteomes of whole cells differed from those of nuclei alone, as expected from subcellular proteomics experiments based on biochemical separation18 (Extended Data Fig. 2a,b).
excerpts.anchor: Results: DVP defines single-cell heterogeneity, p.3
excerpts.note: Demonstrates subcellular (whole-cell vs nucleus) proteome resolution with high replicate reproducibility (r = 0.96).

excerpts.text: ML found three primary nuclei classes (27–37% each) and also identified three rare ones (2–4% each) (Extended Data Fig. 2c). The resulting six distinct nuclei classes had visible differences in size and shape, with class 1 representing mitotic states and the remaining five classes representing interphase with varying feature weighting (Fig. 3e,f).
excerpts.anchor: Results: DVP defines single-cell heterogeneity, p.3
excerpts.note: Shows morphology-defined nuclei classes, including rare states, each with distinct proteomes.

excerpts.text: This revealed that the rarest cell classes had the highest numbers of differentially expressed proteins compared to unclassified ‘bulk’ proteomes (Extended Data Fig. 2d,e).
excerpts.anchor: Results: DVP defines single-cell heterogeneity, p.3
excerpts.note: Reports that the rarest cell classes diverge most from bulk proteomes, motivating single-cell resolution over bulk averaging.

excerpts.text: Comparing our data to a single-cell imaging dataset of cell-cycle-regulated proteins19, we found significant enrichment in our regulated proteins (FDR < 10−6). Nuclear area, one of the driving features among the different classes identified, increased during interphase from G1 to S/G2 cells (Fig. 3e and Extended Data Fig. 3a–c), further supporting the importance of the cell cycle in defining the nuclei classes.
excerpts.anchor: Results: DVP defines single-cell heterogeneity, p.3
excerpts.note: Infers cell-cycle temporal structure (G1 to S/G2) from a fixed snapshot of morphological/proteomic classes, not from live trajectory tracking.

excerpts.text: Our single-cell-type proteomes discovered several uncharacterized proteins, presenting an opportunity to associate them with a potential cellular function. Focusing on C11orf98, C7orf50, C1orf112 and C19orf53, which remained after data filtering (ANOVA P <0.05), showed class-specific expression patterns (Extended Data Fig. 3d).
excerpts.anchor: Results: DVP defines single-cell heterogeneity, p.3
excerpts.note: Reports that unbiased proteomics discovered uncharacterized proteins (ORFs) with cell-class-specific expression.

excerpts.text: These rare, bean-shaped nuclei showed upregulation of specific cytoskeletal and cell adhesion proteins (for example, VIM, TUBB, ACTB and ITGB1), suggesting that these signatures derived from migrating cells undergoing nuclear deformation, suggestive of cellular invasion21,22. Note that we classified nuclei from 2D images, but LMD isolates them in 3D. Thus, samples also probe morphology-driven protein re-localization around the nucleus as exemplified by class 6 nuclei. Likewise, excising the nuclei captures the trafficking of proteins to and from the cytosol to some degree.
excerpts.anchor: Results: DVP defines single-cell heterogeneity, p.3
excerpts.note: Shows morphology-driven subcellular protein re-localization/trafficking is captured by the spatially resolved isolation.

excerpts.text: These cell culture experiments establish that DVP correlates cellular phenotypes, heterogeneity and dynamics with the proteome level in an unbiased way for common and rare phenotypes.
excerpts.anchor: Results: DVP defines single-cell heterogeneity, p.4
excerpts.note: Summary claim that DVP links phenotype/heterogeneity to unbiased single-cell proteome for both common and rare states.

excerpts.text: Billions of patient samples are collected routinely during diagnostic workup and stored in the archives of pathology departments around the world23. The precise proteomic characterization of single cells in their spatial and subcellular context from tissue slides could have a tremendous clinical effect, complementing the emerging field of digital pathology24.
excerpts.anchor: Results: DVP applied to cancer tissue heterogeneity, p.4
excerpts.note: Argues spatial/subcellular single-cell proteomics of archival tissue has large clinical potential.

excerpts.text: Bioinformatics analysis of the measured proteome differences revealed significant biological differences between these neighboring cell types, reflecting their distinct physiological functions. Acinar cells, which produce and secrete saliva in secretory granules, showed high expression of proteins related to vesicle transport and glycosylation along with known acinar cell markers such as α-amylase (AMY1A), CA6 and PIP (Extended Data Fig. 4b). In contrast, ductal cells expressed high levels of mitochondria and metabolism-related proteins required to meet the high energy demand for saliva secretion25 (Extended Data Fig. 4c and Supplementary Table 5).
excerpts.anchor: Results: DVP applied to cancer tissue heterogeneity, p.4
excerpts.note: Shows neighboring cell types separated in situ have functionally distinct proteomes (secretory vs metabolic), tied to physiology.

excerpts.text: DVP allowed us to discover upregulation of interferon response proteins (for example, MX1 and HLA-A; Supplementary Table 6) and the proto-oncogene SRC, both actionable therapeutic targets26 (Fig. 4e).
excerpts.anchor: Results: DVP applied to cancer tissue heterogeneity, p.4
excerpts.note: Reports discovery of disease-specific, druggable protein changes in carcinoma cells via spatial proteomics.

excerpts.text: We co-stained FFPE-embedded primary tumor material preserved for 17 years with two markers, SOX10 and CD146, to map melanoma cells.
excerpts.note: Demonstrates the method works on 17-year-old archival FFPE tumor material.

excerpts.text: ML predicted five classes with clearly defined spatial distribution: class 1, melanoma in situ; class 2, predominantly tumor; class 3, cells of the tumor microenvironment; class 4, enriched in CD146-high regions; and class 5, enriched in CD146-low regions.
excerpts.note: Resolves spatially distinct tumor and microenvironment classes by ML on imaging.

excerpts.text: Including replicates, we isolated and profiled 27 different samples obtained from seven unique regions of the same tissue section, including normal melanocytes, melanoma in situ and primary melanoma from the radial and vertical growth phases (Fig. 5a–d).
excerpts.note: Profiles spatially defined disease-progression stages within a single tissue section.

excerpts.text: Expression of proteins involved in oxidative phosphorylation and mitochondria function gradually increased from melanocytes, melanoma in situ to invasive melanoma, indicating a dependency on mitochondrial respiration in the advanced tumor stages (Fig. 5h–j, Extended Data Fig. 5c and Supplementary Tables 7–9). Conversely, proteins involved in antigen presentation and interferon response were downregulated when compared to melanoma in situ (Fig. 5h–j and Supplementary Tables 7–9), in line with immune evasion strategies in melanoma32.
excerpts.note: Tracks proteome changes (metabolism up, immune presentation down) across spatially ordered disease-progression stages.

excerpts.text: The direct comparison of these spatially defined regions of the same phenotype (class 4 cells) further highlighted critical features of cancer metastasis, such as extracellular matrix (ECM) remodeling (for example, collagen degradation) and upregulated PDGF signaling33 (Fig. 5k,l, Extended Data Fig. 5e and Supplementary Table 10).
excerpts.note: Shows that cells of the same phenotypic class differ proteomically (ECM remodeling, PDGF) depending on their spatial region.

excerpts.text: DVP also discovered a significant upregulation of mRNA splicing in the vertical compared to the radial growth phase. ... The increase in splicing coincided with a significant downregulation of immune-related signaling (interferon signaling and antigen presentation) (Fig. 5l and Supplementary Table 10), suggesting the transition from an immunogenic ‘hot’ to a ‘cold’ tumor zone in the vertical growth phase within the same tumor section.
excerpts.note: Localizes a hot-to-cold immunogenic transition to a specific spatial growth zone within one tumor section.

excerpts.text: In contrast to single-cell transcriptomics, DVP can readily analyze the ECMʼs subcellular structures and spatial dynamics.
excerpts.note: Explicitly contrasts DVP with single-cell transcriptomics, claiming proteomics can analyze ECM and subcellular/spatial structures that scRNA cannot.

excerpts.text: With further improvements in proteomics technology, DVP should also be suited to study proteoforms and post-translational modifications at a single-cell-type level.
excerpts.note: Concedes that proteoform/PTM-level measurement is a future capability not yet realized, indicating present modality coverage is incomplete.

excerpts.text: For FFPE tissue proteome analysis, we collected 50–100 cells per sample (total area collected × slide thickness / average mammalian cell volume of 2,000 µm3; BNID 100434), in agreement with estimations in spatial transcriptomics analysis39.
excerpts.note: Specifies the input requirement of 50-100 cells per tissue proteome sample (not yet true single-cell for tissue), bearing on the resolution-vs-sensitivity trade-off.

figuresNumbers.contains: Mean F1 segmentation scores (0.7-IoU). nucleAIzer/BIAS (OUR) achieved highest scores on most samples, e.g. Salivary gland nuc 0.7684 ± 0.0316 vs unet4nuclei 0.0797, CellProfiler 0.6488, Cellpose 0.0338; U2OS cyto 0.7336 ± 0.0218; Melanoma nuc 0.5498 ± 0.0231.
1,250 high-resolution contours excised per hour; equivalent to 50-100 cells per sample; LMD theoretical accuracy 70 nm (×150 objective), 200 nm in practice.
More than 5,000 proteins quantified per fallopian-tube cell type; cell vs nucleus replicate Pearson r = 0.96; within-group salivary tissue Pearson r > 0.96; normal-vs-cancer Pearson r = 0.8.

Six ML-defined nuclei classes: three primary (27-37% each) and three rare (2-4% each); 515 significantly differentially expressed proteins across five interphase classes; cell-cycle enrichment FDR < 10^-6.
27 samples profiled from seven unique regions of one tissue section; FFPE material preserved 17 years; 1,910 ANOVA-significant (FDR < 0.05) protein groups used for clustering.
Project-specific DIA-NN library: 90,056 precursors, 79,802 elution groups, 7,765 protein groups.

flags: Metadata: DOI confirmed in text (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-022-01302-5); venue inferred from the s41587 prefix = Nature Biotechnology. Volume/page range (40:1231-1240) NOT printed in this cleaned source and was reconstructed from the known DOI/year; treat as unverified.
Dates confirmed in text: Received 8 March 2022; Accepted 30 March 2022; Published online 19 May 2022 (line 51).

Scope note: This is a spatial single-cell proteomics METHOD paper; on-roadmap value is mainly as evidence that high-throughput spatial+subcellular proteomics is feasible (claim 6) and as a contrast to dissociated scRNA-seq (claim 2). It is NOT a virtual-cell/AI-model paper.

Temporal caveat: the cell-cycle 'dynamics' (G1->S/G2) are inferred from a fixed-cell morphological snapshot, not paired/live time-course tracking; relevant to claim 3 (destructive measurement, no paired observation) but the paper does not frame it that way.
OCR quirks preserved verbatim in excerpts/title-page (e.g. 'availabilty', 'predominatly', 'Te' for 'The', 'ml−1', 'µm3', en-dashes); not corrected.

Resolution caveat: for tissue, 50-100 cells are pooled per proteome sample (not single-cell for tissue); single-cell/nucleus proteomes shown mainly for cultured U2OS. Bears on claim 10 (resolution x throughput x destructiveness trade-off).

title: In-depth and high-throughput spatial proteomics for whole-tissue slice profiling by deep learning-facilitated sparse sampling strategy
authors: Ritian Qin, Jiacheng Ma, Fuchu He, Weijie Qin
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/18 - In-depth and high-throughput spatial proteomics for whole-tissue slice profiling_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors developed S4P, a sparse-sampling spatial proteomics strategy with a deep-learning reconstruction model (DeepS4P), to map the spatial distribution of more than 9000 proteins at ~525 um resolution across a whole mouse-brain slice, the largest spatial proteome to date, and found widespread spatial discordance between mRNA and protein distributions.
orientation: Strong support for Claim 6 (high-throughput spatial proteomics is feasible) and Claim 2/Axis C (transcriptomics loses or biases protein spatial information; protein measurement is needed). Also feeds Claim 4 (mRNA-protein non-substitutability) via quantitative correlation data and Claim 10 (resolution x throughput x destructiveness trilemma) via explicit sample-number/MS-time trade-off discussion. The paper itself concedes a temporal limitation (averaging across z-slices, no time course) that can be deployed for Claim 3/Axis B. Can serve as both an ally (proteomics is needed and now achievable) and as a candidate to be critiqued (still no temporal axis, lower resolution than ST, slice-averaging).

excerpts.text: While genome mutations and transcriptome alterations act as drivers of diseases, the proteins that they encode regulate essentially all biological functions and constitute the majority of biomarkers and drug targets for disease diagnostics and treatment.
excerpts.note: Argues proteins, not the upstream genome/transcriptome, regulate biological function and dominate biomarkers and drug targets.

excerpts.text: However, unlike transcriptomics, which has a recent explosion in highthroughput spatial technologies with deep coverage, spatial proteomics capable of reaching bulk tissue-level coverage is still rare in the <sup>fi</sup>eld, due to the non-ampli<sup>fi</sup>able nature of proteins and sensitivity limitation of mass spectrometry (MS).
excerpts.note: States that high-throughput spatial proteomics lags far behind spatial transcriptomics because proteins cannot be amplified and MS sensitivity is limited; protein modality is under-measured.

excerpts.text: More importantly, due to the limited multiplexing capability of the current proteomics methods, whole-tissue slice mapping with high spatial resolution requires a formidable amount of MS matching time.
excerpts.note: Frames the resolution-vs-throughput trade-off: high-resolution whole-slice proteomics demands a prohibitive amount of MS instrument time.

excerpts.text: In this way, we generated the largest spatial proteome to date, mapping more than 9000 proteins in the mouse brain, and discovered potential new regional or cell type markers.
excerpts.note: Claims the largest spatial proteome to date, >9000 proteins mapped in mouse brain.

excerpts.text: The spatial organization of these biomolecules plays a crucial role in determining their function and interaction with their spatial neighborhood in physiological and pathological tissue environments<sup>1,2</sup>.
excerpts.note: Asserts spatial organization and spatial-neighborhood interactions determine biomolecular function in tissue.

excerpts.text: However, it is the gene-encoded proteins that actually regulate essentially all cellular functions and therefore need to be investigated to reveal tissue spatial heterogeneity.
excerpts.note: Argues proteins must be measured directly to reveal tissue spatial heterogeneity, because they execute cellular function.

excerpts.text: Accumulating proteogenomic comparisons revealed that typical Pearson correlation coef<sup>fi</sup>cients for mRNA–protein in mammalian tissues are \~0.35–0.6, and can be even lower at single-cell level<sup>7</sup>.
excerpts.note: Quantifies low mRNA-protein correlation (~0.35-0.6, lower at single-cell level), evidence that RNA cannot substitute for protein measurement.

excerpts.text: Discordant spatial distribution was also revealed by the different zonation patterns of the enterocyte proteins and mRNAs along the villus axis<sup>8</sup>.
excerpts.note: Cites a prior finding that protein and mRNA spatial zonation patterns differ along the intestinal villus axis.

excerpts.text: The poor mRNA–protein correlation may result in biased inference of gene expression distribution using transcriptional data, especially for mammalian brains, considering the large distances between the locations where the proteins are synthesized and transported<sup>9–11</sup>.
excerpts.note: Argues transcriptional data biases inference of protein spatial distribution, especially in brain where proteins are transported far from synthesis sites.

excerpts.text: Imaging mass cytometry (IMC) using lanthanide metal labeling has single-cell sensitivity and resolution. It has gained signi<sup>fi</sup>cant attention for its high throughput, capable of analyzing thousands of cells per minute. However, similar to IHC, it is also a target-based method that is dominated by antibodies<sup>12</sup>. Only several dozen proteins can be analyzed, which makes IMC less attractive for the discoverdriven screening of cellular phenotypes.
excerpts.note: Notes the trade-off that antibody-based IMC has single-cell resolution and high throughput but is limited to several dozen targeted proteins, unsuitable for discovery-driven screening.

excerpts.text: In contrast, liquid chromatography-coupled biological mass spectrometry (LC-MS), which routinely measures \~10,000 unique proteins from bulk cell and tissue samples, is advantageous in capturing the spatial complexity of tissue proteome.
excerpts.note: States LC-MS routinely measures ~10,000 proteins from bulk samples, contrasting with targeted antibody methods.

excerpts.text: 2000 proteins can be obtained at 100-µm spatial resolution due to the limited sample amount<sup>17,18</sup>. Although 3000 proteins at 50-µm resolution was reached in a recent work<sup>19</sup>, a larger scale of proteome identi<sup>fi</sup>cation is more desirable to deeply cover regulatory pathways in tissues for spatially resolved physiological and pathological investigation at the molecular level.
excerpts.note: Documents the resolution-vs-depth trade-off in prior spatial proteomics: ~2000 proteins at 100 um, 3000 at 50 um, limited by sample amount.

excerpts.text: Although the proteome coverage can be improved using region-speci<sup>fi</sup>c anatomic dissection of whole tissue, it relies on histologic anatomy structure and cannot provide the high spatial resolution comparable to that of spatial transcriptomics (ST) or metabolomics<sup>20–22</sup>.
excerpts.note: Concedes that anatomy-based dissection proteomics cannot reach the spatial resolution of spatial transcriptomics or metabolomics.

excerpts.text: Taking a circle tissue slice with a diameter of 1 cm as an example, \~8000 samples, corresponding to more than 8000–10,000 h of MS machine time, are required to achieve whole-tissue-slice coverage with a 100 μm resolution, which makes it a formidable task in routine studies.
excerpts.note: Quantifies the throughput barrier: covering a 1 cm slice at 100 um resolution would need ~8000 samples and >8000-10,000 h of MS time.

excerpts.text: Therefore, whole-tissue-slice-based spatial proteomic approaches that provide reasonable throughput without compromising spatial resolution and proteome depth are urgently needed.
excerpts.note: States the field needs spatial proteomics that balances throughput, spatial resolution, and proteome depth simultaneously.

excerpts.text: Using S4P, we successfully reconstructed the spatial proteome (SP) of a whole-tissue slice of a mouse brain, consisting of the distribution and expression of 9204 proteins with a spatial resolution of 525 μm (Supplementary Fig. S1). In this way, the MS data acquisition was completed within \~200 h of MS time, half of the time required by the “gridding”-like strategy.
excerpts.note: Reports 9204 proteins reconstructed at 525 um resolution in ~200 h MS time, half the time of the gridding strategy.

excerpts.text: For example, an MS time 15–20 times shorter than that of the “gridding”-like strategy can be achieved for a 100 μm spatial resolution (Supplementary Fig. S2).
excerpts.note: Claims sparse sampling cuts MS time 15-20x versus gridding at 100 um resolution.

excerpts.text: The <sup>fi</sup>rst large-scale discovery of different levels of spatial distribution inconsistency between RNAs and proteins in various biological processes, such as synaptic secretion of proteins and neurotransmitters, cellular metabolism, and cognitive & motor function integration, is invaluable for future brain science study.
excerpts.note: Frames the paper's first large-scale finding of spatial RNA-protein inconsistency across biological processes as a key result.

excerpts.text: Considering that ST is currently the dominant technology for mapping the distribution of gene expression products, we performed ST analysis by Visium from 10X Genomics using a tissue slice from the same mouse brain (Supplementary Fig. S13). Limited by the ST chip size, only a partial region of the tissue slice was used for ST analysis (Fig. 7a).
excerpts.anchor: Correlation analysis of S4P and ST mappings
excerpts.note: Notes ST is the dominant gene-expression mapping technology but that the ST chip size limited coverage to only a partial region of the slice.

excerpts.text: 8691 pairs of gene expression products were found from both ST and spatial proteome SP data (Fig. 7b). We further calculated the Pearson correlation of the transcript–protein pairs for their spatial expression. The correlation between different molecule pairs varies greatly, ranging from –0.47 to 0.62 (Fig. 7c).
excerpts.anchor: Correlation analysis of S4P and ST mappings
excerpts.note: Reports spatial transcript-protein Pearson correlations spanning -0.47 to 0.62 across 8691 co-detected gene products, with some strongly anti-correlated.

excerpts.text: For gene products with poor correlation, their inconsistency may be attributed to the complex brain tissue, which comprises hundreds to thousands of cell types, each with potentially different spatial distribution patterns of mRNAs and proteins.
excerpts.anchor: Correlation analysis of S4P and ST mappings
excerpts.note: Attributes mRNA-protein spatial inconsistency partly to cell-type heterogeneity within the brain.

excerpts.text: The spatial discordance between mRNAs and the corresponding functional proteins involved in catabolism processes indicates the necessity of developing SP techniques for exploring the biological mechanism in the brain. Although we cannot fully explain this spatially inconsistent distribution between RNAs and proteins, the results further indicate the advantage of directly mapping protein quantity and location in the tissue.
excerpts.anchor: Correlation analysis of S4P and ST mappings
excerpts.note: Concludes spatial mRNA-protein discordance shows transcriptomics cannot infer protein location and that protein must be directly mapped; authors admit they cannot fully explain the discordance.

excerpts.text: Although a large number of spatial transcriptomics techniques have been developed and successfully applied in studies on brain function, embryonic development, and tumor microenvironment, direct characterization of the quantity and location of proteins at whole-tissue level is indispensable for both biological function and disease mechanism studies, due to the widely discovered low correlation between RNA and protein.
excerpts.note: States direct protein quantity/location measurement is indispensable and not replaceable by spatial transcriptomics, because of low RNA-protein correlation.

excerpts.text: Especially for tissues like the brain, where large-scale proteins are transported away from the sites where they are synthesized, unbiased proteome location measurement is highly demanded to promote elucidation of the spatially organized molecular regulations in the most complex organ.
excerpts.note: Argues protein transport away from synthesis sites in brain makes direct protein localization (not RNA) essential.

excerpts.text: S4P can be used as a complementary strategy to reveal protein distribution that cannot be inferred by spatial transcriptome, especially for the metabolic signaling and protein expression-related genes that are important not only for the understanding of basic biology, but also for the identi<sup>fi</sup>cation of potential targets for future diagnostics and therapies.
excerpts.note: Positions spatial proteomics as complementary to spatial transcriptomics, revealing protein distributions that cannot be inferred from RNA.

excerpts.text: Another limitation is that eight adjacent slices are needed for spatial proteome reconstruction currently. Although we did not observe obvious proteome differences among these slices (Supplementary Fig. S4), we still have to consider that the <sup>fi</sup>nal spatial proteome pro<sup>fi</sup>le obtained is the average result of the eight slices spanning 80 μm along the z axis.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: the reconstructed proteome is an average over eight z-axis slices (80 um), limiting true 3D/single-slice spatial fidelity.

excerpts.text: According to our attempts, \~100 µm in strip width is the current limit for LMDbased sample collection. For even higher spatial resolution, a combination of S4P with a tissue expansion method<sup>18</sup> may be a promising way, in which size of the tissue slice can be evenly expanded \~8 times in every direction to support possible singlecell spatial resolution.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded resolution limit: ~100 um strip width is the current LMD limit, below true single-cell spatial resolution without tissue expansion.

excerpts.text: Therefore, future studies using a combination of S4P and time-resolved mouse models may provide information on the dynamic distribution and functional variation of synaptic vesicle proteins under different physiological and pathological conditions for a more comprehensive understanding of synaptic transmission.
excerpts.anchor: Landscape of protein spatial distribution in the mouse brain
excerpts.note: Implicitly concedes the method captures a static snapshot and lacks a temporal/dynamic axis; proposes future time-resolved models to add dynamics.

figuresNumbers.contains: 9204 protein distribution maps reconstructed at 525 um spatial resolution; 234,768 peptides and 9318 unique proteins mapped/quantified in brain strips; ~200 h MS time, half of the gridding strategy.
Typical Pearson correlation for mRNA-protein in mammalian tissues ~0.35-0.6, lower at single-cell level (ref 7).
8691 co-detected gene-product pairs between ST (Visium) and SP; spatial transcript-protein Pearson correlation ranges from -0.47 to 0.62.

A 1 cm circular slice at 100 um resolution would require ~8000 samples and >8000-10,000 h of MS time; S4P offers 15-20x shorter MS time at 100 um and ~2x deeper coverage at ~500 um vs prior method (ref 18).

8101-8343, 8056-8386, 7591-8010 proteins across three area groups; median CV < 0.13 for same-axial strips; QC samples 7583-8502 proteins, median CV 0.16, Pearson > 0.93; >96% protein overlap across slices; per-slice 9107-9228 proteins.
Current LMD strip-width limit ~100 um; final profile averaged over eight 10-um slices spanning 80 um in z; prior methods ~2000 proteins at 100 um, 3000 at 50 um.

Cosine similarity for co-localized protein pairs: TUBA4A-TUBB4B 0.98, GABBR1-GABBR2 0.99, TRAPPC8-TRAPPC11 0.98, ATP6V1E1-ATP6V1F 0.97, DNM1-DNM2 0.98, SYN1-SYN2 0.94, CTNNB1-CDH2 0.93, NSF-NAPA 0.90; gridding vs S4P median CS 0.869.

Metadata unverified: received/accepted/published dates and volume/issue/pages not present in the cleaned MD; venue given as 'Cell Discovery, 2024' without volume/page.
