title: Spatial single-cell mass spectrometry defines zonation of the hepatocyte proteome
authors: Florian A. Rosenberger, Marvin Thielert, Maximilian T. Strauss, Lisa Schweizer, Constantin Ammar, Sophia C. Mädler, Andreas Metousis, Patricia Skowronek, Maria Wahle, Katherine Madden, Janine Gote-Schniering, Anna Semenova, Herbert B. Schiller, Edwin Rodriguez, Thierry M. Nordmann, Andreas Mund & Matthias Mann
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/19 - Spatial single-cell mass spectrometry defines zonation of the hepatocyte proteome_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors introduce single-cell Deep Visual Proteomics (scDVP), integrating high-content imaging, laser microdissection and multiplexed mass spectrometry to measure the spatial proteome of single murine hepatocytes within intact tissue at a depth of ~1,700 proteins per cell slice, showing that about half of the proteome is spatially regulated along the portal-to-central vein zonation axis, and training a machine-learning model to infer the spatial proteome from imaging data alone.
orientation: A methods paper that directly demonstrates feasibility of high-throughput spatial single-cell proteomics in intact tissue, so it is a strong support for Claim 6 (spatial proteomics feasible) and for the spatial axis and the proteome modality axis. It also feeds Claim 4 (modalities orthogonal): its cross-omics comparison shows low overall RNA-protein correlation and proteins regulated only in RNA or protein, sometimes inversely. It concedes the temporal dimension was not covered and that throughput/sensitivity are limiting, which bears on Claim 3 (temporal axis) and on the resolution-throughput trade-off (Claim 10). Note that this paper's spatial proteome is still NOT a true time course and relies on destructive measurement of fixed tissue, so several lines double as foils. Non-binding routing hint.

excerpts.text: Single-cell proteomics by mass spectrometry is emerging as a powerful and unbiased method for the characterization of biological heterogeneity. So far, it has been limited to cultured cells, whereas an expansion of the method to complex tissues would greatly enhance biological insights.
excerpts.note: States that single-cell MS proteomics had so far been limited to cultured cells, and that extending it to complex (spatially intact) tissues is the advance.

excerpts.text: scDVP resolves the context-dependent, spatial proteome of murine hepatocytes at a current depth of 1,700 proteins from a cell slice. Half of the proteome was diferentially regulated in a spatial manner, with protein levels changing dramatically in proximity to the central vein.
excerpts.note: Reports ~1,700 proteins per cell slice and that about half the proteome is spatially regulated along the zonation axis; keeps OCR quirk 'diferentially'.

excerpts.text: We applied machine learning to proteome classes and images, which subsequently inferred the spatial proteome from imaging data alone. scDVP is applicable to healthy and diseased tissues and complements other spatial proteomics and spatial omics technologies.
excerpts.note: States that a machine-learning model inferred the spatial proteome from imaging alone and that scDVP complements other spatial omics technologies.

excerpts.text: Mass spectrometry (MS)-based single-cell proteomics (scProteomics) has made tremendous progress within just a few years, and can now quantify more than 1,000 proteins in cultured cells<sup>1–3</sup>. While this trajectory is promising, proteome depth, throughput and lack of spatial context limit biological use.
excerpts.note: Concedes that proteome depth, throughput and lack of spatial context limit the biological use of single-cell proteomics.

excerpts.text: We have recently introduced deep visual proteomics (DVP), a spatial technology that combines imaging, cell segmentation, laser microdissection and MS into a single workflow to investigate complex tissues with various cell types and metabolic niches<sup>4</sup>.
excerpts.note: Describes DVP as a spatial proteomics workflow combining imaging, segmentation, laser microdissection and MS for complex tissues.

excerpts.text: DVP overcomes depth and throughput limitations with pooling the required number of cells with similar morphological features and staining patterns to identify statistically and analytically robust cellular phenotypes (‘biological fractionation’). By its nature, it depends on prior knowledge of adequate markers of the cells of interest that resolve their heterogeneity.
excerpts.note: Describes the trade-off that the prior DVP method depends on pooling cells and on prior marker knowledge to overcome depth and throughput limits.

excerpts.text: In this Article, we use scDVP to explore spatial characteristics of hepatocyte subsets in mammalian liver—a highly organized and functionally repetitive tissue, in which the proteome of hepatocytes is determined by paracrine signaling, as well as oxygen and nutrient gradients<sup>5</sup>. These metabolic gradients require distinct functional cell states along the portal vein (PV) to central vein (CV) axis.
excerpts.note: States that the hepatocyte proteome is determined by paracrine signaling and oxygen/nutrient gradients producing distinct functional states along the spatial PV-to-CV axis (spatial microenvironment shapes cell state).

excerpts.text: This phenomenon of liver zonation has been described by single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNAseq) for hepatocytes<sup>6,7</sup>, fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) and MS-based proteomics<sup>8</sup>, and multiplexed imaging<sup>9</sup>. Despite this long and varied background, the extent of spatial heterogeneity and proteome variation in hepatocyte remains an open question.
excerpts.note: Notes that zonation has been studied across modalities (scRNAseq, FACS proteomics, multiplexed imaging) yet the extent of proteome spatial variation remained open; keeps OCR singular 'hepatocyte'.

excerpts.text: This resulted in a doubling of identified proteins with a median number of 1,712 proteins across three biological replicates and 455 single shapes, at twice the previous throughput (Extended Data Fig. 3c). A maximum of 2,769 proteins were identified in one shape, and 3,738 unique proteins were found across all samples (Fig. 2a and Extended Data Fig. 3c).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Multiplex-DIA drastically increases proteome depth
excerpts.note: Quantifies proteome depth: median 1,712 proteins per single shape across 3 replicates / 455 shapes, max 2,769 in one shape, 3,738 unique proteins overall.

excerpts.text: On the basis of the distance ratio of PV and CV, we grouped the data into 20 spatial bins—approximately the maximum number of cells along the zonation axis. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) testing across all bins revealed that 49% of all proteins detected in at least half of the samples were significantly different between zones (false discovery rate (FDR) <0.05; Extended Data Fig. 6a and Supplementary Table S2).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Single-shape proteomes accurately reflect hepatocyte zonation
excerpts.note: Quantifies that 49% of detected proteins differ significantly between spatial zones (FDR<0.05), demonstrating extensive spatial regulation of the proteome.

excerpts.text: A cross-omics comparison with scRNAseq data<sup>6</sup> confirmed the directionality of the most prominent zonation markers (Pearson’s R = 0.97, Extended Data Fig. 7a,b), while correlation was low across all proteins and transcripts (Pearson’s R = 0.12).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Single-shape proteomes accurately reflect hepatocyte zonation
excerpts.note: Reports that, while marker directionality agrees between protein and RNA (R=0.97), the overall protein-transcript correlation is low (R=0.12), indicating RNA and protein carry largely non-redundant information.

excerpts.text: Notably, a number of proteins were regulated only in the RNA or protein dimension, or even inversely correlated (Extended Data Fig. 7c), such as dimethylglycine dehydrogenase in the choline catabolic pathway.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Single-shape proteomes accurately reflect hepatocyte zonation
excerpts.note: States that some proteins are spatially regulated only at the RNA or only at the protein level, or even inversely correlated, evidence that transcriptome cannot substitute for proteome.

excerpts.text: This underlines that the scDVP dataset provides orthogonal insight into liver physiology instead of merely complementing existing datasets.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Single-shape proteomes accurately reflect hepatocyte zonation
excerpts.note: Authors argue the spatial proteome dataset provides orthogonal (non-redundant) biological insight beyond existing RNA and FACS datasets.

excerpts.text: When we added subcellular annotations to our dataset, we found negligible differences to a bulk mouse liver proteome for many compartments including the plasma membrane (summed intensity of 10.6% in the library versus 10.7% in this scDVP data), highlighting that laser microdissection is suitable to excise the entire shape (Fig. 3g and Supplementary Table S6).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Single-shape proteomes accurately reflect hepatocyte zonation
excerpts.note: Validates that scDVP captures subcellular compartment proportions comparable to a bulk liver proteome (e.g. plasma membrane 10.6% vs 10.7%), supporting accuracy of the single-cell spatial measurement.

excerpts.text: Here, we present a single-cell spatial map of the murine liver acquired by MS-based proteomics. Our approach successfully combines microscopic imaging data with ultra-high-sensitivity proteomics, building on four major technological advances: (1) artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted segmentation and laser microdissection, (2) multiplex-DIA (mDIA), (3) low-flow gradients and (4) the ultra-high sensitivity of a timsTOF SCP mass spectrometer.
excerpts.note: Summarizes the contribution as a single-cell spatial proteome map of murine liver built on four named technological advances.

excerpts.text: To date, MS-based scProteomics has been exclusively reported for cell suspensions. State-of-the-art workflows currently reach a proteomic depth of up to 2,000 proteins in cultured cells, with about 250 pg of cellular protein mass.
excerpts.note: Concedes that MS single-cell proteomics had been exclusively done on dissociated cell suspensions (no spatial context), reaching ~2,000 proteins from ~250 pg in cultured cells.

excerpts.text: With our scDVP workflow, we achieved more than 1,700 proteins per single shape (and up to 2,700) despite working from sections that were fixed, stained, imaged and laser dissected. Laser microdissection successfully separated hepatocytes from surrounding material including blood remnants, which holds promise for smaller cell types in more complex tissue environments.
excerpts.note: States >1,700 (up to 2,700) proteins per single shape were achieved from fixed, stained, imaged and dissected tissue, with clean separation from blood remnants.

excerpts.text: The size of our shapes correlated strongly with the number of identified proteins, suggesting that scDVP is currently limited by MS sensitivity and will thus profit from continuous technical developments.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: scDVP proteome depth scales with cell/shape size, so the method is currently limited by MS sensitivity (depth-vs-input trade-off).

excerpts.text: Given the more stable core proteome compared with single-cell transcriptomes<sup>2</sup> and the resulting lower required sample number, scDVP experiments encompassing a few hundred single shapes could be done in just a few days.
excerpts.note: Claims the single-cell proteome core is more stable than the single-cell transcriptome, requiring fewer samples, a property differentiating the protein modality.

excerpts.text: More than half of quantified proteins were significantly different between portal and central zones, in line with scRNAseq data<sup>6,16</sup> and FACS-based proteomics data<sup>8</sup>.
excerpts.note: Reiterates that more than half of quantified proteins differ between portal and central spatial zones, consistent with prior RNA and FACS proteomics.

excerpts.text: A rhythmic expression pattern has been previously shown for a large number of liver transcripts and proteins<sup>16,19</sup>. While we have not covered the temporal aspect here, the scDVP approach could contribute to such studies by adding a spatial dimension.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded scope limit: the study did not cover the temporal/time-course dimension, only the spatial one — a direct foil on the temporal axis.

excerpts.text: By analyzing single cellular shapes without prior assumptions, scDVP now removes the dependency on established markers or features. This makes it a promising approach in heterogeneous tissues with partially or not defined subtypes of cells, such as in many tumor tissues.
excerpts.note: States scDVP removes the dependency on prior markers that constrained pooled DVP, enabling unbiased spatial proteome mapping in heterogeneous tissues.

excerpts.text: Moreover, scDVP can be a method of choice to map proteomic disturbances along gradients of, for instance, signaling factors, nutrients or gases, and in physiological settings that may create impediments for other omics methods, for instance, in extracellular fibrotic scars.
excerpts.note: Proposes scDVP for mapping proteome changes along microenvironmental gradients (signaling factors, nutrients, gases), linking spatial microenvironment to cell state.

excerpts.text: Our results demonstrate that the central challenge of scDVP is the sensitivity of the overall workflow. Although we have here reduced the area required for laser microdissection by 100-fold compared with our initial DVP report<sup>4</sup>, we note that one excised hepatocyte shape contains approximately ten times more protein than the smallest cells of interest, such as typical resting lymphocytes<sup>20</sup>.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: the central challenge is workflow sensitivity; an excised hepatocyte still contains ~10x more protein than the smallest target cells (e.g. resting lymphocytes), bounding applicability.

excerpts.text: There have been advances in the quantification of posttranslational modifications from ultra-low-input material, such as from 1 µg down to the material corresponding to single cell, for instance in the enrichment protocol µPhos<sup>22</sup>. In combination with scDVP, this holds promise for single cells, although the biological information in single-cell phosphoproteomics data would currently be limited to a few high-abundance proteins with high modification stoichiometries.
excerpts.note: Notes that single-cell PTM/phosphoproteomics is only beginning to be feasible and is currently limited to a few high-abundance proteins, indicating the PTM modality remains scarce at single-cell spatial resolution.

excerpts.text: Subtle signaling events, such as the liver-dominant Wnt signaling, will require additional technological developments for in-depth biological description of signaling in single cells by MS.
excerpts.note: Concedes that capturing subtle signaling-network events at single-cell resolution by MS still requires further technological development.

excerpts.text: We have shown that single-cell data can be used to train an accurate ML model that predicts the proteome class from visual information only.
excerpts.note: States that a machine-learning model trained on single-cell data can predict proteome class from imaging features alone, integrating image and proteome modalities.

excerpts.text: The modular nature of scDVP, especially the open format from laser microdissection to 384-well plates for sample preparation, makes it widely applicable and also compatible with other spatial omics technologies such as spatial transcriptomics, epigenomics<sup>25</sup> or multiplexed imaging.
excerpts.note: States scDVP is compatible/integrable with other spatial omics modalities (spatial transcriptomics, epigenomics, multiplexed imaging).

excerpts.text: This model reached an average precision of 0.94 (Extended Data Fig. 9c,d), correctly assigning the proteome class of almost all cells. Errors occurred exclusively between spatially neighboring classes (Fig. 4c).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Spatial context regulates single-cell proteomes
excerpts.note: Quantifies that the imaging-to-proteome-class ML model reached average precision 0.94, with errors only between spatially adjacent classes.

excerpts.text: We used the class probabilities as weights to predict the spatial proteome, which accurately approximated overall protein intensities (R = 0.78 between prediction and measurement, Fig. 4e).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Spatial context regulates single-cell proteomes
excerpts.note: Reports R=0.78 correlation between the imaging-predicted spatial proteome and the measured proteome on a held-out section.

figuresNumbers.contains: Median 1,712 proteins per single shape across 3 biological replicates and 455 single shapes; maximum 2,769 proteins in one shape; 3,738 unique proteins across all samples; five-shape protocol reached median 1,235 proteins across 230 samples.

49% of proteins detected in at least half of samples were significantly different between zones (FDR<0.05); only 5.8% expressed equally in all zones (Shapiro-Wilk P>0.05); 'more than half' of quantified proteins differ between portal and central zones.
Marker directionality vs scRNAseq R=0.97; all-protein-vs-transcript R=0.12; vs FACS-based proteome marker R=0.16 (better overall overlap than R=0.12).
Plasma membrane summed intensity 10.6% (library) vs 10.7% (scDVP); five OXPHOS complexes decreased collectively by >25% from PV to CV.

Average precision 0.94; RandomForest (n_estimators=200) testing accuracy 0.90; 17 features / k-means into 5 classes; predicted-vs-measured spatial proteome R=0.78 on held-out section of 60 single-shape proteomes.

~250 pg protein input per shape (≈ one HeLa cell); 10 µm sections; hepatocyte size 20–30 µm; mean shape size 600 µm2; 10 ng reference proteome co-injected; ~99% dimethyl labeling efficiency; current effective two-plex at 40 samples/day, scalable to effective four-plex at 80 samples/day (320 shapes/day).

flags: Metadata: venue inferred as Nature Methods from DOI prefix s41592 and the peer-review line ('Nature Methods thanks Albert Heck, Shalev Itzkovitz, and Sinem Saka'); issue/volume/page numbers not present in the cleaned text, so 'venue' gives name and year only.
Dates from header: Received 9 December 2022; Accepted 15 August 2023; Published online 2 October 2023.
OCR quirks preserved verbatim in excerpts (e.g. 'diferentially' in e2, 'afiliations', singular 'hepatocyte' in e8); not corrected.
Scope note: this is a methods/technology paper demonstrating spatial single-cell proteomics; it is itself a primary source for Claim 6, so most quantitative claims are first-hand, not cited.

Scope note: the study explicitly did NOT cover the temporal dimension (e21) and works on fixed/destroyed tissue, so its spatial proteome is a snapshot, not a time course - relevant as a foil for the temporal axis (Claim 3).

title: Spatial multi-omics: novel tools to study the complexity of cardiovascular diseases
authors: Paul Kiessling, Christoph Kuppe
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/20 - Spatial multi-omics - novel tools to study complexity of cardiovascular diseases_cleaned.md
oneSentence: A review of spatial multi-omics technologies and computational methods (NGS-, imaging-, and MS-based) for jointly profiling transcriptome, epigenome, proteome, and metabolome in tissue, with a focus on applications and challenges in cardiovascular disease.
orientation: A methods/technology review squarely on the spatial axis (Axis C) plus multi-modality (Axis A) and integration/standardization (standard axis). It strongly supports our claims that dissociated single-cell data loses spatial information (Claim 2), that modalities carry non-substitutable information (Claim 4), that spatial proteomics/metabolomics are feasible and advancing (Claim 6), that microenvironment/neighborhood shapes cell state (Claim 7), that multi-omic integration is needed yet hard (Claim 8), and that the field lacks standards/benchmarks and suffers data-sharing/silo problems (Claim 9), plus resolution-throughput-cost trade-offs (Claim 10). It can also serve as a FOIL: it presents these spatial tools as already-maturing solutions and explicitly concedes severe remaining limitations (no peer review of integration tools, no benchmarks, integration ground-truth correlation < 0.5, temporal/time-course largely absent), which a writer can deploy as the very gaps our standard must close.

excerpts.text: While single-cell and single-nuclei studies of the human heart in health [11, 12] and disease [4, 13–15] have led to valuable insights, spatial information was lacking.
excerpts.note: States that single-cell/single-nuclei studies of the human heart lacked spatial information.

excerpts.text: However, it has become clear that for a comprehensive understanding of intrinsic and extrinsic factors which control cell fate decisions in tissues, it is crucial to consider and include spatial molecular information.
excerpts.note: Argues spatial molecular information is essential to understand factors controlling cell fate in tissues.

excerpts.text: Spatial biology enables researchers to observe and decode these complex patterns and communications of cells within their native tissue environments.
excerpts.note: Frames spatial biology as a way to observe cell communication within native tissue environments.

excerpts.text: These technologies aim to close the gaps inherent to dissociated single-cell data providing information on the colocalization of cell types and cell states, spatial covariance of gene expression changes, and defning cellular tissue niches and how they change in disease (Fig. 1).
excerpts.note: States spatial technologies close gaps inherent to dissociated single-cell data, recovering colocalization and tissue niche information.

excerpts.text: Spatial resolution was crucial for these fndings, as myocarditis shows distinct zonation and border zones with unique infammatory signatures that could potentially be missed in single-cell RNA sequencing studies of dissociated tissue.
excerpts.note: Gives a concrete case where spatially zonated inflammatory signatures would be missed by dissociated scRNA-seq.

excerpts.text: Based on transcriptomic data alone, the diferentiation of naïve or mature immune cells is generally challenging, but additional protein markers such as CD45RO/ CD45RA commonly used in FACS can signifcantly aid the identifcation of the correct immune cell state.
excerpts.note: States transcriptome alone is insufficient to distinguish immune cell states; protein markers add non-substitutable information.

excerpts.text: While transcriptome information is widely used to model protein expression dynamics, the genome-wide correlation between mRNA and protein is estimated to be only around 40% [60, 61].
excerpts.note: Quantifies genome-wide mRNA-protein correlation at ~40%, arguing transcriptome cannot stand in for proteome.

excerpts.text: Furthermore, the transcriptome alone cannot provide information about processes induced by posttranslational protein modifcations, which can have important efects on cell biology.
excerpts.note: States the transcriptome cannot capture PTM-induced processes, supporting modality non-substitutability.

excerpts.text: Compared to the transcripts, resolving the proteome at single-cell resolution or in tissues is much more challenging. Cells typically contain 30,000× more protein molecules than mRNA molecules [67], and proteins are very heterogeneous in size and structure and, unlike nucleic acids, cannot be amplifed. Tis has limited the multiplexing and throughput of spatial proteome measurements.
excerpts.note: Explains why spatial proteomics is harder than transcriptomics (30,000x more protein molecules, no amplification), a concession that limits proteome throughput.

excerpts.text: Nevertheless, enormous progress has been made in generating multiplex proteomics datasets from tissues.
excerpts.note: Asserts substantial recent progress in multiplex tissue proteomics.

excerpts.text: Another approach to enable unbiased singlecell or spatial proteomics is based on highly sensitive LC-MS-based proteomics (reviewed here [67, 74–76]), which has been adapted to spatial proteomics as deep visual proteomics (DVP) [77, 78]. DVP combines laser-capture microdissection of distinct cell types from tissue and performs MS proteomics of the collected tissues extending bulk proteomics of the human heart [79] with cell type and spatially resolved information in the near future.
excerpts.note: Describes DVP as an unbiased MS-based spatial proteomics method extending bulk human-heart proteomics to cell-type and spatial resolution.

excerpts.text: Spatial metabolomics has reached 5–10 μm resolution and has been recently applied to study cell-type-specifc dynamics of metabolism in kidney repair [82].
excerpts.note: States spatial metabolomics reached 5-10 um resolution and resolved cell-type-specific metabolic dynamics.

excerpts.text: MALDI-MSI-based metabolomics combined with 13C-labeled nutrients allowed the authors to study the dynamics of metabolic changes at subcellular resolution. Tey subsequently used multiplexed immunofuorescence microscopy to identify cell types which seemed unnecessary, as cell types could be diferentiated just based on the lipid profles.
excerpts.note: Reports cell types could be distinguished from lipid/metabolite profiles alone, showing metabolomics carries cell-identity information.

excerpts.text: We discuss the advancements in lowresolution and high-resolution spatial multi-omics methods which can resolve up to 10,000 of individual molecules at subcellular level.
excerpts.note: States spatial multi-omics methods can resolve up to 10,000 individual molecules at subcellular level.

excerpts.text: In ISS, mRNA is labeled with specifc nucleotide sequences called padlock probes [91], which are then sequenced with rolling circle amplifcation to increase the specifcity of fuorophore binding (see Table 1). HybISS [92], or the commercialized version by 10× Genomics (Xenium), enables the detection of 100–1000 targets at subcellular resolution, including the detection of mutations.
excerpts.anchor: Spatial multi-omics technologies at single-molecular resolution
excerpts.note: Notes ISS/HybISS reach subcellular resolution but detect only 100-1000 targets, a sensitivity-versus-breadth trade-off.

excerpts.text: In general, both ISS- and FISH-based methods require the selection of a limited number of targets to form a probe panel a priori.
excerpts.anchor: Spatial multi-omics technologies at single-molecular resolution
excerpts.note: States targeted imaging methods require choosing a limited a priori probe panel, a coverage trade-off.

excerpts.text: Unbiased ST methods at subcellular resolution have been developed recently, such as Stereo-Seq [101] and SeqScope [101]. While these approaches allow subcellular resolution transcriptomics (e.g., 220-nm diameter size for Stereo-Seq), assignment of signals to specifc cells is potentially more challenging, as cell boundary stains are not available and difusion dynamics might afect the measurement.
excerpts.anchor: Spatial multi-omics technologies at single-molecular resolution
excerpts.note: Notes unbiased subcellular ST (220 nm) trades resolution against difficulty of cell assignment and diffusion artifacts.

excerpts.text: To unlock the synergy of multi-omic measurements, integrating the datasets with each other is necessary. Tis is a nontrivial task as the data generated by diferent modalities exist in entirely diferent feature spaces.
excerpts.note: States multi-omic integration is necessary but nontrivial because modalities live in entirely different feature spaces.

excerpts.text: Argelaguet et al. [125] proposed three categories of integration tasks: Horizontal integration is used when the same modality is measured across diferent samples. An example could be the measurement of the spatial transcriptome across multiple myocardial infarction samples. Vertical integration describes the integration of measurements in the same cells, such as co-profling of the epigenome and transcriptome in DBiT-seq. Te most challenging case is diagonal integration. Here, neither the cells nor features are shared across datasets, and integration instead relies on a strong biological signal that can be captured with diferent modalities independently from each other.
excerpts.note: Lays out the horizontal/vertical/diagonal integration taxonomy, naming diagonal (no shared cells or features) as most challenging.

excerpts.text: In general, algorithms developed for single-cell experiments like GLUE [136], Seurat WNN [137], or totalVI [138] could be applied to spatial experiments; however, this would dissociate cells from their spatial context and ignores the cellular neighborhood, which is highly informative for integration tasks.
excerpts.note: Warns that applying single-cell integration algorithms dissociates cells from spatial context and ignores the informative cellular neighborhood.

excerpts.text: While these tools show promise, it is important to note that as of the time of this article’s publication, several tools have not undergone peer review. An independent benchmark of spatial-omics integration algorithms is urgently needed and would help scientists in their choice of integration strategy and inform their experimental designs.
excerpts.note: Concedes many integration tools are unbenchmarked/not peer-reviewed and calls an independent benchmark urgently needed.

excerpts.text: Cell typing is much improved when information about both transcriptome and proteome is available, as cells like NK-cells, which play a major role in infammatory heart disease [142, 143], are notoriously hard to classify based solely on their low transcript counts.
excerpts.note: Gives NK-cells as an example where transcriptome is insufficient and adding proteome improves cell typing.

excerpts.text: It is necessary to scrutinize integrated datasets for biological plausibility, especially as a recent review of transcriptomics integration methods found that no tool reached a Pearson correlation coefcient of more than 0.5 compared to ground truth [177]. Multi-omic integration is likely even more challenging as the link between datasets is weaker.
excerpts.note: Reports that no transcriptomics integration tool exceeded Pearson 0.5 vs ground truth, and multi-omic integration is likely harder.

excerpts.text: At the moment, the parallel measurement of multiple modalities of the same cells in a “true” multi-omic experiment is very limited, and researchers instead rely on algorithms that diagonally integrate separate measurements or impute missing data.
excerpts.note: Concedes true same-cell multi-modal measurement is very limited, forcing reliance on diagonal integration or imputation.

excerpts.text: One possible way forward might be the creation of foundation models for spatial multi-omics, analogues to eforts in natural language processing [178], and single-cell transcriptomics [179–181]. Tese models, trained on a large corpus of spatial and single-cell multiomic datasets, might be able to deconvolute underlying patterns necessary for successful integrations.
excerpts.note: Proposes foundation models trained on large spatial/single-cell multi-omic corpora as a route to better integration.

excerpts.text: As with single-cell RNA sequencing, standardized sample preparation is key for successful experiments and high data quality. Especially for the handling of tissues, standardized protocols need to be followed for tissue sampling, fxation, freezing, and tissue sectioning.
excerpts.note: Stresses that standardized sample preparation and protocols are key for data quality.

excerpts.text: A guide on how diferent tissues should be handled for each spatial multi-omics technology is currently lacking but would greatly increase reproducibility in the feld.
excerpts.note: Concedes a tissue-handling guide per technology is currently lacking, hurting reproducibility.

excerpts.text: Despite nontrivial challenges, such as the need for standardization of experimental setups, data analysis, and improved computational tools, the application of spatial multi-omics holds tremendous potential in revolutionizing our understanding of human disease processes and the identifcation of novel biomarkers and therapeutic targets.
excerpts.note: Names standardization of experimental setups and data analysis as a key outstanding challenge for the field.

excerpts.text: Cell segmentation remains challenging for all imagingbased approaches, as discussed above. It is often unclear which algorithm produces the best result, as the generation of ground truth for performance evaluation relies on tedious manual annotation of the target tissue. Furthermore, the performance of segmentation algorithms is highly tissue and disease-state dependent.
excerpts.note: Concedes cell segmentation remains unsolved, lacks ground truth for benchmarking, and is tissue/disease-state dependent.

excerpts.text: Several other technologies are available: CosMx [100] (NanoString), and Molecular Cartography (Resolve Biosciences). However, an independent benchmark that compares sensitivity, specifcity, and other performance metrics has not been carried out.
excerpts.anchor: Spatial multi-omics technologies at single-molecular resolution
excerpts.note: Notes no independent benchmark exists comparing sensitivity/specificity across competing imaging platforms.

excerpts.text: It remains to be seen which approach yields the most biologically relevant information, as no comparative benchmarking has been published so far.
excerpts.note: States no comparative benchmarking exists for spatial cell-cell-communication algorithms.

excerpts.text: Lastly, spatial multi-omics experiments are challenging just based on the amount of data generated. An imaging-based spatial transcriptomics measurement can generate around 5 TB of raw data (e.g., MERFISH, own experience), necessitating a robust data storage strategy to which many academic laboratories might not have access.
excerpts.note: Quantifies the data burden (~5 TB raw per MERFISH experiment) as a practical barrier.

excerpts.text: Multiple platforms have been established to facilitate the sharing of data and easy inbrowser viewing of datasets [182–187]. However, these repositories remain underused, with every website only containing a small subset of published data. Ideally, scientifc journals would require sharing of data in a digestible and convenient fashion as ofered by these data stores.
excerpts.note: Reports data-sharing repositories are underused and each holds only a small subset of published data, a data-silo problem.

excerpts.text: Ecosystems that process spatial multi-omics data in a standardized way are just beginning to emerge. Of note are SpatialData [121], SpatialExperiment [122], GiottoData [123], and SeuratObject [124], which ofer data containers that can store the diverse data generated by spatial-omics experiments. Tis infrastructure is critical to unify the heterogeneous data from diferent technology-specifc vendor formats and the various images, tables, and polygons that a single experiment can generate and makes the development of analysis algorithms that “plug-and-play” possible.
excerpts.note: States standardized data ecosystems are only just emerging and are needed to unify heterogeneous vendor-specific formats.

excerpts.text: Experiments can in general be divided into exploratory investigations that aim to generate hypotheses and confrmatory experiments that aim to prove a well-designed research question. Generally speaking, methods with a larger feature scale such as NGS-based multi-omics and microdissection MS lend themselves well to an exploratory setting, while more targeted approaches like imaging-based multi-omics and ion-label MS shine in confrming well-defned research questions.
excerpts.anchor: Design considerations for spatial multi-omic experiments
excerpts.note: Maps the breadth-versus-targeting trade-off: large feature-scale methods for exploration, targeted methods for confirmation.

excerpts.text: As an example, rare cells, like neuronal cells or pericytes, would best be studied with a high-resolution technique, as detection might not be possible in lower resolution measurements which confate multiple cells into spots. Te analysis of lowly expressed genes like transcription factors is best served by approaches with maximal sensitivity, such as ISS or ISH.
excerpts.anchor: Design considerations for spatial multi-omic experiments
excerpts.note: Notes low-resolution spot methods conflate multiple cells, illustrating a resolution-versus-coverage trade-off.

excerpts.text: With the introduction of spatial omics with subcellular resolution, this search for patterns is not limited to a macroscopic view of tissue composition but can also be extended to structures inside cells. Te cellular location of proteins is often disturbed in disease like in familial atrial fbrillation, for example, which is caused by a mutation that impedes HSP70 import [153].
excerpts.note: Gives subcellular protein localization (HSP70 import defect in familial atrial fibrillation) as disease-relevant spatial information.

excerpts.text: Cell morphology is another resource that remains underutilized in the analysis of spatial datasets. Cardiomyocytes and fibroblasts are known to change their cellular phenotype and morphology in response to stress, such as hypertrophy, elongation, or thickening [117]. These features offer valuable insight into cell state that could be integrated with existing omics measurements.
excerpts.note: Argues cell morphology is an underused modality carrying cell-state information that should be integrated with omics.

excerpts.text: In MI, ANKRD1 and NPPB show a gradient across the border zone of the infarct [4], and the epicardium has been shown to contain distinct niches of plasma B cells [22].
excerpts.note: Gives spatial gene-expression gradients and niche localization in myocardial infarction as microenvironment-dependent signal.

excerpts.text: Tis is however problematic, as most forms of cell-cell communication are short ranged and very much dependent on the spatial tissue organization.
excerpts.note: States cell-cell communication is short-ranged and spatially dependent, so dissociated data is inadequate for it.

excerpts.text: This task is comparatively easier, as the concordance between spatial RNA measurements and single-cell RNA seems to mostly hold across genes [126, 127].
excerpts.note: Notes spatial-RNA to scRNA imputation is comparatively easy because RNA concordance mostly holds across genes (same-modality case).

figuresNumbers.contains: Genome-wide correlation between mRNA and protein estimated at only ~40% (cited refs [60, 61] in this paper).
Cells typically contain 30,000x more protein molecules than mRNA molecules [67]; proteins are heterogeneous in size and cannot be amplified.
A recent review of transcriptomics integration methods found no tool reached Pearson correlation > 0.5 vs ground truth [177].

Resolution ranges: NGS non-deterministic barcoding 1-55 um (Stereo-seq 200 nm); deterministic barcoding (DBiT-Seq) 10 um; multiplex ISH non-diffraction-limited sub-5 nm; ion-labelled antibodies 100 nm-1 um; DVP microdissection 5-10 um (cellular level).
Feature scale per method: NGS RNA 10,000s / protein 100s; ISS 1000s; cyclic IF 1-200; ion-labelled antibodies 1-50; microdissection (DVP) unlimited.
Spatial metabolomics has reached 5-10 um resolution; applied to cell-type-specific metabolism in kidney repair [82].
An imaging-based spatial transcriptomics measurement (e.g., MERFISH) can generate around 5 TB of raw data.
HybISS / Xenium enables detection of 100-1000 targets at subcellular resolution.
Measurement area ranges from mm^2 up to 13.2 cm x 13.2 cm for Stereo-seq.
Visium resolution limited to 55-um diameter spots in a hexagonal array with 100-um distance between spot centers; HDST 2-um resolution; Slide-SeqV2 near-cellular.
SM-Omics scales up to nearly 96 libraries in a little over 2 days, at a resolution of only 100 um.
Single-cell assays scaled from 1 cell [27] to 100,000s with ultrahigh throughput [28, 29] within 10 years.

primarySources.citation: Kuppe C, Ramirez Flores RO, Li Z, Hayat S, Levinson RT, Liao X, et al. Spatial multi-omic map of human myocardial infarction. Nature. 2022;608:766-77.

primarySources.citation: Liu Y, Yang M, Deng Y, et al. High-spatial-resolution multi-omics sequencing via deterministic barcoding in tissue. Cell. 2020;183:1665-81.e18.

primarySources.citation: Mund A, Coscia F, Kriston A, et al. Deep visual proteomics defines single-cell identity and heterogeneity. Nat Biotechnol. 2022;40:1231-40.

title: Optimal-Transport Analysis of Single-Cell Gene Expression Identifies Developmental Trajectories in Reprogramming
authors: Geoffrey Schiebinger, Jian Shu, Marcin Tabaka, Brian Cleary, Vidya Subramanian, Aryeh Solomon, Joshua Gould, Siyan Liu, Stacie Lin, Peter Berube, Lia Lee, Jenny Chen, Justin Brumbaugh, Philippe Rigollet, Konrad Hochedlinger, Rudolf Jaenisch, Aviv Regev, Eric S. Lander
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/21 - Waddington-OT - Optimal-Transport Analysis Developmental Trajectories in Reprogramming_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The paper introduces Waddington-OT, an optimal-transport method that infers ancestor-descendant couplings and developmental trajectories from a dense time course of 315,000 scRNA-seq profiles of iPSC reprogramming, and uses it to predict and experimentally validate that the TF Obox6 and cytokine GDF9 enhance reprogramming efficiency.
orientation: Primarily feeds Claim 3 (destructive sequencing cannot pair-observe the same cell over time, so trajectories must be inferred computationally from distributions): the paper states this constraint explicitly and is itself a canonical example of the trajectory-inference/OT class the context file names. It also touches Claim 10 (resolution x throughput x destructiveness trade-off) by motivating large dense time courses and modeling growth/death, and Claim 1 (data-driven trajectory methods generalize poorly) via its benchmark showing most existing methods fail on time-course data. It can serve as BOTH support (concedes the destructive-measurement / no-paired-observation fact our thesis builds on) and as a foil (it is a model/method advance that distribution-level inference can partly compensate, which our thesis frames as not equivalent to true temporal tracking).

excerpts.text: Because scRNA-seq destroys cells in the course of recording their profiles, one cannot follow expression of the same cell and its direct descendants across time. While various approaches can record information about cell lineage, they currently provide only very limited information about a cell’s state at earlier time points (Kester and van Oudenaarden, 2018).
excerpts.note: States that scRNA-seq is destructive so the same cell cannot be followed over time, and that lineage methods give only very limited information about a cell's earlier state.

excerpts.text: Comprehensive studies of cell trajectories thus rely heavily on computational approaches to connect discrete ‘‘snapshots’’ into continuous ‘‘movies.’’
excerpts.note: Because cells cannot be tracked directly, temporal information must be reconstructed computationally from independent snapshots.

excerpts.text: First, with few exceptions, most methods do not explicitly leverage temporal information (Table S6). Historically, most were designed to extract information about stationary processes, such as adult stem cell differentiation, in which all stages exist simultaneously. However, time courses are becoming commonplace.
excerpts.note: Notes that most existing trajectory-inference methods ignore explicit temporal information and were built for stationary processes.

excerpts.text: Third, few methods account for cellular growth and death during development (Table S6).
excerpts.note: Identifies a gap that few trajectory methods model cell growth and death over time.

excerpts.text: Here, we describe a conceptual framework, implemented in a method called Waddington-OT, that aims to capture the notion that cells at any time are drawn from a probability distribution in gene-expression space, and each cell has a distribution of both probable origins and probable fates (Figure 1). It uses scRNA-seq data collected across a time course to infer how these probability distributions evolve over time, by using the mathematical approach of optimal transport (OT).
excerpts.note: Describes Waddington-OT, which infers each cell's distribution of probable origins and fates from time-course snapshots rather than from direct tracking.

excerpts.text: By sampling this probability distribution P<sub>t</sub> at various time points t, we wish to infer how the differentiation process evolves over time (Figure 1A). From a large number of cells at a given time point (Figure 1B), we can approximate the distribution at that time point, but, because different cells are sampled independently at different time points, we lose the joint distribution of expression between pairs of time points, called temporal coupling.
excerpts.anchor: Results: Reconstruction of Probabilistic Trajectories by Optimal Transport
excerpts.note: Because cells are sampled independently at each time point, the joint distribution (temporal coupling) between time points is lost and must be inferred.

excerpts.text: Absent any constraint on cellular transitions, we cannot infer the temporal coupling, but if we assume that cells move short distances over short time periods, then we can infer the temporal coupling by using the mathematical technique of optimal transport (Figure 1A; Methods S1).
excerpts.anchor: Results: Reconstruction of Probabilistic Trajectories by Optimal Transport
excerpts.note: Temporal coupling is unidentifiable without assumptions; OT recovers it only under an assumption that cells move short distances over short times.

excerpts.text: The optimal-transport calculation (1) implicitly assumes that a cell’s fate depends on its current position but not on its previous history (i.e., the stochastic process is Markov), and (2) captures only the timevarying components of the distribution (see Discussion).
excerpts.anchor: Results: Reconstruction of Probabilistic Trajectories by Optimal Transport
excerpts.note: Author-conceded modeling assumptions: the inferred trajectories assume a memoryless (Markov) process and capture only time-varying components.

excerpts.text: Because current experimental approaches for tracing cell lineage do not describe the transcriptional profile of a cell set’s ancestors, we developed a computational approach to validate the model.
excerpts.anchor: Results: The Model Is Predictive and Robust
excerpts.note: Concedes that current lineage-tracing experiments do not recover the transcriptional profile of ancestor cells, so validation must itself be computational.

excerpts.text: Optimal-transport analysis is only intended to capture the time-varying components of a distribution $\mathbb { P } _ { t } .$ For systems in dynamic equilibrium, $\mathbb { P } _ { t }$ does not change over time and optimal transport would infer that each cell is stationary. (An example would be cells that are asynchronously undergoing cell division. Although each cell is changing, the overall distribution $\mathbb { P } _ { t }$ is constant across time.)
excerpts.anchor: Discussion: An Optimal Transport Framework to Model Cell Differentiation
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: distribution-level inference cannot detect change in cells whose population distribution is at equilibrium even though individual cells change.

excerpts.text: To set Waddington-OT in context, we comprehensively reviewed 62 other approaches (Table S6), which fall into three classes: category 1 (33 tools) is not applicable to developmental time courses with scRNA-seq, category 2 (25 tools) is applicable but does not incorporate time information, and category 3 (4 tools) leverages time information, but does not model cell growth rates over time.
excerpts.anchor: Discussion: An Optimal Transport Framework to Model Cell Differentiation
excerpts.note: Of 62 reviewed trajectory methods, none both incorporate time and model cell growth; most ignore temporal information entirely.

excerpts.text: Category 2 methods produced trajectories that are completely inconsistent with the time course—making huge leaps across time points and, in some cases, going backward in time. For example, Monocle2 produced trajectories in which day 0 cells give rise to day 18 cells, which then give rise to day 8 cells.
excerpts.anchor: Discussion: An Optimal Transport Framework to Model Cell Differentiation
excerpts.note: Benchmark shows widely used trajectory methods that ignore time produce trajectories inconsistent with the actual time course, even reversing temporal order.

excerpts.text: Category 3 methods encounter a distinct challenge, as they do not account for the higher growth of iPSCs and consequently infer that many apoptotic stromal cells must transition to iPSCs.
excerpts.anchor: Discussion: An Optimal Transport Framework to Model Cell Differentiation
excerpts.note: Methods that ignore differential cell growth and death infer biologically impossible transitions (apoptotic stromal cells becoming iPSCs).

excerpts.text: Waddington-OT is the only approach that incorporates temporal information and models cell growth over time (that we can consider a new category 4). It is the only approach that produced reasonable trajectories on our data, suggesting that these features are critical for robust analysis of developmental processes.
excerpts.anchor: Discussion: An Optimal Transport Framework to Model Cell Differentiation
excerpts.note: Claims Waddington-OT alone produced reasonable trajectories, arguing that using time and modeling growth are critical for robust trajectory analysis.

excerpts.text: Third, various methods exist for obtaining lineage information about cells, based on the introduction of barcodes at discrete time points or continuously (Kester and van Oudenaarden, 2018). Barcodes can be used to recognize cells that descend from a recent common ancestor cell but do not currently directly reveal the full gene-expression state of the ancestral cell.
excerpts.anchor: Discussion: Future Prospects for Models and Studies of Differentiation and Development
excerpts.note: Concedes that lineage barcoding identifies common descent but does not recover the full gene-expression state of the ancestral cell.

excerpts.text: We model a differentiating population of cells as a time-varying probability distribution (i.e., stochastic process) on a high-dimensional expression space.
excerpts.anchor: Results: Reconstruction of Probabilistic Trajectories by Optimal Transport
excerpts.note: Frames the supervision/inference signal as distribution-to-distribution over a population rather than paired single-cell observation.

figuresNumbers.contains: 315,000 (>315,000) single-cell RNA-seq profiles total; experiment 1: 65,781 cells over 10 time points across 16 days, sampled every 48 h; experiment 2: 259,155 cells over 39 time points across 18 days, sampled every 12 h (every 6 h between days 8 and 9); 251,203 high-quality cells retained from experiment 2.

62 reviewed trajectory-inference approaches: category 1 = 33 tools (not applicable), category 2 = 25 tools (applicable, no time), category 3 = 4 tools (time, but no growth modeling); Waddington-OT proposed as a new category 4.
iPSC trajectory encompasses 40% of cells at day 8.5, but only 10% at day 10 (2i) and 1% at day 11 (serum); pluripotency signature in 80%-90% of cells days 15-18 in 2i vs peak 10%-15% in serum.

Obox6 and Zfp42 increased reprogramming efficiency ~2-fold in 2i (more in serum); GDF9 increased efficiency ~4- to 5-fold at highest dose, dose-dependent, confirmed by Oct4-GFP colony counts, bulk RNA-seq, and scRNA-seq.

Whole-chromosome aneuploidy in 4.0% of trophoblast and 2.1% of stromal cells vs 1.1% of others; sub-chromosomal events in 6.9% of trophoblasts and 3.2% of stromal cells vs 1.2% others and 0.4% neural.

title: Evolving strategies for lineage tracing: Genetic markers, synthetic barcodes, and natural variants
authors: Z.K., H.C., S.L., S.-W.W., B.Z. (full names not printed in cleaned source; author initials from AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS; B.Z. is a member of the Cell Stem Cell Advisory Board)
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/22 - Evolving strategies for lineage tracing - genetic markers synthetic barcodes and natural variants_cleaned.md
oneSentence: A review synthesizing three methodological pillars of lineage tracing (prospective genetic markers, high-throughput synthetic DNA barcodes, and retrospective endogenous natural variants), their integration with single-cell multi-omics and spatial profiling, and the computational tools used to link lineage history to cell state and decode cell fate.
orientation: A methods review centered on reconstructing temporal lineage history and integrating it with cell state and spatial context. It feeds the temporal axis (Claim 3) most strongly: it details how destructive single-cell sequencing precludes paired before/after observation, forcing reliance on barcodes/variants to retrospectively reconstruct phylogenies, and it documents the limits of state-only trajectory inference (pseudotime/RNA velocity) lacking true temporal constraints. It also bears on the spatial axis (Claim 2/7) via spatial lineage tracing, on multi-omics integration (Claim 8), and on standards/comparability caveats (Claim 9). It can serve as support (it concedes the destructive, non-paired, transcriptome-only limitations our thesis builds on) and as a named opponent/nuance (it offers concrete workarounds and warns that transcriptomic similarity does not equal lineage similarity). Non-binding routing hint.

excerpts.text: Tools such as CaTCH and ClonMapper overcome the destructive nature of single-cell sequencing by using targeted CRISPR activation to induce fluorescent reporters only in specifically barcoded cells, enabling physical isolation of live clones of interest.
excerpts.note: Explicitly names single-cell sequencing as destructive, motivating live-clone-recall methods to work around it.

excerpts.text: Both genetic markers and synthetic barcodes rely on transgenic engineering, precluding their use in human subjects. To uncover natural fate paths and functional outputs directly within native human tissues, retrospective lineage tracing is required— leveraging endogenous variants that accumulate during cell division
excerpts.anchor: Natural lineage tracing with endogenous variants
excerpts.note: States that lineage history in human tissue must be reconstructed retrospectively from accumulated variants, because prospective transgenic labeling is impossible.

excerpts.text: Although scRNA-seq data alone can be utilized to infer differentiation dynamics via pseudotime analysis or RNA velocity, these approaches are inherently limited by the absence of true temporal constraints and are shown to perform poorly in distinguishing early fate bias among progenitors. By explicitly integrating lineage history with transcriptomic state information, the underlying differentiation processes can be captured with significantly higher fidelity.
excerpts.anchor: Computational integration of lineage and state dynamics
excerpts.note: Claims transcriptome-only trajectory inference lacks true temporal constraints and fails at early fate bias; real lineage history is needed for fidelity.

excerpts.text: For instance, LARRY revealed that hematopoietic progenitors commit to specific lineages days before these fate biases manifest transcriptomically.
excerpts.note: States that fate commitment precedes its transcriptomic manifestation, so RNA state alone misses lineage commitment timing.

excerpts.text: However, these multimodal integrations must be applied cautiously, as transcriptomic similarity does not inherently dictate lineage similarity.
excerpts.anchor: Practical challenges in synthetic lineage tracing
excerpts.note: Cautions that transcriptomic similarity is not equivalent to lineage relationship; the two modalities carry distinct information.

excerpts.text: their foundational assumption that lineage similarity dictates fate similarity must be applied cautiously, as it may fail in contexts of divergent or convergent differentiation.
excerpts.anchor: Computational integration of lineage and state dynamics
excerpts.note: Warns that optimal-transport lineage-OT methods can fail when lineage and fate diverge; integration assumptions are not always valid.

excerpts.text: Crucially, these barcodes can be directly integrated with single-cell sequencing to enable high-resolution state-to-fate mapping.
excerpts.note: States synthetic barcodes can be co-captured with the single-cell transcriptome to jointly map state and fate.

excerpts.text: CellTag-multi jointly profiles lineage barcodes and chromatin accessibility in single cells and computationally integrates these data with RNA modalities to enhance fate prediction. Pushing further, Camellia-seq captures lineage barcodes alongside transcriptomic, chromatin accessibility, and DNA methylation profiles.
excerpts.anchor: Integrating lineage history with molecular and spatial states
excerpts.note: Documents multi-omic readouts (lineage + RNA + ATAC + DNA methylation) co-captured in single cells to improve fate prediction.

excerpts.text: In human contexts, MethylTree enables non-invasive, multi-omics lineage tracing by jointly profiling RNA expression and DNA methylation, allowing analysis of lineage, transcriptome, and epigenome in single cells.
excerpts.anchor: Integrating lineage history with molecular and spatial states
excerpts.note: States that joint RNA + DNA-methylation profiling adds an epigenomic lineage layer beyond the transcriptome in human single cells.

excerpts.text: Integrating barcode information with spatial location represents another critical frontier for dissecting how cell-cell interactions and native microenvironments shape cell fate.
excerpts.anchor: Integrating lineage history with molecular and spatial states
excerpts.note: States that recovering spatial location alongside lineage is needed to capture how microenvironment and cell-cell interactions shape fate.

excerpts.text: Although labeled cells can be sorted for single-cell profiling, the lack of lineage barcode diversity fundamentally limits resolution at the single-cell level.
excerpts.anchor: Integrating lineage history with molecular and spatial states
excerpts.note: Notes a trade-off: dissociation-and-sort profiling forfeits the native spatial architecture that classic marker tracing preserves.

excerpts.text: Recent advancements in spatial profiling—progressing from the 55-μm spots of Visium to the 500-nm resolution of Stereoseq—have enabled spatial lineage tracing with synthetic barcodes at high resolution, albeit at high cost.
excerpts.anchor: Integrating lineage history with molecular and spatial states
excerpts.note: Quantifies spatial resolution gains (Visium 55 um to Stereo-seq 500 nm) enabling high-resolution spatial lineage tracing at higher cost.

excerpts.text: Classic marker-based lineage tracing inherently captures spatial information at low cost and high throughput.
excerpts.anchor: Integrating lineage history with molecular and spatial states
excerpts.note: Notes that imaging-based marker tracing natively preserves spatial context, unlike dissociative sequencing readouts.

excerpts.text: However, this approach incurs higher costs and, compared with imaging-based methods, currently offers lower spatial resolution—though newer techniques such as Stereoseq now achieve 500 nm resolution.
excerpts.note: Articulates a resolution-vs-cost-vs-throughput trade-off across sequencing-based and imaging-based spatial readouts.

excerpts.text: Ultimately, the choice of platform is dictated by the competing needs for phylogenetic depth versus the preservation of the native physical microenvironment.
excerpts.anchor: Integrating lineage history with molecular and spatial states
excerpts.note: Frames a core trade-off: deep lineage resolution versus preserving native spatial microenvironment.

excerpts.text: The utility of these variants depends on their faithful mitotic inheritance. Generally, stability follows a clear hierarchy: DNA sequence > DNA methylation > histone modifications > chromatin accessibility (assay for transposaseaccessible chromatin [ATAC]) and RNA expression (Figure 4A). Whereas DNA sequences are stably inherited across successive divisions, ATAC and transcriptomic profiles fluctuate dynamically in response to state changes
excerpts.anchor: Natural lineage tracing with endogenous variants
excerpts.note: Ranks heritable stability across molecular layers; states RNA/ATAC fluctuate while DNA sequence and methylation are stably inherited, distinguishing modalities by information content.

excerpts.text: Highlighting this hierarchy, multi-omics tracing recently demonstrated that DNA methylation patterns of HSCs retain robust clonal memory from embryonic development through adulthood, whereas ATAC and RNA states do not.
excerpts.anchor: Natural lineage tracing with endogenous variants
excerpts.note: States DNA methylation carries durable clonal memory that RNA and chromatin-accessibility states do not, evidence that modalities are non-interchangeable.

excerpts.text: Because the mitochondrial genome mutates at a 10- to 100-fold higher rate than the nuclear genome and is highly transcribed, these variants serve as abundant endogenous barcodes.
excerpts.anchor: Resolving histories via mitochondrial DNA mutations
excerpts.note: Quantifies mtDNA mutation rate (10-100x nuclear) enabling endogenous barcoding co-captured with cell state on standard single-cell platforms.

excerpts.text: Although SNVs should, in principle, offer the highest fidelity for phylogenetic reconstruction, their extremely low natural mutation rate (roughly one per cell division) makes them difficult to detect against background sequencing errors.
excerpts.anchor: Resolving histories via somatic DNA mutations
excerpts.note: Quantifies the limit of retrospective SNV tracing: ~1 mutation per division is hard to detect against sequencing noise.

excerpts.text: high-resolution phylogenetic trees can be reconstructed, where branch lengths directly correlate with elapsed time
excerpts.anchor: Resolving histories via somatic DNA mutations
excerpts.note: States that colony-WGS SNV phylogenies can encode real elapsed time in branch lengths, a partial recovery of the temporal axis from endogenous variants.

excerpts.text: the ability to jointly profile somatic mutations and transcriptomic states represents a major leap forward for natural variant tracing in human tissues.
excerpts.anchor: Resolving histories via somatic DNA mutations
excerpts.note: Notes that joint capture of somatic mutations and transcriptome from the same cell advances human lineage tracing.

excerpts.text: However, mitochondrial tracing introduces severe biologica and analytical limitations due to the non-deterministic inheritance of mtDNA. Because mitochondria segregate randomly during cell division, heteroplasmic mutations are subjected to intracellular selection and neutral drift, driving variants toward either complete fixation or total loss in descendant cells.
excerpts.anchor: Resolving histories via mitochondrial DNA mutations
excerpts.note: Concedes mtDNA drift/random segregation make phylogenetic reconstruction error-prone, limiting it to short-term clonal events.

excerpts.text: This creates a severe structural bottleneck known as ‘‘inter-site dropout,’’ where a single large deletion can erase previously encoded mutations, irreversibly destroy ancestral information, and fragment the downstream phylogenetic tree.
excerpts.note: Describes a structural failure mode of CRISPR cumulative barcodes: edits overwrite ancestral information, limiting recoverable temporal depth.

excerpts.text: The second challenge is data sparsity, which occurs when only a small fraction of cells within a clone are ultimately sampled (Figure 3Cb). This incomplete capture can create the illusion of smaller, highly coherent clones, leading to erroneous conclusions of early fate priming. Sparsity stems from two main factors: the high cost of deep single-cell sequencing and the low barcode recovery rates typical of many in vivo synthetic barcodes—the CARLIN mouse model, for instance, recovers only about 10% of barcodes.
excerpts.anchor: Practical challenges in synthetic lineage tracing
excerpts.note: Quantifies data sparsity and low barcode recovery (CARLIN ~10%); warns sparse sampling produces erroneous fate conclusions.

excerpts.text: To circumvent this artifact, the effective diversity of the barcode library should be empirically assessed and must vastly outnumber the target population size at the time of labeling; specifically, achieving an error rate below 1% requires a barcode diversity at least 100 times larger than the target population.
excerpts.anchor: Practical challenges in synthetic lineage tracing
excerpts.note: Gives a quantitative design standard (100x diversity for <1% homoplasy error) needed for reliable, comparable barcode-based clonal analysis.

excerpts.text: To further mitigate the risk of misinterpretation, rigorous statistical validation can be applied by shuffling barcode data to generate a null baseline, enabling evaluation of the statistical significance of observed lineage relationships.
excerpts.anchor: Practical challenges in synthetic lineage tracing
excerpts.note: Recommends null-shuffle baselines and statistical validation, an explicit standards/QC prescription for lineage claims.

excerpts.text: Historically, viral barcoding was restricted to in vitro or transplantation assays that often fail to reflect true native physiology.
excerpts.note: Concedes that in vitro/transplantation barcoding does not reflect native in vivo physiology, an in-vitro-vs-in-vivo trade-off.

excerpts.text: Crucially, both approaches require transgenesis, restricting their use to model organisms. Finally, natural variants eliminate the need for genetic engineering entirely, making them uniquely applicable to human samples. Although they capture high lineage diversity, their lineage recovery varies depending on the natural variant used and sequencing depth; furthermore, they lack temporal control and pose significant computational challenges for data interpretation.
excerpts.note: Summarizes the three pillars' trade-offs; concedes natural-variant tracing lacks temporal control and is computationally hard, while transgenic methods cannot be used in humans.

excerpts.text: Seamlessly integrating continuous lineage trees with next-generation spatial profiling technologies will eventually illuminate how the physical microenvironment and localized niche interactions cooperatively prime cell fate decisions over time.
excerpts.note: Frames as a future goal the joint capture of lineage, spatial microenvironment, and time, the very spatiotemporal gap our thesis targets.

excerpts.text: This requires moving beyond standard transcriptomics to incorporate co-profiling of the epigenome, direct mapping of physical cell-cell contacts, and logging of historical molecular signaling events.
excerpts.note: Calls to move beyond transcriptomics to epigenome, physical contacts, and signaling history, arguing single-modality state is insufficient.

excerpts.text: Despite these recent advances, the computational integration of lineage and state information remains a formidable challenge. Resolving these analytical bottlenecks may require additional analytical paradigms that can account for challenges such as complex kinetics, heterogeneous sampling, barcode dropout, and the absence of ancestral states.
excerpts.anchor: Computational integration of lineage and state dynamics
excerpts.note: Concedes that integrating lineage with state remains unsolved due to sampling heterogeneity, dropout, and missing ancestral states.

figuresNumbers.contains: Mitotic-inheritance stability ranking: DNA sequence > DNA methylation > histone modifications > chromatin accessibility (ATAC) and RNA expression.
Mitochondrial genome mutates 10- to 100-fold higher than the nuclear genome; highly transcribed; suited to short-term clonal events.
Somatic SNVs accumulate at roughly one per cell division; difficult to detect against background sequencing errors.
~0.001 per CpG site per division; roughly 10,000 epimutations per cell cycle across the human genome.
LARRY >70%; DARLIN ~70%; CARLIN mouse recovers only ~10% of barcodes.
Error rate below 1% requires barcode diversity at least 100x larger than the target population at labeling.
Visium 55-um spots; Stereo-seq 500-nm resolution.

SMALT base-editing trees: median statistical support ~90%; MethylTree benchmark accuracy nearly 100% vs synthetic-barcode ground truth; MethylTree estimated ~250 de novo HSC clones in early murine blood development.

Pillar comparison: genetic marker (lineage diversity low, 1-10; high recovery; temporal control yes; model organisms only); synthetic barcode (high diversity; variable recovery; partial temporal control; model organisms/in vitro); natural variant (high diversity; low/variable recovery; no temporal control; applicable to human).
