title: Connecting past and present: single-cell lineage tracing
authors: Cheng Chen, Yuanxin Liao, Guangdun Peng
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/23_cleaned.md
oneSentence: A technical review of single-cell lineage tracing (SCLT), which combines single-cell RNA-seq with DNA-barcoding strategies (integration, polylox, CRISPR, and base-editing barcodes) to record clonal history alongside cell state, surveying their mechanisms, applications, and technical limitations.
orientation: Sits squarely on the temporal axis (Axis B) and Claim 3: it documents why dissociative scRNA-seq plus pseudotemporal/RNA-velocity inference cannot recover true lineage paths, and how lineage-recording barcodes attempt to restore the temporal/history dimension that destructive measurement loses. It can serve BOTH as support (evidence that scRNA-seq alone loses true temporal trajectory, that destructive readout forces inference rather than direct observation, that spatial information is lost on dissociation) and as a foil/nuance (the proposed barcoding fix has its own throughput, diversity, invasiveness, and standardization limits). Secondary touches: spatial loss (Claim 2 / Axis C), data integration and lack of common standards (Claim 9 / standard axis), and resolution-throughput-destructiveness trade-offs (Claim 10).

excerpts.text: As mentioned earlier, current research based on single-cell transcriptomics heavily relies on pseudotemporal ordering to infer cell trajectories and connective lineage relationships. However, route descriptions of state transitions may or may not be equivalent to the true lineage paths of a progenitor population, and severe limitations remain to be resolved before accurate inferences can be achieved.
excerpts.note: States that single-cell transcriptomics relies on pseudotemporal ordering to infer trajectories, and that inferred state transitions may not equal the true lineage paths.

excerpts.text: First, a confident inference of cell trajectory requires substantial sampling of cells, which may be impeded by the incomplete or insufficient coverage of scRNA-seq in some cases (Tanay and Regev, 2017). Second, cell fates may converge in specific situations. A well-known example of lineage convergence is that the definitive endoderm has both extra-embryonic and embryonic origins (Kwon et al., 2008; Chan et al., 2019). Third, the cell state transition may undergo rapid divergence with the change of a few molecular drivers, which is challenging to distinguish with transcriptome analysis alone (Wagner et al., 2018; Packer et al., 2019; Tritschler et al., 2019; Wagner and Klein, 2020).
excerpts.note: Enumerates three limitations of trajectory inference from scRNA-seq state alone: insufficient sampling/coverage, lineage convergence, and rapid divergence driven by few molecular drivers.

excerpts.text: However, the ability to infer cell fate transitions for RNA velocity relies on assumptions such as constant gene-specific splicing rates, which may be violated in some situations (La Manno et al., 2018; Svensson and Pachter, 2018; Tritschler et al., 2019; Bergen et al., 2020; Lederer and La Manno, 2020).
excerpts.note: States that RNA velocity inference of fate transitions rests on assumptions (e.g. constant splicing rates) that can be violated.

excerpts.text: Altogether, although current computation tools may reveal the cell state trajectories by various pseudo-time analysis algorithms, more defined information with the ability to record the cell history is crucial to fully clarify the true path of lineage specification.
excerpts.note: Concludes that computational pseudo-time tools are insufficient and that information able to record cell history is needed to recover the true lineage path.

excerpts.text: As a substantial improvement in depicting and predicting cell behaviors, recent developments of single-cell lineage tracing, which combines single-cell omics and lineage tracing, enable detecting cell state and clonal relationship in parallel, thereby illustrating a credible route of lineage segregation by integrating the information of the clonal history and state transition.
excerpts.note: Frames SCLT as combining single-cell omics with lineage tracing to capture clonal history and state in parallel, restoring a history dimension to scRNA-seq snapshots.

excerpts.text: Nevertheless, detecting thousands and millions of barcodes that label thousands of progenies is a strenuous task. In this regard, transcribing the barcode information into the mRNA pool and retrieving these barcodes by high-throughput sequencing such as scRNA-seq is more practical.
excerpts.anchor: From traditional lineage tracing to single-cell lineage tracing
excerpts.note: Notes the scale challenge of detecting millions of barcodes and that piggybacking barcodes onto the mRNA pool for scRNA-seq readout is the practical route.

excerpts.text: First, SCLT can identify the progenitor-progeny relationship by barcode evolution and define the states of each cell by scRNA-seq, thus enabling the revelation of integrated lineage kinship and molecular trajectory.
excerpts.anchor: From traditional lineage tracing to single-cell lineage tracing
excerpts.note: Describes SCLT's first key feature: jointly recovering progenitor-progeny lineage kinship and molecular state/trajectory per cell.

excerpts.text: However, the readout of somatic mutations usually requires costly deep sequencing of the whole genome or exome of single cells, given the low frequency of these somatic mutations and their sparse genomic distribution (Woodworth et al., 2017; Baron and van Oudenaarden, 2019; Bizzotto et al., 2021; Spencer Chapman et al., 2021).
excerpts.note: States that endogenous somatic-mutation lineage readout demands costly whole-genome/exome single-cell deep sequencing because such mutations are rare and sparse.

excerpts.text: Recently, some researchers tried to use CRISPR/Cas9 or base editor to target some specific endogenous loci of the genome. These modifications increase the frequency of detectable mutations, but might violate the normal development of cells (Hwang et al., 2019; Cotterell et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2020).
excerpts.note: Concedes that engineered editing to raise detectable-mutation frequency can perturb normal cell development (a measurement-perturbs-biology trade-off).

excerpts.text: Theoretically, the diversity of a barcode library with 10 bp random nucleotides can approach 1 million, although such diversity is hard to achieve after multiple steps of molecular cloning.
excerpts.note: Gives a quantitative ceiling (about 1 million) for integration-barcode library diversity while noting cloning erodes the achievable diversity.

excerpts.text: Strikingly, an estimated maximum of 1.8 million distinct barcodes can be generated by random recombination of the polylox fragment (Pei et al., 2017, 2019).
excerpts.note: Quantifies the diversity of polylox recombination barcodes (up to about 1.8 million distinct barcodes).

excerpts.text: Currently, one major concern for polylox barcodes is the requirement of expensive long-read sequencing. A few conceptual designs of polylox barcodes for short-read sequencing have been proposed, but their feasibility in mammalian cells remains elusive (Peikon et al., 2014; Weber et al., 2016).
excerpts.note: Concedes a cost/feasibility limitation: polylox barcodes need expensive long-read sequencing, with short-read alternatives unproven in mammalian cells.

excerpts.text: To date, there is no common practice about how to implement CRISPR barcodes.
excerpts.note: States there is no common practice/standard for implementing CRISPR barcodes.

excerpts.text: However, the best practice on CRISPR barcodes awaits further improvements on various aspects of experimental considerations such as barcode design, barcoding induction timing, tree reconstruction algorithms.
excerpts.note: Concedes that no best-practice standard yet exists across barcode design, induction timing, and tree-reconstruction algorithms.

excerpts.text: However, trajectory analysis may fail to recapitulate the true differentiation paths with states alone under certain circumstances. The SCLT method can address this issue by accessing the shared barcodes, which connect cells with states in the early and late developmental stages (Fig. 2C).
excerpts.anchor: Analyzing trajectory directions in state-fate maps
excerpts.note: States trajectory analysis from states alone can fail to recover true differentiation paths, and that shared barcodes connecting early and late cells address this.

excerpts.text: Of note, few of the above statistical models or mathematical models on cell fate decisions are built based on SCLT data.
excerpts.note: Concedes that existing population/statistical models of cell-fate dynamics are largely not yet built on actual SCLT (lineage-recorded) data.

excerpts.text: Given the wide application of random insertion in mutagenesis or enhancer trap, it's possible that random integration disrupts the regulatory elements and changes the cell state without killing the cells, which may mislead the interpretation of biological results (Sivasubbu et al., 2007; Trinh le and Fraser, 2013).
excerpts.note: Concedes that random-integration barcoding can disrupt regulatory elements and alter cell state, i.e. the labeling measurement can perturb the very biology being recorded.

excerpts.text: The current throughput of commercial scRNA-seq platforms (e.g., 10x Genomics) is around 10,000 cells per experiment, but high cost makes it impractical to sequence millions of cells, which may be necessary for organism-level tracing analysis. Insufficient sampling results in the doubt of whether selected cells can represent the whole population, thus affecting the accuracy and completeness of lineage couplings.
excerpts.anchor: Recovery rates of cells and barcodes
excerpts.note: Quantifies a throughput/cost trade-off: ~10,000 cells per scRNA-seq run, with millions needed for organism-level tracing, and insufficient sampling degrading lineage coupling accuracy.

excerpts.text: Finally, invasive barcoding strategies may have latent effects to the behaviors of barcoded cells, which is often difficult to evaluate. For example, CRISPR/Cas9-mediated gene editing could cause DNA double-strand breaks, leading to subsequent p53 activation and even cell death (Haapaniemi et al., 2018; Ihry et al., 2018; Chan et al., 2019), which might alter the lineage routes as well as lose the barcoded cells.
excerpts.anchor: Recovery rates of cells and barcodes
excerpts.note: Concedes that invasive barcoding (e.g. CRISPR-induced double-strand breaks, p53 activation, cell death) can alter lineage routes and lose cells, a measurement-induced perturbation.

excerpts.text: A thorough and ambitious understanding of cell lineage is to illustrate the relationship of every single cell in an organism in a single lineage tracing experiment, which is impractical for current technology. A more practical way is to integrate information from multiple lineage tracing experiments, as the current knowledge of developmental lineages is also assembled from multiple lineage experiments with various techniques.
excerpts.note: States complete single-experiment lineage capture is impractical, so knowledge must be integrated across multiple heterogeneous lineage-tracing experiments.

excerpts.text: However, apart from technical limitations, the cell lineage of human development isn't invariant per se due to the stochasticity/plasticity of each cell fate decision (Zechner et al., 2020), making it unrealistic to assemble multiple small lineage trees collectively to create a complete lineage tree in the organismal level.
excerpts.note: Notes that human developmental lineage is non-invariant due to stochasticity/plasticity, making naive assembly of small lineage trees into a complete organismal tree unrealistic.

excerpts.text: However, obtaining an accurate and systematic lineage tree of a species remains a challenging task for current practices of SCLT, and new designs are necessary for further improvements.
excerpts.note: Concedes that obtaining an accurate, systematic species-level lineage tree remains challenging for current SCLT, requiring new designs.

excerpts.text: Recently, the fast development of spatial transcriptomics has shed light on the understanding of the spatial organization of cell types and their lineage relationships (Peng et al., 2019; Peng et al., 2020; Marx, 2021). Several pioneering investigations have proven the concept of combining lineage tracing and spatial transcriptomics by imaging-based readouts (Chow et al., 2021; Frieda et al., 2017).
excerpts.note: Notes that spatial transcriptomics recovers spatial organization of cell types and lineage relationships, and that combining lineage tracing with spatial transcriptomics is being pioneered (implying standard SCLT/scRNA-seq lacks spatial context).

excerpts.text: Anchored by cell's location, combining lineage-information-based spatial transcriptomics and in toto imaging will build a connection between ground-truth cellular history and final molecular interrogation. Future advances to integrate SCLT, spatial multi-omics, and imaging techniques should boost the synthesized studies of animal development and disease modeling in both space and time (Fig. 4A).
excerpts.note: Argues the future is integrating lineage history, spatial multi-omics, and imaging so that cellular history and molecular state are studied jointly in both space and time.

figuresNumbers.contains: A barcode library with 10 bp random nucleotides can theoretically approach 1 million distinct barcodes, though hard to achieve after molecular cloning.
Estimated maximum of 1.8 million distinct barcodes generated by random recombination of the polylox fragment (10 loxP sites).

Without integration identifiers only 155 barcodes are generated by polylox recombination, while 514 barcodes are retrieved when distinguishing the same recombination by integration identifiers (Kim et al., 2020).
Commercial scRNA-seq platforms (e.g. 10x Genomics) yield around 10,000 cells per experiment; sequencing the millions of cells needed for organism-level tracing is cost-impractical.

title: RNA velocity of single cells
authors: Gioele La Manno, Ruslan Soldatov, Amit Zeisel, Emelie Braun, Hannah Hochgerner, Viktor Petukhov, Katja Lidschreiber, Maria E. Kastriti, Peter Lönnerberg, Alessandro Furlan, Jean Fan, Lars E. Borm, Zehua Liu, David van Bruggen, Jimin Guo, Xiaoling He, Roger Barker, Erik Sundström, Gonçalo Castelo-Branco, Patrick Cramer, Igor Adameyko, Sten Linnarsson, Peter V. Kharchenko
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/24_cleaned.md
oneSentence: Introduces RNA velocity—the time derivative of gene expression estimated from the ratio of unspliced to spliced mRNA already present in standard single-cell RNA-seq—and uses it to predict each cell's near-future transcriptional state and reconstruct directed differentiation lineages in mouse and human tissues.
orientation: Sits squarely on roadmap Claim 3 (destructive sequencing cannot pair-observe the same cell over time). It explicitly concedes that scRNA-seq yields only a static snapshot (supports our problem framing) yet its central contribution is a computational route to recover temporal dynamics from that one snapshot—the very 'infer time from a distribution rather than a real trajectory' approach we critique—so it is a strong candidate FOIL for Claim 3 (and a methodological neighbour of Waddington-OT / lineage tracing). It is purely transcriptomic, so it also lightly touches Claim 4 (modality narrowness) as an instance of RNA-only inference; its limitations on extrapolation horizon and failure modes are useful nuance.

excerpts.text: However, this approach captures only a static snapshot at a point in time, posing a challenge for the analysis of time-resolved phenomena such as embryogenesis or tissue regeneration.
excerpts.note: Concedes that single-cell RNA-seq measures only a static snapshot and cannot directly observe time-resolved processes.

excerpts.text: Here we show that RNA velocity—the time derivative of the gene expression state—can be directly estimated by distinguishing between unspliced and spliced mRNAs in common single-cell RNA sequencing protocols. RNA velocity is a high-dimensional vector that predicts the future state of individual cells on a timescale of hours.
excerpts.note: Proposes inferring per-cell future state (a temporal dynamic) computationally from a single static snapshot, rather than from paired longitudinal measurement.

excerpts.text: During development, differentiation occurs on a timescale of hours to days, which is comparable to the typical half-life of mRNA. The relative abundance of nascent (unspliced) and mature (spliced) mRNA can be exploited to estimate the rates of gene splicing and degradation, without the need for metabolic labelling, as previously shown in bulk2–4.
excerpts.note: States the inference mechanism: dynamics are reconstructed from the unspliced/spliced ratio within one sample, exploiting mRNA kinetics rather than tracking the same cell over time.

excerpts.text: The balance of unspliced and spliced mRNA abundance is, therefore, an indicator of the future state of mature mRNA abundance, and thus the future state of the cell.
excerpts.note: Core claim that a single-timepoint molecular ratio predicts the cell's future state.

excerpts.text: RNA velocity estimates of the individual cells accurately recapitulated the transcriptional dynamics within this dataset, including general movement of the differentiating cells towards a chromaffin fate (Fig. 2d), as well as the movement towards and away from the intermediate differentiation state.
excerpts.note: Reports that computationally inferred velocity matched the true differentiation direction validated by lineage tracing.

excerpts.text: On the basis of pulse labelling of chromaffin progenitor cells with 5-ethynyl-2′-deoxyuridine (EdU) (Supplementary Note 2 Section 6), we estimate that we were able to extrapolate 2.5–3.8 h into the future (Fig. 2f, g), which is also consistent with the ability to resolve cell-cycle events. Given the linear nature of the extrapolation, however, this predictive timescale will depend on the shape of the gene expression trajectory (that is, the curvature of the expression manifold).
excerpts.note: Concedes the inferred temporal horizon is short (a few hours) and trajectory-shape dependent—an admitted limit of recovering time from one snapshot.

excerpts.text: Because cells can have RNA velocities along multiple independent components simultaneously, such as differentiation, maturation and proliferation, care must be taken when interpreting low-dimensional representations, as cells that lack apparent velocity in one particular embedding can nevertheless have substantial velocity in some subspace that is not visualized.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded interpretive caveat: inferred velocity is embedding-dependent and can be missed or misread.

excerpts.text: Failures in specific cases had several apparent causes, including genes observed exclusively far from equilibrium, uneven contribution of non-coding transcripts, and alternative splicing leading to multiple rates of γ across the measured populations (Supplementary Note 2 Section 4).
excerpts.note: Author-conceded failure modes of the velocity inference at the gene level.

excerpts.text: Using a Markov random-walk model on the velocity field, the terminal and root states could be automatically identified (Fig. 3c), demonstrating the power of RNA velocity to orient the lineage tree without prior knowledge about the developmental process.
excerpts.note: Claims the directed lineage tree (root and terminal states) can be reconstructed from a single snapshot without prior developmental knowledge.

excerpts.text: Examining two adjacent neuroblasts just at the entrance to the branching point between CA and granule fates (Fig. 3e), we found that although their current states were neighbours (in gene-expression space), their futures were already tilted towards different fates, distinguished by activation of Prox1 (Fig. 3c, inset).
excerpts.note: Argues future fate of individual cells can be read from present molecular state even when current expression states are nearly identical.

excerpts.text: As RNA velocity is grounded in real transcription kinetics, this approach promises to bring a more solid quantitative foundation to our understanding of the dynamics of cells in gene-expression space during differentiation.
excerpts.note: Positions the method as a quantitatively grounded substitute for direct temporal observation of differentiation dynamics.

excerpts.text: RNA abundance is a powerful indicator of the state of individual cells. Single-cell RNA sequencing can reveal RNA abundance with high quantitative accuracy, sensitivity and throughput1.
excerpts.note: Frames cell state through RNA abundance alone; an instance of RNA-centric reasoning where transcript level stands in for cell state.

excerpts.text: Metabolic labelling showed that for most genes, changes in the spliced/unspliced ratio would be detectable after 10–100 min (Extended Data Fig. 2).
excerpts.note: Quantifies the kinetic detection window underlying velocity, bounding the temporal resolution recoverable from one snapshot.

figuresNumbers.contains: 15–25% of reads contained unspliced intronic sequences across SMART-seq2, STRT/C1, inDrop and 10x Genomics Chromium protocols; in agreement with bulk (14.6%) and single-cell (~20%) prior observations.

83% of all genes displayed expression time courses consistent with simple first-order kinetics (consistent with unspliced reads representing nascent mRNA); Extended Data Fig. 2 reports 83.4% concordant vs 16.6% discordant genes.

Steady-state behaviour of most genes consistent with a single fixed slope γ; 11% (Extended Data Fig. 3: 10.8%) of genes showed distinct slopes in different tissue subsets, suggesting tissue-specific splicing/degradation.
Velocity extrapolated 2.5–3.8 h into the future (chromaffin); distribution mode of optimal extrapolation time = 2.1 h (Fig. 2g).
Changes in spliced/unspliced ratio detectable after 10–100 min (Extended Data Fig. 2).

Most genes showed positive correlation between velocity estimates and empirically observed expression derivatives; 75% of genes differentially regulated along pseudotime showed positive correlation with the empirical derivative (Extended Data Fig. 8).

Mouse hippocampus n = 18,213 cells (P0: 8,113; P5: 10,100); chromaffin E12.5 n = 385; human forebrain glutamatergic n = 1,720 (post-conception week 10); oligodendrocytes n = 6,307; bone marrow n = 3,018; intestinal epithelium n = 2,683.

flags: Metadata partially unverified: text confirms journal Nature, year 2018, DOI 10.1038/s41586-018-0414-6, Received 13 Dec 2017, Accepted 3 Jul 2018, Published online 8 Aug 2018; volume and page range are NOT stated in the source and are left unverified in venue.

OCR quirks kept verbatim in excerpts and references: 'u= γs', 'SMARTseq2', 'principle component analysis (PCA)' (sic), reference superscript markers rendered inline as digits (e.g. 'protocols1', 'metabolic labelling2–4').

Source inconsistency in reference list: ref 18 (Plass et al., Science 360, eaaq1723, 2018) carries a title duplicated from ref 17 (Iwano et al., Prox1...)—an OCR/typesetting error in the cleaned MD; the correct ref 18 is the planarian whole-organism single-cell lineage atlas. Title not used as a primary source here due to the corruption.

Scope note: bulk of the paper is methodological (velocity estimation, splicing biology, pipeline parameters) and developmental-neuroscience application, which is off-roadmap; only the temporal-inference framing (Claim 3) and the RNA-only-as-cell-state framing (Claim 4) were captured.
Read at source: ref #2 (Zeisel 2011) and ref #4 (Gaidatzis 2015) are the first-hand origins of the velocity model/intronic-read approach—worth citing directly rather than via this paper.

title: Generalizing RNA velocity to transient cell states through dynamical modeling
authors: Volker Bergen, Marius Lange, Stefan Peidli, F. Alexander Wolf, Fabian J. Theis
venue: Nature Biotechnology, 2020 (volume/issue/pages not present in cleaned source; see flags)
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/25_cleaned.md
oneSentence: Presents scVelo, a likelihood-based dynamical model that solves the full transcriptional dynamics of splicing kinetics via EM, generalizing RNA velocity to transient cell states and inferring gene-specific reaction rates plus a gene-shared latent time that reconstructs a cell's position in a differentiation process from a single static scRNA-seq snapshot.
orientation: A methods paper from the Theis group on inferring directed temporal dynamics (RNA velocity, latent time, trajectory inference) from a single scRNA-seq snapshot. It is most useful for our temporal axis: it concedes head-on that scRNA-seq is destructive and yields only static snapshots, and that snapshots fundamentally underconstrain the possible dynamics (requiring extra information such as lineage-tracing assays). Thus it can serve as SUPPORT for Claim 3 (destructive sequencing cannot pairwise-observe time; time must be reconstructed, not measured) and as a NAMED FOIL where the writer notes the field's response to the time gap is computational reconstruction from distributions rather than true paired time-course data, with the authors themselves listing limits and identifiability issues. It also lightly concedes the spatial gap (Claim 2, spatial axis) and the protein/metabolic-labeling extension need (Claim 4) only in the Discussion as future work. Routing hint only, non-binding.

excerpts.text: A central challenge in trajectory inference is the destructive nature of single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq), which reveals only static snapshots of cellular states. To move from descriptive toward predictive trajectory models, additional information is required to constrain the space of possible dynamics that could give rise to the same trajectory.
excerpts.note: States scRNA-seq is destructive and reveals only static snapshots, and that snapshots alone underconstrain the dynamics, requiring additional information.

excerpts.text: As such, lineage-tracing assays can add information via genetic modification to enable the reconstruction of lineage relationships. However, these assays are not straightforward to set up and are technically limited in many systems, such as human tissues.
excerpts.note: Notes lineage-tracing assays can recover lineage relationships but are hard to set up and technically limited in many systems including human tissues.

excerpts.text: The concept of RNA velocity has enabled the recovery of directed dynamic information by leveraging the fact that newly transcribed, unspliced pre-mRNAs and mature, spliced mRNAs can be distinguished in common scRNA-seq protocols, the former detectable by the presence of introns. Assuming a simple per-gene reaction model that relates abundance of unspliced and spliced mRNA, the change in mRNA abundance, termed RNA velocity, can be inferred.
excerpts.note: Describes RNA velocity as recovering directed dynamic information from the unspliced/spliced mRNA ratio within a single static snapshot, rather than from paired time-course measurement.

excerpts.text: The inferred latent time represents the cell's internal clock, which accurately describes the cell's position in the underlying biological process. In contrast to existing similarity-based pseudo-time methods, this latent time is grounded only on transcriptional dynamics and accounts for speed and direction of motion.
excerpts.note: Defines latent time as a computationally inferred internal clock describing a cell's position in a process, derived from transcriptional dynamics of a snapshot.

excerpts.text: On simulated splicing kinetics, latent time is able to reconstruct the underlying real time at near-perfect correlation and correct scale, clearly outperforming diffusion pseudo-time.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Solving the full gene-wise transcription dynamics
excerpts.note: Claims latent time reconstructs underlying real time at near-perfect correlation on simulated data; reconstruction is validated against simulation, not paired experimental time.

excerpts.text: To test whether scVelo's velocity estimates allow identification of more complex population kinetics, we considered a scRNA-seq experiment from the developing mouse dentate gyrus comprising two time points (P12 and P35) measured using droplet-based scRNA-seq (10× Genomics Chromium Single Cell Kit V1; Methods).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Resolving the heterogeneous population kinetics in dentate gyrus development
excerpts.note: The experimental temporal information is just two discrete sampled time points (P12, P35), used to corroborate inferred directionality rather than to follow individual cells over time.

excerpts.text: Whether a cell type is still in transition or already terminal is indicated by two experimental time points (Supplementary Fig. 6a) and experimental analysis, both supporting the overall velocity-inferred directionality.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Resolving the heterogeneous population kinetics in dentate gyrus development
excerpts.note: Confirms that ground-truth temporal information here is only two discrete experimental time points used to validate velocity-inferred direction.

excerpts.text: We compared pseudo-time and latent time in the chronology of endocrine cell fates. In real time, α-cells are produced earlier (before E12.5) than β-cells (E12.5–E15.5). This ordering is captured by latent time but not by pseudo-time (Fig. 3d).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Relating cell fates and disentangling dynamical regimes through latent time
excerpts.note: Shows latent time recovers the real-time ordering of α- versus β-cell production; the real-time reference comes from prior external knowledge, not paired observation of the same cells.

excerpts.text: In particular, spatial single-cell RNA profiling at transcriptome scale might provide additional information on relative cell positions necessary to resolve spatial dependencies in gene regulation. Spatial coordinates as well as experimental time might also be leveraged as additional constraints to extend the concept of latent time—for instance, to capture the progression around a cell cycle.
excerpts.note: Concedes that the method lacks spatial information and that spatial coordinates and experimental time would provide additional constraints, motivating spatially resolved and temporally constrained data.

excerpts.text: Extending the kinetic model to protein translation has been proposed within the steady-state formulation and can be likewise included into the dynamical model. Metabolic labeling, for example using single-cell SLAM-seq, enables the quantification of total RNA levels together with newly transcribed RNA. This additional readout can be easily included into the dynamical model, incorporating varying labeling lengths as additional prior.
excerpts.note: Concedes the RNA-only readout is incomplete and points to protein translation and metabolic-labeling readouts as extensions, implicitly acknowledging additional modalities carry information RNA splicing alone does not.

excerpts.text: scVelo's suitability for characterizing transient populations makes it a promising candidate for studying cellular responses to perturbation, which often display drastic switching behaviors.
excerpts.note: Suggests velocity/latent-time inference as a tool for perturbation-response (cell state transition) studies, framed for snapshot data.

excerpts.text: Another difficulty concerns insufficiently observed splicing kinetics. For instance, we might detect only a small portion of the overall dynamics at the very ends of the process (Supplementary Fig. 12a). This manifests as a straight line in the unspliced to spliced phase portraits rather than a curve (Supplementary Fig. 12b). Observing partial dynamics in this way leads the steady-state and stochastic models to incorrectly fit this line and erroneously assign positive and negative velocities.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Accounting for different kinetic regimes and insufficiently observed kinetics
excerpts.note: Concedes that when a snapshot captures only partial dynamics, velocity inference becomes ambiguous and models can assign erroneous direction — a limit of reconstructing time from a single snapshot.

excerpts.text: The latter case can occur when a dynamical process occurs in a fast or synchronous fashion such that the snapshot captured in an scRNA-seq data set recovers little of the full dynamics. Here, the overall developmental time scale of the sampled population might be far shorter than the potential duration of the kinetics.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Accounting for different kinetic regimes and insufficiently observed kinetics
excerpts.note: Concedes that a single snapshot can recover little of the true dynamics when a process is fast or synchronous, requiring a root prior from the first experimental time point or known progenitor population.

excerpts.text: Mathematically, these scenarios result in two local optima, thus entailing an identifiability issue, which can be solved only with additional information.
excerpts.anchor: Methods — Accounting for insufficiently observed kinetics with prior information
excerpts.note: States that snapshot-based velocity inference has a fundamental identifiability issue solvable only with additional (e.g., temporal/progenitor) information.

excerpts.text: These assumptions are often violated, in particular when a population comprises multiple heterogeneous subpopulations with different kinetics.
excerpts.note: Notes the steady-state RNA-velocity model's assumptions (full dynamics observed, common splicing rate) are often violated in heterogeneous populations.

figuresNumbers.contains: Pearson correlation between true and inferred steady-state ratio increases from 0.71 to 0.97 when using the dynamical model instead of the steady-state model.
With overall time scale of 20 h imposed as prior, the dynamical model recovers true splicing-kinetics parameters at correlations of 0.85 and higher.

scVelo achieves more than a tenfold speedup over the original implementation (velocyto) for steady-state and stochastic models; latent-time approximation gives a 30-fold speedup over exact optimal time assignment.

Full pipeline on pancreas development with 25,919 transcriptome profiles runs in under 1 min (steady-state/stochastic); full dynamical inference ~20 min for 1,000 genes across 35,000 profiles. velocyto cannot process >40,000 cells (64 GB RAM); scVelo scales to >300,000 cells.
2,000 log-normally distributed parameters sampled per reaction rate; total time in a transcriptional state varied between 2 and 10 h.
α-cells produced before E12.5; β-cells produced E12.5–E15.5 (real-time ordering recovered by latent time but not pseudo-time).

Orientation note: The paper's affirmative contribution (recovering 'time' computationally from a snapshot) can be read as the field's workaround for the destructive-measurement time gap; deployable as a named foil to our point that reconstruction-from-distribution is not true paired time-course measurement. title: A comparison of single-cell trajectory inference methods
authors: Wouter Saelens, Robrecht Cannoodt, Helena Todorov, Yvan Saeys
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/26_cleaned.md
oneSentence: Benchmarks 45 single-cell trajectory inference (TI) methods on 110 real and 229 synthetic datasets for accuracy, scalability, stability and usability, finding strong complementarity between methods and that the best method depends mostly on dataset dimensions and trajectory topology.
orientation: A methods benchmark for inferring temporal/developmental ordering (pseudotime) from single-cell snapshot data; it is the canonical empirical entry point for our Claim 3 (destructive sequencing yields only snapshots, so time must be computationally reconstructed rather than observed pairwise) and the temporal axis. It can serve as SUPPORT for our standard/comparability claims (it argues for standardization of TI input/output interfaces and demonstrates cross-method incomparability) AND as a FOIL the writer can name (the entire field substitutes inferred pseudotime for a real time course, exposing the gap our thesis names). Also feeds Claim 1 (benchmark variability, no one-size-fits-all, poor generalization across datasets) and Claim 10 (scalability trade-offs).

excerpts.text: Trajectory inference approaches analyze genome-wide omics data from thousands of single cells and computationally infer the order of these cells along developmental trajectories.
excerpts.note: Defines TI as computational inference of cell ordering from snapshot omics data, i.e., time is reconstructed, not directly observed.

excerpts.text: Such dynamic processes can be modeled computationally using trajectory inference (TI) methods, also called pseudotime analysis, which order cells along a trajectory based on similarities in their expression patterns
excerpts.note: Pseudotime ordering is derived from expression similarity among cells, not from tracking the same cell over time.

excerpts.text: TI methods offer an unbiased and transcriptome-wide understanding of a dynamic process, thereby allowing the objective identification of new (primed) subsets of cells, delineation of a differentiation tree and inference of regulatory interactions responsible for one or more bifurcations.
excerpts.note: States the upside of TI for resolving dynamics from transcriptomic snapshots; a foil line showing the field treats pseudotime as a route to dynamics.

excerpts.text: A plethora of TI methods has been developed over the past few years and even more are being created every month (Supplementary Table 1).
excerpts.note: Documents the proliferation of pseudotime/TI methods, all working around the lack of direct temporal observation.

excerpts.text: Given the diversity in TI methods, it is important to quantitatively assess their performance, scalability, robustness and usability. Many attempts at tackling this issue have already been made, but a comprehensive comparison of TI methods across a large number of different datasets is still lacking.
excerpts.note: Concedes a comprehensive cross-dataset benchmark of methods was lacking, a comparability/standardization gap.

excerpts.text: We found substantial complementarity between current methods, with different sets of methods performing most optimally depending on the characteristics of the data.
excerpts.note: Method performance is data-dependent; no single method generalizes, supporting that results hinge on data not the model alone.

excerpts.text: To make the outputs from different methods directly comparable to each other, we developed a common probabilistic model for representing trajectories from all possible sources (Fig. 1b).
excerpts.note: Builds a common representation because method outputs were otherwise not comparable, a standardization move.

excerpts.text: These different priors can range from weak priors that are relatively easy to acquire, such as a start cell, to strong priors, such as a known grouping of cells, that are much harder to know a priori, and which can potentially introduce a large bias into the analysis (Fig. 2a).
excerpts.note: TI often needs externally supplied prior information (start cell, groupings) that can bias the inferred trajectory; a limitation of reconstructing time from snapshots.

excerpts.text: We found that method performance was very variable across datasets, indicating that there is no ‘one-size-fits-all’ method that works well on every dataset (Supplementary Fig. 3a).
excerpts.note: No method works well on every dataset; performance is dataset-contingent, the canonical no-free-lunch finding.

excerpts.text: The performance of a method was strongly dependent on the type of trajectory present in the data (Fig. 3b).
excerpts.note: Accuracy depends on the underlying data structure (trajectory topology), not on method quality alone.

excerpts.text: This analysis therefore indicates that detecting the right topology is still a difficult task for most of these methods, because methods tend to be either too optimistic or too pessimistic regarding the complexity of the topology in the data.
excerpts.note: Methods systematically misjudge trajectory topology, a conceded accuracy limitation of inferring dynamics from snapshots.

excerpts.text: On all datasets, using one method resulted in getting a top model about 27% of the time. This increased up to 74% with the addition of six other methods (Fig. 4a).
excerpts.note: Quantifies that a single method rarely (~27%) gives a top result; needing 7 methods to reach 74% underscores model/result instability.

excerpts.text: Altogether, this shows that there is considerable complementarity between the different methods and that users should try out a diverse set of methods on their data, especially when the topology is unclear a priori.
excerpts.note: Recommends running many methods because no one suffices; an admission that results are not robustly reproducible across methods.

excerpts.text: While early TI methods were developed at a time where profiling more than a thousand cells was exceptional, methods now have to cope with hundreds of thousands of cells, and perhaps soon with more than ten million.
excerpts.note: Notes the escalating scale of single-cell datasets that methods must handle.

excerpts.text: Moreover, the recent application of TI methods on multi-omics single-cell data also showcases the increasing demands on the number of features.
excerpts.note: Acknowledges TI is now being applied to multi-omics single-cell data, raising feature-count demands.

excerpts.text: We found that the scalability of most methods was overall very poor, with most graph and tree methods not finishing within an hour on a dataset with ten thousand cells and ten thousand features (Fig. 3c), which is around the size of a typical droplet-based single-cell dataset.
excerpts.note: Most methods fail to scale to a typical droplet dataset within an hour, a throughput/computational trade-off.

excerpts.text: We found that more than half of all methods had a quadratic or superquadratic complexity with respect to the number of cells, which would make it difficult to apply any of these methods in a reasonable time frame on datasets with more than a thousand cells (Supplementary Fig. 4b).
excerpts.note: Over half of methods scale quadratically or worse in cell number, limiting applicability at scale.

excerpts.text: Critical to the broad applicability of TI methods is the standardization of the input and output interfaces of TI methods, so that users can effortlessly execute TI methods on their dataset of interest, compare different predicted trajectories and apply downstream analyses
excerpts.note: Explicitly argues standardized input/output interfaces are critical so trajectories can be compared and reused; a direct standardization call.

excerpts.text: In the future, this framework could be extended to allow additional input data, such as spatial and RNA velocity information, and easier downstream analyses.
excerpts.note: Notes spatial information is not yet part of the standard TI input and is a future extension, implying current TI ignores spatial structure.

excerpts.text: In addition, further discussion within the field is required to arrive at a consensus concerning a common interface for trajectory models, which can include additional features such as uncertainty and gene importance.
excerpts.note: Concedes the field still lacks a consensus common interface/standard for trajectory models.

excerpts.text: We found that the performance of a method can be very variable between datasets, and therefore included a large set of both real and synthetic data within our evaluation, leading to a robust overall ranking of the different methods.
excerpts.note: Reiterates strong cross-dataset performance variability, motivating large benchmark suites.

excerpts.text: Real datasets were classified as ‘gold standard’ if the reference trajectory was not extracted from the expression data itself, such as via cellular sorting or cell mixing. All other real datasets were classified as ‘silver standard’.
excerpts.note: Even the benchmark's ground truth trajectories are mostly not independently observed; only gold-standard ones come from sorting/mixing rather than from the expression data, underscoring scarcity of true temporal ground truth.

excerpts.text: Discrete time course: for each cell a time point from which it was sampled. If available, this was directly extracted from the reference trajectory; otherwise the geodesic distance from the root milestone was used.
excerpts.note: Real per-cell sampling times are often unavailable and are substituted by a geodesic distance proxy, illustrating that true time labels are scarce and reconstructed.

excerpts.text: Providing prior information to a TI method can be both a blessing and a curse. In one way, prior information can help the method to find the correct trajectory among many, equally likely, alternatives. On the other hand, incorrect or noisy prior information can bias the trajectory towards current knowledge.
excerpts.note: Concedes that snapshot-based trajectories are underdetermined (many equally likely alternatives) and that injected priors can bias the inferred dynamics.

figuresNumbers.contains: 45 TI methods evaluated; 110 real and 229 synthetic datasets; list of 71 TI tools gathered, 45 included after exclusions
Accuracy (HIM topology, F1_branches, cor_dist cell positions, wcor_features), scalability, stability, usability
One method gives a top model (>=95% of best) ~27% of the time on all datasets; rises to 74% when six other methods are added

Most graph/tree methods do not finish within 1 h on 10,000 cells x 10,000 features; only PAGA, PAGA Tree, Monocle DDRTree, Stemnet, GrandPrix complete within a day on 1 million cells; >half of methods quadratic or superquadratic in cell number
PAGA, Slingshot and SCORPIUS performed well across the board; Slingshot and Celltrails had nearly perfect usability scores
Execution limits: >1 h time limit, 16 GB memory limit (10 GB for scalability runs); most methods <=12 GB memory
Seven trajectory topology types from linear/cyclic up to connected and disconnected graphs

title: The dynamics and regulators of cell fate decisions are revealed by pseudotemporal ordering of single cells
authors: Cole Trapnell, Davide Cacchiarelli, Jonna Grimsby, Prapti Pokharel, Shuqiang Li, Michael Morse, Niall J Lennon, Kenneth J Livak, Tarjei S Mikkelsen & John L Rinn
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/27_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The paper introduces Monocle, an unsupervised algorithm that orders single-cell RNA-Seq profiles in 'pseudotime' along a trajectory (minimum spanning tree / PQ-tree reconstruction) to recover transcriptional dynamics of differentiation without prior marker knowledge, applied to human myoblast differentiation where it resolved sequential waves of gene regulation and identified new regulators.
orientation: This is a foundational trajectory-inference / pseudotime method and is directly relevant to the temporal axis and Claim 3 (destructive sequencing cannot pair-observe the same cell over time). It both embodies the workaround we critique (reconstructing a temporal axis computationally from a single destructive snapshot, i.e. ordering distinct cells as surrogate time points) AND concedes the underlying constraint (each cell is measured once, time is inferred not observed). It can therefore serve as support for 'time is reconstructed from distributions, not real trajectories' and as the named method our thesis says is a proxy for missing time-course data. It also touches Claim 1 (averaging artifacts / Simpson's paradox in bulk data motivating single-cell) and Claim 2 (dissociation/in-vitro context).

excerpts.text: Here we describe Monocle, an unsupervised algorithm that increases the temporal resolution of transcriptome dynamics using single-cell RNA-Seq data collected at multiple time points.
excerpts.note: States Monocle's purpose: increase temporal resolution of dynamics from scRNA-Seq collected at multiple real time points.

excerpts.text: In essence, one RNA-Seq experiment would constitute a time series, with each cell representing a distinct time point along a continuum.
excerpts.note: Core conceptual move: distinct individual cells are treated as surrogate time points; a static snapshot of many cells stands in for a time series.

excerpts.text: Monocle orders single-cell expression profiles in ‘pseudotime’—a quantitative measure of progress through a biological process.
excerpts.note: Defines pseudotime as a computed measure of progress, explicitly distinct from real elapsed time.

excerpts.text: Each point in the image of the function is the state of the cell at s, which can be thought of as progress through the biological process, rather than time. That is, two cells might execute exactly the same sequence of changes as they differentiate but take different amounts of time to do so.
excerpts.anchor: Online Methods - The single cell ordering problem
excerpts.note: Explicitly distinguishes inferred 'progress' from real time; pseudotime decouples ordering from elapsed time.

excerpts.text: This ‘pseudotemporal’ scale of differentiation is numerically arbitrary and will of course vary from experiment to experiment, but nevertheless is useful for downstream analysis.
excerpts.note: Concedes pseudotime is a numerically arbitrary, relative scale, not an absolute physical time measurement.

excerpts.text: single-cell proteomic methods rely on a priori knowledge of key distinguishing markers
excerpts.note: Characterizes contemporary single-cell proteomic methods as requiring a priori known markers (a stated limitation of those methods).

excerpts.text: Recently, several studies carried out at single-cell resolution revealed high cell-to-cell variation in the expression of most genes, even key developmental regulators, during the differentiation process. Such high variability can complicate analysis of these experiments.
excerpts.note: Notes high cell-to-cell expression variability complicates analysis, motivating ordering methods over raw averages.

excerpts.text: In general, averages of measurements from two or more distinct groups of data points can follow trends that qualitatively differ from the trend that describes each group, a phenomenon known as Simpson’s paradox.
excerpts.note: Invokes Simpson's paradox: averaging across heterogeneous cells can produce misleading aggregate trends.

excerpts.text: As a population of cells captured at the same time may include many distinct intermediate differentiation states, considering only its average properties would mask trends occurring across individual cells.
excerpts.note: Argues that averaging over an asynchronous population masks real per-cell trends; single-cell resolution is needed.

excerpts.text: Solving this problem by experimental synchronization of cells or by stringent isolation of precursors at distinct stages is challenging and can sharply alter differentiation kinetics.
excerpts.note: Concedes that the experimental route to clean temporal data (synchronization / stage isolation) is hard and perturbs the very kinetics being measured, motivating the computational surrogate.

excerpts.text: However, because these algorithms operate on bulk expression measurements, they are sensitive to mixture effects arising from Simpson’s paradox and other averaging artifacts.
excerpts.note: Bulk-based ordering algorithms are confounded by mixture/averaging artifacts, a limitation single-cell ordering aims to overcome.

excerpts.text: A time-series analysis of myoblast differentiation with bulk RNA-Seq identified up- and downregulated genes but did not identify the transient clusters or distinguish the early from late regulation visible with pseudotemporally ordered single cells (Supplementary Fig. 9). Furthermore, dynamic range of expression was compressed for most genes, confirming that failure to account for variability in progress through differentiation leads directly to the effects associated with Simpson’s paradox.
excerpts.note: Empirically shows bulk time-series misses early/late and transient structure and compresses dynamic range, while pseudotime ordering recovers it.

excerpts.text: even cells in a genetically and epigenetically clonal population might progress through differentiation at different rates in vitro, depending on positioning and physical contact with neighboring cells.
excerpts.anchor: Online Methods - The single cell ordering problem
excerpts.note: Notes that a cell's progression rate depends on its position and physical contact with neighbors (spatial/microenvironment dependence of cell state).

excerpts.text: Moreover, single-cell analysis distinguishes cells of interest from contaminating cell types such as interstitial mesenchymal cells without experimental isolation that might disrupt cell-cell interactions important in the in vivo niche.
excerpts.note: Argues experimental isolation/purification can disrupt cell-cell interactions important in the in vivo niche; concedes the relevance of spatial niche context.

excerpts.text: maintaining in vitro differentiation kinetics that resemble physiological cell crosstalk occurring in the in vivo niche.
excerpts.note: Frames in-vitro kinetics as only resembling in-vivo niche crosstalk, implicitly conceding the in-vivo spatial niche is the reference standard.

excerpts.text: We captured between 49 and 77 cells at each of four time points after the switch to DM using the Fluidigm $\mathrm { C _ { 1 } }$ microfluidic system. RNA from each cell was isolated and used to construct a single mRNA-Seq library per cell, which was then sequenced to a depth of \~4 million reads per library, resulting in a complete gene expression profile for each cell
excerpts.note: Describes the destructive single-cell workflow: cells dissociated and lysed, RNA isolated per cell, one library per cell, only four discrete real time points captured.

excerpts.text: Monocle placed subsets as small as 50 cells in pseudotemporal order highly similar (Spearman ≥ 0.8) to their relative order in the full data set. The algorithm retained the ability to detect dynamically regulated genes with high precision (≥95%) over all designs and with increasing recall as more of the cells were included
excerpts.note: Quantifies robustness of pseudotemporal ordering (Spearman >= 0.8 down to 50 cells; precision >= 95%).

excerpts.text: This pseudotime ordering pinpoints key events in differentiation, such as the ID1/MYOG switch, that are masked both by conventional bulk cell expression profiling and by single-cell expression profiles ordered by time collected.
excerpts.note: Claims pseudotime reveals events masked both by bulk profiling and by ordering cells by their real collection time.

excerpts.text: Sequencing-based measurements of small RNAs and mRNAs from the same cell will provide answers to such systems-level questions.
excerpts.note: Anticipates joint measurement of multiple molecular layers (small RNA + mRNA) from the same cell to answer systems-level questions.

excerpts.text: Looking at average behavior in a group of cells is thus not necessarily faithful to the process through which an individual cell transits.
excerpts.anchor: Online Methods - The single cell ordering problem
excerpts.note: Restates that population-average behavior is not faithful to the individual-cell trajectory.

figuresNumbers.contains: Between 49 and 77 cells captured at each of four time points (0, 24, 48, 72 h after switch to differentiation medium), via Fluidigm C1.
~4 million reads per single-cell C1 library; 10-20 million reads per bulk TruSeq library; 100-bp paired-end on HiSeq 2500.
1,061 genes dynamically regulated during differentiation at FDR < 5% (Fig. 2c).
Subsets as small as 50 cells ordered with Spearman >= 0.8 vs full-data order; dynamically-regulated-gene detection precision >= 95% across all designs.

Upregulated genes enriched for regulatory elements containing binding motifs for 175 transcription factors; loss-of-function screen on 11 candidate factors with 44 distinct shRNAs; significantly altered differentiation (FDR < 5%) for MZF1, ZIC1, XBP1, USF1; eight previously unappreciated transcription factors identified.
Libraries with < 1 million reads or < 80% of fragments mapping to nonmitochondrial protein-coding genes were excluded.

Scope note: this paper is in-scope chiefly for the temporal axis / Claim 3 (computational reconstruction of a time axis from destructive single-snapshot scRNA-Seq). Its myogenesis biology, specific transcription-factor regulators, and detailed algorithmic internals (PQ-tree, ICA, GAM/Tobit) are off-roadmap and largely not captured.

Direction note: Monocle's pseudotime is tagged both 'challenges' (it computationally recovers a temporal axis, partially countering 'we have no temporal data') and 'nuances' (it concedes pseudotime is inferred progress, numerically arbitrary, and not real paired time-course observation) - a downstream agent can deploy it as either the workaround we critique or the concession we build on.

title: Whole-cell segmentation of tissue images with human-level performance using large-scale data annotation and deep learning
authors: Noah F. Greenwald, Geneva Miller, Erick Moen, Alex Kong, Adam Kagel, Thomas Dougherty, Christine Camacho Fullaway, Brianna J. McIntosh, Ke Xuan Leow, Morgan Sarah Schwartz, Cole Pavelchek, Sunny Cui, Isabella Camplisson, Omer Bar-Tal, Jaiveer Singh, Mara Fong, Gautam Chaudhry, Zion Abraham, Jackson Moseley, Shiri Warshawsky, Erin Soon, Shirley Greenbaum, Tyler Risom, Travis Hollmann, Sean C. Bendall, Leeat Keren, William Graf, Michael Angelo, David Van Valen
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/28_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors built TissueNet, a tissue-image segmentation dataset of >1 million manually labeled cells via a crowdsourced human-in-the-loop pipeline, and trained Mesmer, a deep-learning algorithm that performs whole-cell and nuclear segmentation of multiplexed tissue images at human-level accuracy across diverse tissue types and imaging platforms, enabling automated extraction of subcellular protein localization, N/C ratio, and cell morphology.
orientation: A methods/resource paper on the spatial-imaging modality axis: it operates on intact tissue rather than dissociated cells, so it most directly feeds the spatial roadmap claim (scRNA-seq loses spatial info; imaging preserves it) and the modality axis (imaging/morphology, subcellular protein localization). Its benchmarking finding (architectures converge once trained on TissueNet; training-data scale, not model, drives accuracy) is usable support for 'bottleneck is data, not model.' It also touches the standard axis via the explicit need for a standardized cross-tissue antibody panel and the absence of generalized tools. Can serve as support (imaging recovers spatial/morphological information dissociation destroys; data is the bottleneck) and, more weakly, as a foil/nuance on temporal (its over-time analysis compares patient cohorts, not paired same-cell tracking).

excerpts.text: Unlike flow cytometry or single-cell RNA sequencing, tissue imaging is performed with intact specimens.
excerpts.note: States that tissue imaging works on intact specimens, in contrast to flow cytometry and scRNA-seq.

excerpts.text: Understanding the structural and functional relationships present in tissues is a challenge at the forefront of basic and translational research. Recent advances in multiplexed imaging have expanded the number of transcripts and proteins that can be quantified simultaneously, opening new avenues for large-scale analysis of human tissue samples.
excerpts.note: Multiplexed imaging quantifies many transcripts and proteins simultaneously while preserving tissue structural and functional relationships.

excerpts.text: Ambitious collaborative efforts such as the Human Tumor Atlas Network, the Human BioMolecular Atlas Program and the Human Cell Atlas are using these methods to comprehensively characterize the location, function and phenotype of cells in the human body. However, the tools needed for analysis and interpretation of these datasets at scale do not yet exist. The clearest example is the lack of a generalized algorithm for locating single cells in images.

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 9 (data islands / no standard / not comparable)

excerpts.note: Large atlas consortia generate spatial location/function/phenotype data, but the analysis tools to interpret these datasets at scale, including a generalized single-cell-locating algorithm, do not yet exist.

excerpts.text: Since the features extracted through this process are the basis for downstream analyses, inaccuracies at this stage can have far-reaching consequences for interpreting image data. The difficulty of achieving accurate, automated cell segmentation is due in large part to the differences in cell shape, size and density across tissue types.

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 9 (data islands / no standard / not comparable)

excerpts.note: Segmentation errors propagate into all downstream single-cell analyses; cross-tissue heterogeneity makes automated segmentation hard.

excerpts.text: A common pitfall is the need to perform manual, image-specific adjustments to produce useful segmentations. This lack of full automation poses a prohibitive barrier given the increasing scale of tissue imaging experiments.

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 9 (data islands / no standard / not comparable)

excerpts.note: Prior machine-learning segmentation required manual, image-specific tuning, which does not scale.

excerpts.text: These algorithms are capable of achieving high accuracy, but require substantial amounts of annotated training data. Generating ground-truth data for cell segmentation is time-intensive, and, as a result, existing datasets are of modest size (10⁴–10⁵ annotations).

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 1 (data is bottleneck; data-driven models generalize poorly)

excerpts.note: Deep learning accuracy hinges on large annotated training data, but ground-truth generation is laborious so existing datasets stay small (10^4-10^5).

excerpts.text: Moreover, most public datasets annotate the location of cell nuclei rather than whole cells, meaning that models trained on these datasets are capable only of performing nuclear segmentation, not cell segmentation. Thus, the lack of available data, combined with the difficulties of deploying pretrained models to the life science community, has hampered progress in whole-cell segmentation.

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 1 (data is bottleneck; data-driven models generalize poorly)

excerpts.note: Scarcity of whole-cell (vs nuclear) annotated data, plus deployment difficulty, has been the limiting factor for progress.

excerpts.text: As a result of the scalability of our human-in-the-loop approach to data labeling, TissueNet is larger than the sum total of all previously published datasets (Fig. 1b), with 1.3 million whole-cell annotations and 1.2 million nuclear annotations. TissueNet contains 2D data from six imaging platforms (Fig. 1c), nine organs (Fig. 1d), and includes both histologically normal and diseased tissue (for example, tumor resections). TissueNet also encompasses three species, with images from human, mouse and macaque. Constructing TissueNet required >4,000 person-hours, the equivalent of nearly 2 person-years of full-time effort (Fig. 1e).
excerpts.anchor: A human-in-the-loop approach for constructing TissueNet
excerpts.note: TissueNet aggregates 1.3M whole-cell and 1.2M nuclear annotations across 6 platforms, 9 organs, 3 species, normal and diseased tissue, requiring >4,000 person-hours.

excerpts.text: In contrast, Cellpose and StarDist obtained equivalent performance to Mesmer when trained on TissueNet (Fig. 2b).
excerpts.anchor: Mesmer is a deep learning algorithm for accurate whole-cell segmentation

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 1 (data is bottleneck; data-driven models generalize poorly)

excerpts.note: Distinct architectures (Cellpose, StarDist) reach Mesmer-equivalent accuracy once trained on the same TissueNet data, implying training data, not architecture, drives accuracy.

excerpts.text: These comparisons allowed us to understand the relative contributions of deep learning methodology and training data to overall accuracy.
excerpts.anchor: Mesmer is a deep learning algorithm for accurate whole-cell segmentation

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 1 (data is bottleneck; data-driven models generalize poorly)

excerpts.note: The benchmarking is explicitly designed to separate the contribution of methodology versus training data to accuracy.

excerpts.text: In general, specialist models had poor performance when evaluated on data types not seen during training (Extended Data Fig. 3b). Dataset size likewise played an important role, as models trained on small subsets of TissueNet did not perform as well as those trained on the entire dataset (Extended Data Fig. 3d–h).
excerpts.anchor: Mesmer achieves human-level performance for segmentation

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 1 (data is bottleneck; data-driven models generalize poorly)

excerpts.note: Models trained on narrow/small data subsets generalize poorly to unseen data types; diversity and scale of training data are needed for generalization.

excerpts.text: One example is predicting the subcellular localization of proteins in cells, which can be used to quantify the nuclear translocation of transcription factors or degree of membrane staining of HER2 for the assessment of breast cancer.
excerpts.anchor: Mesmer enables accurate downstream analysis of tissue imaging data
excerpts.note: Whole-cell segmentation enables quantifying subcellular (nuclear vs membrane) protein localization, e.g. TF nuclear translocation and HER2 membrane staining.

excerpts.text: Subcellular localization predictions from Mesmer agreed with those from the ground-truth data (Fig. 4b). We observed predominantly nuclear expression for known nuclear markers (for example, Ki67 and HH3) and non-nuclear expression for membrane markers (for example, E-cadherin and HER2; Fig. 4b).
excerpts.anchor: Mesmer enables accurate downstream analysis of tissue imaging data
excerpts.note: Imaging-based segmentation recovers correct subcellular spatial distribution of protein markers (nuclear markers nuclear, membrane markers non-nuclear).

excerpts.text: This analysis identified a subpopulation of cells with an N/C ratio of zero (Fig. 4e), indicating that no nucleus was observed in that cell. These cells arise when the imaging plane used to acquire the data captures the cytoplasm but not the nucleus.
excerpts.anchor: Mesmer enables accurate downstream analysis of tissue imaging data
excerpts.note: 2D imaging planes can capture a cell's cytoplasm but miss its nucleus, an out-of-plane-nucleus artifact of imaging a single tissue section.

excerpts.text: Cells with out-of-plane nuclei are missed by nucleus-based segmentation approaches but are captured by Mesmer.
excerpts.anchor: Mesmer enables accurate downstream analysis of tissue imaging data
excerpts.note: Whole-cell (vs nucleus-based) segmentation recovers cells whose nucleus is out of the imaging plane.

excerpts.text: However, some tissue types have complex morphologies that cannot be accurately captured with only two channels of imaging data. For example, the decidua, the mucosal membrane of the uterus, shows substantial variation in cell size and cell shape arising from the interaction between maternal and fetal cells.

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 7 (spatial organization / microenvironment alters cell state)

excerpts.note: Complex tissue morphology (decidua) arises from maternal-fetal cell interaction and requires more than two imaging channels to capture.

excerpts.text: These additional markers provide crucial information about cell morphology during model training that is lost when they are combined into a single channel.
excerpts.note: Additional lineage-specific marker channels carry morphological information that is lost when markers are collapsed into a single channel.

excerpts.text: To determine how these cellular morphologies changed over time in the human decidua, we divided the samples into two groups based on age: early (6–8 weeks) and late (16–18 weeks) gestational age.
excerpts.note: Temporal change in cell morphology is inferred by comparing two cross-sectional patient age groups (early vs late gestation), not by tracking the same cells over time.

excerpts.text: Our analysis demonstrates that whole-cell segmentation can make cell morphology a quantitative observable, bridging the historical knowledge of pathologists and modern multiplexed imaging methods.
excerpts.note: Segmentation turns cell morphology into a quantitative, measurable feature from multiplexed imaging.

excerpts.text: Cell segmentation has been a principal bottleneck for the tissue imaging community, as previous methods required extensive manual curation and parameter tuning to produce usable results. Our experience has shown that these shortcomings can lead to months-long delays in analysis.

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 9 (data islands / no standard / not comparable)

excerpts.note: Segmentation has been a principal bottleneck because prior methods needed manual curation and tuning, causing months-long analysis delays.

excerpts.text: Previous tissue datasets were not large enough to train accurate whole-cell segmentation models. As a result, previous efforts to generate accurate tissue-segmentation models have focused on nuclear segmentation, meaning that nearly all previous benchmarking has been limited to the evaluation of nuclear segmentation models.

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 1 (data is bottleneck; data-driven models generalize poorly)

excerpts.note: Insufficient dataset size, not method, confined prior tissue segmentation work and benchmarking to nuclear-only models.

excerpts.text: Our experience developing TissueNet and Mesmer raises a natural question: how much data is enough? We observed diminishing returns to training data at \~10⁴–10⁵ labels (Extended Data Fig. 3c). We believe that the effort required to go beyond this scale is warranted when accuracy is a paramount concern, for example for models applied across projects or in clinical contexts, which is the case for Mesmer.

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 1 (data is bottleneck; data-driven models generalize poorly)

excerpts.note: Accuracy shows diminishing returns at ~10^4-10^5 labels; going beyond is justified only when high accuracy across projects/clinical use is required.

excerpts.text: Future challenges include the need for a standardized, cross-tissue antibody panel for cell segmentation. Development of such a panel would be a significant advance and would synergize with the work presented here.

touchpoints.claimId: Claim 9 (data islands / no standard / not comparable)

excerpts.note: The authors concede the field lacks a standardized cross-tissue antibody panel and that creating one would be a significant advance.

excerpts.text: Whole-cell segmentation in three-dimensional (3D) is another challenge that will become more prominent as imaging throughput increases to allow routine collection of such datasets. Existing deep learning approaches for 3D instance segmentation are promising, but a 3D equivalent of TissueNet to power future models currently does not exist.
excerpts.note: 3D whole-cell segmentation remains an unsolved challenge because no 3D-equivalent training dataset yet exists; current work is 2D slices of 3D tissue.

excerpts.text: Multiplexed imaging platforms analyze tissue slices that represent a two-dimensional cut through a 3D structure. As a result, sometimes the nucleus of a given cell is not captured in the image plane/ tissue section, whereas the rest of the cell is.
excerpts.anchor: Methods - Assessing frequency of out-of-plane nuclei
excerpts.note: Multiplexed imaging measures 2D cuts of 3D tissue, so a cell's nucleus may fall outside the imaged section, an inherent spatial-sampling limitation.

excerpts.text: Although some highly multiplexed platforms are capable of imaging dozens of markers at once, restricting TissueNet to include only the minimum number of channels necessary for whole-cell segmentation maximizes the number of imaging platforms where the resulting models can be used.
excerpts.note: Highly multiplexed platforms image dozens of protein markers at once, but TissueNet deliberately uses only two channels to maximize cross-platform applicability.

figuresNumbers.contains: 1.3 million whole-cell annotations and 1.2 million nuclear annotations; >1 million manually labeled cells total; an order of magnitude more than all previously published segmentation training datasets; twice as many nuclear and 16 times as many whole-cell labels as all previously published datasets combined.
6 imaging platforms, 9 organs, 3 species (human, mouse, macaque), normal and diseased tissue; >4,000 person-hours (~2 person-years) to construct.
Mesmer F1 = 0.82 (text) / 0.820 (table); FeatureNet F1 = 0.63 (text) / 0.583 (table); Cellpose F1 = 0.41 (text) / 0.40 (table). Table also: Mesmer Prec .83, Recall 0.81, Jacc 0.84.
Mesmer only 13% slower than FeatureNet despite larger model capacity; Mesmer 20 times faster than Cellpose.

No significant difference between human-to-human and human-to-Mesmer F1 scores (P = 0.93); five independent expert annotators; four board-certified pathologists rated Mesmer and annotator predictions equivalently.
Pearson's r = 0.87 correlation between predicted and ground-truth nuclear-to-whole-cell (N/C) ratios across all cells in TissueNet.
n = 1069 cells; nuclear markers Ki67 and HH3 predominantly nuclear, membrane markers E-cadherin and HER2 non-nuclear.
Training-data accuracy shows diminishing returns at ~10^4-10^5 labels; existing prior datasets were 10^4-10^5 annotations.
Web portal for moderate data (<10^3 1-megapixel images); local Docker/container deployment for larger volumes (>10^3 1-megapixel images).
