title: Cellpose: a generalist algorithm for cellular segmentation
authors: Carsen Stringer, Tim Wang, Michalis Michaelos and Marius Pachitariu
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/29_cleaned.md
oneSentence: Introduces Cellpose, a generalist deep-learning cell-segmentation method trained on a new, highly varied dataset of over 70,000 segmented objects, which segments cells across many microscopy image types without retraining or parameter adjustment, plus a 3D extension that reuses the 2D model without 3D-labeled data.
orientation: Methods/tool paper on segmenting cells from microscopy images; an enabling prerequisite for spatial/imaging modalities (Axis A imaging, Axis C spatial). It is most useful as support for the imaging/morphology modality and for the generalization critique of specialized deep models (Claim 1): it repeatedly states that automated DL methods trained on specialized datasets do not generalize and need new human-labeled data per image type. It also concedes temporal/tracking as a not-yet-built extension (Axis B foil) and frames its community-benchmark/reproducibility angle (Claim 9). It motivates segmentation by the need to assign both RNA and protein expression to single cells.

excerpts.text: uantitative cell biology requires measurements of multiple cellular properties such as shape, position, RNA expression and protein expression<sup>1</sup>. To assign these properties to individual cells, one must first segment an image volume into cell bodies, usually based on a cytoplasmic or membrane marker<sup>2–8</sup>.
excerpts.note: States that single-cell quantification of multiple properties (shape, position, RNA, protein) requires first segmenting the image into cell bodies; positions imaging/segmentation as prerequisite to assigning per-cell molecular and spatial properties.

excerpts.text: Fully automated methods have many advantages, such as reduced human effort, increased reproducibility and better scalability to big datasets from large screens. However, these methods are typically trained on specialized datasets, and do not generalize well to other types of data, requiring new human-labeled images to achieve good performance on any one image type.
excerpts.note: States that automated deep-learning segmentation methods trained on specialized datasets do not generalize to other data types and require new labeled images per image type.

excerpts.text: Recognizing this generalization problem in the context of nuclear segmentation, a recent Data Science Bowl challenge amassed a dataset of varied images of nuclei from many different laboratories<sup>15</sup>. Methods trained on this dataset can generalize far more widely than those trained on data from a single laboratory.
excerpts.note: Frames the generalization problem and notes that models trained on a varied, multi-lab dataset generalize far more widely than single-lab-trained models.

excerpts.text: Previous approaches did not perform well on this dataset, and we therefore developed a new model with better expressive power called Cellpose.
excerpts.note: States prior segmentation approaches failed on their diverse cell dataset, motivating a new, higher-capacity generalist model.

excerpts.text: However, all models trained on the specialized data alone performed poorly on the full dataset (Fig. 4e), motivating the need for a generalist algorithm.
excerpts.note: Specialist-trained models performed poorly on the full diverse dataset, an explicit demonstration of poor generalization of narrowly-trained data-driven models.

excerpts.text: On the generalized data alone, the generalist Cellpose model had an average precision of 0.77 at a threshold of 0.5, significantly outperforming Mask R-CNN and Stardist that had average precisions of 0.61 and 0.61 respectively (Fig. 4g).
excerpts.note: Quantitative generalization benchmark: generalist Cellpose AP 0.77 vs 0.61 for Mask R-CNN and Stardist on diverse generalized data at IoU 0.5.

excerpts.text: Altogether, these results show that the inclusion of more images in the training set does not saturate the capacity of the neural network, indicating that Cellpose might have even more spare capacity for additional training data, which we hope to identify via community contributions.
excerpts.note: Argues that adding more (diverse) training data keeps improving the model and motivates community data contribution rather than per-lab siloed datasets.

excerpts.text: However, generalizing to out-of-sample data is a notoriously difficult problem for neural networks (for example, Fig. 4e), and more work will be needed to best take advantage of new types of training data.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: out-of-sample generalization remains a notoriously difficult problem for neural networks even with their generalist approach.

excerpts.text: Other extensions of Cellpose are possible, for example to cell tracking<sup>40</sup>, which could be addressed by adding a ‘temporal gradient’ dimension to the spatial gradient dimensions.
excerpts.note: Cell tracking over time is only a proposed future extension (temporal gradient), conceding that temporal/trajectory handling is not yet built into the method.

excerpts.text: Combined with new histology methods such as spatial transcriptomics<sup>41</sup>, Cellpose has the potential to aid and enable new approaches in quantitative single-cell biology that are reproducible and scalable to large datasets.
excerpts.note: Positions cell segmentation as an enabler for spatial transcriptomics and reproducible, scalable single-cell biology.

excerpts.text: We evaluated the performance of Cellpose3D and the other models on a manually annotated 3D test volume in which the DNA and RNA were costained to serve as nuclear and cytoplasmic markers, respectively (Fig. 6f). The human annotator found 183 cells, while Cellpose3D predicted 172 cells, with 17 false positives at an IoU threshold of 0.5 (Fig. 6g, Videos 1 and 2).
excerpts.note: Demonstrates 3D (volumetric/spatial) cell segmentation performance, extending segmentation into the spatial-volume dimension for tissue imaging.

excerpts.text: This step can be straightforward when cells are sufficiently separated from one other, for example in monodispersed cultures in vitro. However, in many tissues, cells are tightly packed together and difficult to separate.
excerpts.note: Notes segmentation is easy for dissociated/in-vitro cultures but hard for cells tightly packed in intact tissue, contrasting dissociated vs in-situ tissue contexts.

figuresNumbers.contains: New dataset of highly varied cell images containing over 70,000 segmented objects; cytoplasm dataset of 608 images (100 Cell Image Library + 508 additional; 69 reserved for testing); nucleus dataset of 1,139 images (111 reserved for testing).

Trained/tested on specialized Cell Image Library data, at IoU 0.5 Cellpose matched 485/521 ground-truth ROI with 18 false positives, average precision 0.91, vs 0.76 for Stardist and 0.80 for Mask R-CNN.
On generalized data at IoU 0.5: generalist Cellpose AP 0.77 vs Mask R-CNN 0.61 and Stardist 0.61.
On a 3D cortical test volume: human annotator 183 cells; Cellpose3D 172 cells with 17 false positives at IoU 0.5; Cellpose3D outperformed ilastik, 2D-stitched methods and U-Net 3D extensions.
Refined object-size prediction reached correlation 0.93 (cytoplasm) and 0.97 (nuclei) with ground truth on test images.

Scope note: this is a microscopy cell-segmentation tool paper; on-roadmap relevance is as an enabling imaging/spatial method and as evidence on poor generalization of specialized deep models (Claim 1), not a direct molecular-modality study.
Read at source: ref #15 (Caicedo 2019 Data Science Bowl) worth first-hand for the multi-lab-generalization point.

title: DeepSea is an efficient deep-learning model for single-cell segmentation and tracking in time-lapse microscopy
authors: Abolfazl Zargari, Gerrald A. Lodewijk, Najmeh Mashhadi, Nathan Cook, Celine W. Neudorf, Kimiasadat Araghbidikashani, Robert Hays, Sayaka Kozuki, Stefany Rubio, Eva Hrabeta-Robinson, Angela Brooks, Lindsay Hinck, S. Ali Shariati
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/30_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors develop DeepSea, a compact trainable deep-learning model and user-friendly software that segments and tracks individual cells across sequences of phase-contrast time-lapse microscopy images, building full cell-cycle lineage trajectories and outperforming existing segmentation and tracking tools, and apply it to show that smaller-born mouse embryonic stem cells lengthen their G1 phase, indicating cell-size control.
orientation: This is a methods paper for label-free time-lapse microscopy, an inherently NON-destructive imaging modality that directly observes the SAME individual cell continuously over many days and reconstructs real birth-to-division trajectories and lineage trees. It is most useful to our roadmap as a counterpoint/complement to Claim 3 (destructive sequencing cannot pair-observe the time axis): imaging can do exactly the paired, longitudinal observation that destructive omics cannot. It also feeds Axis B (temporal: high-temporal-resolution single-cell dynamics) and the imaging/morphology modality entry of Axis A. As a foil, it underscores the trilemma (Claim 10): imaging is non-destructive and temporally resolved but carries only morphological/size readouts, not molecular (RNA/protein/metabolite) content. Non-binding routing hint.

excerpts.text: Time-lapse microscopy is the only method that can directly capture the dynamics and heterogeneity of fundamental cellular processes at the single-cell level with high temporal resolution.
excerpts.note: Asserts time-lapse microscopy uniquely captures single-cell dynamics directly with high temporal resolution, i.e. a non-destructive route to the temporal axis.

excerpts.text: Single-cell quantitative live microscopy can directly capture both dynamics and heterogeneity of cellular decisions by continuous long-term measurements of cellular features.
excerpts.note: States that live microscopy enables continuous long-term measurement of the same cells, capturing dynamics directly rather than inferring them.

excerpts.text: In recent years, it has become clear that single-cell-level analysis over time is essential for revealing the dynamics and heterogeneity of individual cells.
excerpts.note: Frames over-time single-cell analysis as essential for revealing dynamics and heterogeneity.

excerpts.text: Widely available microscopy techniques such as label-free phasecontrast live microscopy allow for monitoring the dynamics of morphological features such as the size and shape of the cells.

touchpoints.otherLabel: Imaging/morphology modality is non-destructive but reads only shape/size, not molecular content

excerpts.note: Notes label-free phase-contrast imaging is non-toxic/widely available but its readout is restricted to morphological features (size, shape).

excerpts.text: Typical live-cell imaging of biological features of cells is a multi-day experiment that produces several gigabytes of images collected from thousands of cells.
excerpts.note: Quantifies live-imaging experiments as multi-day, gigabyte-scale across thousands of cells, indicating throughput of longitudinal observation.

excerpts.text: Most critically, they divide into two new daughter cells during mitosis, which is unique and not comparable with other phenomena we encounter in conventional object-tracking applications.
excerpts.note: Highlights that cell division (mitosis) is a tracking-specific challenge distinguishing lineage tracing from generic object tracking.

excerpts.text: With this model, we could monitor multiple cellular phenotypes and several cell division cycles across the microscopy image sequences to generate lineage tree structures of cells.
excerpts.anchor: RESULTS — Designing and training a cell segmentation and tracking model
excerpts.note: States the model links cells frame to frame and detects divisions to build lineage tree structures, i.e. true observed lineages rather than inferred ones.

excerpts.text: DeepSea was able to outperform existing state-of-the-art models such as CellPose, 15 StarDist,16 and 2D-UNET21,26 i n terms of latency and mean average precision (mAP) when trained on the same training sets and tested on the same test sets at all pre-defined IoU thresholds (Figures 1A and 1B).

touchpoints.otherLabel: Benchmark comparison of image-analysis models under matched train/test sets

excerpts.note: Reports DeepSea beats CellPose, StarDist and 2D-UNET on latency and mAP under matched training and test sets.

excerpts.text: While our model achieved $0 . 9 8 \pm \ : 0 . 2$ precision (@0.5 IoU threshold) for tracking single cells, the precision of our model for mitosis detection was around $0 . 8 9 \pm 0 . 3$ (@0.5 IoU threshold) (Figures 4A and 4B).
excerpts.note: Quantifies tracking precision 0.98 for single-cell linking and 0.89 for mitosis detection (@0.5 IoU), showing reliable continuous tracking of the same cell across division.

excerpts.text: It shows that the higher sampling rate reduces the tracking model failures, especially for the cells that move and change quickly over time.
excerpts.note: Finds tracking accuracy is sensitive to frame sampling rate; higher temporal sampling reduces failures for fast-changing cells.

excerpts.text: Our DeepSea tracking model achieved a MOTA value of 0.94 ± 0.2 compared with the Trackmate model, which had a MOTA of 0.29 ± 0.7 (Table S3).
excerpts.note: Reports DeepSea full-cell-cycle tracking MOTA 0.94 versus Trackmate 0.29 over 228 ground-truth trajectories.

excerpts.text: We also would like to note that rule-based tools like Trackmate are not trainable to be rapidly adapted to any specialized dataset.
excerpts.note: Notes rule-based tracking tools are not trainable/adaptable to specialized datasets, contrasting with trainable deep-learning approaches.

excerpts.text: By measuring the size of the sister cells at birth, we showed that 42% of divisions resulted in daughter cells of different sizes (Figure 6B).
excerpts.anchor: RESULTS — Cell cycle duration is adjusted based on birth size
excerpts.note: Quantifies that 42% of divisions yield differently sized daughters, a paired single-cell measurement enabled by continuous tracking.

excerpts.text: In support of this hypothesis, we observed that smaller-born cells increase their cell cycle duration by about 2 h compared with their larger sister cells (Figure 6C).
excerpts.anchor: RESULTS — Cell cycle duration is adjusted based on birth size
excerpts.note: Reports smaller-born cells lengthen their cell cycle by ~2 h versus larger sisters, a temporal-dynamic finding obtainable only by following individual cells over time.

excerpts.text: The segmentation and tracking of cell bodies and sub-cellular organelles from microscopy images are critical steps for nearly all microscopybased biological analysis applications.
excerpts.note: States segmentation/tracking of cell bodies and sub-cellular organelles is foundational to microscopy-based analysis, linking imaging to spatial/sub-cellular localization.

excerpts.text: The lack of a comprehensive, high-quality annotated dataset of cells prevents the full utilization of DL-based models for microscopy image analysis systems.
excerpts.note: Identifies the absence of high-quality annotated datasets as the bottleneck preventing full use of deep-learning models for microscopy analysis.

excerpts.text: We also showed that more frequent imaging of microscopy frames would increase the accuracy of tracking the full cell cycle by providing more information about the cell features right before cell division.
excerpts.note: Reiterates that denser temporal sampling improves full-cell-cycle tracking accuracy near division.

excerpts.text: We would like to note that our dataset and models are limited to the phase contrast 2D images of three cell types.

touchpoints.otherLabel: Imaging modality limited to 2D phase-contrast and few cell types (generalization limit)

excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: dataset and models confined to 2D phase-contrast images of three cell types.

excerpts.text: A larger dataset of samples from different cell types and different imaging modalities would be useful for testing our proposed model's generalization, reliability, and robustness.
excerpts.note: Concedes that more diverse cell types and imaging modalities are needed to test the model's generalization, reliability and robustness.

figuresNumbers.contains: Single-cell tracking precision 0.98 ± 0.2 and mitosis-detection precision 0.89 ± 0.3, both at the 0.5 IoU threshold (Figures 4A, 4B).
Full-cell-cycle tracking MOTA: DeepSea 0.94 ± 0.2 vs Trackmate 0.29 ± 0.7, over 228 ground-truth cell-cycle trajectories (each >3 consecutive frames).
Segmentation model achieved IoU 0.90 ± 0.2 at the 0.5 IoU matching threshold (DISCUSSION).
DeepSea segmentation model has only 1.9 million parameters; tracking model has 2.1 million parameters, vs >20 million for ROLO/DeepSort/TrackRCNN.

figuresNumbers.item: Pre-trained baseline performance on these images
figuresNumbers.contains: Original pre-trained StarDist models reached ~43% and ~5% average precision on the authors' test sets; existing tracking tools' segmentation precision on these images was lower than 50%.
42% of divisions produced daughter cells of different sizes; smaller-born cells lengthened their cell cycle by about 2 h vs larger sisters; mESC cycle ~10 h with ~2 h G1.
Three phase-contrast datasets: mouse embryonic stem cells (31 sets, 1074 images), bronchial epithelial cells (7 sets, 2010 images), mouse C2C12 muscle progenitor cells (7 sets, 540 images).
Sub-sampling intervals of 5, 10 and 15 min tested (Figure S7C); higher sampling rate reduced tracking failures.

primarySources.topic: CellPose generalist cell-segmentation algorithm (benchmark baseline)
primarySources.citation: Stringer, C., Wang, T., Michaelos, M., Pachitariu, M. (2021). Cellpose: a generalist algorithm for cellular segmentation. Nat. Methods 18, 100-106

primarySources.topic: StarDist star-convex polygon cell detection (benchmark baseline)
primarySources.citation: Schmidt, U., Weigert, M., Broaddus, C., Myers, G. (2018). Cell Detection with Star-Convex Polygons. Springer, pp. 265-273

primarySources.topic: U-Net architecture underlying the segmentation model
primarySources.citation: Ronneberger, O., Fischer, P., Brox, T. (2015). U-net: convolutional networks for biomedical image segmentation. Preprint at arXiv:1505.04597

primarySources.topic: TrackMate cell-tracking tool (benchmark baseline)
primarySources.citation: Ershov, D., Phan, M.-S., Pylvänäinen, J.W., et al. (2022). TrackMate 7. Nat. Methods 19, 829-832

primarySources.topic: Molecular mechanisms of animal cell-size homeostasis (cell-size control basis)
primarySources.citation: Zatulovskiy, E., Skotheim, J.M. (2020). On the molecular mechanisms regulating animal cell size homeostasis. Trends Genet. 36, 360-372

flags: Metadata verified from text: DOI 10.1016/j.crmeth.2023.100500 (line 33); venue Cell Reports Methods 3(6) (line 15), ISSN 2667-2375; Received July 11 2022 / Accepted May 17 2023 / Published June 12 2023 (lines 182-185).
OCR quirks preserved verbatim in excerpts e9 and e11 (e.g. '$0 . 9 8 \pm \ : 0 . 2$', '0.94 ± 0.2') and in e8 ('i n terms', 'CellPose, 15 StarDist,16'); numbers reconciled in figuresNumbers.
Source inconsistency: tracking MOTA reported as 0.94 ± 0.2 in RESULTS (line 111) but as 0.94 ± 0.3 in DISCUSSION (line 127); both retained as printed.

Scope note: paper is a deep-learning image-analysis methods/tool paper; core relevance to our thesis is as a NON-destructive, directly-observed temporal/lineage modality (counterpoint to destructive-sequencing Claim 3) plus the imaging/morphology modality of Axis A. Its molecular readout is limited to morphology (size/shape), not RNA/protein/metabolite — captured as a trilemma nuance under 'Other'.

Modeling-method internals (UNET scaling, residual blocks, progressive learning, loss functions, augmentation) are out of roadmap scope (context boundary: we do not write modeling methods) and were not captured as excerpts.

title: Mass-spectrometry-based proteomics: from single cells to clinical applications
authors: Tiannan Guo, Judith A. Steen & Matthias Mann
venue: Nature, 2025, 639(8053):pages not stated in source; see flags
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/31 - Mass-spectrometry-based proteomics - from single cells to clinical applications_cleaned.md
oneSentence: A Nature Review surveying recent advances in MS-based proteomics technology (sample prep, instrumentation, DIA/DDA acquisition, AI) and its applications across expression, interaction, PTM, structural and chemo-proteomics, single-cell and spatial proteomics, and clinical biomarker discovery, arguing the field is now robust, sensitive and high-throughput enough to move from basic research toward the clinic.
orientation: Strongly on-roadmap and authored by our own group (T. Guo, Westlake): it is the field-level evidence base for the protein modality. It feeds Claim 4/5/6 (protein carries orthogonal, non-substitutable information vs the transcriptome; proteomics directly interrogates the functional machinery; high-throughput spatial proteomics is now feasible), Claim 2/7 (spatial proteomics preserves tissue context that single-cell loses), Claim 8 (multimodal/multi-omics integration), and Claim 9 (data standards, comparability, IVD/QC gaps). It can serve as BOTH ally (proteomics as an indispensable, under-used modality with spatial+clinical capability) and foil (it concedes single-cell proteomics is currently expression-only, that intensity values across MS workflows are not directly comparable, and that biomarker datasets are often underpowered with little cross-study standardization). Note on temporal axis: the paper says little about destructive measurement / paired temporal observation; the few touchpoints to Claim 3 are nuancing concessions, not core support.

excerpts.text: As the direct effector of biological functions and phenotypes, the proteome reflects the complex interplay between genetic predisposition, environmental factors and lifestyle influences. Whereas genomics and transcriptomics inform on cellular potential, and metabolomics focuses on downstream small molecules, proteomics directly interrogates the functional machinery inside and outside cells.
excerpts.note: Positions the proteome as the direct functional effector that genomics/transcriptomics (potential) and metabolomics (downstream) do not capture.

excerpts.text: Although genomics has been the central focus of the past 20 years, the importance of proteomics is becoming more and more evident<sup>1</sup>. The increased complexity at the protein level is due to alternative splicing, varying translation rates, post-translational modifications (PTMs) and proteolytic cleavage.
excerpts.note: Argues protein-level complexity (splicing, translation rates, PTMs, cleavage) exceeds the genome/transcriptome, justifying a distinct proteomic modality.

excerpts.text: Unlike affinity-based methods<sup>2–4</sup>, MS-based proteomics directly identifies and quantifies amino acid sequences, offering unbiased, systematic analyses of protein expression, modifications and interactions across various conditions<sup>5,6</sup> (Box 1). Its strengths include unrivalled quantitative accuracy, specificity and universal applicability across all organisms without requiring specific binding reagents or previous system knowledge except a sequenced genome.
excerpts.note: Frames MS-based proteomics as unbiased, sequence-direct and universally applicable, contrasting it with affinity-based binder methods.

excerpts.text: Recent technological advances have overcome many previous limitations, including completeness of proteome characterization, workflow robustness, throughput and sample quantity requirements. These improvements have expanded the scope of proteomics from bulk analyses to single-cell studies and spatial profiling, providing remarkable insights into cellular heterogeneity and tissue organization.
excerpts.note: Claims technical advances have extended proteomics from bulk to single-cell and spatial scales, addressing throughput and sample-quantity barriers.

excerpts.text: The Evosep One system, which uses preformed gradients, exemplifies this trend, allowing the analysis of 30 to 100+ samples per day with high reproducibility<sup>15</sup>. Another innovation is the μPAC chip system, which replaces packed columns with chip columns created by microlithography, ensuring consistent peptide retention times across laboratories<sup>16</sup>.
excerpts.anchor: The technology of MS-based proteomics, p.2
excerpts.note: Cites preformed-gradient LC (30-100+ samples/day) and micro-lithography chip columns giving consistent retention times across labs as throughput and cross-lab reproducibility gains.

excerpts.text: Data-independent acquisition (DIA) has gained prominence, selecting sequential windows across the entire mass-to-charge range and fragmenting all peptides in each cycle<sup>22,23</sup>. Also compatible with ion mobility<sup>24</sup>, this approach overcomes the stochasticity of DDA, reducing missing values between runs.
excerpts.anchor: The technology of MS-based proteomics, p.2
excerpts.note: States DIA overcomes DDA stochasticity and reduces missing values between runs, improving completeness and comparability across measurements.

excerpts.text: Targeted proteomics remains crucial for clinical applications, allowing sensitive and absolute quantification of specific peptides<sup>41,42</sup>. It can now be scaled to measure hundreds of samples on a single instrument per day using short LC gradients and target thousands of peptides in a single run<sup>43</sup>. We believe the time has come for the community to provide a proteome-wide heavy proteome resource, which would greatly benefit the field for workflow-independent absolute quantification and for quick independent verification for proteins or special biological or clinical importance.
excerpts.anchor: The technology of MS-based proteomics, p.2
excerpts.note: Calls for a community proteome-wide heavy (isotope) standard resource to enable workflow-independent absolute quantification, framed as currently missing.

excerpts.text: Expression proteomics, the large-scale study of protein abundance changes across different conditions or cell types, has become increasingly comprehensive and quantitative, and provides complementary and different information from the transcriptome.
excerpts.note: States expression proteomics gives complementary and different information from the transcriptome.

excerpts.text: In human cell lines, cutting-edge technologies can now quantify more than 10,000 protein groups with minimal measuring time and sample amounts. Adding upfront fractionation yielded near-comprehensive proteomes of more than 13,000 protein groups, indicating that common cell lines express at least 70% of the 20,000 human protein-coding genes<sup>47,48</sup>.
excerpts.note: Quantitative depth: >10,000 protein groups routinely, >13,000 with fractionation, covering at least 70% of 20,000 protein-coding genes in cell lines.

excerpts.text: Furthermore, the proteome has turned out to be a close proxy for the functional state of a cell. The Human Proteome Project has found protein-level evidence for 90% of all protein-coding genes, validating and expanding our understanding of the expressed genome<sup>50</sup>.
excerpts.note: Asserts the proteome is a close proxy for the functional state of a cell; 90% of protein-coding genes have protein-level evidence.

excerpts.text: Improved accuracy in both proteomics and transcriptomics measurements should have increased reported correlations by reducing independent experimental noise, but this does not seem to have happened<sup>52</sup>. Instead, it has become apparent that transcriptome and proteome regulation are distinct in many dynamic situations, such as during development or disease progression<sup>53</sup>.
excerpts.note: Key orthogonality evidence: better measurement did not raise mRNA-protein correlation; transcriptome and proteome regulation are distinct in dynamic states (development, disease).

excerpts.text: Secreted proteins represent another area in which transcriptome– proteome correlations are often low; these proteins have crucial roles in circulation and the microenvironment and can be directly measured by proteomics<sup>54</sup>.
excerpts.note: Secreted proteins (circulation, microenvironment) show low mRNA-protein correlation and are only directly measurable by proteomics.

excerpts.text: Both transcriptomics and proteomics can estimate copy numbers, with proteins typically present at 100-fold or higher copy numbers than their corresponding transcripts, especially in post-mitotic tissues<sup>57</sup>. Although transcripts are often expressed at less than one copy per cell on average, functional proteins tend to have at least 30 copies per cell, even for very-low-abundance complexes in yeast, and more than 100 in mammalian cells<sup>58</sup>.
excerpts.note: Quantitative: proteins present at ~100-fold higher copy number than transcripts; transcripts often <1 copy/cell while functional proteins have >=30 (yeast) to >100 (mammalian) copies.

excerpts.text: The proteome exhibits a much higher dynamic range—the difference between the lowest and the highest expressed protein—than the transcriptome<sup>59</sup>. Transcriptome-to-proteome divergence reflects the complex regulation of protein abundance through synthesis, degradation and PTMs, highlighting the importance of direct proteomic measurements for understanding cellular function.
excerpts.note: States proteome dynamic range exceeds the transcriptome and divergence arises from synthesis/degradation/PTM regulation, so direct protein measurement is needed.

excerpts.text: Proteins, as cellular nanomachines, perform specific functions through interactions with other proteins, RNA<sup>52</sup>, DNA<sup>60,61</sup>, metabolites and small-molecule drugs<sup>62</sup>. Mapping these interactions is fundamental to understanding cellular processes in biological or disease contexts.
excerpts.note: Frames protein-protein, protein-RNA, protein-DNA, protein-metabolite interactions (interactome modality) as fundamental, an information layer beyond abundance.

excerpts.text: A prime example is the recent elucidation of an essentially complete yeast interactome, providing an unprecedented view of the protein interaction landscape in a eukaryotic organism<sup>63</sup>.
excerpts.note: Cites an essentially complete yeast interactome as evidence of proteome-scale interaction (complex) mapping now achievable.

excerpts.text: PTMs have crucial roles in regulating protein function, localization and interactions, with profound consequences for cellular processes, disease mechanisms and therapeutic interventions. MS-based proteomics has emerged as the premier tool for discovering and quantifying PTMs, with recent technological advances substantially increasing the depth and breadth of PTM analysis<sup>69,70</sup>.
excerpts.note: Establishes PTMs as a functionally decisive layer (function, localization, interactions) measurable by MS, not inferable from RNA.

excerpts.text: Researchers can now quantify tens of thousands of PTMs localized to specific amino acids, providing insights into cellular signalling networks and their dynamics in response to stimuli or inhibitors<sup>74–76</sup>.
excerpts.note: Quantitative: tens of thousands of site-localized PTMs now measurable, informing signalling network dynamics.

excerpts.text: It has also become clear that the average phosphorylation stoichiometry is often relatively high—for instance, in the circadian rhythm<sup>80</sup>—whereas it is generally very low for ubiquitylation<sup>81</sup>.
excerpts.note: Stoichiometry differs by PTM type (high for phosphorylation, low for ubiquitylation), detail unavailable from transcript measurement.

excerpts.text: The success of single-cell transcriptomics in defining new cell types has generated tremendous interest in single-cell proteomics, which can provide a direct readout of the functional state of individual cells. However, the limited amount of protein in a single cell (typically 50–500 pg) has posed major challenges for MS-based analysis<sup>108</sup>. Note that unlike transcriptomics, proteomics cannot amplify the quantity measured; however, there is typically more than 100-fold more protein than transcript for every expressed gene, and those proteins reflect the cell-to-cell variance much better.
excerpts.note: Concedes the no-amplification sensitivity barrier (50-500 pg/cell) but argues >100-fold more protein than transcript makes proteins reflect cell-to-cell variance better.

excerpts.text: The introduction of the SCoPE (single-cell proteomics) method, which uses isobaric labelling to combine single cells with a carrier channel, enabled the quantification of about 1,000 proteins across 100 single cells<sup>111</sup>.
excerpts.note: Quantitative milestone: SCoPE quantified ~1,000 proteins across 100 single cells.

excerpts.text: The reduction of LC flow rates has resulted in proportional MS signal gain, allowing the quantification of up to 2,000 proteins across the cell cycle without multiplexing in single HeLa cells using the timsTOF SCP instrument<sup>113</sup>.
excerpts.note: Quantitative: up to 2,000 proteins per single HeLa cell across the cell cycle, label-free, on timsTOF SCP.

excerpts.text: Recent developments— such as multiplexed DIA<sup>114,115</sup> and narrow-window DIA (nDIA) on the Astral mass analyser—have pushed the boundaries even further, quantifying more than 5,000 proteins in single oocytes in a ‘One-Tip’ set-up that eliminates sample loss<sup>116,117</sup>.
excerpts.note: Quantitative: >5,000 proteins in single oocytes via nDIA on Astral with a One-Tip workflow.

excerpts.text: Note that unlike transcriptomics, proteomics cannot amplify the quantity measured; however, there is typically more than 100-fold more protein than transcript for every expressed gene, and those proteins reflect the cell-to-cell variance much better. ... The fact that there are many more copies of proteins than transcripts in single cells means that they are not subject to Poisson or shot noise. This potentially enables single-cell proteomics to define cell states with much fewer cells than transcriptomics allows.
excerpts.note: Argues higher protein copy number means single-cell proteomics is free of Poisson/shot noise and can define cell states with fewer cells than transcriptomics (data-quality argument). OCR note: the two sentences are non-contiguous in source, joined here with an ellipsis.

excerpts.text: Single-cell proteomics: Analysis of protein content of individual cells, revealing cellular heterogeneity—currently limited to expression proteomics.
excerpts.note: Concedes single-cell proteomics is currently limited to expression proteomics (no PTM/interaction/structure at single-cell scale yet) - a foil to the full multimodal-protein argument.

excerpts.text: As technology continues to improve, integration with other singlecell modalities, such as transcriptomics and metabolomics, promises to provide a more comprehensive view of cellular states and functions. The ability to perform parallel transcriptome and proteome analysis from the same single cell is now within reach, offering unprecedented insights into cellular biology.
excerpts.note: States parallel transcriptome+proteome from the same single cell is now within reach, motivating multimodal integration.

excerpts.text: Although single-cell approaches provide valuable information about cellular heterogeneity, they often lack spatial context within tissues. Spatial proteomics aims to bridge this gap by characterizing proteomes while preserving spatial information, providing crucial insights into tissue organization and cell–cell interactions.
excerpts.note: Directly states single-cell (dissociated) approaches lack spatial context; spatial proteomics preserves it to reveal tissue organization and cell-cell interactions.

excerpts.text: One pioneering approach is deep visual proteomics (DVP), which combines imaging of tissue slides with AI-driven cell classification and microdissection, followed by deep proteomic analysis of specific cell types and states<sup>119</sup>. The effective throughput of the method is high, and functional differences between cell types and states in the same tissue context are straightforward to interpret.
excerpts.note: Describes DVP (imaging + AI classification + microdissection + deep proteomics) as a high-throughput spatial-proteomics method preserving tissue context.

excerpts.text: For example, DVP was recently used to uncover key molecular alterations that drive a lethal drug-induced skin rash called toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), with hyperactivation of the JAK–STAT pathway identified as a key driver<sup>120</sup>. This discovery led to the successful repurposing of an approved inhibitor to cure this condition, showcasing the potential of spatial proteomics in personalized medicine.
excerpts.note: Worked example: spatial proteomics (DVP) identified JAK-STAT driver of TEN in tissue context, leading to a curative drug repurposing - spatial signal driving phenotype/therapy.

excerpts.text: Tissue expansion technology, when combined with proteomics, has enabled the study of subcellular structures, such as single nuclei from mouse hepatocytes<sup>121</sup>. The use of micro-scaffolds created by customized three-dimensional printing technology has allowed the generation of complete proteome maps of tissue slices with high spatial resolution<sup>122</sup>.
excerpts.note: Subcellular-to-tissue spatial proteomics: expansion proteomics resolves single nuclei; 3D-printed micro-scaffolds give high-resolution whole-tissue-slice proteome maps.

excerpts.text: Blood plasma, being in contact with all organs, has long been considered a treasure trove of potential biomarkers for various diseases<sup>123,124</sup>. However, its extreme dynamic range—spanning more than ten orders of magnitude, from the most abundant proteins, such as albumin, to the least abundant, such as cytokines—poses a formidable analytical challenge.
excerpts.anchor: Bringing proteomics to the clinic, p.6
excerpts.note: Quantitative: plasma proteome dynamic range spans >10 orders of magnitude (albumin to cytokines), the central analytical challenge for biomarker discovery.

excerpts.text: Current MS-based proteomics workflows routinely measure 300–500 proteins in a single-injection analysis of neat plasma<sup>125–127</sup>. For instance, a workflow using liquid handlers, Evosep HPLC systems and a timsTOF mass spectrometer enabled the rapid analysis of 1,612 samples, successfully uncovering biomarkers of sepsis<sup>128</sup>.
excerpts.anchor: Bringing proteomics to the clinic, p.6
excerpts.note: Quantitative throughput: 300-500 proteins/single-injection neat plasma; 1,612-sample sepsis biomarker study.

excerpts.text: Unlike MS-based proteomics, affinity-based methods (such as Olink and SomaLogic) have been used for population-scale protein quantitative trait loci (QTL) analysis<sup>134</sup>. Evaluating the specificity of the binders used across various sample matrices is essential, especially because limited correlation among major binder platforms has been observed<sup>135,136</sup>. Orthogonal validation with MS-based methods is crucial to address this limitation<sup>137</sup>.
excerpts.anchor: Bringing proteomics to the clinic, p.6
excerpts.note: Limited correlation among major affinity binder platforms (Olink, SomaLogic) requires orthogonal MS validation - a comparability/standards problem across measurement platforms.

excerpts.text: The Clinical Proteomic Tumor Analysis Consortium (CPTAC), launched in 2011, has characterized proteogenomic and PTM changes in more than 1,500 samples and 10 cancer types, revealing new insights, including the identification of proteomic subtypes, prioritization of driver mutations and elucidation of cancer-relevant signalling pathways and PTMs<sup>143,144</sup>. It has also been valuable in pioneering standardization efforts across several clinical centres, and in multi-omics integration.
excerpts.anchor: Bringing proteomics to the clinic, p.6
excerpts.note: CPTAC cited as a large multi-omics, multi-centre standardization effort (>1,500 samples, 10 cancer types) integrating proteogenomic and PTM data.

excerpts.text: Given that the intensity values of different MS workflows are not directly comparable, such models are likely to be constrained to specific instruments unless spike-in standards are used.
excerpts.anchor: Bringing proteomics to the clinic, p.6
excerpts.note: Explicit concession: intensity values across different MS workflows are not directly comparable, constraining models to specific instruments without spike-in standards - core cross-platform-comparability foil.

excerpts.text: With many technological barriers now overcome, it has become increasingly clear that the correct design of cohorts and studies is crucial for obtaining generalizable biomarker panels. For instance, MS-based proteomics datasets for biomarkers studies have often been greatly underpowered. Implementing a requirement for raw data to be deposited in archives enables, in principle, meta-analyses to test and better power biomarker discovery<sup>153</sup>.
excerpts.anchor: Bringing proteomics to the clinic, p.6
excerpts.note: Concedes biomarker datasets are often greatly underpowered and study design / data-sharing standards (raw-data deposition) are needed for generalizable, poolable results.

excerpts.text: However, better means of linking de-identified clinical data with raw files must be implemented to avoid violating privacy regulations.
excerpts.anchor: Bringing proteomics to the clinic, p.6
excerpts.note: Notes a standards/metadata gap: linking de-identified clinical data to raw files is needed for reuse without privacy violations.

excerpts.text: Recent advances in MS-based proteomics have enabled proteomics from biopsy-scale tissue samples, expanding diagnostic or prognostic capabilities<sup>125,145,146</sup> when compared with RNA-based tests on biopsy specimens in clinical settings<sup>147</sup>. The main obstacles to the acceptance of MS-based protein diagnostics are not technical, but more related to the clinical community’s lack of awareness of technological advancements in proteomics.
excerpts.anchor: Bringing proteomics to the clinic, p.6
excerpts.note: Claims biopsy-scale proteomics now expands diagnostic capability vs RNA-based biopsy tests; remaining obstacles are awareness, not technical.

excerpts.text: Note that most tissue-based proteomics studies only report potentially novel protein biomarkers discovered from a limited number of ... retrospective specimens<sup>143,144,150,151</sup>, without further development or validation in the clinical setting. Although some studies have reported in-depth mechanistic investigations of selected proteins and validated protein biomarkers or panels in one or several independent cohorts, few have progressed into clinically applicable tests.
excerpts.anchor: Bringing proteomics to the clinic, p.6
excerpts.note: Concedes most tissue-proteomics studies report biomarkers from limited retrospective cohorts without validation; few reach clinical tests - a reproducibility/validation gap. OCR note: sentence interrupted in source by a stray figure-caption line, joined with an ellipsis.

excerpts.text: More ambitiously, with sufficient proteomics data—in particular, perturbation proteomics data<sup>156</sup>—it might be possible to develop foundation models that embed knowledge about the underlying biology of cells. Although the single-cell transcriptomics community is already exploring this approach<sup>157</sup>, the quantitative and functional nature of proteomics might ultimately make it a more powerful candidate for such models.
excerpts.anchor: Outlook and the role of AI in proteomics, p.7
excerpts.note: Argues that with sufficient (especially perturbation) proteomics data, foundation models could embed cell biology, and the quantitative/functional nature of proteomics may make it a stronger substrate than single-cell transcriptomics - directly engages the virtual-cell foundation-model premise.

excerpts.text: Combining proteomics with genomics, transcriptomics and metabolomics promises a comprehensive understanding of biological systems. Multimodal data integration, facilitated by AI and advanced computational methods, will elucidate intricate molecular networks, revealing the interplay between biomolecules and their functional consequences.
excerpts.anchor: Outlook and the role of AI in proteomics, p.7
excerpts.note: States multimodal integration of proteomics with genomics/transcriptomics/metabolomics is required for a comprehensive systems-level understanding.

excerpts.text: Standardizing experimental protocols, data formats and analysis pipelines will facilitate data integration, reproducibility and cross-study comparisons, ultimately accelerating scientific progress.
excerpts.anchor: Outlook and the role of AI in proteomics, p.7
excerpts.note: Explicit call for standardized protocols, data formats and pipelines to enable integration, reproducibility and cross-study comparison.

excerpts.text: We are delighted that this potential is also increasingly recognized outside the field—as exemplified by the planned massive investment in the π-HuB (proteomic navigator of the human body) project in China<sup>158</sup>.
excerpts.anchor: Outlook and the role of AI in proteomics, p.7
excerpts.note: Cites the large-scale pi-HuB (proteomic navigator of the human body) initiative as evidence of field-scale momentum and resourcing for proteomics.

figuresNumbers.contains: typically 50-500 pg of protein in a single cell; proteomics cannot amplify; >100-fold more protein than transcript per expressed gene
>10,000 protein groups routinely; >13,000 with upfront fractionation; cell lines express at least 70% of the ~20,000 human protein-coding genes
protein-level evidence for 90% of all protein-coding genes
proteins ~100-fold or higher copy number than transcripts; transcripts often <1 copy/cell; functional proteins >=30 copies/cell (yeast), >100 (mammalian); proteome dynamic range exceeds transcriptome

SCoPE ~1,000 proteins across 100 single cells; ~1,500 proteins per circulating tumour cell; up to 2,000 proteins/single HeLa cell (timsTOF SCP); >5,000 proteins in single oocytes (nDIA, Astral, One-Tip)

plasma dynamic range >10 orders of magnitude; 300-500 proteins per single-injection neat plasma; 1,612-sample sepsis study; ~1,000 proteins at up to 100 samples/day on Astral; perchloric acid workflow reaching 1,600+ proteins
tens of thousands of site-localized PTMs quantifiable; up to 32 TMT samples compared directly
launched 2011; >1,500 samples; 10 cancer types; proteogenomic + PTM characterization
definitions of DDA, DIA, DVP, single-cell proteomics (noted as currently limited to expression proteomics), spatial profiling, TMT, PASEF, etc.

title: Automated single-cell proteomics providing sufficient proteome depth to study complex biology beyond cell type classifications
authors: Claudia Ctortecka, Natalie M. Clark, Brian W. Boyle, Anjali Seth, D. R. Mani, Namrata D. Udeshi & Steven A. Carr
venue: Nature Communications, 2024, vol/issue/pages unverified; see flags
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/32 - Automated single-cell proteomics with sufficient proteome depth beyond cell-type classification_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors present an automated label-free single-cell proteomics workflow (proteoCHIP EVO 96 + Evosep One + Bruker timsTOF Ultra) that quantifies on average 3500 (up to 4000) protein groups per single HEK-293T cell without a carrier or match-between-runs, and use it to recapitulate LPS-induced proteome changes in single THP-1 cells.
orientation: A mass-spectrometry single-cell proteomics methods paper. It feeds the protein-modality axis (Claim 4/5) by directly measuring thousands of proteins per single cell and arguing proteins/PTMs drive cellular identity and function in ways sequencing cannot capture, and it supplies Claim 6 (high-throughput single-cell proteomics is becoming feasible). It is a strong concrete example of the resolution-vs-throughput-vs-depth trilemma (Claim 10) via its explicit 40SPD-vs-80SPD depth/throughput trade-off. It can also serve as a FOIL: it is a dissociated, destructive single-cell approach with no spatial context and no per-cell time course (it borrows a separate bulk time-course study for validation), so its own design embodies the spatial/temporal gaps our thesis critiques; and it shows the standardization/missing-value/normalization concerns of Claim 9. Non-binding routing hint.

excerpts.text: Mass spectrometry (MS)-based proteomics is ideally suited to study cellular identity and functionality as those are mostly driven by proteins or their post-translational modi<sup>fi</sup>cations (PTMs)<sup>12,13</sup>.
excerpts.note: Asserts that cellular identity and function are mostly driven by proteins and their PTMs, motivating protein-level measurement.

excerpts.text: In the past few years, single-cell proteomics (SCP) has developed into a viable complement to other sequencing-based omics techniques<sup>6–11</sup>.
excerpts.note: Positions single-cell proteomics as a complement to sequencing-based single-cell omics.

excerpts.text: Currently, most single-cell approaches aim at resolving biological heterogeneity by describing diverse sub-populations or unexpected cell types within a given sample<sup>4,5</sup>.
excerpts.note: States that prevailing single-cell approaches are mostly used for sub-population/cell-type description; the paper's title frames going beyond cell-type classification.

excerpts.text: This study demonstrates that the proteoCHIP EVO 96-based sample preparation with the timsTOF Ultra provides suf<sup>fi</sup>cient proteome depth to study complex biology beyond cell-type classi<sup>fi</sup>cations.
excerpts.note: Central claim: single-cell proteomics now reaches enough depth to study complex biology, not only cell-type classification.

excerpts.text: the newest generation timsTOF Ultra identi<sup>fi</sup>es up to 4000 with an average of 3500 protein groups per single HEK-293T without a carrier or match-between runs.
excerpts.note: Quantitative headline result: up to 4000 (average 3500) protein groups per single cell without carrier or MBR.

excerpts.text: Until recently, the limited protein content of most mammalian cells, which is only about 50–300 pg, represented a signi<sup>fi</sup>cant challenge to MS-based SCP<sup>14</sup>. Even though this is over 1000 times lower than standard input of current MS-based proteomics studies, the latest advances in dedicated work<sup>fl</sup>ows and liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) instrumentation have begun to overcome these sensitivity limitations<sup>15–24</sup>.
excerpts.note: Quantifies the core sensitivity constraint of single-cell proteomics (50-300 pg, >1000x below bulk input) and claims recent advances are overcoming it.

excerpts.text: These strategies, however, suffer from inherent reporter ion signal suppression or ratio compression of isobaric labels, in standard data dependent acquisition (DDA), which negatively impacts quantitative accuracy<sup>26–28</sup>. Additionally, stochastic precursor selection in DDA results in missing data when analyzing large numbers of single cells in multiple TMT plexes<sup>10,29–31</sup>.
excerpts.note: Describes quantitative-accuracy and missing-data problems (ratio compression, stochastic precursor selection) in multiplexed single-cell proteomics.

excerpts.text: The manually transferred samples yielded a median of 582 protein groups per analytical run, while the direct loading of the Evotips resulted in a median of 812 proteins per single cell. This indicated that automated transfer of the sample increased protein groups by 29% in comparison to the manually pipetted ones (Fig. 1b).
excerpts.anchor: Results - The proteoCHIP EVO 96 minimizes adsorptive peptide losses
excerpts.note: Quantifies how manual sample handling reduces yield (582 vs 812 protein groups; 29% gain from automation), bearing on reproducibility/standardization.

excerpts.text: Using the modi<sup>fi</sup>ed diaPASEF acquisition method, together with the 40SPD method, we identi<sup>fi</sup>ed 3000 proteins per single HEK-293T cell (Fig. 1d).
excerpts.anchor: Results - The proteoCHIP EVO 96 minimizes adsorptive peptide losses
excerpts.note: Reports 3000 proteins per single HEK-293T cell with the 40SPD diaPASEF method on the timsTOF SCP.

excerpts.text: Most standard SCP projects require the acquisition of hundreds to thousands of single cells to resolve tissue or population heterogeneity and to understand the underlying biology.
excerpts.anchor: Results - Doubling throughput still yields 3500 proteins
excerpts.note: States that meaningful single-cell studies need hundreds to thousands of cells, framing the throughput requirement.

excerpts.text: As expected, with the short 80SPD separation method, the median number of proteins identi<sup>fi</sup>ed decreased to 1200 per single cell, representing a drop of \~60% compared to 40SPD (Fig. 2a).
excerpts.anchor: Results - Doubling throughput still yields 3500 proteins
excerpts.note: Direct depth-vs-throughput trade-off: doubling throughput (80SPD) drops proteins per cell ~60% to a median of 1200.

excerpts.text: This demonstrates that 2-fold higher single-cell analysis throughput is feasible, at decreased analysis depth. Therefore, the experimental design must take into consideration whether the acquisition of hundreds or thousands of single cells are required for statistical signi<sup>fi</sup>cance and if the proteins of interest can still be reliably quanti<sup>fi</sup>ed to address a speci<sup>fi</sup>c biological question.
excerpts.anchor: Results - Doubling throughput still yields 3500 proteins
excerpts.note: Authors explicitly frame the throughput-vs-depth trade-off as a design decision the experimenter must balance.

excerpts.text: The increased throughput using the 80SPD separation method reduced the dynamic range of single-cell analysis by one order of magnitude (Fig. 2c).
excerpts.anchor: Results - Doubling throughput still yields 3500 proteins
excerpts.note: Higher throughput costs dynamic range (reduced by one order of magnitude), losing low-abundance proteins.

excerpts.text: Specifically, using the 40SPD method, we identify over 50 E3 ubiquitin-protein ligases in single cells while 13 are recovered using the 80SPD method (Fig. 2c).
excerpts.anchor: Results - Doubling throughput still yields 3500 proteins
excerpts.note: Demonstrates depth: over 50 E3 ubiquitin-protein ligases (a functionally important protein family) detected at 40SPD vs only 13 at 80SPD.

excerpts.text: We demonstrate that single HEK-293T cells acquired on the timsTOF Ultra with the 40SPD chromatographic separation yield up to 4000 protein groups and on average 3500 protein groups spanning 4 orders of magnitude in abundance.
excerpts.note: Restates headline depth: up to 4000 / average 3500 protein groups per single cell over 4 orders of magnitude in abundance.

excerpts.text: We demonstrate that protein identi<sup>fi</sup>cations are reduced by close to 60% when decreasing the gradient length by 50%. This additionally reduces the overall protein abundance and decreases detection limits by an order of magnitude.
excerpts.note: Restates the trade-off in the discussion: halving gradient length cuts identifications ~60% and worsens detection limits by an order of magnitude.

excerpts.text: Importantly, along with sample preparation capabilities and instrument sensitivity, the cell size highly impacts proteome depth.
excerpts.note: Concedes cell size strongly determines achievable proteome depth (HEK-293T 26.7 um vs THP-1 13.7 um), a biological constraint on single-cell measurement.

excerpts.text: Compared to bulk data, SCP datasets exhibit distinct considerations for normalization, due to technical variability throughout the sample processing, or differences in cell size<sup>60,61</sup> (Supplementary Fig. 3; HEK-293T – 26.7 µm, THP-1 – 13.7 µm). Thus, we normalized our SCP data using SCnorm<sup>62</sup>, which is an approach originally developed for single-cell transcriptomics studies
excerpts.anchor: Results - LPS-induced proteome changes are validated in single cells
excerpts.note: Notes single-cell proteomics needs distinct normalization for technical variability and cell-size differences, here borrowing a single-cell-transcriptomics normalization method (SCnorm).

excerpts.text: After normalization, we evaluated what proportion of the variance could be due to technical effects by processing the cells on different days using PCA regression (see Methods) and observed that approximately 9.28% of the variance can be explained by this batch effect (Supplementary Fig. 5a). We therefore batch-corrected our data using the limma package<sup>63</sup>
excerpts.anchor: Results - LPS-induced proteome changes are validated in single cells
excerpts.note: Quantifies a batch effect (9.28% of variance from processing on different days) requiring explicit batch correction, illustrating cross-batch comparability challenges.

excerpts.text: Additionally, we compared our results to a previous in-depth proteomics analysis of bulk THP-1 cells treated with LPS<sup>57</sup>. Mulvey and co-authors pro<sup>fi</sup>led the effects of LPS treatment across a 24-hour time course and identi<sup>fi</sup>ed 4882 proteins. We found 26% of those quanti<sup>fi</sup>ed proteins also in our single cell THP-1 dataset, while the bulk study identi<sup>fi</sup>ed 3009 additional proteins (Supplementary Fig. 6a).
excerpts.anchor: Results - LPS-induced proteome changes are validated in single cells
excerpts.note: The temporal (24-h time course) information comes from a separate bulk study; the single-cell dataset captures only a static snapshot and recovers just 26% of the bulk-identified proteins (bulk found 3009 more).

excerpts.text: This and the regulation of other key LPS response proteins corroborates previous <sup>fi</sup>ndings that even though fold changes between bulk and single cell proteomics datasets do not scale, the biological regulation correlates (Supplementary Fig. 6b)<sup>17</sup>.
excerpts.anchor: Results - LPS-induced proteome changes are validated in single cells
excerpts.note: Concedes that fold changes between bulk and single-cell proteomics do not scale (only the direction of regulation correlates), a cross-platform comparability caveat.

excerpts.text: For our comparative studies, we combine only single cells without the usage of a carrier sample for data analysis to eliminate the possibility of identi<sup>fi</sup>cation transfer without controlling the false discovery rate across the entire dataset<sup>50,51</sup>.
excerpts.anchor: Results - Doubling throughput still yields 3500 proteins
excerpts.note: Methodological rigor note: avoids carrier samples to prevent uncontrolled identification transfer / FDR issues, relevant to data-quality standards.

excerpts.text: Similarly, the analysis of single cells in combination with higher input samples might impact identi<sup>fi</sup>cation accuracy due to the lack of false discovery rate <sup>fi</sup>ltering post-match between runs. In this case, identi<sup>fi</sup>cations are heavily in<sup>fl</sup>uenced by the representation of sub-populations within higher input samples.
excerpts.note: Conceded limitation: combining single cells with higher-input carrier/reference samples can bias identifications due to missing post-MBR FDR control.

figuresNumbers.contains: timsTOF Ultra identifies up to 4000 protein groups, average 3500 per single HEK-293T cell at 40SPD, without carrier or match-between-runs; spans 4 orders of magnitude.

Manual transfer: median 582 protein groups per run; direct Evotip loading: median 812 per single cell; automation gives 29% more protein groups. Peptide sequence overlap 99% (37 unique peptides in no-transfer sample).
nanoElute 30-min gradient: 1547 proteins/cell average; modified diaPASEF + 40SPD Evosep: 3000 proteins/single HEK-293T cell (~2-fold more, 30% higher throughput).
Direct Evosep gives 12% greater data completeness vs manual nanoElute; samples span close to 5 orders of magnitude.
timsTOF Ultra 40SPD: >3500 proteins/cell (15% higher than timsTOF SCP); 80SPD: median 1200 proteins/cell (~60% drop vs 40SPD).
>50 E3 ubiquitin-protein ligases detected at 40SPD vs 13 at 80SPD; 80SPD reduces dynamic range by one order of magnitude; proteins with MS1 abundance <1e3 in 40SPD drop below detection in 80SPD.

THP-1 LPS experiment: up to 1537 protein groups, median 1149 per single cell; PCA PC1 75.8% variance; 214 proteins significantly up, 15 down (229 significant, adj. p <= 0.05). LPS n=77/84, DMSO control, 12 h, 200 ng/mL.
HEK-293T diameter 26.7 um; THP-1 diameter 13.7 um (~2-fold smaller), correlating with lower protein IDs per THP-1 cell.

~9.28% of variance explained by day-of-processing batch effect; corrected with limma; SCnorm (originally single-cell transcriptomics) used for normalization; pan-human DIA-NN library (301,643 precursors, 10,886 protein groups).

Mulvey et al. bulk THP-1 LPS 24-h time course identified 4882 proteins; single-cell dataset recovered 26% of those; bulk found 3009 additional proteins. Fold changes do not scale but direction of regulation correlates.

primarySources.citation: Mulvey, C. M. et al. Spatiotemporal proteomic profiling of the pro-inflammatory response to lipopolysaccharide in the THP-1 human leukaemia cell line. Nat. Commun. 12, 5773 (2021)

primarySources.topic: Cellular identity/function driven by proteins and PTMs
