flags: Metadata partially unverified: venue is Nature Communications 2024 (DOI 10.1038/s41467-024-49651-w, '(c) The Author(s) 2024'), but volume/issue/article-number/page range are not present in the cleaned MD; volume set to 'unverified; see flags'. Received/accepted/published dates absent from cleaned text.

OCR quirks throughout the source: many ligature/superscript artifacts (e.g. 'suf<sup>fi</sup>cient', 'identi<sup>fi</sup>cation', 'work<sup>fl</sup>ow', '\~30-min', '\~60%'); verbatim excerpts copy these as-is.

Scope note: this is an MS-based single-cell proteomics METHODS paper; most of it (chip/LC/MS engineering detail) is off-roadmap and was not captured. Captured excerpts focus on protein-modality value (Claim 4/5), feasibility of high-throughput SCP (Claim 6), the depth/throughput/dynamic-range trade-off (Claim 10), and normalization/batch/comparability issues (Claim 9).

Dual-use note: the paper can serve as SUPPORT (protein modality reaches functional depth in single cells) AND as a FOIL (it is a dissociated, destructive, snapshot-only single-cell method with no spatial context and no per-cell time course; temporal info is imported from a separate bulk study, ref 57).

Read at source: ref #57 (Mulvey et al., Nat. Commun. 2021) is the spatiotemporal/time-course bulk dataset and is worth citing first-hand for the temporal axis (Axis B). Ref #17 (Specht SCoPE2) worth first-hand for the bulk-vs-single-cell fold-change-do-not-scale point.

'Other'-style angle considered but mapped to roadmap: the borrowed single-cell-transcriptomics normalization (SCnorm) and pan-human library reflect Claim 9 standardization/comparability, so no 'Other' touchpoint was emitted.

title: Simultaneous quantification of mRNA and protein in single cells reveals posttranscriptional effects of genetic variation
authors: Christian Brion, Sheila M Lutz, Frank Wolfgang Albert
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/33 - Simultaneous quantification of mRNA and protein in single cells reveals post-transcriptional effects_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors built a CRISPR-based dual fluorescent reporter that simultaneously quantifies mRNA production and protein abundance of the same gene in single live yeast cells, then mapped 86 trans-acting loci across ten genes and found that fewer than 20% had concordant effects on mRNA and protein, with most loci affecting protein but not mRNA.
orientation: Direct ammunition for the roadmap claim that modalities carry orthogonal, non-substitutable information: it shows, in a confounder-controlled same-cell design, that genetic effects on protein largely do not match effects on mRNA, so RNA cannot stand in for protein. It also feeds the 'cross-study non-comparability / lack of standards' claim (the paper's whole motivation is that prior eQTL-vs-pQTL discrepancies were confounded by being measured in different labs, tissues, and conditions) and the 'destructive/lysis vs throughput' measurement-tradeoff claim (its reporter avoids lysis to gain throughput). Could serve as support for protein-as-irreplaceable-modality and as a methodological foil illustrating the confounding problem.

excerpts.text: Less than 20% of these loci had concordant effects on mRNA and protein of the same gene. Most loci influenced protein but not mRNA of a given gene.
excerpts.note: Headline result: genetic effects on mRNA and protein of the same gene are largely non-overlapping, with protein-specific effects dominating.

excerpts.text: These results demonstrate complex, posttranscriptional genetic effects on gene expression.
excerpts.note: States that protein levels are shaped by post-transcriptional processes acting independently of mRNA. (Echo of e1's central thesis; touchpoint left to the strongest distinct statements.)

excerpts.text: Post-transcriptional mechanisms can shape protein levels independently of mRNA abundance (Buccitelli and Selbach, 2020; Csa´rdi et al., 2015; McCarthy, 1998), raising important questions about how DNA variation shapes protein levels compared to mRNA abundance.
excerpts.note: Frames the premise that mRNA abundance does not fully determine protein abundance.

excerpts.text: It is unclear whether most eQTLs, which by definition affect mRNA, also affect the protein abundance of the same genes, such that they also result in a protein-QTL (pQTL) (Damerval et al., 1994). It is also unclear to what extent there are protein-specific pQTLs that influence protein abundance without affecting the mRNA of the same gene.
excerpts.note: Poses the open question of whether mRNA-level variation maps onto protein-level variation. (Framing echo; not restamped.)

excerpts.text: However, other work found many cis-eQTLs whose effects on protein were smaller than those on mRNA, suggesting the existence of mechanisms that buffer protein levels against variation in mRNA abundance (Battle et al., 2015; Ghazalpour et al., 2011; Mirauta et $a \mathbf { l . } _ { }$ 2020).
excerpts.note: Reports prior evidence of buffering that decouples protein from mRNA, even in cis. (Prior-work echo of the decoupling theme; not restamped.)

excerpts.text: In mouse crosses and some yeast crosses, (Mirauta et al., 2020) transeQTLs and trans-pQTLs mapped for the same genes showed very little overlap, suggesting that while many trans-eQTLs are buffered at the protein level, there are also many trans-pQTLs that affect proteins via mechanisms that do not affect mRNA (Chick et al., 2016; Foss et al., 2011; Foss et al., 2007; Ghazalpour et al., 2011; Williams et al., 2016).
excerpts.note: Prior literature: trans-acting genetic effects on mRNA and protein show very little overlap. (Background echo; this paper's own data carry the stamp.)

excerpts.text: In yeast and humans, genetic effects on translation as measured by ribosome profiling were similar to those on mRNA, suggesting that translation cannot account for protein-specific pQTLs (Albert et al., 2014a; Battle et $a \mathbf { l . } _ { \mathbf { \ell } }$ 2015; Cenik et $a \mathbf { l . }$ 2015). Instead, proteinspecific effects may arise from protein degradation, especially for proteins that are part of protein complexes (Chick et $a \mathbf { l . } _ { \mathbf { \ell } }$ 2016; Großbach et $a \mathbf { l . } _ { \mathbf { \ell } }$ 2019; He et $a \mathbf { l . } _ { \mathbf { \ell } }$ 2020).
excerpts.note: Identifies protein degradation (not translation) as the layer producing protein-specific variation invisible at the mRNA level.

excerpts.text: Together, this literature suggests that genetic variants can independently affect the different layers of gene expression regulation.
excerpts.note: Summarizes that the regulatory layers (mRNA vs protein) are independently controllable, i.e. not mutually substitutable readouts. (Summary echo of cited literature; not restamped.)

excerpts.text: First, small sample sizes of dozens to a few hundred individuals were used in most studies to date. Most eQTLs and pQTLs change the expression of the gene they affect by 10% or less (Albert et al., 2018). Detecting such small-effect loci requires high statistical power... With limited sample size, real loci can be missed... Thus, low statistical power can inflate discrepancies between eQTLs and pQTLs.
excerpts.note: Caveat conceded by the authors: underpowered studies can artificially inflate apparent mRNA-protein discordance; a foil to a naive reading of orthogonality.

excerpts.text: Second, experimental differences between studies may also inflate discrepancies between eQTLs and pQTLs. In nearly all cases, eQTLs and pQTLs were compared between experiments conducted in different laboratories, sometimes using different experimental designs, and, in the case of some human studies, comparing eQTLs and pQTLs mapped in different tissues. These comparisons are likely to be confounded by environmental influences, which can drastically alter the effects of regulatory variants.
excerpts.note: Core motivation: cross-study, cross-lab, cross-tissue comparison of separately produced datasets is confounded and non-comparable.

excerpts.text: To date, no study of regulatory variation has measured mRNA and protein with high statistical power and in the same samples. This challenge is not easy to overcome, because combined application of RNA-sequencing (RNA-seq) and mass spectrometry in thousands of matched samples remains prohibitively expensive.
excerpts.note: Concedes that paired, same-sample multimodal (mRNA + protein) measurement at scale is prohibitively expensive and had not been done.

excerpts.text: We reasoned that such an approach would equalize all environmental confounders and most of the technical biases that could obscure the relationship between eQTLs and pQTLs.
excerpts.note: Argues that only same-cell, same-condition measurement removes the environmental and technical confounders that plague cross-study integration.

excerpts.text: Although our approach was explicitly designed to maximize the chance of detecting similar genetic effects on mRNA and protein, the majority of the identified loci affected only mRNA or protein for a given gene, or had discordant effects on mRNA and protein. These results demonstrate considerable differences in the genetic basis of variation in mRNA vs protein abundance.
excerpts.note: Even a design biased toward finding agreement still found mRNA and protein variation to be largely independent. (Abstract-level restatement of e1; quote kept, touchpoint not duplicated.)

excerpts.text: After correcting for cell size (Figure 3—figure supplement 2), mCherry and GFP were positively correlated for all tested genes (Figure 3—figure supplement 1B). The strength of the correlation varied from gene to gene. Lower correlations between mCherry and GFP were observed for the genes with many differences between published eQTLs and pQTLs compared to those with more concordant eQTLs and pQTLs. Thus, different genes are influenced by mRNA-specific or protein-specific variation to different degrees.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Simultaneous mapping of genetic variation affecting mRNA and protein levels
excerpts.note: Single-cell mRNA-protein correlation is positive but partial and gene-dependent, indicating gene-specific mRNA- or protein-specific regulation.

excerpts.text: Across the ten genes, we detected 78 protein-QTLs and 44 mRNA-QTLs (Figure 4—source data 2 and Figure 4—source data 3). By design, all detected loci were trans-acting, and most were located on a different chromosome than the tagged gene.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Simultaneous mapping of genetic variation affecting mRNA and protein levels
excerpts.note: Quantifies that far more protein-QTLs than mRNA-QTLs were detected across the ten genes. (Count also captured in figuresNumbers; the layer-specific breakdown carries the stamp at e16.)

excerpts.text: Of these 86 loci, 16 affected mRNA and protein of a given gene in the same direction... A majority of the loci corresponded to protein-QTLs that did not overlap an mRNA-QTL. These 52 protein-specific QTLs may arise from variants that affect translation or protein degradation, without an effect on mRNA production.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Differences between mRNA-QTLs and protein-QTLs
excerpts.note: Breakdown of 86 loci: only 16 concordant, 52 protein-specific; protein variation that mRNA cannot report on.

excerpts.text: There were eleven mRNA-QTLs that did not overlap with a protein-QTL and seven loci where mRNA-QTLs and protein-QTLs overlapped but had discordant effects. These two categories may occur when protein abundance and mRNA production of the same gene are regulated separately, through two different trans-acting pathways.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Differences between mRNA-QTLs and protein-QTLs
excerpts.note: Documents mRNA-specific and discordant (opposite-direction) loci, arguing mRNA and protein of one gene can be regulated through separate pathways.

excerpts.text: While more than 73% of loci were specific for mRNA or protein, this difference might be inflated by loci that are truly concordant, but at which either the mRNA-QTL or the protein-QTL narrowly failed to meet the significance threshold. To bypass this potential limitation, we compared effect sizes, expressed as DAF, at significant mRNA-QTLs or protein-QTLs, irrespective of the significance of the locus in the other data (Figure 5B).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Differences between mRNA-QTLs and protein-QTLs
excerpts.note: Author-conceded caveat that significance thresholds can inflate apparent specificity; they perform a threshold-free effect-size analysis to address it.

excerpts.text: When considering all loci, we observed a significant, positive correlation between mRNA and protein effect sizes (r = 0.41, p-value=8.4 - 10<sup> 5</sup>, Figure 5B). This overall correlation was almost exclusively driven by the concordant QTL pairs, whose effect sizes showed a strong correlation $( \mathsf { r } = 0 . 8 8 \mathsf { p } \mathsf { - v a l u e } = 9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } )$ . In sharp contrast, neither protein-specific QTLs (r = 0.2, p-value=0.23) nor mRNA-specific QTLs (r =  0.05, p-value=0.9) had correlated effects across the two data types, as expected if these loci specifically affected only mRNA or only protein.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Differences between mRNA-QTLs and protein-QTLs
excerpts.note: Threshold-free analysis: the weak overall mRNA-protein effect-size correlation comes only from concordant loci; specific loci have essentially zero cross-modality correlation.

excerpts.text: Overall, only 64% of QTLs affected mRNA and protein in the same direction. While this was more than the 50% expected by chance (binomial test p-value=0.006), it left 36% of loci with discordant effects... Together, these results are consistent with the existence of many QTLs that specifically affect mRNA production or protein abundance.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Differences between mRNA-QTLs and protein-QTLs
excerpts.note: Even by direction alone, 36% of loci move mRNA and protein in opposite directions; mRNA direction is an unreliable proxy for protein direction. (Direction stat; correlation stat at e19 carries the distinct Claim 4 stamp.)

excerpts.text: Consistent with a protein-specific trans-effect on $G P D 1 ,$ deletion of YAK1 in a strain in which GPD1 was tagged with GFPgRNA caused a reduction of green fluorescence but had no detectable effect on mCherry fluorescence (Figure 6F). Further, qPCR indicated no difference in the level of GPD1-GFP mRNA in $\yen 4K 1^ { \circ 5 7 8 ^ { \star } }$ or yak1D compared to matched YAK1<sup>wt</sup> (Figure 6F).
excerpts.anchor: Results — A premature stop mutation in YAK1 affects gene expression posttranscriptionally
excerpts.note: Validated causal example: a YAK1 stop variant changes Gpd1 protein with no change in GPD1 mRNA, a clean protein-specific trans-effect.

excerpts.text: By comparing mass spectrometry and RNA-seq results, we classified genes as affected only at the mRNA level (58 genes up, and 118 genes down-regulated), only at the protein level (60 genes up, and 50 genes down-regulated), or at both mRNA and protein (15 genes up, and 27 genes down-regulated).
excerpts.anchor: Results — A premature stop mutation in YAK1 affects gene expression posttranscriptionally
excerpts.note: Genome-wide RNA-seq + mass-spec comparison of one variant: large protein-only and mRNA-only gene sets, only a minority affected at both layers.

excerpts.text: Nevertheless, of the 86 trans-acting loci that contributed to variation in the expression of ten genes, 84% did not have concordant effects on mRNA production and protein abundance. This result demonstrates the importance of variants that act on specific layers of gene expression.
excerpts.note: Restates the central finding: 84% of loci act layer-specifically rather than concordantly on mRNA and protein. (Restatement of e1/e16; quote kept for the precise 84% figure, touchpoint not duplicated.)

excerpts.text: While half of the mRNA-QTLs we detected had concordant effects on protein (16 out of 34), most protein-QTLs had no effects on mRNA (52 out of 75), in line with observations from prior work (Albert et al., 2014b; Albert et al., 2018; Foss et al., 2011). That 70% of our protein-QTLs had protein-specific effects suggests that the causal variants underlying many of these loci affect posttranscriptional processes, i.e. mRNA stability, translation, or protein degradation.
excerpts.note: 70% of protein-QTLs are protein-specific, attributed to post-transcriptional mechanisms invisible to mRNA measurement. (Restates e16/e24 proportions; mechanism stamp held at e7/e16.)

excerpts.text: A well-studied example of this phenomenon is the regulation of expression of genes that encode members of a protein complex, in which excess protein molecules that cannot be incorporated in the complex are rapidly degraded (Taggart et al., 2020). Among the genes we investigated, RPS10A encodes a part of the ribosome small subunit complex. RPS10A showed the highest number of mRNA-specific QTLs, possibly because Rps10a is subject to buffering mechanisms.
excerpts.note: Protein-complex stoichiometry buffering is offered as a mechanism decoupling protein abundance from mRNA, illustrating non-substitutability of the modalities.

excerpts.text: Standard methods for mRNA quantification require lysis of cell cultures or tissues, constraining sample throughput and statistical power for mapping regulatory variation. Single-cell RNA-seq (Picelli, 2017; Tang et al., 2009) and in situ fluorescent hybridization techniques... are improving rapidly... However, these approaches still have throughput that is orders of magnitude below that available using FACS. Further, they involve cell lysis or fixation, precluding bulk segregant approaches that rely on growing cells after sorting.
excerpts.note: Frames the throughput-vs-lysis tradeoff: standard mRNA quantification destroys cells, and live-cell FACS-based readout is what enables high-throughput, growth-after-sort approaches.

excerpts.text: By contrast, our reporter allows quantification of mRNA production of a given gene within millions of single, live cells by flow cytometry.
excerpts.note: Live-cell, non-destructive single-cell readout at millions-of-cells throughput, contrasting with destructive lysis-based methods.

excerpts.text: The mCherry used here has a maturation time of about 40 min (Merzlyak et al., 2007), which limits the temporal resolution at which we can observe dynamic expression changes. Fluorescent proteins with faster maturation times and shorter half-lives, could enable precise investigation of rapid temporal change in mRNA production.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: reporter maturation time caps temporal resolution for observing dynamic / time-resolved expression changes.

excerpts.text: Yet, fewer than 20% of the detected trans-acting loci we detected had concordant effects on mRNA and protein levels, providing strong support for the existence of discordant trans effects on mRNA vs proteins... The fact that the majority of trans-QTLs identified here were protein-specific suggests that protein abundance is under more complex genetic control than mRNA abundance.
excerpts.note: Concluding claim that protein abundance is under more complex, distinct genetic control than mRNA, so protein is an irreplaceable functional readout.

figuresNumbers.contains: 86 trans-acting loci mapped across ten genes; <20% (84% non-concordant) had concordant effects on mRNA and protein.
78 protein-QTLs and 44 mRNA-QTLs detected across ten genes (before/after MKT1 removal: mRNA-QTL median per gene = 3, protein-QTL median = 7).
Of 86 loci: 16 concordant (same direction), 7 discordant (opposite direction), 11 mRNA-specific, 52 protein-specific.
All loci: mRNA vs protein effect-size r = 0.41 (p = 8.4e-5); concordant pairs r = 0.88 (p = 9e-6); protein-specific r = 0.2 (p = 0.23); mRNA-specific r = -0.05 (p = 0.9).
Only 64% of QTLs affected mRNA and protein in the same direction (binomial p = 0.006), leaving 36% discordant; protein-specific 63% same-direction (p = 0.04); mRNA-specific 55% (p = 0.5, n.s.).
Protein-QTL effects correlated with prior pQTLs across studies, Pearson r = 0.73 (p < 1e-15); mRNA-QTL effects r = 0.44 (p = 5e-6).

Of 5755 mRNA transcripts, 262 up / 310 down (BH p < 0.05); of 2590 proteins, 82 down / 82 up (BH p < 0.05); classified mRNA-only (58 up, 118 down), protein-only (60 up, 50 down), both (15 up, 27 down).
Linear mCherry-vs-mRNA relationship up to ~half of ACT1 mRNA abundance; 95% of S. cerevisiae genes fall below this threshold, so >half of genome can be dual-quantified.
Hammerhead ribozyme self-cleavage rate 1.5 per minute; median yeast mRNA half-life 3.6 min; mCherry maturation time ~40 min; LOD threshold 4.5 at FDR ~7%.

OCR quirks preserved verbatim in several excerpts (e.g. garbled LaTeX-like fragments around author 'et al.' citations and r-values such as 'r = 0.88', and the YAK1 variant repeatedly mis-rendered as '$\yen 4K 123,456$' / '$\yen 4K 1^{Q578*}$' — the intended notation is YAK1 Q578*).

Scope note: this paper is yeast (S. cerevisiae) bulk-segregant QTL mapping; cited for the modality-orthogonality (mRNA cannot predict protein) and cross-study-non-comparability arguments, not as a spatial/single-cell-organism example.

Read at source: Battle et al., 2015 (Science) and Buccitelli & Selbach 2020 (Nat Rev Genet) are strong first-hand sources for the general mRNA-protein decoupling claim if the writer wants a non-yeast or review citation.

Axis A (modality=protein) is the paper's core relevance but is captured under Claim 4 / Claim 5 touchpoints rather than as a separate 'Axis A' claimId, since the roadmap's Axis A is a table dimension not a numbered claim; Axis B used for the one temporal-resolution limitation.

title: Experimental reproducibility limits the correlation between mRNA and protein abundances in tumor proteomic profiles
authors: Swathi Ramachandra Upadhya and Colm J. Ryan
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/34 - Experimental reproducibility limits the correlation between mRNA and protein abundances_cleaned.md
oneSentence: By analyzing tumor and cell-line datasets with replicate proteomic (and transcriptomic) profiles, the authors quantify how reproducibly individual proteins/transcripts are measured and show that measurement reproducibility explains a substantial fraction of the otherwise-unexplained variation in mRNA-protein correlation, with more reproducibly measured proteins having higher mRNA-protein correlation.
orientation: Sits at the intersection of our Claim 4 (modalities are non-substitutable; RNA does not predict protein) and Claim 9 (cross-study incomparability / lack of standards). It is a double-edged source: it documents the well-known moderate mRNA-protein correlation (support for 'RNA cannot stand in for protein'), but it argues a large part of that gap is measurement error/reproducibility rather than post-transcriptional biology, which nuances naive 'orthogonal-information' readings. It is strong, quote-rich evidence for Claim 9 (pipeline choices, summary statistics, inclusion criteria, and platforms make reported correlations non-comparable across papers) and touches Claim 1 (limits of predicting protein from mRNA via ML).

excerpts.text: Large-scale studies of human proteomes have revealed only a moderate correlation between mRNA and protein abundances. It is unclear to what extent this moderate correlation reflects post-transcriptional regulation and to what extent it reflects measurement error.
excerpts.note: States the central observed fact: mRNA and protein abundances are only moderately correlated, and the cause (biology vs measurement error) is unresolved.

excerpts.text: As transcriptomes are easier to quantify than proteomes, mRNA abundances are often used as a proxy for protein abundances. However, the relationship between mRNA abundances and protein abundances is complex and non-linear and varies significantly from protein to protein.
excerpts.note: mRNA is commonly used as a proxy for protein, but the mRNA-protein relationship is complex, non-linear, and protein-specific.

excerpts.text: Consistent with this, largescale studies in humans and model organisms have revealed that for most genes there is only a moderate correlation between mRNA and protein abundances (Buccitelli and Selbach, 2020; Vogel and Marcotte, 2012).
excerpts.note: For most genes mRNA and protein are only moderately correlated across humans and model organisms.

excerpts.text: These studies have reported an average mRNA-protein correlation in the range of 0.2–0.5 (Mertins et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2014, 2016). This moderate correlation between mRNA and protein abundances can be attributed to both biological and technical factors. Major biological factors that influence mRNA-protein correlation include translation rates that vary across proteins and conditions, highly variable halflives for both proteins and mRNAs, and post-translational modifications that can alter protein stability and degradation (Buccitelli and Selbach, 2020).
excerpts.note: Quantifies the typical tumor mRNA-protein correlation (0.2-0.5) and lists biological drivers (translation rate, half-life, PTMs) that decouple RNA from protein.

excerpts.text: Our technical ability to accurately and reproducibly quantify both mRNAs and proteins is potentially a major factor that influences the mRNA-protein correlation. If the error in our measurements is large, we would expect this error to reduce the correlation between mRNA and protein even in the absence of the biological factors outlined above.
excerpts.note: Core thesis: measurement error itself depresses mRNA-protein correlation independent of biology; nuances the interpretation of low correlation as evidence of orthogonal biological information.

excerpts.text: The average mRNA-protein correlation reported for different tumor proteomic profiling efforts varies substantially across studies—ranging from 0.23 in an early proteomic study of colorectal cancer (Zhang et al., 2014) to 0.53 in a recent study of lung adenocarcinoma (Gillette et al., 2020) (Table 1). However, it is not meaningful to directly compare the reported correlations because the methods used to quantify the mRNA-protein correlation have varied across studies—different studies have used different summary statistics (mean versus median), different correlation metrics (Pearson versus Spearman), and different criteria for protein inclusion (e.g., no missing values, at least 30% measured values, only the 10% most variable proteins) (Table 1).
excerpts.anchor: Results: A standardized pipeline reveals differences in the mRNA-protein correlation across studies
excerpts.note: Reported mRNA-protein correlations are not directly comparable across studies because of differing summary statistics, correlation metrics, and protein-inclusion criteria; a standardized pipeline is needed.

excerpts.text: For example, the correlation recalculated for endometrial cancer (0.48) was the same as originally reported (Dou et al., 2020), while the recalculated correlation for colon cancer was much lower than that reported by the authors (0.27 versus 0.48) (Vasaikar et al., 2019). This is because the colon cancer study reported the mean mRNA-protein correlation for only the 10% most variable proteins rather than the full set of proteins. These highly variable proteins have higher than average mRNA-protein correlations.
excerpts.anchor: Results: A standardized pipeline reveals differences in the mRNA-protein correlation across studies
excerpts.note: Concrete example of cross-study incomparability: an analytic choice (top 10% most variable proteins) inflated a reported correlation from 0.27 to 0.48.

excerpts.text: More recent studies appear to have higher mRNA-protein correlations, e.g., we observe a mean of 0.49 for studies published after 2019 versus 0.35 for studies published in 2016 or earlier (Table 1). This cannot simply be attributed to differences in the cancer types studied in different years, as the two cancer types profiled twice (colon and breast) see an improvement from the earlier studies (Table 1). This would suggest that technical and experimental factors may influence the reported mRNA-protein correlations and that improvements in either technology or experimental protocols have resulted in improved mRNA-protein correlations over time.
excerpts.anchor: Results: A standardized pipeline reveals differences in the mRNA-protein correlation across studies
excerpts.note: Correlation rose over time (0.35 pre-2016 to 0.49 post-2019) with technology/protocol improvements, implying the apparent mRNA-protein decoupling is partly a moving technical artifact, not a fixed biological constant.

excerpts.text: Many biological factors that influence mRNA-protein correlation, such as post-transcriptional regulation, are not relevant in the case of replicate measurements of proteins, and so we expected the replicate proteomic profiles to be more highly correlated than mRNA and protein profiles. This was indeed the case for all studies. The median protein-protein reproducibility for the replicate proteomic profiles from the CCLE dataset was 0.72 (Figure 1B; Table S2), whereas the median mRNA-protein correlation was only 0.48 (Table 1).
excerpts.anchor: Results: The correlation across replicate proteomic profiles is only moderate
excerpts.note: Even technical replicate proteomes of the same samples correlate only moderately (CCLE median 0.72), quantifying the irreducible measurement noise in proteomics.

excerpts.text: Overall, we can conclude that although protein-protein reproducibility is consistently higher than mRNA-protein correlations, the protein-protein reproducibility is still only moderate.
excerpts.anchor: Results: The correlation across replicate proteomic profiles is only moderate
excerpts.note: Replicate-to-replicate protein measurement reproducibility is itself only moderate, setting an upper bound on achievable mRNA-protein correlation.

excerpts.text: We found, for all three studies, that the median mRNAprotein correlation increases with protein-protein reproducibility (Figure 2). The colon cancer study shows a difference in the median mRNA-protein correlation of 0.33 between the first and last deciles of protein reproducibility. Similarly, ovarian cancer data show a difference of 0.35, and the CCLE data show a difference of 0.37. This indicates that the reproducibility of proteomic measurements has a major impact on the calculated mRNA-protein correlation.
excerpts.anchor: Results: Proteins with higher reproducibility have higher mRNA-protein correlation
excerpts.note: mRNA-protein correlation rises by ~0.33-0.37 from the lowest to highest reproducibility decile, showing measurement reproducibility, not just biology, drives the observed correlation.

excerpts.text: This suggests that noise in the quantification of protein abundances explains much more (on average 3 times) of the variance in mRNA-protein correlation than the most predictive previously identified factor.
excerpts.anchor: Results: Proteins with higher reproducibility have higher mRNA-protein correlation
excerpts.note: Measurement noise explains ~3x more variance in mRNA-protein correlation than protein-complex membership, the top previously identified (biological) factor.

excerpts.text: We then used this aggregated list to assess the extent to which ‘‘average’’ protein reproducibility explains the varying mRNA-protein correlations observed in ten other studies ... For all these studies, we find that proteins with more reproducible measurements tend to have higher mRNA-protein correlations. Although the aggregated ranks are based on data from cancer studies, we observe the same trend in healthy tissues obtained from the GTEx project (Figure 4J).
excerpts.anchor: Results: An integrated ranking of protein reproducibility partially explains the variable mRNA-protein correlation in 10 additional studies
excerpts.note: A transferable aggregate reproducibility rank reproduces the reproducibility-vs-correlation trend across 10 further studies and in healthy GTEx tissue, generalizing the finding beyond cancer.

excerpts.text: We hypothesized that proteins whose measurements are highly reproducible could be predicted better using machine-learning algorithms. ... We observed a stark difference in the prediction scores of the lowest and highest deciles of the protein reproducibility (Figures S4A and S4B). While the lowest decile has a correlation of 0.35 between the measurements and predictions, the highest decile has a correlation of 0.7.
excerpts.anchor: Results: An integrated ranking of protein reproducibility partially explains the variable mRNA-protein correlation in 10 additional studies
excerpts.note: ML prediction of protein from mRNA is far better for reproducibly measured proteins (decile correlation 0.7 vs 0.35), implying measurement noise limits how well models can learn the mRNA-to-protein map.

excerpts.text: We found a clear relationship between the mean protein abundance and the aggregated protein reproducibility rank—more abundant proteins are more reproducibly measured (Figure 5A).
excerpts.anchor: Results: Protein measurement reproducibility is influenced by abundance, variance, and unique peptides
excerpts.note: Low-abundance proteins are measured less reproducibly, a systematic, abundance-dependent bias in proteomic data.

excerpts.text: To assess the impact of transcriptomic reproducibility on mRNA-protein correlation, we compared transcriptomic profiles for 382 cancer cell lines from the CCLE (Ghandi et al., 2019) with those generated in a separate profiling effort (Klijn et al., 2015). We find that the median gene-wise Spearman correlation across studies was 0.75 (STAR Methods; Figure 6A). Again, this varied significantly across transcripts, ranging from 0.05 to 0.96.
excerpts.anchor: Results: Transcriptomic reproducibility also contributes to the variance in mRNA-protein correlation
excerpts.note: Cross-study transcriptomic reproducibility is itself only ~0.75 and highly variable (0.05-0.96), so even RNA-seq, the relatively mature modality, is not perfectly reproducible across efforts.

excerpts.text: These observations suggest that the reproducibility in transcriptomic and proteomic data contribute strongly and somewhat independently to the variability observed in mRNA-protein correlation.
excerpts.anchor: Results: Transcriptomic reproducibility also contributes to the variance in mRNA-protein correlation
excerpts.note: Both transcriptomic and proteomic measurement reproducibility independently degrade the observed mRNA-protein correlation.

excerpts.text: However, our observation that both protein-protein measurement reproducibility and mRNA-mRNA measurement reproducibility contribute significantly to the variation in mRNA-protein correlation across genes suggests an alternative explanation—some pathways may have higher- or lowerthan-average mRNA-protein correlations simply because their component proteins are more reproducibly measured.
excerpts.anchor: Results: Metabolic pathways with higher-than-average mRNA-protein correlations may reflect differential reproducibility rather than differential posttranscriptional regulation
excerpts.note: Pathway-level differences in mRNA-protein correlation, previously read as differential post-transcriptional regulation, may instead reflect differential measurement reproducibility.

excerpts.text: We found in both studies that the ‘‘housekeeping’’ protein complexes were still identified as being enriched among proteins with lower-than-average mRNA-protein correlations but that the metabolic pathways were no longer enriched in proteins with higher-than-average mRNA-protein correlations (Figures 7 and S7; Tables S3 and S4).
excerpts.anchor: Results: Metabolic pathways with higher-than-average mRNA-protein correlations may reflect differential reproducibility rather than differential posttranscriptional regulation
excerpts.note: After adjusting for reproducibility, metabolic-pathway high correlation vanishes (a measurement artifact) while complex-subunit low correlation persists (a genuine biological/post-transcriptional signal).

excerpts.text: Here, we have demonstrated that the reproducibility of protein and transcript measurements is a very significant factor in the observed correlations between mRNA and protein abundances. After taking this into account, we found that some pathways previously identified as having a high mRNA-protein correlation are likely just more reproducibly measured. We therefore suggest that conclusions about functional groups with higher or lower mRNA-protein correlations, especially with regard to the potential role played by post-transcriptional regulation, should be made only after accounting for variation in the measurement reproducibility of their constituent proteins.
excerpts.note: Headline conclusion and a methodological caution: claims about post-transcriptional regulation drawn from mRNA-protein correlation are confounded by measurement reproducibility and must be reproducibility-adjusted.

excerpts.text: We found here that proteins that are more reproducibly measured across experimental replicates are better predicted using machine-learning. This suggests that one of the factors limiting the accuracy of machine-learning methods to predict protein abundances is that the protein abundance measurements themselves are not reproducible. It may therefore be worth evaluating future methods on the subset of proteins that can be reproducibly measured.
excerpts.note: Data quality/reproducibility, not only model design, is a ceiling on ML prediction of protein from mRNA; benchmarks should account for measurement reproducibility.

excerpts.text: There are of course additional factors that influence the global reproducibility of proteomes and transcriptomes quantified from ‘‘replicates’’ of the same sample. These include real biological variation (e.g., tumor heterogeneity resulting in two samples of the same tumor having different profiles) and technical variation (e.g., variation in sample preparation between different runs of the same sample). We have not been able to address how much of the variance in the measurements of individual proteins can be attributed to these global factors. It is likely that reducing these sources of global variation, e.g., through automated sample preparation, will improve the overall reproducibility of protein measurements.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: cannot separate biological from technical sources of replicate variation; automated/standardized sample prep is proposed to improve reproducibility.

excerpts.text: Our results from analyzing the CCLE dataset, where the replicate correlation is highest, give what is likely the most realistic assessment of proteomic reproducibility using modern MS pipelines. The replicates in the CCLE study were generated by the same lab, using the same methodology, 1 year apart. In contrast, the ovarian cancer study contains replicates generated in different labs (introducing significant measurement heterogeneity), while the colon cancer study makes use of replicates generated using two entirely different MS approaches (label free versus TMT). Consequently, these likely represent lowerbound estimates of the reproducibility that can be observed using modern MS proteomic pipelines.
excerpts.note: Reproducibility depends strongly on whether replicates span labs, methods, or MS platforms; cross-lab and cross-platform replicates are markedly less reproducible (lower-bound estimates).

excerpts.text: Previous work has demonstrated that statistical modeling that integrates multiple mRNA and protein datasets and explicitly takes into account different sources of noise and error can be used to provide improved estimates of mRNA-protein correlation within samples (Csa´ rdi et al., 2015).
excerpts.note: Notes that integrating multiple mRNA and protein datasets while explicitly modeling noise yields better mRNA-protein correlation estimates, pointing toward multi-dataset integration with error modeling.

figuresNumbers.contains: Standardized-pipeline recomputation across 13 studies: median recalculated Spearman mRNA-protein correlation = 0.43; max 0.55 (LUAD, Gillette 2020), min 0.21 (CRC, Zhang 2014). Reported vs recomputed examples: colon 0.48 reported vs 0.27 recomputed; endometrial 0.48 vs 0.48; ccRCC 0.43 vs 0.41. Time trend: mean 0.49 (post-2019) vs 0.35 (<=2016).

Median protein-protein replicate reproducibility (Spearman): CCLE 0.72, ovarian 0.57, colon 0.28; corresponding median mRNA-protein correlations 0.48, 0.41, 0.21. CCLE per-protein replicate correlation range 0.2-1.0; ovarian 0.6-1.0; colon 0.2-0.8.

mRNA-protein correlation difference between lowest and highest reproducibility decile: colon 0.33, ovarian 0.35, CCLE 0.37. Variance in mRNA-protein correlation explained by protein-protein reproducibility: ovarian ~14%, CCLE ~17%, colon ~23%. Protein-complex membership alone explains ~3% (ovarian), ~8% (CCLE), ~6.7% (colon). Combined reproducibility + complex membership: ~17%, ~23%, ~26%.

Aggregated reproducibility rank built on 5,211 proteins (quantified in >=2 of 3 replicate studies); explains 10%-20% (median 14%) of variation in mRNA-protein correlation across 10 additional studies; trend holds for healthy GTEx tissue and for DIA/SWATH-MS (NCI-60).

ML prediction (DREAM challenge best model): lowest reproducibility decile measurement-vs-prediction correlation 0.35, highest decile 0.7; aggregated reproducibility rank explains 25% (breast) and 26% (ovarian) of variation in prediction scores.

Protein measurement reproducibility increases with mean protein abundance, protein variance (variance alone explains 20% of aggregated reproducibility rank), and number of unique peptides per protein; proteins with long half-life have higher median reproducibility (p = 9.70e-25, Mann-Whitney U).

Transcriptomic reproducibility (CCLE 382 cell lines vs Klijn 2015): median gene-wise Spearman 0.75, range 0.05-0.96; explains median 15% of mRNA-protein correlation variance across 13 studies (CCLE outlier 40%). Protein + mRNA reproducibility together explain 48% of variance for CCLE and 14%-26% for the other 12 studies; protein-protein reproducibility on average 2.8x more influential than mRNA-mRNA when sample sizes equalized (18 cell lines). Spearman between aggregated protein reproducibility and CCLE transcriptomic reproducibility = 0.37 across 4,795 proteins.

title: Phospho-seq: integrated, multi-modal profiling of intracellular protein dynamics in single cells
authors: John D. Blair, Austin Hartman, Fides Zenk, Philipp Wahle, Giovanna Brancati, Carol Dalgarno, Barbara Treutlein, Rahul Satija
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/35 - Phospho-seq - integrated multi-modal profiling of intracellular protein dynamics in single cells_cleaned.md
oneSentence: Phospho-seq is a single-cell multimodal workflow that quantifies cytoplasmic, nuclear, and phosphorylated proteins (via custom benchtop-conjugated antibody panels) together with chromatin accessibility and, through experimental or computational integration, transcriptomes, and applies this to cell lines and retinal/brain organoids to link signaling states to gene regulation.
orientation: A single-cell multimodal METHOD paper that strongly supports our roadmap claims that modalities are orthogonal/non-substitutable (Claim 4/5) and that protein, PTM/phospho, and protein-state layers carry information RNA and genome-wide assays cannot recover (modality axis: proteome, PTM). It also supplies first-hand evidence of the multimodal resolution-vs-quality trade-off (Claim 10) and of cross-technology benchmarking/standardization (Claim 9, standard axis). It can ALSO serve as a foil on the spatial axis: it relies on dissociation (cells are dissociated into single cells), discards spatial context, and only proposes spatial profiling as future work; temporally it reconstructs trajectories computationally from destructive snapshots rather than tracking real cells over time.

excerpts.text: This re<sup>fl</sup>ects a biological context where phosphorylation measurements are highly informative for distinguishing cellular states even when genome-wide modalities cannot.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Phospho-seq simultaneously quantifies phosphorylated, cytoplasmic, and nuclear proteins
excerpts.note: Phosphorylation (protein-state) measurements distinguish cell states in cases where chromatin/genome-wide modalities cannot.

excerpts.text: We also observed higher phosphorylation of the nuclear transcription factor STAT3 in iPSCs compared to K562 cells, and found that only pSTAT3 levels (as opposed to total protein levels) correlated with STAT3 transcription factor activities (Fig S1L). We conclude that we can therefore measure phosphorylation states for both cytoplasmic and nuclear activities, and that phosphorylated protein levels are more re<sup>fl</sup>ective of cellular state and transcription factor activities compared to total protein levels.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Phospho-seq simultaneously quantifies phosphorylated, cytoplasmic, and nuclear proteins
excerpts.note: Only the phosphorylated (PTM) state, not total protein level, tracks TF activity; PTM carries information neither total protein nor RNA recovers.

excerpts.text: We identi<sup>fi</sup>ed a speci<sup>fi</sup>c stage of the developmental trajectory where protein and RNA levels were discordant and con<sup>fi</sup>rmed that in these cells, only SOX2 protein levels (and not RNA levels) were correlated with chromVAR scores (Fig. S7C).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Intracellular protein staining highlights cell type differences in human brain organoids
excerpts.note: Protein and RNA levels diverge during differentiation; protein (not RNA) tracks TF activity, evidencing RNA-protein non-substitutability.

excerpts.text: While  gene expression decreased at the <sup>SOX2</sup>initial stages of differentiation, we observed a developmental lag in the decrease of downstream modalities including SOX2 ADT levels and SOX2 transcription factor activity as estimated by chromVAR (Fig. S7B).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Intracellular protein staining highlights cell type differences in human brain organoids
excerpts.note: SOX2 protein and TF activity lag behind RNA changes during differentiation, so RNA does not predict downstream protein-layer timing.

excerpts.text: While pRPS6 is often utilized as an indicator for mTOR pathway activity, RPS6 can actually be phosphorylated by multiple kinases from different pathways—including the ERK pathway via RPS6KA1 or the mTOR pathway via RPS6KB1<sup>44</sup> (Fig. 4G).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Observing signaling network activity with Phospho-seq
excerpts.note: A single phospho-state can be produced by distinct signaling pathways, so functional interpretation requires the protein-state layer plus regulatory context.

excerpts.text: no existing approaches can quantify intracellular proteins (including phosphorylation states), transcriptional output, and chromatin pro<sup>fi</sup>ling in the same biological system.
excerpts.note: Before this work, no single assay jointly captured intracellular/phospho protein, transcription, and chromatin in one system, motivating multimodal integration.

excerpts.text: While powerful, these technologies focus exclusively on the pro<sup>fi</sup>ling of cell surface proteins. They are therefore widely applied to analyze hematopoietic samples, where well-characterized panels of cell surface proteins are associated with distinct cell states, but have limited utility in other contexts, including neurodevelopment.
excerpts.note: Existing single-cell protein methods (CITE-seq etc.) measure only surface proteins, leaving intracellular and phospho protein states unmeasured in most tissues.

excerpts.text: While each of these methods addresses key challenges, they perform pro<sup>fi</sup>ling of small (3–7) intracellular panels due to a reliance on commercially conjugated antibodies. QuRIE-seq aims to pro<sup>fi</sup>le larger panels, but requires custom instrumentation and is not compatible with primary cell samples<sup>17</sup>.
excerpts.note: Prior intracellular single-cell protein methods were limited to very small panels or required custom instrumentation, a feasibility constraint this work aims to relax.

excerpts.text: By optimizing a broadly accessible antibody conjugation strategy<sup>24</sup>, we designed custom panels to pro<sup>fi</sup>le up to 64 intracellular proteins (including 20 phospho-states), and applied this work<sup>fl</sup>ow to both retinal organoids and brain organoids.
excerpts.note: High-throughput intracellular protein/phospho profiling (up to 64 proteins, 20 phospho-states) is demonstrated as feasible at single-cell scale.

excerpts.text: While Phospho-seq exhibited a higher SI on average (mean SI = 1.26 vs. 0.74. for <sup>fl</sup>ow cytometry), we did not identify a statistically signi<sup>fi</sup>cant difference between the two ( = 0.375; <sup>p</sup>Fig. S2C), suggesting that our Phospho-seq data is comparable to goldstandard approaches.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Phospho-seq simultaneously quantifies phosphorylated, cytoplasmic, and nuclear proteins
excerpts.note: Quantitative benchmarking (staining index) shows sequencing-based protein quantification is statistically comparable to gold-standard flow cytometry.

excerpts.text: Comparing the mean expression of pRPS6 between gated populations in Phosphoseq, we observed quantitative agreement ( <sup>2</sup> = 0.90), highlighting the <sup>R</sup>consistency of Phospho-seq with ground truth measurements (Fig. S2L). With ASAP-seq, we observed a weaker correlation ( <sup>2</sup> = 0.12; <sup>R</sup>(Fig. S2K).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Phospho-seq simultaneously quantifies phosphorylated, cytoplasmic, and nuclear proteins
excerpts.note: Head-to-head against flow-cytometry ground truth, Phospho-seq (R2=0.90) outperforms the comparable technology ASAP-seq (R2=0.12), a cross-technology comparability metric.

excerpts.text: While this dataset was informative and enabled cell type identi<sup>fi</sup>- cation from transcriptomic pro<sup>fi</sup>les, the simultaneous acquisition of multiple modalities came at a substantial cost in data quality and throughput when compared to either our existing Phospho-seq data, or scRNA-seq on retinal organoids<sup>32</sup>. Speci<sup>fi</sup>cally, we observed a 70% reduction in molecular sensitivity for scATAC-seq pro<sup>fi</sup>les and a 91% reduction for gene expression pro<sup>fi</sup>les (Fig. S4C, D).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Extending Phospho-seq to incorporate transcriptomic measurements
excerpts.note: Author-conceded trade-off: experimentally acquiring more modalities at once cuts per-modality sensitivity sharply (70% ATAC, 91% RNA), evidencing the throughput-vs-quality tension.

excerpts.text: Regardless, these data are consistent with previous studies<sup>20</sup> that exhibit lower permodality molecular sensitivity when using the 10x multiome kit, and demonstrate the inherent challenges for multimodal technologies to generate high-quality data from each measurement type.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Extending Phospho-seq to incorporate transcriptomic measurements
excerpts.note: Authors generalize the trade-off: multimodal assays inherently struggle to produce high-quality data for every measured modality simultaneously.

excerpts.text: Although this approach requires running additional experiments, we and others have found that simultaneous pro<sup>fi</sup>ling of ATAC and RNA in cells or nuclei reduces the data quality associated with each modality<sup>49</sup>.
excerpts.note: Conceded again in Discussion: joint ATAC+RNA profiling degrades each modality's quality, motivating computational integration over simultaneous measurement.

excerpts.text: We, therefore, explored an alternative approach aiming to integrate Phospho-seq and scRNA-seq reference datasets using our recently introduced ‘bridge integration’ procedure (Fig. 3D)<sup>23</sup>. We have previously shown that this work<sup>fl</sup>ow can successfully integrate distinct modalities collected in different experiments by leveraging a separately obtained multiomic dataset as a ‘bridge’, even if the bridge dataset has reduced technical quality.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Extending Phospho-seq to incorporate transcriptomic measurements
excerpts.note: Computational bridge integration is used to harmonize modalities collected in separate experiments via a shared multiomic reference.

excerpts.text: The bridge integration procedure can successfully integrate data for both discrete cell types as well as continuous developmental trajectories, but requires that the multiomic dataset is biologically representative (i.e. inclusive of all cell types and states) of the single-modality datasets.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Extending Phospho-seq to incorporate transcriptomic measurements
excerpts.note: Cross-experiment multimodal integration requires a biologically representative multiomic bridge, a precondition (standardization constraint) for combining datasets.

excerpts.text: we used bridge integration to link these peaks to transcription factors (TF) that regulate their accessibility.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Using Phospho-seq data to identify gene regulatory networks in early neurodevelopment
excerpts.note: Cross-modal integration (protein + chromatin + RNA) reconstructs gene regulatory networks beyond what any single modality could yield.

excerpts.text: We reasoned that we could extend this analysis using protein measurements from our Phospho-seq panel, which may be more re<sup>fl</sup>ective of TF levels, rather than RNA measurements.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Using Phospho-seq data to identify gene regulatory networks in early neurodevelopment
excerpts.note: Protein measurements are argued to reflect transcription-factor levels better than RNA, motivating the protein modality for regulatory inference.

excerpts.text: Strikingly, we found that our Phospho-seq data for GLI3 could help predict which genomic regions would respond to perturbation. Examining the 5108 DARs identi<sup>fi</sup>ed in the perturbation experiment, we found that the accessibility of these sites was correlated to GLI3 protein levels in our Phospho-seq data, in contrast to >300,000 non-DAR ( -value = <0.001)(Fig. 5G).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Identification of transcriptional co-factors through ADT-motif correlation
excerpts.note: GLI3 protein levels predict which regions respond to functional perturbation, demonstrating the protein modality carries functional/causal information.

excerpts.text: These <sup>fi</sup>ndings suggest that GLI3-binding alone is insuf<sup>fi</sup>cient to regulate chromatin accessibility and gene expression, and therefore, that CUT&Tag data is a poor predictor of which genomic regions will respond to  perturbation.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Identification of transcriptional co-factors through ADT-motif correlation
excerpts.note: A binding-only modality (CUT&Tag) poorly predicts functional regulatory response, arguing that complementary modalities are needed to capture function.

excerpts.text: Photoreceptors are among the most metabolically demanding cell types in the body, requiring high levels of mTOR signaling, which in turn phosphorylates RPS6, to properly function<sup>34</sup>. Among photoreceptors, cones require more energy than rods due to their more consistent depolarized state<sup>35</sup> and the relative levels of pRPS6 we observe between these two cell types re<sup>fl</sup>ect this.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Intracellular protein staining highlights cell type differences in human retinal organoids
excerpts.note: Cell-type differences in metabolic/signaling state (rods vs cones) are read out via a phospho-protein (pRPS6), a functional state not captured by transcriptome alone.

excerpts.text: Finally, applying Phospho-seq in concert with spatially-resolved pro<sup>fi</sup>ling technologies<sup>77</sup> may shed light on both intercellular and intracellular signaling networks.
excerpts.note: Authors concede spatial resolution is absent and only a future extension; the current dissociated assay discards spatial/microenvironment context.

excerpts.text: In brief, samples are dissociated into single-cells, <sup>fi</sup>xed, permeabilized, hashed, stained for intracellular proteins with self-conjugated DNA-bound antibodies, and run through the 10X Genomics single-cell ATAC-seq protocol (Fig. 1A and Methods).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Benchtop conjugation enables antibody panel customization in Phospho-seq
excerpts.note: The workflow begins by dissociating tissue into single cells, removing spatial organization before measurement.

excerpts.text: Future in-vivo experiments will reveal whether these signaling differences replicate in vivo, and how they in<sup>fl</sup>uence cellular decision-making and fate.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Observing signaling network activity with Phospho-seq
excerpts.note: Author caveat that observations in dissociated in-vitro organoids may not replicate in vivo, an in-vitro-vs-in-vivo trade-off.

excerpts.text: We conclude that, while it is possible to use the Phosphoseq work<sup>fl</sup>ow to perform experimental trimodal pro<sup>fi</sup>ling, this comes with a cost in data quality and thus integrated analysis may result in improved data quality for multiple modalities.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Extending Phospho-seq to incorporate transcriptomic measurements
excerpts.note: Authors conclude experimental trimodal measurement trades off data quality, so computational integration of separately collected modalities is preferable.

figuresNumbers.contains: Custom panels profiling up to 64 intracellular proteins including 20 phospho-states; retinal organoid panel = 46 antibodies; brain organoid panel = 64 antibodies with 40 paired cell-signaling antibodies.
Phospho-seq mean SI = 1.26 vs 0.74 for flow cytometry; difference not statistically significant (p = 0.375).
Phospho-seq vs flow-cytometry ground truth R2 = 0.90; ASAP-seq vs ground truth R2 = 0.12.
70% reduction in scATAC-seq molecular sensitivity and 91% reduction in gene-expression sensitivity vs single-modality data; 45-70% reduction in ADT staining index across three cell-type pairs.
1474 cells recovered from 5000 input; median 866 RNA UMIs, 8326 ATAC fragments, 39 ADT UMIs per cell.
Retinal organoid Phospho-seq: 8136 cells (34-week organoids). Brain organoid Phospho-seq: 9028/9034 cells (3-month, four iPS donors); scRNA reference 19,280 cells, multiome bridge 4958 cells.
45 of 64 proteins differentially expressed between at least one pair of clusters; 50% of ADTs above 80th-percentile rank-correlation with imputed RNA.

CUT&Tag found 331,131 GLI3 binding sites; only 5108 (1.5%) showed differential accessibility on GLI3 perturbation; DARs enriched for GLI3 motifs (fold 2.11) and more strongly for HD/2 motifs (median fold 3.02); DAR-adjacent genes log2FC -0.22 vs 0.04 for non-DAR (p<0.001).
Benchtop click-chemistry conjugation ~$8/conjugation; optimal 15 pmol oligonucleotide per ug antibody (2-4 oligo copies per antibody).

flags: Metadata verified from text: DOI 10.1038/s41467-025-56590-7 (Additional information and footer), venue Nature Communications (peer review note line 491), year 2025 (copyright line 499). Volume/issue/page numbers not printed in cleaned text, so venue given as 'Nature Communications, 2025' without Vol(Issue):Pages.

OCR quirks pervasive: <sup>fi</sup>/<sup>fl</sup> ligature tags, displaced superscripts (e.g. SOX2/RPS6KA1 gene names rendered out of order), and broken inline stats (R2, p-values). Excerpts copied verbatim including these artifacts.

Scope note: this is a single-cell DISSOCIATED method (no spatial resolution); spatial profiling appears only as proposed future work (e2 e22 e23). Captured as spatial-axis counter/foil evidence rather than spatial support.

Scope note: 'temporal' here is computational developmental-trajectory/pseudotime inference from destructive snapshots (e4, pseudotime Methods), not paired real-time tracking of the same cells; relevant to Claim 3 (destructive measurement cannot pair observations over time) as a methodological illustration.

Read at source: ref 49 (lower data quality of simultaneous ATAC+RNA) is load-bearing for the multimodal trade-off (e14) but its full citation is not present in the provided reference excerpt; worth first-hand lookup.
Cell-count inconsistency in source: brain organoid final dataset stated as both 9,028 (Results) and 9,034 (Methods) cells; retinal pilot/benchmark text states 14,931 singlet cells.

title: Integrative single-cell metabolomics and phenotypic profiling reveals metabolic heterogeneity of cellular oxidation and senescence
authors: Ziyi Wang, Siyuan Ge, Tiepeng Liao, Man Yuan, Wenwei Qian, Qi Chen, Wei Liang, Xiawei Cheng, Qinghua Zhou, Zhenyu Ju, Hongying Zhu & Wei Xiong
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/36 - Integrative single-cell metabolomics and phenotypic profiling reveals metabolic heterogeneity_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors built SCLIMS, a cross-modality platform combining single-cell live-cell fluorescence imaging with single-cell mass spectrometry, to pair each cell's metabolome with its oxidative-stress phenotype, showing that single-cell metabolomic heterogeneity predicts and predetermines cellular oxidative/senescent fate and that supplementing key metabolites lowers oxidative stress and extends C. elegans lifespan.
orientation: Primarily feeds the modality axis (metabolome as a distinct, non-substitutable layer measured at single-cell resolution) and the multi-modal integration / phenotype-grounding claims: it argues bulk metabolomics hides single-cell heterogeneity, integrates metabolome with cellular phenotype, and shows the metabolome predicts phenotype. It also lightly touches the temporal axis via pseudotime/trajectory inference over destructively-measured single cells (a distribution-level proxy, not paired longitudinal observation), and concedes spatial scope is limited to cultured/imaged cells. A non-binding routing hint, usable as support for 'modalities carry orthogonal information' and as a foil/limitation example for 'destructive measurement cannot pair cells over time.'

excerpts.text: Metabolism serves as both a re<sup>fl</sup>ection and regulator of cellular function<sup>15</sup>. Currently, most metabolomics research remains con<sup>fi</sup>ned to homogenate-level analysis involving the preparation of samples using bulk tissue or cell suspension. Consequently, the enigmatic metabolic heterogeneity exhibited by cells during biological processes and the underlying mechanisms at the single-cell level remain shrouded in ambiguity.
excerpts.note: States that metabolomics has been confined to bulk/homogenate analysis, which obscures single-cell metabolic heterogeneity; metabolism both reflects and regulates function.

excerpts.text: These new techniques mainly focused on the interpretation of the metabolome without the phenotype of single cells, omitting cross-modality features important for the better understanding of cellular metabolism and function. However, the integration of metabolic heterogeneity with phenotypic heterogeneity, such as distinct levels of oxidative or senescent states within individual cells, remains a formidable technical challenge.
excerpts.note: Notes that prior single-cell metabolomics omitted phenotype, and that integrating metabolome with phenotype (cross-modality) remains a technical challenge.

excerpts.text: A groundbreaking advancement in single-cell research lies in the seamless integration of multiple features or functions within individual cells, known as cross-modality analysis. This includes integrating single-cell omics with cellular function or phenotype, resulting in a truly remarkable and sophisticated approach<sup>9,10</sup>.
excerpts.note: Frames cross-modality integration of single-cell omics with function/phenotype as a key advancement.

excerpts.text: In the present study, we employ a combination of single-cell mass spectrometry (SCMS) and live-cell imaging techniques to simultaneously capture the metabolomic features and cellular identities of individual cells, thereby establishing a link between the metabolome and cellular status at a single-cell resolution.
excerpts.note: States the method pairs single-cell mass spectrometry with live-cell imaging to link single-cell metabolome to phenotype.

excerpts.text: In m/z ranging from 67 to 1000, a total of more than 500 ion signals, with signal-to-noise ratio greater than 3 (refs. 27,28) and detected at a frequency greater than 20% in all single cells were used in subsequent analysis. Among these signals, 162 matched annotations in the HMDB database through MS spectra. The annotated metabolites underwent further scrutiny via MS/MS, resulting in con<sup>fi</sup>rmation of 83 metabolites that were subsequently employed for pathway enrichment analysis (Supplementary Data File 1).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Integration of live-cell imaging and single-cell mass spectrometry
excerpts.note: Reports single-cell metabolome coverage: >500 ion signals, 162 HMDB-annotated, 83 MS/MS-confirmed metabolites per analysis.

excerpts.text: Among the whole metabolome, we observed a total of 254 metabolites signi<sup>fi</sup>cantly correlated with OS level (P < 0.05). The majority (61.4%) of the metabolites correlated with OS level (P < 0.05) exhibited an inverse correlation, which is nearly double the number of metabolites that showed a positive correlation with cellular OS.
excerpts.anchor: Results — The SCLIMS reveals correlation between cellular metabolism and oxidative levels
excerpts.note: Quantifies that 254 metabolites correlate with the oxidative-stress phenotype, 61.4% inversely, linking metabolome to cellular state.

excerpts.text: Machine learning is utilized to ascertain whether the metabolic pro<sup>fi</sup>le of individual cells can accurately predict heterogeneous subtypes with distinct OS levels. ... In multiclassi<sup>fi</sup>cation, the ROC curve had an average area under curve (AUC) of 0.98 (Fig. 4b). In cluster prediction, the model achieved an accuracy ranging from 77.8% to 100% (Fig. 4c). These results demonstrate that the single-cell metabolic pro<sup>fi</sup>les can directly predict metabolic subtypes.
excerpts.anchor: Results — The SCLIMS reveals capability of the single-cell metabolome in predicting cellular OS status
excerpts.note: Reports that single-cell metabolome predicts phenotypic subtype with AUC 0.98 and 77.8-100% accuracy; metabolome predicts cellular phenotype.

excerpts.text: Thus, through the implementation of SCLIMS technique and its multi-modal integration, we have successfully demonstrated a link between single-cell metabolome and cellular OS states in two different cell types for the very <sup>fi</sup>rst time, suggesting the potential role of metabolome in determining cellular phenotype.
excerpts.anchor: Results — The SCLIMS reveals capability of the single-cell metabolome in predicting cellular OS status
excerpts.note: Claims first demonstration of a multi-modal link between single-cell metabolome and phenotype across two cell types.

excerpts.text: We surprisingly found that while there was minimal heterogeneity in cellular OS levels among the initial cells (Fig. 5a and Supplementary Table 1), there existed heterogeneity in their single-cell metabolome (Fig. 5b, c).
excerpts.anchor: Results — The SCLIMS unveils the causal relationship between metabolic heterogeneity and OS status
excerpts.note: Finds that baseline cells homogeneous in the phenotypic readout still carry hidden single-cell metabolomic heterogeneity (metabolome reveals what the phenotype readout misses).

excerpts.text: Interestingly, despite no initial cells being subjected to OS stimulation, the metabolic disparity between the two cell subtypes in these initial cells already demonstrated a consistent pattern with the metabolic variation observed in cells with varying levels of OS.
excerpts.anchor: Results — The SCLIMS unveils the causal relationship between metabolic heterogeneity and OS status
excerpts.note: Reports that pre-existing baseline metabolic state foreshadows later divergent oxidative outcomes (baseline metabolome predicts future fate).

excerpts.text: We then performed a pseudotime analysis using single-cell metabolomics data and generated a trajectory of the six subtypes. The trajectory originated from subtype C1, which exhibited the lowest level of OS, and gradually progressed towards other subtypes. ... These <sup>fi</sup>ndings suggest a metabolism-guided stepwise progression of cellular OS.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Cell types identified by the SCLIMS exhibit divergent OS levels
excerpts.note: Infers a temporal trajectory of oxidative progression from static single-cell snapshots via pseudotime, not from longitudinally re-observed cells.

excerpts.text: However, following OS induction, the MROS cells exhibited 2.7-fold higher of DCFDA intensity compared to their MROR counterparts (Fig. 6i, j), indicating an elevated state of OS. Furthermore, SA-β-Gal staining con<sup>fi</sup>rmed the sensitivity of MROS cells in OS-induced senescence (Fig. 6k, l). Thus, the data from the SCLIMS suggest that the metabolic features of an initial cell may dictate its destiny under OS and OSinduced senescence.
excerpts.anchor: Results — The metabolic heterogeneity of cells determines their senescence fate under OS
excerpts.note: Shows initial metabolic state (sorted by GSH) determines later divergent phenotypic fate (2.7-fold higher oxidative stress, more senescence).

excerpts.text: Supplementation of key metabolites screened by SCLIMS resulted in a reduction in cellular oxidative levels and an extension of C. elegans lifespan.
excerpts.note: States that metabolite supplementation (a metabolic perturbation) lowers oxidative levels and extends lifespan, grounding metabolome in phenotype.

excerpts.text: Strikingly, the supplement of hypotaurine, phosphocreatine and O-phosphoethanolamine at various doses caused a remarkable extension in the lifespan of C. elegans by approximately 33%-50% (Fig. 8b and Supplementary Fig. 16a).
excerpts.anchor: Results — The SCLIMS-identified protective metabolites promote healthy aging and extend lifespan
excerpts.note: Quantifies that supplementation of three metabolites extends C. elegans lifespan by ~33-50%, an organismal phenotypic readout of a metabolic intervention.

excerpts.text: The current study has certain limitations that we would like to address, along with potential solutions for future studies. Firstly, the identi<sup>fi</sup>cation of metabolites at the single-cell level poses a signi<sup>fi</sup>cant challenge due to the complexity of MS/MS analysis. Acquiring MS/MS spectra for hundreds of m/z in the metabolome is indeed arduous.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: single-cell metabolite identification is hard because MS/MS for hundreds of m/z is arduous (a measurement trade-off).

excerpts.text: Secondly, the SCLIMS utilized in this current study was meticulously designed to incorporate cultured cells and cellular models of OS. The versatility of SCLIMS extends to tissue-embedded cells, as they can also be effectively labeled with <sup>fl</sup>uorescence markers. However, the potential application of SCLIMS in tissue-embedded cells remains an intriguing area for future exploration and investigation.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: the method is demonstrated on cultured (dissociated) cells, not tissue-embedded cells in their spatial/micro-environmental context.

excerpts.text: The single-cell metabolome and the cellular phenotype such as OS status are directly linked and integrated. The heterogeneous states are explained with single-cell metabolome and metabolic pathways.
excerpts.note: Asserts metabolome carries information that explains heterogeneous cellular states, positioning metabolome as an explanatory layer distinct from transcriptome.

excerpts.text: The cells were patched with a high-quality seal (>1 GΩ) and the cell membrane was disrupted by rapid application of negative pressure. Mild negative pressure was then applied to the pipette to obtain cytoplasmic chemical constituents, which were subsequently analyzed using mass spectrometry after removal of the pipette from the bath solution.
excerpts.anchor: Methods — The workflow and experimental setting of SCLIMS
excerpts.note: Describes the destructive sampling step (membrane disruption + cytoplasm aspiration), so each cell's metabolome is measured terminally and cannot be re-observed over time.

excerpts.text: Subsequently, the cells were captured using a <sup>fl</sup>uorescent microscope to record their spatial distribution. The acquired images were subsequently subjected to analysis, enabling the calculation of oxidative stress levels in individual cells.
excerpts.anchor: Methods — The workflow and experimental setting of SCLIMS
excerpts.note: Notes imaging records spatial distribution of cells prior to sampling, a partial spatial registration of cultured cells (not tissue micro-environment).

figuresNumbers.contains: >500 ion signals (S/N > 3, detected in >20% of cells); 162 metabolites HMDB-annotated by MS spectra; 83 metabolites MS/MS-confirmed for pathway analysis.
254 metabolites significantly correlated with OS level (P < 0.05); 61.4% inversely correlated (nearly double the positively correlated count).
Multiclass ROC average AUC = 0.98; cluster prediction accuracy 77.8%-100%.
Predicted vs real single-cell OS level correlation r = 0.88 (P = 1.45e-20).
Classification AUC = 0.99, average accuracy 88.42%; predicted vs true OS r = 0.6 (P < 0.0001).
Six metabolic subtypes C1-C6 with distinct OS levels; n = 55, 34, 14, 41, 29, 17 for C1-C6; One-way ANOVA F(5,184)=5.880, P=4.57e-5.
148 metabolites downregulated and 64 upregulated from C1 (lowest OS) to C6 (highest OS); MEFs M1-to-M6: 195 down, 134 up.

Two baseline metabolic subtypes Cluster-I (n=43) and Cluster-II (n=183); minimal OS-level heterogeneity but strong metabolome heterogeneity (1740 initial vs 960 OS cells; W=58627, P<2.2e-16). GSH/ATP/hypotaurine 82%/42%/87% lower in Cluster-II. GSH correlated with 15 metabolites in Cluster-I vs 5 in Cluster-II; GSH most-rewired metabolite.
DCFDA differs only 3% between MROR and MROS at baseline; after OS, MROS shows 2.7-fold higher DCFDA; SA-β-Gal confirms MROS senescence sensitivity.

Three key metabolites (hypotaurine, phosphocreatine, O-phosphoethanolamine) cut OS by average 67% (DCFDA), reduced SA-β-Gal intensity by average 48%, rescued growth arrest and mitochondrial membrane potential.

Metabolite supplementation extended lifespan ~33%-50%; DHE oxidative signal reduced by average 16%; thrashing decline (43%) ameliorated; free-moving speed 1.4-1.8 fold higher at Day 9, 1.1-1.2 fold at Day 5 vs vehicle.
