Scope note: this paper's core subject is single-cell metabolomics + phenotype integration (modality + multi-modal axes). It is largely off-roadmap for the AIVC virtual-cell modeling thesis except as evidence that (a) the metabolome is a distinct non-substitutable layer worth measuring per-cell, (b) cross-modality integration is feasible, and (c) destructive single-cell sampling forces trajectory inference rather than paired temporal observation.

Temporal touchpoints (e10, e11) are 'nuances' not 'supports' on Claim 3/temporal: the paper reconstructs a temporal trajectory via pseudotime and via baseline-state prediction from destructively-measured snapshots, illustrating exactly the distribution-to-distribution / no-paired-observation constraint rather than true longitudinal tracking.
Read at source: refs #17 and #18 (Zhu et al.) are the primary SCMS method this work extends and are worth citing first-hand.

title: Integrated analysis of multimodal single-cell data
authors: Y. Hao, S. Hao, E. Andersen-Nissen, W.M. Mauck III, S. Zheng, A. Butler, M.J. Lee, A.J. Wilk, C. Darby, M. Zager, P. Hoffman, M. Stoeckius, E. Papalexi, E.P. Mimitou, J. Jain, A. Srivastava, T. Stuart, L.B. Fleming, B. Yeung, A.J. Rogers, J.M. McElrath, C.A. Blish, R. Gottardo, P. Smibert, R. Satija
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/37 - Integrated analysis of multimodal single-cell data_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The paper introduces weighted-nearest neighbor (WNN) analysis, an unsupervised framework that learns cell-specific modality weights to integrate paired measurements (e.g., RNA + surface protein in CITE-seq), and applies it to a 211,000-cell PBMC atlas with up to 228 antibodies, showing that joint multimodal analysis resolves cell states that scRNA-seq alone cannot.
orientation: Strong support for the modality-orthogonality / non-substitutability axis (Claim 4) and for the multimodal-integration axis (Claim 8): the paper repeatedly shows transcriptome-alone fails to separate functionally distinct cell states that protein or chromatin modalities resolve. It also bears on Claim 2 (limits of scRNA-seq) and Claim 9 (reference mapping / cross-dataset standardization via Azimuth). Note: this is a single-cell, dissociation-based, non-spatial, non-temporal method paper, so it can also serve as a foil illustrating the very RNA-centric, snapshot paradigm our thesis critiques on the temporal and spatial axes.

excerpts.text: Although established technologies like single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) are capable of discovering new cell types and states in heterogeneous tissues, transcriptomics alone is often incapable of separating molecularly similar, but functionally distinct, categories of immune cells.
excerpts.note: States that transcriptomics alone often cannot separate functionally distinct cell categories.

excerpts.text: More broadly, this exhibits the challenge of defining cell states based on the transcriptome alone, because important sources of cellular heterogeneity may not correlate strongly with transcriptomic features despite being identifiable in other modalities.
excerpts.note: Argues important cellular heterogeneity is not captured by transcriptomic features but is identifiable in other modalities (modality orthogonality).

excerpts.text: This reflects technical challenges driven by the minimal RNA content of T cells coupled with high RNase expression (Andreeff et al., 1978; Lu et al., 2018; Sercan Alp et al., 2015), which hampers scRNAseq data quality.
excerpts.note: Notes a concrete technical limitation of scRNA-seq: low RNA content and high RNase in T cells degrade data quality.

excerpts.text: Each of these approaches offers an exciting solution to overcome the inherent limitations of scRNA-seq and to explore how multiple cellular modalities affect cellular state and function (Zhu et al., 2020).
excerpts.note: Frames multimodal technologies as overcoming inherent limitations of scRNA-seq.

excerpts.text: Importantly, these strategies must be robust to potentially large differences in the data quality and information content for each modality.
excerpts.note: Highlights that modalities differ in data quality and information content, a challenge for integration.

excerpts.text: The varying information content of each modality, even across cells in the same dataset, represents a pressing challenge for the analysis and integration of multimodal datasets.
excerpts.note: States that each modality carries varying information content, even cell-to-cell, complicating integration.

excerpts.text: For example, CD8<sup>+</sup> and CD4<sup>+</sup> T cells were partially blended together when analyzing the transcriptome but separated clearly in the protein data. Contrastingly, conventional dendritic cells (cDCs), along with a rare population of erythroid progenitors and spiked-in murine 3T3 controls, formed distinct clusters when analyzing RNA but were intermixed with other cell types based on surface protein abundance.
excerpts.anchor: Results: Quantifying the relative utility of each modality in each cell
excerpts.note: Direct demonstration that RNA and protein each resolve cell states the other cannot, in opposite directions (orthogonal information).

excerpts.text: We found that for CD8<sup>+</sup> T cells, the most similar RNA neighbors often reflected a mix of CD8<sup>+</sup> and CD4<sup>+</sup> T cells (in the RNA KNN graph, there are a total of 944 incorrect edges that connect CD8<sup>+</sup> to CD4<sup>+</sup> T cells). By contrast, protein neighbors were predominantly correctly identified as CD8<sup>+</sup> T cells (in the protein KNN graph, 12 CD8<sup>+</sup>/CD4<sup>+</sup> edges were identified).
excerpts.anchor: Results: Quantifying the relative utility of each modality in each cell
excerpts.note: Quantifies how protein data resolves CD8/CD4 T cells far better than RNA (944 vs 12 incorrect edges).

excerpts.text: However, although these cell types are defined by both RNA and protein markers, the statistical power in unsupervised analysis of either modality separately was insufficient to identify these populations, demonstrating the importance of joint analysis.
excerpts.anchor: Results: WNN analysis is a robust and flexible approach
excerpts.note: Neither modality alone has the statistical power to identify certain populations; joint analysis is required.

excerpts.text: These results suggest that integrated WNN analysis can provide necessary flexibility and allow one data type to compensate for weaknesses in another.
excerpts.anchor: Results: WNN analysis is a robust and flexible approach
excerpts.note: Claims one modality can compensate for another's weaknesses through integration.

excerpts.text: However, in addition to the published findings, WNN analysis identified a novel population of Basal cells that exhibits distinct chromatin accessibility profiles, but does not exhibit unique transcriptomic characteristics (Figure S3).
excerpts.anchor: Results: WNN analysis is a robust and flexible approach (SHARE-seq mouse skin)
excerpts.note: A cell population distinguishable only by chromatin accessibility, not by transcriptome, demonstrating chromatin carries information RNA does not.

excerpts.text: Taken together, these findings demonstrate that the ability of WNN to identify subpopulations that are masked by scRNA-seq alone is not limited to immune or CITEseq datasets.
excerpts.anchor: Results: WNN analysis is a robust and flexible approach
excerpts.note: Generalizes that subpopulations masked by scRNA-seq alone are recoverable with added modalities across tissue and assay types.

excerpts.text: Our integrated WNN analysis reveals a rich diversity of T cell states that is not typically captured in scRNA-seq analyses, including CD4<sup>+</sup> regulatory T cells, MAIT cells, multiple subpopulations of gd and double-negative T cells, along with heterogeneous subpopulations of naive, memory, and effector states.
excerpts.anchor: Results: Multimodal heterogeneity within lymphoid populations
excerpts.note: Multimodal analysis reveals lymphoid diversity not typically captured by scRNA-seq alone.

excerpts.text: We conclude that multimodal integration is essential for the unsupervised discovery and annotation of immune cell states; however, once these states are enumerated, supervised differential analyses are capable of sensitively describing markers that define their molecular state.
excerpts.anchor: Results: A multimodal atlas of the human PBMCs
excerpts.note: Asserts multimodal integration is essential for unsupervised discovery of immune cell states.

excerpts.text: Integrative multimodal analysis therefore provides a path forward to move beyond the partial and transcriptomefocused view of a cell and toward a unified definition of cellular behavior, identity, and function.
excerpts.note: Frames transcriptome-focused single-cell analysis as partial and calls for multimodal integration toward a unified cellular definition.

excerpts.text: As future technologies enable the simultaneous measurement of modalities spanning the central dogma including chromatin state, DNA methylation, transcription, lineage, spatial location, and protein levels—WNN analysis may help to reveal how subpopulations of cells differentially utilize these modalities to regulate their current state and future potential.

touchpoints.otherLabel: Lineage and spatial location named as additional modalities yet to be jointly measured (gap framing)

excerpts.note: Lists modalities across the central dogma (including spatial location and lineage) that future technologies should measure simultaneously; implicitly notes these are not yet jointly captured.

excerpts.text: Last, we note that the modality weights learned in our procedure serve not only as a proxy for the technical quality of a measurement type, but may also reflect the biological importance of each modality in determining cellular identity. For example, our analyses of human bone marrow demonstrated that progenitor cells and differentiated cells exhibited divergent modality weights.
excerpts.note: Argues different cell types depend on different modalities to define identity (modality importance is cell-state dependent).

excerpts.text: We accomplish this via a supervised version of principal component analysis to identify the best transcriptomic modules which delineate our WNN-defined cell types. Supervised mapping represents an attractive alternative to unsupervised analysis, and we show how this workflow can improve cell type identification and robustly integrate samples from multiple donors and disease states.
excerpts.note: Describes a reference-based mapping workflow that integrates samples across donors and disease states onto a common reference.

excerpts.text: To assist the community in utilizing our resource, we have created a web application, freely available at https://azimuth.hubmapconsortium.org/, which enables users to rapidly map their own datasets online, automating the process of visualization and annotation.
excerpts.note: Provides a public reference (Azimuth) for mapping new datasets onto a shared annotated atlas, a step toward cross-dataset comparability.

excerpts.text: Second, WNN assumes that modalities do not define conflicting sets of cell states. Although we have not observed this when using molecular data such as chromatin state, gene expression, and surface protein abundance, this assumption may be problematic when integrating morphological, functional, and molecular data.

touchpoints.otherLabel: Integration assumption breaks down across morphological/functional/molecular data types

excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: the integration method assumes modalities do not define conflicting cell states, which may fail for morphological/functional data.

excerpts.text: First, WNN requires a dimensional reduction to describe the neighborhood structure between cells. This requirement is not compatible with categorical or low-dimensional data.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: the integration method cannot handle categorical or low-dimensional modalities.

excerpts.text: In addition, our circulating immune atlas was constructed from PBMCs and therefore contains few cells with no nuclei (erythrocytes) or multi-lobed nuclei (granulocytes).

touchpoints.otherLabel: Dissociation/PBMC sampling bias excludes whole cell populations

excerpts.note: Concedes the dissociation-based PBMC atlas systematically omits certain cell types (erythrocytes, granulocytes).

excerpts.text: We observed strong quantitative agreement (R = 0.911) in the fraction of MAIT cells predicted by scRNA-seq and measured by CyTOF in the original cohort (Figure 7E).
excerpts.anchor: Results: Mapping query datasets to multimodal references
excerpts.note: Reports R = 0.911 concordance between scRNA-seq reference-mapping predictions and an orthogonal CyTOF measurement, supporting cross-platform validation.

figuresNumbers.contains: 210,911 total cells after QC and doublet filtration; 161,764 cells via 10x 3' with 228 TotalSeq A antibodies (avg 8,003 unique RNA molecules/cell, 5,251 unique ADT/cell); plus 49,147 cells with 54 antibodies via ECCITE-seq (10x 5'). 8 volunteers, ages 20-49, 3 time points (day 0, 3, 7) post VSV-vectored HIV vaccine, 24 samples.
8,617 cord blood mononuclear cells, panel of 10 immunophenotypic markers (Stoeckius et al., 2017).
30,672 mononuclear cells, panel of 25 antibodies.
RNA KNN graph: 944 incorrect CD8+/CD4+ edges; protein KNN graph: 12; WNN graph: 20.

figuresNumbers.item: Naive CD8/CD4 incorrect edges (10x Multiome ATAC+RNA, 11,351 cells)
figuresNumbers.contains: RNA KNN: 984; ATAC KNN: 373; WNN: 322.

34,774 mouse skin cells; WNN recapitulated all 23 originally described populations and identified a novel Basal cell population (Basal_4) distinguishable only by chromatin accessibility (CTCF and p53 motifs).

57 clusters in WNN analysis; three annotation levels (level 1: 8 categories; level 2: 30 categories; level 3: 57 categories). Cell groupings: CD4+ T (12 clusters), CD8+ T (12), unconventional T (7), NK (6), B/plasma/plasmablasts (8), DC and monocytes (8).
A single marker achieved >=10-fold enrichment for 45 clusters; a 3-marker panel achieved 10-fold enrichment for 55 clusters.
WNN analysis up to 15-fold faster than MOFA+/totalVI on the full dataset.
CD14+ classical and CD16+ non-classical monocytes upregulated a shared module of 62 genes enriched for type I interferon response; Siglec-1 (CD169) induced only at day 3.

ASDC dendritic cells (AXL+ SIGLEC6+) at 0.02% frequency; 97% of TCR clones were single-cell; 31 expanded clones (>=10 cells) excluding MAIT/iNKT; CMV-positive volunteers accounted for 91% of expanded-clone cells.
Pearson R = 0.911 between MAIT-cell fraction predicted by scRNA-seq reference mapping and measured by CyTOF.
A dataset of 50,000 cells can be fully processed and mapped in less than 5 min.

It does not directly support roadmap Claim 3 (destructive sequencing / temporal pairing) or the spatial axis (Claim 2/7 spatial); on those axes it exemplifies the paradigm our thesis critiques (potential foil), captured via excerpts e16 (notes spatial/lineage not yet jointly measured) and e22 (dissociation sampling bias).

title: Modeling intercellular communication in tissues using spatial graphs of cells
authors: David S. Fischer, Anna C. Schaar and Fabian J. Theis
venue: Nature Biotechnology, 2022 (online 27 October 2022); volume/issue/pages not printed in cleaned text
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/38 - Modeling intercellular communication in tissues using spatial graphs of cells_cleaned.md
oneSentence: Introduces node-centric expression models (NCEM), graph-neural-network models that predict a cell's gene expression from its cell type plus the composition of its spatial niche, applied to six imaging-based spatial molecular datasets (MERFISH, CODEX, MIBI-TOF, MELC, chip cytometry) and deconvoluted Visium, recovering cell-communication signatures and showing cell-cell dependencies on physiologically relevant length scales (~69 µm).
orientation: Spatial-omics methods paper whose explicit motivation is that current cell-communication models are 'based on molecular profiles of dissociated cells ... and ignore spatial proximity in situ' — directly supports our spatial-loss critique (Claim 2, Claim 7, Axis C). It operates across both RNA imaging (MERFISH, Visium) and protein imaging (CODEX, MIBI-TOF, MELC, chip cytometry), so it feeds Axis A modality and multimodal-integration (Claim 8). It also concedes spatial-data limitations (limited ligand/receptor capture in targeted assays; need for 3D data, more cells, perturbations of niche structure) which feed Claim 1 and Axis B (temporal/perturbation). A routing hint, non-binding: most useful as support for 'space matters / dissociation destroys it' and as a concrete example that niche composition is a measurable, predictive variable.

excerpts.text: Models of intercellular communication in tissues are based on molecular profiles of dissociated cells, are limited to receptor–ligand signaling and ignore spatial proximity in situ.
excerpts.note: States that existing intercellular-communication models rely on dissociated-cell profiles and ignore in situ spatial proximity, the spatial-loss gap our thesis targets.

excerpts.text: We present node-centric expression modeling, a method based on graph neural networks that estimates the effects of niche composition on gene expression in an unbiased manner from spatial molecular profiling data. We recover signatures of molecular processes known to underlie cell communication.
excerpts.note: Introduces NCEM, which models the effect of spatial niche composition on a cell's gene expression from spatial molecular profiling data.

excerpts.text: Cells interact on multiple length-scales through direct contact of surface-bound receptors and ligands, tight junctions and mechanical effects, and through indirect mechanisms, including soluble factors. Usually, these communication events cannot be directly observed but are critical to understand emergent phenomena in tissue niches<sup>1</sup>.
excerpts.note: States cells communicate across multiple length-scales via diverse mechanisms and that these niche-level events, though usually unobserved, are critical to tissue function.

excerpts.text: We infer cell communication from image-structured molecular profiling assays of RNA or proteins with subcellular resolution (Fig. 1a). We defined an NCEM as a graph neural network that predicts a cell’s observed gene expression vector from its cell type label and its niche<sup>5</sup> (Fig. 1b and Methods).
excerpts.note: States the method operates on image-based assays of either RNA or protein at subcellular resolution, predicting expression from cell type plus spatial niche.

excerpts.text: Cell–cell dependencies may be caused by diverse molecular mechanisms not limited to ligand–receptor-based communication. Therefore, we consider the effects of niche composition on all genes in an unbiased manner.
excerpts.note: Argues spatial cell-cell dependencies arise from diverse mechanisms beyond ligand-receptor signaling, motivating an unbiased genome-wide niche-effect model.

excerpts.text: We demonstrate cell communication inference with NCEMs on six datasets measured with MERFISH<sup>11,12</sup>, CODEX<sup>13</sup>, MIBI-TOF<sup>14</sup>, MELC<sup>15</sup> and chip cytometry<sup>16</sup> (Extended Data Fig. 2 and Methods).
excerpts.note: Lists the spatial assays used, spanning RNA imaging (MERFISH) and several protein/multiplexed-imaging modalities (CODEX, MIBI-TOF, MELC, chip cytometry).

excerpts.text: On average, intracell-type variance accounted for 40.6% of the total variance (Supplementary Fig. 1 and Methods).
excerpts.note: Quantifies that within-cell-type variation is a large fraction (40.6%) of total variance, the variation NCEM attributes partly to spatial niche.

excerpts.text: Linear NCEMs were most predictive on an intermediate length scale of 69 µm across the six datasets (Fig. 1c), showing that cell–cell dependencies appear on length scales characteristic of molecular mechanisms of cell communication.
excerpts.note: Reports that spatial cell-cell dependencies peak at ~69 µm, a physiologically meaningful length scale for cell communication.

excerpts.text: NCEMs outperformed nonspatial baseline models consistently by an average ΔR<sup>2</sup> (Online Methods) of 0.016 (Fig. 1c). As expected, the ΔR<sup>2</sup> is small compared to the baseline model R<sup>2</sup> that characterizes between-cell-type variance (0.39–0.79) because cell type identity accounts for a large fraction of variance in single-cell gene expression assays<sup>17</sup>.
excerpts.note: Spatial models beat nonspatial baselines by a small but consistent ΔR2 of 0.016; nuance that cell-type identity dominates variance so the incremental spatial signal is modest.

excerpts.text: The spatial effect on model performance varies between target cell types, suggesting that cell-type-specific molecular mediators of cell–cell dependency are captured (Supplementary Fig. 3).
excerpts.note: Spatial-niche effect on expression varies by cell type, interpreted as capturing cell-type-specific mediators of cell-cell dependency.

excerpts.text: NCEMs can be extended to spot transcriptomics if within-cell-type variation can be recovered from spot transcriptomics datasets in deconvolution analyses<sup>18,19</sup>. Here NCEMs model the expression variation within cell types across spots as a function of the inferred spot composition (Fig. 1d and Methods).
excerpts.note: Extends the niche model to spot (Visium) transcriptomics via deconvolution; nuance that spot data needs computational recovery of single-cell-level variation.

excerpts.text: We found putatively interacting ligand–receptor pairs for almost all type couplings in CellPhoneDB<sup>3</sup> and NicheNet<sup>2</sup> analyses of matched single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data, thus demonstrating the need for a quantitative description of statistical couplings in niches (Extended Data Fig. 4e–g).
excerpts.note: Argues that scRNA-seq-based ligand-receptor inference is non-specific (finds pairs for almost all couplings), motivating spatially-grounded quantitative niche descriptions.

excerpts.text: We identified a bidirectional dependency of B cells and follicular dendritic cells (FDCs) that is indicative of positive feedback between both cell types in germinal center organization<sup>20</sup> (Fig. 2a,b and Extended Data Fig. 4c).
excerpts.note: Recovers a known biological niche interaction (B cell–FDC positive feedback in germinal centers), validating that spatial niche structure encodes real cell-state dependencies.

excerpts.text: The interpretation of spatial dependencies inferred on targeted spatial molecular profiling assays is constrained by the limited capture of ligand–receptor pairs.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: targeted spatial assays measure only a limited panel of genes, restricting ligand-receptor and niche interpretation.

excerpts.text: We imputed the cell-wise gene expression in the MERFISH fetal liver data using corresponding scRNA-seq data<sup>21</sup> (Extended Data Fig. 10).
excerpts.note: Combines targeted spatial (MERFISH) data with scRNA-seq by imputation to expand measurable genes, an example of integrating complementary modalities to fill measurement gaps.

excerpts.text: The peak predictive performance of the ligand–receptor nonlinear NCEM was much higher compared to the nonlinear NCEM (R<sup>2</sup> of 0.799 and 0.947), demonstrating the increased complexity of the input compared to categorical cell type labels.
excerpts.note: Reports that a ligand-receptor graph kernel raises predictive R2 from 0.799 to 0.947, showing molecular niche signals add information beyond categorical cell-type labels.

excerpts.text: The statistical identifiability of cell type couplings may improve with increased capture of niche heterogeneity, through the inclusion of three-dimensional data, by increasing the number of cells measured and by increasing the variation in the training data through perturbations of niche structure.
excerpts.note: Author-conceded data limitations: better inference needs 3D spatial data, more cells, and perturbation-driven variation in niche structure, framing data quantity/design as the bottleneck.

excerpts.text: Uncertainty in segmentation of cells or nuclei can be improved on the level of the spatial measurement<sup>22</sup> or may be addressed in model extensions.
excerpts.note: Concedes that cell/nucleus segmentation uncertainty is a measurement-level limitation affecting spatial niche inference.

excerpts.text: Nonlinear NCEMs learn a cellular representation within the spatial graph<sup>23</sup>, and we demonstrated that these representations can model niches and may be exploited for unsupervised analysis of tissue structures.
excerpts.note: States learned spatial-graph representations capture tissue niche structure and enable unsupervised analysis of tissue organization.

excerpts.text: Notably, the nonlinear models did not outperform linear models in gene expression prediction, which suggests that the spatial dependencies in the given datasets are well described by linear models (Extended Data Fig. 8b).
excerpts.note: Reports nonlinear deep models did not beat linear models, implying the spatial signal recoverable from current data is limited rather than model-capacity-limited.

figuresNumbers.contains: Linear NCEMs most predictive at an intermediate length scale of 69 µm averaged across the six datasets; NCEMs outperform nonspatial baselines by an average ΔR2 of 0.016; baseline model R2 (between-cell-type variance) ranges 0.39–0.79.
Intracell-type variance accounted for 40.6% of total variance on average across datasets.
Peak R2 of ligand–receptor nonlinear NCEM 0.947 vs 0.799 for the standard nonlinear NCEM on imputed MERFISH fetal liver data.

MERFISH fetal liver: 132 genes, 40,864 (WT) + 54,970 (Tet2-/-) cells. MERFISH brain: 254 genes, 284,098 cells, 2 mice. Chip cytometry colon: 19 genes, 11,321 cells, 1 patient. MIBI-TOF cancer: 36 genes, 63,747 cells, 4 individuals. MELC tonsils: 51 genes, 9,512 cells, 1 patient. CODEX cancer: 57 genes, 272,266 cells, 35 patients. Visium lymph node deconvoluted with cell2location.
10% of all nodes across images/patients held out as test set; 10% of the remainder as validation.
Paired t-test p-values for best spatial vs baseline: MERFISH brain p_IND=0.033; chip cytometry colon p_GCN=0.026; MIBI-TOF cancer p_IND=0.005 and p_GCN=0.036.

Scope note: this is a spatial-graph cell-communication MODELING method paper, but its motivation (dissociated profiles ignore spatial proximity) and its cross-modality RNA+protein imaging application make it on-roadmap for the spatial-loss (Claim 2/7, Axis C) and multimodal (Axis A, Claim 8) arguments; the modeling-method details themselves are out of our scope per context section 6.
Read at source: ref #1 (Palla et al., Nat. Biotechnol. 2022, 'Spatial components of molecular tissue biology') is a review worth first-hand for the general spatial-tissue-biology framing.

title: LIANA+ provides an all-in-one framework for cell–cell communication inference
authors: Daniel Dimitrov, Philipp Sven Lars Schäfer, Elias Farr, Pablo Rodriguez-Mier, Sebastian Lobentanzer, Pau Badia-i-Mompel, Aurelien Dugourd, Jovan Tanevski, Ricardo Omar Ramirez Flores & Julio Saez-Rodriguez
venue: Nature Cell Biology, 2024 (unverified journal name; see flags)
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/39 - LIANA+ - an all-in-one framework for cell-cell communication inference_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The paper presents LIANA+, a scalable Python framework that unifies and extends many cell–cell communication (CCC) inference methods across single-cell and spatially resolved (multi-)omics data, demonstrated on a murine Parkinson metabolome–transcriptome dataset and a human myocardial-infarction single-nucleus + spatial dataset.
orientation: A methods/tooling paper that repeatedly concedes the structural data gaps our perspective argues: dissociated single-cell CCC inference loses tissue architecture and only sees co-expression (never protein/function), spatial data faces a coverage–resolution trade-off, modalities beyond transcriptomics (metabolites, proteins) are largely ignored, and CCC benchmarks lack ground truth. It most plausibly feeds Claim 2 (scRNA loses spatial info), Claim 4/8 (modalities are non-substitutable and must be integrated), Claim 9 (resource heterogeneity/comparability/standards), and Claim 10 (resolution-coverage-destructiveness trade-off). It can serve as a foil (the method-proliferation, transcriptomics-centric paradigm we critique) AND as support (its own conceded limitations and its move to integrate spatial + dissociated multi-omics).

excerpts.text: Most methods have focused on protein-mediated interactions, predominantly inferred from transcriptomics data<sup>2,3</sup>, and only a few from multi-omics data<sup>10</sup>. As a consequence, other modes of intercellular signalling have been typically ignored<sup>3</sup> except for limited metabolite-mediated CCC predictions from transcriptomics data<sup>11–14</sup>. Emerging multi-omics technologies<sup>15</sup> are anticipated to provide a broader picture of molecular mediators and in turn prompt the development of new CCC tools.
excerpts.note: The field is overwhelmingly transcriptomics-centric and protein-mediated; non-transcriptomic modes of signalling (e.g. metabolite-mediated) have typically been ignored, and only emerging multi-omics is expected to broaden the picture.

excerpts.text: All single-cell methods are based on multiple assumptions, including that gene co-expression across dissociated cells, or groups of cells, reflects CCC within tissues<sup>3</sup>.
excerpts.note: Single-cell CCC inference rests on the assumption that gene co-expression in dissociated cells reflects communication within intact tissue — a spatial-information loss conceded as an assumption.

excerpts.text: Finally, all these developments use various infrastructures, with each CCC tool being typically designed for a specific task or data type.
excerpts.note: Tools are fragmented — each designed for a specific task or data type on differing infrastructures, motivating a unifying framework.

excerpts.text: leading to multiple heterogeneous databases and potential inconsistencies caused solely by the choice of resource<sup>3</sup>.
excerpts.note: Prior-knowledge resources are heterogeneous, and results can differ purely because of which resource was chosen — a comparability/standardization problem.

excerpts.text: LIANA+ uses standardized scverse<sup>28</sup> input and output formats (Fig. 2a), enabling interoperability with external packages and the straightforward extensions of CCC approaches.
excerpts.anchor: Results — LIANA+ as an all-in-one solution to model CCC
excerpts.note: LIANA+ adopts standardized scverse input/output formats to enable interoperability — a proposed remedy to the fragmentation/standardization problem.

excerpts.text: A particular challenge of emerging spatial technologies is that, on a single tissue section, they can combine different technologies<sup>15</sup>, and hence the observations from each technology may correspond to distinct spatial locations that need to be aligned<sup>26,29</sup>.
excerpts.anchor: Results — LIANA+ enables modelling CCC across spatial modalities
excerpts.note: Combining multiple spatial technologies on one section yields modality observations at distinct locations that must be aligned — a concrete cross-modal integration challenge.

excerpts.text: Identifying co-localized genes from spatially resolved data alone can help us to pinpoint relevant interactions driving disease. However, it remains limited by a common coverage–resolution trade-off in most spatial technologies<sup>39,40</sup>: they either quantify a limited fraction of molecules or they capture multiple cells within spots, relying on deconvolution to quantify cell-type frequencies within them<sup>39,40</sup>.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Ligand–receptor inference weakly reflects co-localization
excerpts.note: Spatial technologies face a coverage–resolution trade-off: either limited molecular coverage, or multi-cell spots requiring deconvolution — a direct statement of the resolution/throughput trade-off.

excerpts.text: We found weak associations between ligand–receptor interactions predicted in a spatially agnostic manner and the co-localization of their ligands, receptors and cell types (Fig. 4 and Supplementary Note 5).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Ligand–receptor inference weakly reflects co-localization
excerpts.note: Ligand–receptor predictions made without spatial information correlate only weakly with the actual co-localization of the partners — empirical evidence that dropping spatial context degrades CCC inference.

excerpts.text: Single-cell technologies capture cellular heterogeneity but typically lose tissue architecture information during dissociation, leading to many false positives in spatially agnostic CCC inference, as our evaluations demonstrate.
excerpts.note: Dissociation loses tissue architecture, producing many false positives in spatially agnostic CCC inference — explicitly supported by the paper's own evaluations.

excerpts.text: Dissociated and spatially resolved data generation has commonly focused on transcriptomics<sup>2,3</sup>, limiting CCC method development to protein-mediated interactions. Yet emerging multi-omics technologies enable the quantification of diverse molecules<sup>15,26,58</sup>.
excerpts.note: Both dissociated and spatial data generation have focused on transcriptomics, which has limited methods to protein-mediated interactions; multi-omics is needed to quantify diverse molecule types.

excerpts.text: Second, CCC from dissociated single-cell data remains limited to the co-expression of communication partners, which may not translate to the protein level, let alone imply a functional interaction<sup>3</sup>. Likewise, while spatially resolved data is a step further from its dissociated counterparts, it is limited to the co-localization of molecules.
excerpts.note: Conceded limitation: dissociated CCC inference sees only co-expression (RNA), which may not translate to protein level or functional interaction — RNA cannot stand in for protein/function; spatial data is limited to co-localization.

excerpts.text: First, while each of its methods can flexibly infer interactions between any set of variables, they typically use prior knowledge, which is limited, often exhibiting biases and a trade-off between coverage and quality<sup>3,24,67</sup>.
excerpts.note: Prior knowledge underlying these methods is limited and biased, with a coverage–quality trade-off — a data/resource-quality limitation.

excerpts.text: Finally, while our preliminary evaluations support the ability of LIANA+ to generate CCC insights across a range of technologies, systematic benchmarks of CCC methods are still pending. Ours and other evaluations remain limited by the lack of ground truth<sup>3</sup>, using instead orthogonal modalities, such as spatial data<sup>3,69</sup> or downstream signalling<sup>3</sup>.
excerpts.note: Conceded: systematic CCC benchmarks are still pending and evaluations lack ground truth, relying on orthogonal modalities (spatial data, downstream signalling) as proxies — a benchmark/comparability gap, and an argument that orthogonal modalities supply complementary truth.

excerpts.text: In the meantime, LIANA+— like other CCC inference methods—remains a tool for hypothesis generation.
excerpts.note: The authors concede that LIANA+ and other CCC methods remain hypothesis-generation tools rather than validated predictors — an admission of limited reliability without better data/ground truth.

excerpts.text: As emerging technologies<sup>70–72</sup> that capture bona fide CCC events become measurable at scale and widely available, LIANA+ will facilitate such benchmarks and comparisons.
excerpts.note: Forward-looking: only when technologies that capture genuine CCC events become scalable will proper benchmarking/comparison be possible — current comparability is bottlenecked by data.

excerpts.text: Using LIANA+, we jointly analysed the CCC events following myocardial infarction from single-nucleus and spatially resolved transcriptomics data<sup>27</sup>, thereby addressing the resolution limitations of spatial transcriptomics data<sup>15,39</sup> and the anticipated high false positive rates from dissociated data.
excerpts.note: Demonstrates integrating dissociated single-nucleus with spatial data to compensate for the resolution limits of spatial data and the false-positive rates of dissociated data — modalities used as mutual complements.

excerpts.text: Several metabolite peaks were explained relatively well by the joint model $( R ^ { 2 } > 0 . 5 )$ , including dopamine and its derivative 3-methoxytyramine, among other potentially deregulated, but unannotated, metabolite peaks (Extended Data Fig. 3a). Focusing on dopamine, we saw a large difference in explained variance between the intact (R<sup>2</sup> = 0.535) and lesioned hemispheres (R<sup>2</sup> ≈ 0; Extended Data Fig. 3a), which is expected due to the absence of dopamine in the striatum of the lesioned hemisphere<sup>26</sup> (Fig. 3e and Extended Data Fig. 4).
excerpts.anchor: Results — LIANA+ enables modelling CCC across spatial modalities
excerpts.note: Quantitative metabolite-mediated CCC result: dopamine explained variance R2=0.535 (intact) vs ~0 (lesioned), showing a non-RNA (metabolite) modality carries signalling information recoverable via spatial multi-omics.

excerpts.text: this unsupervised and synergistic analysis highlighted the importance of FB and myeloid cells (MY) as potential drivers of the disease trajectory following early myocardial infarction in both single-cell and spatial data.
excerpts.anchor: Results — LIANA+ infers intercellular programmes from dissociated data
excerpts.note: Cross-modal corroboration: the same fibroblast-myeloid signalling axis emerges in both dissociated single-cell and spatial data, illustrating value of integrating modalities.

excerpts.text: We additionally incorporated co-localization information to mitigate the anticipated high false positive rates (Supplementary Note 5).
excerpts.anchor: Results — LIANA+ infers intercellular programmes from dissociated data
excerpts.note: Spatial co-localization information is explicitly added to mitigate the high false-positive rate of dissociated-data CCC inference — concedes that dissociated data alone is error-prone without spatial context.

excerpts.text: To our knowledge, it is currently the only approach that considers the sign of the molecular interactions underlying inter- and intracellular signalling, a key feature of signal transduction.

touchpoints.otherLabel: Sign/direction of molecular interactions (regulatory wiring) as information beyond expression magnitude

excerpts.note: Claims LIANA+ is the only method accounting for the sign (activating/inhibitory) of molecular interactions — a regulatory/network-wiring layer of information beyond raw expression.

figuresNumbers.contains: Over 100 tools have contributed to CCC inference from single-cell and spatial transcriptomics data.
Joint multi-view model explained dopamine intensity with R2 = 0.535 in the intact hemisphere vs R2 ≈ 0 in the lesioned hemisphere; several metabolite peaks had R2 > 0.5.
29 single-nucleus and 28 10X Visium spatial samples from human myogenic, fibrotic and ischaemic heart regions after myocardial infarction.
Five intercellular programmes (factors) obtained across 28 spatial slides; factors 1, 2, 5 enriched in ischaemic samples, factor 4 in fibrotic, factor 3 in myogenic.
Dissociated single-nucleus factor 1 sample loadings differed across conditions (P = 9.9 × 10^-12); fibroblast-to-myeloid signalling axis R2 = 25.1%.
Eight local metrics implemented; spatially weighted cosine chosen as default for interpretability and consistent performance.
Scalability tested on simulated datasets from 1,000 to 100,000 cells; multi-view learning RAM ~2,900 MB (±100 MB).

title: CZ CELLxGENE Discover: a single-cell data platform for scalable exploration, analysis and modeling of aggregated data
authors: CZI Cell Science Program; Shibla Abdulla, Brian Aevermann, Pedro Assis, Seve Badajoz, Sidney M. Bell, Emanuele Bezzi, Batuhan Cakir, Jim Chaffer, Signe Chambers, J. Michael Cherry, Tiffany Chi, Jennifer Chien, Leah Dorman, Pablo Garcia-Nieto, Nayib Gloria, Mim Hastie, Daniel Hegeman, Jason Hilton, Timmy Huang, Amanda Infeld, Ana-Maria Istrate, Ivana Jelic, Kuni Katsuya, Yang Joon Kim, Karen Liang, Mike Lin, Maximilian Lombardo, Bailey Marshall, Bruce Martin, Fran McDade, Colin Megill, Nikhil Patel, Alexander Predeus, Brian Raymor, Behnam Robatmili, Dave Rogers, Erica Rutherford, Dana Sadgat, Andrew Shin, Corinn Small, Trent Smith, Prathap Sridharan, Alexander Tarashansky, Norbert Tavares, Harley Thomas, Andrew Tolopko, Meghan Urisko, Joyce Yan, Garabet Yeretssian, Jennifer Zamanian, Arathi Mani, Jonah Cool and Ambrose Carr
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/40 - CZ CELLxGENE Discover - a single-cell data platform for scalable exploration, analysis and modeling_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The paper presents CZ CELLxGENE Discover, an open data platform that aggregates, curates and standardizes community-contributed single-cell transcriptomic data (over 93 million unique cells under a minimal versioned schema) and provides visual and programmatic tools (Explorer, Gene Expression, Census) for cross-corpus exploration, analysis and machine-learning modeling.
orientation: Strong support for our 'data silos / non-comparability / lack of standards' claim: the paper's entire raison d'etre is that single-cell datasets are inconsistently curated, non-interoperable and hard to meta-analyze, and it builds a standardized schema to fix this. Simultaneously it is a clean foil for our spatial/temporal/multimodal gaps: the corpus is overwhelmingly dissociated RNA transcriptomic, the authors concede spatial information and non-RNA modalities are only emerging support, and there is no temporal/trajectory dimension. It also feeds the 'data powers foundation models' axis (scGPT, Geneformer trained on this data). Routing hint, non-binding.

excerpts.text: Despite the promise of these and emerging analytical tools for analyzing large amounts of data, the sheer number of datasets, data models and accessibility remains a challenge.
excerpts.note: States the core problem the platform addresses: heterogeneity of datasets and data models impedes reuse.

excerpts.text: Built-for-purpose portals enable rapid publication of studies and dissemination of unique biological features in specific datasets but lack the scalability and standardization needed for efficient meta-analysis. Even in the presence of such portals, efforts to explore or (re)analyze many datasets face a requirement to first standardize across individual data portals and resources, with only an estimated 25% of publicly available datasets providing the cell-level metadata needed for reuse (9).
excerpts.note: Existing portals lack standardization; only ~25% of public datasets carry the cell-level metadata needed for reuse.

excerpts.text: Data interoperability is a particularly important challenge for both individual users and the broader community to realize the promise of single-cell biology both now and in future applications that involve training models or assembling integrated references.
excerpts.note: Frames data interoperability as the gating challenge for both model training and integrated reference assembly.

excerpts.text: CZ CELLxGENE is differentiated from other single-cell data portals by its enforcement of a standardized schema for gene, cell, assay and donor metadata that evolves to address contributor requirements.
excerpts.note: The platform's distinguishing feature is enforcement of a single standardized, evolving metadata schema.

excerpts.text: CZ CELLxGENE serves only matrix-formatted data and metadata, which precludes the submission of raw sequence data.
excerpts.note: Scope of the resource: only matrix-formatted data and metadata, not raw sequence.

excerpts.text: Single-cell transcriptomic datasets from a large number of laboratories, institutions and consortia are available via websites and portals (12). Each portal serves the needs of its dataset, but typically fails to enable collective use. This presents a barrier to downstream use of the data and queries that run across many datasets.
excerpts.anchor: Results — CZ CELLxGENE data is universally standardized to a minimal data schema
excerpts.note: Per-dataset portals fail to enable collective use, barring cross-dataset queries.

excerpts.text: Metadata is critical for the reuse of data but often inconsistently captured or represented in single-cell datasets.
excerpts.note: Metadata is essential for reuse yet inconsistently captured across single-cell datasets.

excerpts.text: Integration enables the compilation of datasets into atlases and has become a core computational task within single-cell biology (13,14). It allows for the inference of multi-modal measurements, cell type prediction and cross-species analysis that underpins insights into a wide array of biological questions as well as the ambitions of community efforts to integrate individual datasets into references (15).
excerpts.note: Integration is a core computational task enabling multi-modal inference, cell-type prediction and cross-species analysis.

excerpts.text: To avoid deterring or inhibiting data submission and adoption, we limit the schema to 11 required fields considered most valuable for data integration and reuse.
excerpts.note: The required schema is deliberately minimal at 11 fields to balance adoption against integration value.

excerpts.text: The required fields represent attributes that are often variable within or across studies and are often identified as strong covariates correlated with gene expression variation within cells (20,21).
excerpts.note: Required metadata fields capture covariates that strongly correlate with cross-study gene expression variation.

excerpts.text: CZ CELLxGENE hosts the largest curated collection of publicly available single-cell data, with data obtained from both single cells and single nuclei across 449 tissues and 40 unique assay types transcriptomics (Figure 2A–C). The CZ CEL-LxGENE schema and underlying architecture have been designed for extensibility to new modalities and growth of the data corpus. Current data is primarily single-cell transcriptomic, although CZ CELLxGENE accepts additional modalities to support the growing number of studies that incorporate a multitude of assays and co-assays (Figure 2B).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Community resource for standardized cell resolved measurements
excerpts.note: Concedes the corpus is primarily single-cell transcriptomic; other modalities are only an extensibility goal, not present at scale.

excerpts.text: Of the 93+ million unique cells available in the data corpus, 63% of the total cells in the corpus are human, 32% are mouse and <5% are from other species (Figure 2C). Of the human data, ∼62% is defined as coming from healthy donors, and the remaining 38% span 132 unique diseases.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Community resource for standardized cell resolved measurements
excerpts.note: Reports composition of the >93M-cell corpus by species and by healthy vs diseased human donors.

excerpts.text: While CZ CELLxGENE data corpus remains the largest publicly available single-cell data resource, significant data deficiencies exist in many tissues and systems across both mouse and human (Figure 2D). When considering the comprehensiveness of human data available in the data corpus, samples from female tissue donors are slightly more numerous across the data corpus, and diverse ethnicities and age groups are grossly underrepresented, with the majority of data coming from adults of European or Unknown ethnicity (Figure 2E–G).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Community resource for standardized cell resolved measurements

touchpoints.otherLabel: Coverage gaps / representational bias in the data corpus

excerpts.note: Concedes significant tissue/system gaps and gross under-representation of ethnicities and age groups even in the largest corpus.

excerpts.text: The majority of single-cell visualization portals rely on precomputed values that limit interactive features on datasets and generally are tuned to host datasets of 250–500k cells (30). Portals have struggled to keep up with the growth of dataset size, now reaching the order of millions of cells per study (Figure 3A) (34,35). As the field scales, and perhaps importantly, as more integrated datasets become available, it is critical to have the ability to dynamically visualize and perform basic analysis functions on millions of cells.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Explorer allows interactive exploration and analysis of individual single-cell datasets up to 4 M cells
excerpts.note: Existing tools struggle to scale to the millions-of-cells-per-study regime; scalable infrastructure is needed.

excerpts.text: Single-cell transcriptomic data suffers from batch effects which distort biological signal when comparing between datasets. Despite many advances in methods development for data integration, the current state-of-the-art methods produce output in a lower-dimensional space than the original count matrix, making them unsuitable for our application.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Aggregation partially mitigates batch effects
excerpts.note: Batch effects distort cross-dataset comparison; SOTA integration methods output low-dimensional spaces unsuitable for serving full count matrices.

excerpts.text: This suggests that batch effects are at least partially mitigated by aggregation and log normalization compared to raw counts; however, users should be advised that the displayed values may still be affected by batch effects, especially for rare cell types.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Aggregation partially mitigates batch effects
excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: aggregation only partially mitigates batch effects; displayed values may remain biased, especially for rare cell types.

excerpts.text: We found an average recall of 33% (Figure 5). The probability of observing this level of sensitivity by chance for each cell type ranges from $P = 0 . 0 0 – 0 . 0 4 ;$ the moderately low sensitivity values are likely influenced by the fact that the HuBMAP dataset we use as our ‘ground truth’ includes annotations from a variety of different assay types and are quite noisy.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Gene Expression provides computationally derived marker genes for most cell types
excerpts.note: Marker-gene recall averages only 33%, partly attributed to noisy heterogeneous-assay ground-truth annotations.

excerpts.text: Many tools that are widely used for analysis, or even meta-analysis, are not designed to operate on datasets of this scale (38).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Census provides efficient programmatic access
excerpts.note: Widely used analysis tools are not designed for atlas-scale datasets, motivating new scalable infrastructure.

excerpts.text: While these tools are increasing the scale to which computational biologists can access and analyze data, many of these solutions are still limited to only millions of cells and more importantly, lack of interoperability between R and Python. To fill this gap, and in particular, to allow interoperable and scalable data access beyond 10s and 100s of millions of cells, we co-developed TileDB-SOMA
excerpts.anchor: Results — Census provides efficient programmatic access
excerpts.note: Existing scalable tools lack R/Python interoperability and cap at millions of cells; TileDB-SOMA built to enable interoperable access beyond tens/hundreds of millions of cells.

excerpts.text: TileDB-SOMA was then used to build Census – a large data object hosted in the cloud with initial data covering all RNA non-spatial transcriptomic data from CZ CELLxGENE.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Census provides efficient programmatic access
excerpts.note: Census initially covers only RNA, non-spatial transcriptomic data, explicitly excluding spatial data.

excerpts.text: Modeling of single-cell data at scale is an important aspect of Census, as well as the broader single-cell field, given its demonstrated uses for in silico experimentation (51,52), data integration and annotation (53,54), cell state prediction (55,56) and clinical applications (57–59).
excerpts.anchor: Results — Census provides efficient programmatic access
excerpts.note: Positions standardized data as the substrate for scalable modeling (in silico experimentation, prediction, clinical applications).

excerpts.text: PyTorch (60) is one of the most popular machine learning frameworks in singlecell, with notable models built using the PyTorch library including scvi-tools, Geneformer (61) and scGPT (62). To allow existing and new machine learning models to be trained on Census-scale data, we implemented PyTorch iterable data loaders that work natively with Census via TileDB-SOMA.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Census provides efficient programmatic access
excerpts.note: Census provides PyTorch data loaders so foundation models (Geneformer, scGPT) can train on corpus-scale data.

excerpts.text: Initial datasets included in Census were generated using non-spatial RNA technologies, contain cells from human or mouse, provide raw counts and utilize only standardized cell and gene metadata as described in the CZ CELLx-GENE dataset schema description above.
excerpts.anchor: Results — Census provides efficient programmatic access
excerpts.note: Reiterates that the modeling-ready Census corpus is non-spatial, RNA-only, human/mouse.

excerpts.text: Compilation and standardization of single-cell transcriptomic datasets reduces barriers to meta analysis. CZ CEL-LxGENE’s cell-level schema is important for this work as it enables corpus-wide analysis but also user-defined subsetting of the data to related collections of cells that span multiple datasets.
excerpts.note: Standardization of datasets is presented as the mechanism that lowers barriers to meta-analysis and corpus-wide queries.

excerpts.text: A recent demonstration of the power of CZ CELLxGENE data is on its application to train scGPT, a generative pretrained model, on 33 M cells, which the authors used for cell-type annotation, multi-batch integration, multi-omic integration, genetic perturbation prediction and gene network inference.
excerpts.note: scGPT was trained on 33M CZ CELLxGENE cells for tasks including perturbation prediction and gene network inference.

excerpts.text: However, users do not have the ability to directly interact with models via the hosted web interface. Workflows, such as cell type prediction, projecting a new dataset onto the reference corpus and alignment of unanalyzed modalities, still require downloading an embedding and working outside of the platform.

touchpoints.otherLabel: Platform limitation: model interaction not yet integrated

excerpts.note: Author-conceded limitation: models cannot be used directly in the platform; model-based workflows require external work.

excerpts.text: Importantly, the field has acknowledged the importance of spatial information in the context of single-cell biology (69,70), and ongoing work in both CZ CELLxGENE Explorer as well as Census seeks to support these efforts and technologies. Finally, we recognize the ongoing importance of new modalities beyond dissociated RNA and have begun to provide support for a growing array of data modalities.
excerpts.note: Concedes spatial information and modalities beyond dissociated RNA are important but only emerging in the platform; acknowledges the field recognizes spatial importance.

excerpts.text: In summary, the CZ CELLxGENE platform not only enables data sharing and data reuse through its standardized schema and curation pipeline but also allows researchers across expertise to access and visualize the largest single-cell dataset to date, with over 90 million cells across 449 tissues.
excerpts.note: Summary: standardized schema plus curation pipeline enables sharing and reuse of the largest single-cell dataset to date.

excerpts.text: Critically, the Census has unlocked a new potential for biological insights at scale and provides a low-friction bridge between single-cell biology and the machine learning community.
excerpts.note: Positions Census as the bridge feeding standardized single-cell data into machine learning.

excerpts.text: The data model behind the SOMA specification is flexible and extensible, and it is inspired by existing single-cell data formats, notably AnnData. It can accommodate multiple measurements from derivative views (e.g. spatial and non-spatial data) and embeddings of sparse and dense data, along with both observation (e.g. cells) and feature (e.g. genes) axis annotations. Importantly the flexibility of the SOMA data model allows for representations beyond the single-dataset paradigm and enables managing single-cell data from multiple modalities (e.g. RNA, spatial, epigenomics) across joint or disjoint observations.
excerpts.anchor: Materials and methods — TileDB-SOMA development for census
excerpts.note: SOMA data model is designed to represent multiple modalities (RNA, spatial, epigenomics) across joint or disjoint observations, beyond a single dataset.

excerpts.text: With each schema update, previously submitted data are migrated to meet the new schema, even if that requires re-curation (see the ‘Materials and methods’ section). These data migrations ensure that all datasets are consistently described by the most current standards at all times, and users will have consistent filtering and integration experiences independent of when a given dataset was submitted.
excerpts.note: All previously submitted data are re-migrated on each schema update so the entire corpus stays under one current standard.

excerpts.text: Any cell that has <500 genes expressed is excluded, which filters out about 8% of all data and does not eliminate any cell type in its entirety. This filter reduces noise in the gene expression estimates.
excerpts.anchor: Materials and methods — Removal of low coverage cells
excerpts.note: Quality filter removes cells with <500 genes (~8% of data), a concrete QC criterion.

figuresNumbers.contains: Over 1550 datasets and 169.3 million cells (93.6 million unique cells).
Over 93 million unique cells; growing community-contributed corpus.
Species composition: 63% human, 32% mouse, <5% other species. Human data ~62% healthy donors, 38% spanning 132 unique diseases.
Data across 449 tissues and 40 unique transcriptomic assay types.
Only an estimated 25% of publicly available datasets provide the cell-level metadata needed for reuse.
Minimal schema limited to 11 required fields (Figure 1B shows 10 generally available categories plus is_primary_data).

Dynamically explore individual datasets up to 4.3 million cells in <1 min; interactive comparison of populations up to 1.5 million cells in under 60 s. Existing portals typically tuned to 250-500k cells.
Average recall of 33% across 127 cell types from 14 tissues vs HuBMAP ground truth; P = 0.00-0.04.
For ln(CPM+1), 9/10 (dataset_id) and 6/10 (assay) P values >0.05 aggregated over marker and housekeeping genes; for most cell types no significant difference across covariates.
Out-of-core computations across 65 million cells and >60,000 genes on a regular 8 GB memory laptop; 900+ datasets spanning human and mouse.
scGPT generative pretrained model trained on 33 M cells from CZ CELLxGENE.
Cells with <500 genes expressed excluded, filtering ~8% of all data.
UMAP of all 483,152 cells in the Tabula Sapiens dataset visualized in Explorer.

flags: Metadata unverified: the paper's own venue, year and DOI are not printed in the cleaned text. 'Supplementary Data are available at NAR Online' (line 250) plus the manuscript repo name 'cellxgene-manuscript-2023' strongly suggest Nucleic Acids Research; corpus snapshot is dated 1 October 2024, implying publication ~2024-2025. venue/year/doi therefore set to 'unverified; see flags'/null rather than asserted.
Author block lists CZI Cell Science Program as a group author followed by ~50 named individuals; corresponding authors are Arathi Mani, Jonah Cool and Ambrose Carr (acarr@chanzuckerberg.com).

Scope note: 'Other'-tagged excerpts (corpus composition e12, coverage/representation gaps e13, platform model-interaction limit e26, QC filters e32) are recorded as context/QC color; e13 and e32 also bear on our 'data standard / QC / comparability' theme and could be re-routed to claim 9 / standard axis downstream.

Direction note on e27/e30: the paper concedes spatial and non-RNA modalities are currently weak (challenges our gap claims) while announcing schema/SOMA support for them (a forward-looking nuance); both directions captured rather than sanitized to agreement.
Roadmap-claim id 'A' (modality axis) and 'C' (spatial axis) and 'standard' used as claimId values per the context file's organizing axes; numbered claims 1,2,4,8,9 used as printed.
